Parallel Paths?} Q -->|A equals 2| W[Wave Winding
High Voltage, Low Current] Q -->|A equals P| L[Lap Winding
Low Voltage, High Current] Q -->|A equals m times P| M[Multiplex Lap
Special Applications] W --> R[EMF Equation
E sub b equals P Phi NZ over 120] L --> R2[EMF Equation
E sub b equals Phi NZ over 60] M --> R3[EMF Equation
E sub b equals P Phi NZ over 60 m P] ``` --- ## 4.4 Speed Control Block (From EMF Equation) ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Supply Voltage V] --> Sum{Summing Junction} Eb[Back EMF E sub b] --> Sum IaRa[I sub a times R sub a
Armature Drop] --> Sum Sum --> Vnet[Net Effective Voltage] Vnet --> K[K equals PZ over 60A] Phi[Flux Phi] --> K K --> N[Speed N in RPM
N equals V minus I sub a R sub a over K Phi] N --> Feedback[Speed Feedback
to Drive Controller] Feedback --> V ``` --- # 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the EMF equation of a DC motor. What is the significance of back EMF?** **Model Answer:** The EMF equation of a DC motor is: $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ Where: - **E_b** = Back EMF in Volts - **P** = Number of poles - **Φ** = Flux per pole in Weber - **N** = Speed of armature in RPM - **Z** = Total number of armature conductors - **A** = Number of parallel paths **Significance of Back EMF:** The back EMF (E_b) is the voltage induced in the armature conductors due to their rotation in the magnetic field. It opposes the applied voltage (per Lenz's Law), thereby limiting the armature current and providing **automatic speed regulation**. At steady state, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, and E_b forms the basis of **speed-torque characteristics** and **speed control strategies** used in modern electric drives. Without back EMF, the armature would draw dangerously high currents and the motor would have no inherent speed regulation. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Equation (1) + Significance (1) = **3 marks** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **A 4-pole DC motor with wave-connected armature has 600 conductors and runs at 1500 RPM. Calculate the back EMF if the flux per pole is 0.05 Wb.** **Model Answer:** Given: - P = 4 - Wave winding → A = 2 - Z = 600 - N = 1500 RPM - Φ = 0.05 Wb Applying the EMF equation: $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ $$E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 600}{60 \times 2}$$ $$E_b = \frac{180000}{120}$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 1500 \text{ V}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that **A = 2 for wave winding** (not A = P). This leads to a doubled answer (3000 V) and **full mark deduction**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine from first principles. A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω. At rated load, the armature current is 30 A and the speed is 1200 RPM. Calculate: (a) The back EMF at rated load. (b) The new speed when the armature current doubles due to load increase, assuming flux remains constant.** --- #### **Part (a) — Back EMF at Rated Load [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Identify Given Data** - Supply voltage V = 250 V - Armature resistance R_a = 0.5 Ω - Armature current I_a = 30 A - Speed N₁ = 1200 RPM **Step 2: Apply the Voltage Equation** For a DC motor, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) on the armature circuit gives: $$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$ **Step 3: Solve for E_b** $$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$ $$E_b = 250 - (30 \times 0.5)$$ $$E_b = 250 - 15$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 235 \text{ V}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — New Speed at Doubled Armature Current [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Identify New Condition** New armature current: $I_{a2} = 2 \times 30 = 60$ A Flux remains constant: $\Phi_1 = \Phi_2 = \Phi$ **Step 2: Calculate New Back EMF** $$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a$$ $$E_{b2} = 250 - (60 \times 0.5)$$ $$E_{b2} = 250 - 30$$ $$E_{b2} = 220 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Apply the EMF Equation Ratio Method** Since $E_b \propto \Phi N$, and flux is constant: $$\frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$ $$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}}$$ $$N_2 = 1200 \times \frac{220}{235}$$ $$\boxed{N_2 \approx 1123.4 \text{ RPM}}$$ **Interpretation:** As load increases (higher I_a), the IR drop increases, reducing back EMF, which causes the motor to **slow down** slightly. This natural droop characteristic is fundamental to drive stability analysis. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **A DC machine has 8 poles, 1200 armature conductors, and is lap-wound. The flux per pole is 0.04 Wb. (a) Calculate the back EMF when running at 800 RPM. (b) If the machine is rewound as a wave winding while keeping all other parameters the same, what will be the new back EMF at the same speed and flux? Compare the results and justify the difference.** --- #### **Part (a) — Back EMF with Lap Winding [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Identify Given Data** - P = 8 - Z = 1200 - Lap winding → A = P = 8 - Φ = 0.04 Wb - N = 800 RPM **Step 2: Apply EMF Equation** $$E_{b,\text{lap}} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ $$E_{b,\text{lap}} = \frac{8 \times 0.04 \times 800 \times 1200}{60 \times 8}$$ $$E_{b,\text{lap}} = \frac{307200}{480}$$ $$\boxed{E_{b,\text{lap}} = 640 \text{ V}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Back EMF with Wave Winding [7 Marks]** **Step 1: New Winding Configuration** Wave winding → A = 2 (independent of P) **Step 2: Apply EMF Equation** $$E_{b,\text{wave}} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ $$E_{b,\text{wave}} = \frac{8 \times 0.04 \times 800 \times 1200}{60 \times 2}$$ $$E_{b,\text{wave}} = \frac{307200}{120}$$ $$\boxed{E_{b,\text{wave}} = 2560 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 3: Comparison and Justification** $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{b,\text{wave}}}{E_{b,\text{lap}}} = \frac{2560}{640} = 4$$ The wave winding produces **4 times** more back EMF than lap winding for the same machine. **Justification:** - In lap winding, A = P = 8 parallel paths, so only Z/A = 150 conductors are in series per path. - In wave winding, A = 2 parallel paths, so Z/A = 600 conductors are in series per path — **4× more conductors in series**. - Since EMFs in series add, the wave winding produces **higher terminal EMF**. **Industrial Implication:** - **Lap winding** → Low voltage, high current applications (e.g., traction motors, elevators) - **Wave winding** → High voltage, low current applications (e.g., generators for transmission) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks on EMF Equation Questions:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert RPM to rps (missing the "60" factor). > > 2. **Wrong Parallel Paths (–3 marks):** Using A = P for wave winding or A = 2 for lap winding. Always confirm: **"A = 2 for wave, A = P for lap."** > > 3. **Sign Convention Errors (–1 mark):** Writing $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ (generator formula) instead of $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ (motor formula). > > 4. **Skipping Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** Always write **"Volts"** explicitly. > > 5. **Not stating assumptions in derivations (–2 marks):** KTU examiners expect mention of **uniform flux, constant speed, and Faraday's Law** before derivation. > > 6. **Confusing EMF with Voltage (–1 mark):** Back EMF is **induced**, not applied. Mention "induced EMF" explicitly. --- ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam!** - ✅ **Master Equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ — Write it 10 times before the exam. - ✅ **Three Critical Relationships from EMF Equation:** - $E_b \propto \Phi$ (at constant N) - $E_b \propto N$ (at constant Φ) - $E_b \propto \dfrac{1}{A}$ (winding type effect) - ✅ **Winding Rule of Thumb:** - **Wave** → A = 2 (always), High EMF - **Lap (Simplex)** → A = P, Low EMF, High Current - ✅ **Motor Voltage Equation (Most Used in Problems):** $$V = E_b + I_a R_a \implies E_b = V - I_a R_a$$ - ✅ **Speed Control Foundation:** $$N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} \quad \text{where} \quad K = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$$ - ✅ **Torque Equation (Sister Equation):** $$T = K_T \Phi I_a \quad \text{where} \quad K_T = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$$ - ✅ **Power Balance:** $$P_{\text{electrical}} = V I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$ $$P_{\text{mechanical}} = E_b I_a = T \omega$$ - ✅ **Conversion Factor:** 1 RPM = $\dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s = $\dfrac{\pi}{30}$ rad/s - ✅ **Key Insight:** Doubling the flux halves the speed (at constant V) — this is the principle of **field weakening** in modern drives. - ✅ **Exam Shortcut:** When Φ and V are constant, $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}}$ - ✅ **Real-World Link:** The same $E = k\omega$ relationship governs BLDC, PMSM, and induction motor drives — understanding DC EMF equation unlocks all modern drive analysis.

topics.title: Shunt and series excited DC generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Shunt and series excited DC generators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a DC Generator? A **DC Generator** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts mechanical energy (input via a prime mover) into direct current (DC) electrical energy based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The induced EMF is made unidirectional by the action of a **commutator** and **brush-gear assembly**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In the context of the course *PEAUT525 – Electric Drives and Controls for Automation*, DC generators are studied as **controlled DC voltage sources** that feed the armature circuits of DC motors in closed-loop drives (e.g., Ward-Leonard system). Therefore, emphasis is placed on their **no-load / load characteristics**, **voltage regulation**, and **field excitation schemes**. ### 1.2 Classification Based on Field Excitation The classification relevant to this module is based on how the field winding is energized: | Excitation Type | Field Winding Connection | Power Rating Range | |---|---|---| | **Separately Excited** | External DC source | Small to Medium | | **Self-Excited Shunt** | In parallel with armature | Small to Medium | | **Self-Excited Series** | In series with armature | Medium to Large | | **Compound (Long/Short Shunt)** | Both series + shunt | Large | > [!NOTE] > The word **"Excited"** in the topic title refers to the energization of the **field winding** that produces the working flux $\Phi$ in the air gap. Without field excitation, no useful EMF is induced. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pump Model Think of a DC generator as an intelligent **water pump system**: - The **prime mover** (steam turbine / diesel engine / motor) acts like the person pumping the handle — it provides the **mechanical input**. - The **armature** is like the **piston chamber** where energy conversion occurs. - The **field winding** is the **control valve** — turn it (current) up or down, and you regulate the **pressure** (voltage) of water coming out. - The **commutator–brush set** is the **one-way valve** that converts pulsating AC inside into smooth DC outside. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** In a *shunt* generator, the control valve (field) is connected across the main pipe — so it always gets nearly full pressure, giving a **stable output**. In a *series* generator, the valve is in-line with the flow — so output rises sharply when more water (current) is drawn. This single mental model explains **90 % of their behaviour**. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Symbols | Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | |---|---|---| | $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | **Weber (Wb)** | | $P$ | Number of poles | dimensionless | | $Z$ | Total armature conductors | dimensionless | | $A$ | Number of parallel paths | dimensionless | | $N$ | Speed of rotation | **rpm** | | $E_g$ | Generated EMF | **Volt (V)** | | $I_a$ | Armature current | **Ampere (A)** | | $I_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | **Ampere (A)** | | $I_{se}$ | Series field current | **Ampere (A)** | | $I_L$ | Load current | **Ampere (A)** | | $R_a$ | Armature resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | $R_{sh}$ | Shunt field resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | $R_{se}$ | Series field resistance | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | | $V_t$ | Terminal voltage | **Volt (V)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The EMF Equation of a DC Generator (Universal) This is the **single most important equation** in the entire DC machine module. **Derivation Logic — Step by Step:** 1. Let $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed in **rpm**. 2. Flux cut by one conductor in **one revolution** = $\Phi \cdot P$ Wb (since it passes under $P$ poles). 3. Number of revolutions per **second** = $N/60$. 4. Therefore, average EMF induced per conductor = $\Phi P \cdot N/60$ volts. 5. These conductors are grouped into **$A$ parallel paths**, each path containing $Z/A$ conductors in **series**. 6. Total generated EMF per path = $(Z/A) \cdot \Phi P \cdot N/60$. $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > - For a **wave-wound** armature: $A = 2$ (always). > - For a **lap-wound** armature: $A = P$. > - Most KTU numerical problems specify $A = 2$ unless lap-wound is stated. ## 2.2 Generalised Voltage Equation For **any DC generator** under load: $$V_t = E_g - I_a R_a - V_{brush}$$ Where $V_{brush}$ (brush contact drop) is often neglected ($= 0$) in KTU problems, giving the simplified form: $$V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!NOTE] > The table below is the **single-page revision sheet** every KTU topper memorises. Pay attention — every cell here has appeared in past papers. | # | Quantity | Shunt Generator | Series Generator | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Field current relation | $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ | $I_{se} = I_a = I_L$ | | 2 | Armature current | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | $I_a = I_L$ | | 3 | Generated EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | | 4 | Voltage equation | $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ | $V_t = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | | 5 | Flux expression | $\Phi \propto I_{sh}$ (linear region of OCC) | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (non-linear) | | 6 | Voltage regulation | Poor ($\sim$ 5–10 %) | Poor–Very Poor ($\sim$ 30–50 %) | | 7 | No-load $V_t$ | Maximum (≈ $E_g$) | Zero (since $I_a = 0 \Rightarrow \Phi = 0$) | | 8 | Short-circuit current | Limited (safe) | **Dangerously high** (no-field protection needed) | > [!TIP] > **Important substitution trick (very common in KTU problems):** > Combine $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ with $E_g = k\Phi N$ to obtain: > $$V_t = k\Phi N - I_a R_a$$ > When the **field rheostat** is changed, $\Phi$ changes $\Rightarrow V_t$ changes — this is the principle behind **armature-voltage and field-control of DC motors** studied later in this course. ## 2.4 Shunt Excited DC Generator — Operating Principle **Constructional details:** - The field winding is made of **many turns of thin wire** (high resistance, typically 50–500 $\Omega$). - The field winding is connected in **parallel** with the armature. - The shunt field current is **small** (1–5 % of rated armature current). **Operational sequence:** 1. Residual magnetism in poles induces a small initial EMF ($\approx$ 2–5 V) on rotation. 2. This small EMF circulates a small current through the shunt field. 3. If the field connection polarity aids the residual flux → flux builds → EMF builds → process continues until saturation → **steady-state no-load voltage** is established. 4. On load, terminal voltage drops slightly due to: - Armature resistance drop ($I_a R_a$) - Armature reaction (demagnetising effect of armature flux) > [!WARNING] > **Voltage Build-up Failure Modes (commonly asked 3-mark question):** > - No residual magnetism → no initial EMF → no build-up. > - Field connection reversed → opposes residual flux → no build-up. > - Field rheostat set above critical resistance → no build-up. > - Speed too low → cannot overcome armature & brush drops. ## 2.5 Series Excited DC Generator — Operating Principle **Constructional details:** - The field winding consists of **few turns of thick wire** (low resistance, typically 0.01–1 $\Omega$). - The field winding is connected in **series** with the armature and the load — therefore it carries the **full load current**. - The series field current produces flux: $\Phi = f(I_a)$ (non-linear saturation curve). **Operational sequence:** 1. At no-load, $I_L = 0 \Rightarrow I_{se} = 0 \Rightarrow \Phi = \Phi_{residual}$ only. 2. Therefore, **no-load terminal voltage is very small** (just due to residual flux). 3. As load draws current, $I_{se} = I_L$ increases → flux increases → EMF increases → $V_t$ rises. 4. After magnetic saturation, $\Phi$ becomes almost constant and $V_t$ starts falling due to $I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ drop. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Real-World Utility:** > - **Series generators** were historically used as **arc-lamp generators, voltage boosters (in line with series distribution systems), and as the basic source in the Ward-Leonard speed-control scheme** (drive control) — directly relevant to PEAUT525. > - **Shunt generators** are used in **battery charging, excitation supply for synchronous machines, and as stable DC supplies in control panels**. ## 2.6 Load Characteristics (Most Important Graph) The load characteristic (also called the **external characteristic**) is the graph of $V_t$ vs $I_L$ at constant speed $N$ and constant field rheostat setting. **For Shunt Generator:** Three droops in $V_t$ as $I_L$ increases: - (a) $I_a R_a$ drop — ohmic. - (b) Armature reaction — demagnetising. - (c) Reduction in $I_{sh}$ because $V_t$ itself is falling (since $I_{sh} = V_t/R_{sh}$). **For Series Generator:** Two distinct regions: - (a) Initial linear rise (unsaturated region): $V_t \propto I_L$. - (b) After saturation: $V_t$ peaks and falls due to $I_a(R_a+R_{se})$ drop. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation We start from **Faraday's First Law** of electromagnetic induction: $$\text{EMF} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ For a single conductor rotating in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$, sweeping area $A_c$ in time $t$: $$e_{avg} = \frac{\text{Flux linked}}{\text{Time per revolution}}$$ **Step 1 — Flux cut per conductor per revolution:** A conductor passes under **P poles** in one revolution. Since each pole carries flux $\Phi$, total flux cut per revolution: $$\Phi_{total} = P \cdot \Phi \quad [\text{Weber}]$$ **Step 2 — Time for one revolution:** Speed is $N$ rpm → revolutions per second = $N/60$ → time per revolution: $$t = \frac{60}{N} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$ **Step 3 — Average EMF per conductor:** $$e_{avg} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi}{60/N} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$ **Step 4 — Conductors in series per parallel path:** Total conductors $Z$ are divided into $A$ parallel paths, each containing $Z/A$ conductors in series: $$Z_{series} = \frac{Z}{A}$$ **Step 5 — Total generated EMF (sum of all series conductors in a path):** $$\boxed{E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}}$$ This completes the universal EMF derivation. All DC generator numerical problems reduce to this single equation. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Current, Voltage, Power Relations Let us derive the **complete electrical relationships** for each generator type from Kirchhoff's Laws. ### 3.2.1 Shunt Excited Generator **Step 1 — Apply KCL at the armature node:** $$\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$$ The armature supplies both the load and the shunt field: $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law to the shunt field:** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply KVL around the armature circuit:** $$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$$ **Step 4 — Combine all three to obtain load characteristic implicitly:** $$E_g = V_t + \left(I_L + \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}}\right) R_a$$ Solving for $I_L$ as a function of $V_t$: $$I_L = \frac{E_g - V_t}{R_a} - \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 5 — Power balance:** $$P_{conv} = E_g I_a = V_t I_L + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + I_a^2 R_a + P_{mech,losses}$$ ### 3.2.2 Series Excited Generator **Step 1 — Apply KCL:** Since field is in series, the same current flows through armature, field, and load: $$I_a = I_{se} = I_L$$ **Step 2 — Apply KVL around the closed loop:** $$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se}$$ Substituting $I_a = I_L$: $$\boxed{V_t = E_g - I_L(R_a + R_{se})}$$ **Step 3 — Flux–current relationship (from magnetisation curve):** In the unsaturated linear region: $$\Phi = k I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_g = k \Phi N = k^2 N I_L$$ Substituting in the voltage equation: $$V_t = k^2 N I_L - I_L(R_a + R_{se})$$ $$V_t = I_L \left[ k^2 N - (R_a + R_{se}) \right]$$ This shows that in the **unsaturated region**, $V_t \propto I_L$ — a straight line passing through the origin. **Step 4 — At magnetic saturation:** $\Phi \approx \Phi_{sat} =$ constant, so: $$E_g \approx \frac{P Z N \Phi_{sat}}{60 A} = K N$$ And $V_t$ falls linearly with $I_L$ as $V_t = K N - I_L(R_a + R_{se})$. ## 3.3 Voltage Regulation — Formal Definition **Voltage Regulation (VR)** is the measure of change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage: $$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$ **Derivation for a Shunt Generator:** $$V_{NL} = E_g - I_a R_a \big\vert_{NL} \approx E_g \quad (\text{since } I_{a,NL} \approx I_{sh} \text{ is small})$$ $$V_{FL} = E_g - I_{a,FL} R_a = E_g - (I_L + I_{sh,FL}) R_a$$ $$V_{NL} - V_{FL} = (I_{a,FL} - I_{a,NL}) R_a + \text{armature reaction effect}$$ $$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{(I_{a,FL} - I_{a,NL}) R_a + \Delta V_{AR}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$ **Derivation for a Series Generator:** $$V_{NL} \approx \frac{P Z N \Phi_{residual}}{60 A} \approx 0$$ So the voltage regulation of a series generator is mathematically: $$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = -100\%$$ Wait — this negative value indicates that **no-load voltage is less than full-load voltage** in a series generator, which is why series generators are **never used for ordinary constant-voltage DC supply** — a critical KTU conceptual point. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style) > **Problem:** A 4-pole, wave-wound, 220 V shunt DC generator has 480 armature conductors. The armature and shunt field resistances are $0.1 \, \Omega$ and $110 \, \Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole is $8 \, \text{mWb}$. Calculate: (a) the speed at full load, (b) the armature current at full load delivering 100 A to the load, (c) the generated EMF at full load, (d) the no-load terminal voltage assuming armature reaction is negligible. $\;\;\;$[KTU University Exam - May 2024] ### Solution: **Given:** $P = 4$, wave-wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $Z = 480$, $V_t = 220 \, \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$, $\Phi = 8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Wb}$, $I_L = 100 \, \text{A}$. **Part (a) — Speed at full load:** First, find $I_{sh}$: $$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \, \text{A}$$ Armature current: $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2 = 102 \, \text{A}$$ Generated EMF at full load: $$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a = 220 + 102 \times 0.1 = 220 + 10.2 = 230.2 \, \text{V}$$ Using the EMF equation to solve for $N$: $$E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$ $$230.2 = \frac{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 480 \times N}{60 \times 2}$$ $$230.2 = \frac{15.36 \, N}{120} = 0.128 \, N$$ $$\boxed{N = \frac{230.2}{0.128} \approx 1798.4 \, \text{rpm}}$$ **Part (b) — Armature current at full load:** $$\boxed{I_a = 102 \, \text{A}}$$ **Part (c) — Generated EMF at full load:** $$\boxed{E_g = 230.2 \, \text{V}}$$ **Part (d) — No-load terminal voltage (no armature reaction):** At no-load, $I_L = 0$, so $I_a = I_{sh} = 2 \, \text{A}$ (only field current flows): $$E_g = V_{NL} + I_a R_a = V_{NL} + 2 \times 0.1 = V_{NL} + 0.2$$ Since flux is unchanged, $E_g = 230.2 \, \text{V}$ (same speed assumed): $$V_{NL} = 230.2 - 0.2 = 230.0 \, \text{V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{NL} \approx 230 \, \text{V}}$$ **Voltage Regulation:** $$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{230.0 - 220}{220} \times 100 \approx 4.55\%$$ This excellent regulation confirms the shunt generator's reputation for **stable DC supply**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Schematic — Shunt Excited DC Generator The following Mermaid block renders a detailed block-level architecture of the **shunt DC generator system**, including the prime mover, field circuit, armature circuit, and load. This is the standard diagram reproduced in every KTU textbook. ```mermaid graph LR PM[Prime Mover
Turbine or Engine] -- Mechanical Shaft --> A[Armature
Conductors on Rotating Drum] A -- Induced EMF E_g --> C[Commutator and Brushes] C -- DC Output --> N1[Node A_top] N1 -- V_t --> L1[Load Terminal Positive] N1 -- V_t --> F1[Shunt Field Winding
R_sh = 110 ohm] F1 -- I_sh --> N2[Node A_bot] L1 -- I_L --> LOAD[External Load
R_L] LOAD -- Return --> N2 N2 -- Return Path --> C C -- I_a back --> A N1 --> METER_V[Voltmeter reads V_t] N2 --> METER_V2[Ground Reference] ``` > [!NOTE] > Observe carefully that the **shunt field winding** is in **parallel** with both the **armature** and the **load**. This is the defining structural feature of the shunt configuration. ## 4.2 Circuit Schematic — Series Excited DC Generator ```mermaid graph LR PM[Prime Mover] -- Mechanical Shaft --> A[Armature
Conductors on Rotating Drum] A -- Induced EMF E_g --> C[Commutator and Brushes] C -- I_a --> N1[Node B_top] N1 -- Current Path --> SF[Series Field Winding
R_se - Few Turns Thick Wire] SF -- I_se --> N2[Node B_mid] N2 -- Same Current --> L1[Load Terminal Positive] L1 --> LOAD[External Load
R_L] LOAD --> N3[Node B_bot] N3 -- Return --> C METER_V[Voltmeter V_t across load] -.Measures.-> N2 METER_V -.Measures.-> N3 ``` > [!TIP] > **Key Structural Difference:** In the series generator, the **same current $I_a = I_{se} = I_L$** flows through the armature, field, and load in a single **series loop**. The shunt generator, by contrast, has a **parallel split** at the armature node. ## 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture — Functional Differences ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SHUNT["Shunt Generator"] S1[Armature EMF E_g] --> S2{KCL Node} S2 -->|I_L| S3[Load Branch] S2 -->|I_sh = V_t / R_sh| S4[Shunt Field Branch] S3 --> S5[V_t across load] S4 --> S5 S1 -.Flux Phi_prop I_sh.-> S2 end subgraph SERIES["Series Generator"] T1[Armature EMF E_g] --> T2[Single Series Loop] T2 -->|I_a| T3[Series Field Winding] T3 -->|Same I_a| T4[Load] T4 -->|Return| T2 T1 -.Flux Phi_prop I_a.-> T3 end SHUNT -.Comparison.-> SERIES ``` ## 4.4 Magnetic Characteristic Curves (Block Diagram of Behaviour) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph OCC["Open Circuit Characteristic"] O1[I_sh on X-axis] --> O2[Phi on Y-axis] O2 --> O3[Linear region
Phi proportional to I_sh] O3 --> O4[Saturation Knee] O4 --> O5[Saturation Region
Phi almost constant] end subgraph LOAD_CHAR["External Load Characteristics"] L1[I_L on X-axis] --> L2[V_t on Y-axis] L2 --> L3[Shunt curve: Mild droop] L3 --> L4[Series curve: Rises then falls] end ``` > [!WARNING] > **Diagram Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** — which is flux vs field current — with the **External Characteristic** — which is terminal voltage vs load current. In KTU board valuations, these two are checked separately. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Why is the no-load terminal voltage of a series DC generator almost zero?"** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** At no-load, the load current $I_L = 0$. Since the series field is connected in series with the load, the series field current $I_{se} = I_L = 0$. Hence flux $\Phi$ is only that due to residual magnetism ($\Phi_{res}$), which is very small. The generated EMF $E_g = P\Phi Z N/(60 A)$ is therefore negligible, and terminal voltage $V_t = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) \approx 0$. $\;\;\blacksquare$ ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **"Mention any three conditions for voltage build-up in a self-excited DC shunt generator."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The three essential conditions are: 1. There must be **residual magnetism** in the field poles to initiate EMF build-up. 2. The **field winding connection polarity** must be such that the induced field current **aids** (rather than opposes) the residual flux. 3. The **field circuit resistance** (including the rheostat) must be **less than the critical field resistance** at the operating speed. 4. *(Bonus — speed must be above the critical speed.)* $\;\;\blacksquare$ ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, Module 1 contains a Part-B question of **14 marks** carrying **internal choice** — i.e., the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions below are fully solved. --- ### Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the construction and working of a **shunt-excited DC generator**. Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The armature and shunt field resistances are $0.05 \, \Omega$ and $100 \, \Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole is $0.02 \, \text{Wb}$. If the generator delivers 200 A to the load, calculate: (i) armature current, (ii) generated EMF, (iii) speed of the generator in rpm. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):** **Construction (3 marks):** A DC shunt generator consists of: - **Yoke** — outer frame, supports the pole cores. - **Pole cores and pole shoes** — laminated silicon steel, carry the field winding. - **Field winding** — many turns of fine copper wire wound on the pole cores, connected in parallel with the armature, total resistance $R_{sh}$ = 50–500 $\Omega$. - **Armature core** — slotted laminated cylinder mounted on the shaft, carries the armature winding. - **Armature winding** — closed winding of $Z$ conductors, brought out to the commutator. - **Commutator** — cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica. - **Brush gear** — carbon brushes ride on the commutator, provide DC output. - **Shaft and bearings** — supported by end shields with ball/roller bearings. **Working (2 marks):** When the prime mover rotates the armature in the magnetic flux produced by the shunt field (energised by the induced EMF from residual magnetism), an alternating EMF is induced in each armature conductor. The commutator converts this to unidirectional EMF at the brushes. Under load, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ flows through the armature. **EMF Equation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1: $$E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \quad [\text{Volts}]$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_t = 250 \, \text{V}$, $P = 4$, wave-wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $Z = 600$, $R_a = 0.05 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 100 \, \Omega$, $\Phi = 0.02 \, \text{Wb}$, $I_L = 200 \, \text{A}$. **Step 1 — Shunt field current:** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 2 — (i) Armature current:** $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 200 + 2.5 = \boxed{202.5 \, \text{A}}$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Generated EMF:** $$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a = 250 + 202.5 \times 0.05 = 250 + 10.125 = \boxed{260.125 \, \text{V}}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) Speed:** $$E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$ $$260.125 = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 600 \times N}{60 \times 2} = \frac{48 N}{120} = 0.4 N$$ $$\boxed{N = \frac{260.125}{0.4} = 650.31 \, \text{rpm}}$$ --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the construction and working of a **series-excited DC generator**. Discuss its load (external) characteristic and state two practical applications. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC series generator has 800 armature conductors. The armature and series field resistances are $0.08 \, \Omega$ and $0.04 \, \Omega$ respectively. When delivering a load current of 50 A at 220 V, the flux per pole is $0.025 \, \text{Wb}$. Find: (i) the generated EMF, (ii) the speed of the generator, (iii) the no-load terminal voltage (assuming flux reduces to 10 % of full-load value at no-load). *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):** **Construction (2 marks):** A DC series generator has field winding of **few turns of thick wire** (carrying full armature current) wound on the pole cores, connected in series with the armature. Resistance $R_{se}$ is very small (0.01–1 $\Omega$). **Working (2 marks):** Since field current $I_{se} = I_a = I_L$, the flux $\Phi$ depends on load current via the magnetisation curve. At no-load, $I_L = 0$, so flux $\approx$ residual and $V_t \approx 0$. As load current increases, $V_t$ rises (unsaturated region) and then saturates and falls due to $I_a(R_a+R_{se})$ drop. **External Characteristic (2 marks):** Plot of $V_t$ vs $I_L$: - Initial linear rise (unsaturated region): $V_t \propto I_L$. - Peak at saturation knee. - Linear fall in saturation: $V_t = K N - I_L(R_a + R_{se})$. **Applications (1 mark):** 1. Arc-lamp generators. 2. Series boosters in distribution feeders. 3. *(Bonus)* Excitation supply in special control circuits. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $P = 4$, lap-wound $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$, $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.08 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.04 \, \Omega$, $I_L = 50 \, \text{A}$, $V_t = 220 \, \text{V}$, $\Phi_{FL} = 0.025 \, \text{Wb}$. **Step 1 — Armature current (series → same as load):** $$I_a = I_L = 50 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 2 — (i) Generated EMF at full load:** $$E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 + 50 \times (0.08 + 0.04) = 220 + 50 \times 0.12 = 220 + 6 = \boxed{226 \, \text{V}}$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Speed:** $$E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$ $$226 = \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 800 \times N}{60 \times 4} = \frac{80 N}{240} = \frac{N}{3}$$ $$\boxed{N = 226 \times 3 = 678 \, \text{rpm}}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) No-load terminal voltage:** At no-load: $\Phi_{NL} = 0.1 \times 0.025 = 0.0025 \, \text{Wb}$. No-load $I_a \approx 0$, so $V_t \approx E_{g,NL}$. $$E_{g,NL} = \frac{P \Phi_{NL} Z N}{60 A} = \frac{4 \times 0.0025 \times 800 \times 678}{60 \times 4}$$ $$E_{g,NL} = \frac{5424}{240} = \boxed{22.6 \, \text{V}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $A$ for wave vs lap winding** — This is the **#1 error** in DC generator numericals. Wave = $A=2$, Lap = $A=P$. A single mistake here gives wrong answers for ALL subsequent parts. **Always write $A$ explicitly at the start.** > 2. **Sign convention for current directions** — In KTU board valuations, forgetting $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ for shunt generator (instead writing $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$) costs **2 marks outright**. > 3. **Brush contact drop** — Many textbooks neglect $V_{brush} \approx 2 \, \text{V}$. In KTU 2024 Scheme, unless specifically mentioned, **assume $V_{brush} = 0$**. > 4. **Series generator no-load voltage** — Students frequently write $V_{NL} = 0$ which is technically only true if there is zero residual flux. Always mention **"≈ small value due to residual magnetism"** to gain full marks. > 5. **Not stating the EMF equation** — In Part (a) theory questions, you must **write down the EMF equation** explicitly with a labelled derivation. Without it, you lose 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — print this out before every KTU exam.** - ✅ **EMF Equation (universal):** $E_g = P\Phi Z N/(60 A)$ — must be memorised with units of every symbol. - ✅ **Winding constant:** Wave $\Rightarrow A=2$; Lap $\Rightarrow A=P$. Write this **before** every numerical. - ✅ **Shunt generator:** Field in parallel; $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$; high field resistance (50–500 $\Omega$); good voltage regulation (5–10 %); used for stable DC supply, battery charging, excitation. - ✅ **Series generator:** Field in series; $I_a = I_{se} = I_L$; low field resistance (0.01–1 $\Omega$); poor regulation; $V_t \approx 0$ at no-load; used as boosters and arc-lamp generators. - ✅ **Voltage build-up conditions:** Residual magnetism + correct polarity + field resistance < critical + speed > critical. - ✅ **Voltage Regulation formula:** $\text{VR \%} = (V_{NL} - V_{FL})/V_{FL} \times 100$. - ✅ **Shunt droop causes:** $I_aR_a$ drop + armature reaction + $I_{sh}$ reduction. - ✅ **Series characteristic shape:** Rises (unsaturated) → peaks (knee) → falls (saturated). - ✅ **Power flow:** $P_{mech} \rightarrow P_{conv} = E_g I_a \rightarrow P_{out} = V_t I_L$ + losses ($I_a^2 R_a$, $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$, iron, friction, windage). - ✅ **Armature Reaction:** Demagnetising effect of armature flux weakens main field → reduces $E_g$ → drops $V_t$. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** Ward-Leonard drives (uses series generator as source), cumulative vs differential compounding (extension of series concept), and self-excitation build-up stability. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SASP"**: > - **S**eries = **S**ame current everywhere. > - **A**rmature in shunt = **A**dd shunt current ($I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$). > - **S**eries field = **S**mall resistance (thick wire). > - **P**arallel/shunt field = **P**rohibitively large resistance (thin wire).

topics.title: General idea of armature reaction, OCC and load characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** General idea of armature reaction, OCC and load characteristics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Armature Reaction — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Armature Reaction (AR)** in a DC machine is defined as the *distortion and weakening of the main field flux distribution* produced by the **armature flux** (flux created by current flowing through the armature conductors) when the machine operates under loaded conditions. When a DC machine runs on no-load, only the **main field winding** (stator field poles) produces flux. The brush axis coincides with the **Geometric Neutral Axis (GNA)** — the axis perpendicular to the main field axis. However, when the armature carries current, a new magnetic field (armature flux) is established by the armature conductors, which is stationary in space but rotates with the armature. This armature flux **distorts the original main field flux**, shifting and weakening it. ### 1.2 The Two Harmful Effects of Armature Reaction | Effect | Description | Impact | |---|---|---| | **Demagnetizing Effect** | The component of armature flux directly *opposes* the main field flux | Reduces net flux, lowers induced EMF, reduces voltage regulation | | **Cross-Magnetizing Effect** | The component of armature flux is *perpendicular* to the main field | Distorts the flux distribution, shifts the **Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA)** away from GNA, causing poor commutation and sparking at brushes | ### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "River Current" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are rowing a boat straight across a calm river (this represents the main magnetic field of a DC machine on no-load — uniform, straight, and predictable). Now imagine a strong side current pushes against your boat at an angle (this represents the armature reaction). The result? Your boat no longer moves in a straight line — it gets **deflected** and **slowed down**. The side current is analogous to the armature flux distorting the main field. To "fix" this in DC machines, engineers add **interpoles** (small auxiliary poles) and use **compensating windings** to neutralize the armature flux. ### 1.4 Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC)**, also called the **No-Load Saturation Characteristic (NSC)**, is the curve plotted between the **induced EMF at no-load (E₀)** on the Y-axis and the **field current (I_f)** on the X-axis, with the armature terminals open-circuited and the machine driven at **rated constant speed (N)**. Mathematically, OCC is a plot of: $$E_0 = f(I_f) \quad \text{at } I_a = 0, \; N = N_{rated}$$ ### 1.5 Load Characteristic (External Characteristic) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load Characteristic** (or **External Characteristic**) of a DC generator is the curve plotted between the **terminal voltage (V)** on the Y-axis and the **load current (I_L)** on the X-axis, with the machine operating at **rated speed** and **constant field current**. Mathematically, the load characteristic is: $$V = f(I_L) \quad \text{at } N = N_{rated}, \; I_f = \text{constant}$$ ### 1.6 Why These Characteristics Matter in KTU > [!NOTE] > - **OCC** is essential for determining the **critical field resistance**, **critical speed**, and for finding the **voltage regulation** of a generator. > - **Load characteristics** help in **generator selection** for specific applications (e.g., battery charging requires nearly constant voltage → flat external characteristic). > - Both are fundamental building blocks for **switches and protection schemes** discussed later in this course. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Armature Reaction #### 2.1.1 Origin of Armature Flux When current flows through the armature conductors, each conductor experiences a force **F = BIL**. The collective effect of all such forces (or, equivalently, the magnetomotive force of the armature winding) produces a magnetic field. By applying the **Right-Hand Grip Rule** to the armature conductors, we observe: - The armature flux passes **across the pole faces** (from one pole tip to the other). - The axis of the armature MMF is along the **brush axis** (which lies along the GNA when brushes are set correctly). - The armature MMF is **perpendicular** to the main field MMF axis. #### 2.1.2 Why Brushes are Shifted from GNA In a practical DC machine, brushes are shifted from GNA by an angle **θ** to ensure: 1. The commutator segments short-circuited by the brush span only those coils undergoing commutation. 2. The coil undergoing commutation has **zero rate of change of flux linkage** (i.e., it lies in the MNA). This brush shift angle causes the armature reaction flux to be resolved into **two components**: | Component | Direction | Effect | |---|---|---| | **Demagnetizing MMF (F_d)** | Anti-parallel to main field flux | **Reduces** net flux per pole | | **Cross-magnetizing MMF (F_c)** | Perpendicular to main field flux | **Distorts** flux distribution, shifts MNA | #### 2.1.3 Mathematical Expressions for Demagnetizing and Cross-Magnetizing Ampere-Turns For a wave-wound machine with brush shift angle **θ** (mechanical degrees) from GNA: $$F_d = Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\frac{\theta_m}{180°}\right) \quad \text{[AT/pole, demagnetizing]}$$ $$F_c = Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right) \quad \text{[AT/pole, cross-magnetizing]}$$ For a lap-wound machine with brush shift angle **θ**: $$F_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{\theta_m}{180°}\right)$$ $$F_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$$ Where: - **Z** = Total number of armature conductors - **I_a** = Armature current (in Amperes) - **P** = Number of poles - **θ_m** = Mechanical brush shift angle in degrees > [!NOTE] > In modern KTU problems, θ is often given as electrical degrees. The mechanical angle = electrical angle / (P/2). ### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) #### 2.2.1 Derivation of Induced EMF Equation The EMF induced in a DC machine is given by: $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}\right)$$ Where: - **E_g** = Generated EMF (Volts) - **Φ** = Flux per pole (Webers) - **Z** = Total armature conductors - **N** = Speed in RPM - **P** = Number of poles - **A** = Number of parallel paths (= P for lap winding, = 2 for wave winding) #### 2.2.2 Shape of the OCC Curve The OCC is non-linear because of **magnetic saturation** of the iron core: 1. **Initial Region (Linear):** Low I_f → Φ ∝ I_f → straight line (E₀ ∝ I_f) 2. **Knee Region:** Iron begins to saturate → slope decreases 3. **Saturation Region:** Further increase in I_f yields negligible increase in Φ → curve flattens > [!IMPORTANT] > The **OCC does not start from the origin** because of **residual magnetism** in the pole cores. This is critical for the **self-excitation** principle in DC shunt generators. #### 2.2.3 Critical Field Resistance and Critical Speed - **Critical Field Resistance (R_c):** The maximum slope of the field resistance line that allows the generator to **self-excite**. If R_f > R_c, the generator fails to build up voltage. - **Critical Speed (N_c):** The minimum speed at which the generator can build up rated voltage for a given R_f. ### 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Load Characteristics #### 2.3.1 Voltage Equation of a DC Generator $$V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) \quad \text{[For Compound Generator]}$$ $$V = E_g - I_a R_a \quad \text{[For Shunt/Separately Excited Generator]}$$ Where: - **V** = Terminal voltage - **E_g** = Generated EMF - **I_a** = Armature current - **R_a** = Armature resistance - **R_se** = Series field resistance #### 2.3.2 Causes of Voltage Drop in Load Characteristic 1. **Ohmic drop (I_a R_a):** Resistance of armature winding 2. **Armature reaction drop:** Demagnetizing effect reduces Φ, hence reduces E_g 3. **Brush contact drop:** Typically ~2V (constant, not dependent on I_a) #### 2.3.3 Voltage Regulation $$\text{Voltage Regulation (\%)} = \frac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$ Where **E₀** = No-load terminal voltage, **V_FL** = Full-load terminal voltage. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **EMF Equation** | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | All DC machines | | 2 | **Demagnetizing AT/pole (Lap)** | $F_d = \dfrac{Z I_a}{P} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_m}{180°}$ | With brush shift | | 3 | **Cross-mag AT/pole (Lap)** | $F_c = \dfrac{Z I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$ | With brush shift | | 4 | **Voltage Equation (Shunt Gen)** | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Generator loaded | | 5 | **Voltage Equation (Comp Gen)** | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Compound generator | | 6 | **Voltage Regulation** | $\%VR = \dfrac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Quality of regulation | | 7 | **OCC Relation** | $E_0 \propto \Phi \propto I_f$ (linear region) | Magnetic circuit | | 8 | **Condition for Self-Excitation** | $R_f \leq R_{critical}$ | Shunt generator build-up | | 9 | **Shunt Field Current** | $I_f = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | At constant terminal V | | 10 | **Armature Current (Shunt Gen)** | $I_a = I_L + I_f$ | KCL at terminal | | 11 | **Generated EMF (Compensated)** | $E_g \approx V + I_a R_a$ (approx) | Quick calculation | | 12 | **Critical Speed** | $N_c = N \cdot \dfrac{V_{oc}}{E_g}$ | For voltage build-up | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Automobile Alternators/Generators:** Armature reaction compensation is critical in modern EV traction motors. > - **Battery Chargers:** Use **differentially compounded** generators whose load characteristic is a **rising curve** (drooping is unwanted). > - **Power Station DC Generators:** Use **interpoles** and **compensating windings** to neutralize armature reaction effects. > - **Welding Generators:** Use specially designed **differentially compounded** generators with sharply drooping external characteristics for constant current output. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EMF Equation of a DC Machine #### Step 1: Setup and Definitions Consider a DC machine with the following parameters: - **P** = Number of poles - **Φ** = Flux per pole (in Webers) - **Z** = Total number of armature conductors - **N** = Speed of armature (in RPM) - **A** = Number of parallel paths in armature winding #### Step 2: Flux Cut by One Conductor per Revolution As the armature rotates through one complete revolution, each conductor cuts the magnetic flux produced by **all P poles**. $$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P \quad \text{[Weber-revolutions]}$$ #### Step 3: Time for One Revolution The armature completes N revolutions in 60 seconds, so the time per revolution is: $$\text{Time per revolution} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ #### Step 4: Average EMF Induced per Conductor By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is: $$e_{avg} = \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{\Phi P}{\left(\frac{60}{N}\right)} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{[Volts per conductor]}$$ #### Step 5: Total EMF Generated in the Armature Since there are **Z conductors** connected in **A parallel paths**, the number of conductors **in series per path** is **Z/A**. The total generated EMF is the EMF per parallel path (since each path is in series): $$E_g = e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$ $$E_g = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$ $$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \quad \text{[Volts]}}$$ #### Step 6: Verification with Special Cases - For **Lap winding**: A = P, so $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}$ (independent of P) - For **Wave winding**: A = 2, so $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{120}$ (depends on P) > [!NOTE] > This explains why **lap-wound machines** are preferred for high-current applications (more parallel paths share the current), while **wave-wound machines** are preferred for high-voltage applications (more conductors in series per path). --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Demagnetizing and Cross-Magnetizing Ampere-Turns #### Step 1: Concept of Zones in Armature Winding The armature winding (with brush shift angle θ_m from GNA) can be divided into two distinct zones: | Zone | Angle Range | Effect | |---|---|---| | **Demagnetizing Zone** | $2\theta_m$ (centered on inter-polar axis) | Produces MMF opposing main field | | **Cross-magnetizing Zone** | $360° - 2\theta_m$ | Produces MMF perpendicular to main field | #### Step 2: Total Conductor Distribution Total armature conductors = **Z** Conductors in the demagnetizing zone = $\dfrac{Z \cdot 2\theta_m}{360°} = \dfrac{Z \cdot \theta_m}{180°}$ Conductors in the cross-magnetizing zone = $Z - \dfrac{Z \cdot \theta_m}{180°} = Z \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\theta_m}{180°}\right)$ #### Step 3: Current in Demagnetizing Conductors (Lap Wound) For a lap-wound machine with P poles and A = P parallel paths: - Current per conductor = $I_a / A = I_a / P$ (for simple lap, conductors carry $I_a/A$) Wait — for a more rigorous derivation, in a **simple lap winding**, each parallel path carries $I_a / A$, and there are 2 conductors per parallel path between adjacent brushes. Each conductor carries current $I_c = I_a / A$. For a wave winding, each conductor carries $I_a / 2$. #### Step 4: General Formula (Per Pole Basis) The general demagnetizing AT/pole for a lap-wound machine is: $$F_d = \text{(conductors in demag zone per pole)} \times \text{(current per conductor)}$$ $$F_d = \left(\frac{Z}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°}\right) \times \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right)$$ Since for lap winding, A = P: $$\boxed{F_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°} \quad \text{[AT/pole]}}$$ Similarly: $$\boxed{F_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right) \quad \text{[AT/pole]}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — KTU Style **Problem:** A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 480 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.1 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 100 Ω. The brush is shifted by 3° mechanical from GNA. If the machine delivers 100 A to the load, calculate: 1. Demagnetizing AT/pole 2. Cross-magnetizing AT/pole 3. Generated EMF (neglecting armature reaction) **Given Data:** - V = 250 V - P = 4 - Z = 480 - Wave wound → A = 2 - R_a = 0.1 Ω - R_sh = 100 Ω - θ_m = 3° (mechanical) - I_L = 100 A **Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Shunt Field Current** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \; A$$ **Step 2: Calculate Armature Current** $$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \; A$$ **Step 3: Calculate Demagnetizing AT/pole** For a wave-wound machine, the general formula is: $$F_d = Z \cdot I_c \cdot \left(\frac{\theta_m}{180°}\right) \quad \text{per pole}$$ Where $I_c$ is the current per conductor. For wave winding, A = 2, so $I_c = I_a / 2 = 51.25 \; A$ $$F_d = 480 \times 51.25 \times \left(\frac{3}{180}\right)$$ $$F_d = 480 \times 51.25 \times 0.01667$$ $$F_d = 410 \; \text{AT/pole}$$ **Step 4: Calculate Cross-Magnetizing AT/pole** $$F_c = Z \cdot I_c \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$$ $$F_c = 480 \times 51.25 \times \left(1 - \frac{6}{180}\right)$$ $$F_c = 480 \times 51.25 \times 0.9667$$ $$F_c = 23770 \; \text{AT/pole}$$ **Step 5: Calculate Generated EMF (neglecting AR)** $$E_g = V + I_a R_a = 250 + 102.5 \times 0.1 = 250 + 10.25$$ $$E_g = 260.25 \; V$$ **Summary of Results:** | Parameter | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | I_sh | 2.5 | A | | I_a | 102.5 | A | | F_d | 410 | AT/pole | | F_c | 23770 | AT/pole | | E_g | 260.25 | V | --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Generator Types | Generator Type | Field Connection | Load Characteristic Shape | Voltage Regulation | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Separately Excited** | External DC source | Drooping (due to AR & I_aR_a) | ~3-5% (good) | Laboratory, precise control | | **Self-Excited Shunt** | Across armature | Slightly drooping | ~5-10% | General lighting, battery charging | | **Series Generator** | In series with armature | Rising curve | Poor (negative) | Boosters, series arc lamps | | **Cumulative Compound** | Series field aids shunt | Nearly flat / rising | ~2-5% (excellent) | Power distribution, general purpose | | **Differential Compound** | Series field opposes shunt | Sharply drooping | Negative | Welding, constant current loads | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Armature Reaction Effect ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Main Field Winding
DC Excitation I_f] nodeB[Main Field Flux
Phi_main along Pole Axis] nodeC[Armature Conductors
Carry Current I_a] nodeD[Armature MMF
along Brush Axis GNA] nodeE[Resultant Flux
Phi_net shifted and reduced] nodeF[Magnetic Neutral Axis
MNA shifts forward] nodeG[Commutation Sparking
at Brushes] nodeH[Reduced EMF
E_g decreases] nodeI[Poor Voltage
Regulation] nodeJ[Interpoles and
Compensating Windings] nodeK[Compensating Flux
Opposes Armature Flux] nodeL[Net Armature Flux
Approximately Zero] nodeM[Restored Original
Flux Distribution] nodeA --> nodeB nodeC --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM ``` ### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Open Circuit Characteristic Build-Up ```mermaid graph LR stepA[Residual Flux
Phi_r in Poles] stepB[Armature Rotated
at Speed N] stepC[Small EMF Induced
E_0 = k Phi_r N] stepD[Current Flows
in Field Winding] stepE[Field Flux Increases
Phi increases] stepF[More EMF Induced
E_0 increases] stepG[Iterative Build-Up
Continues] stepH[Saturation Reached
Steady State E_0] stepI[OCC Curve
Plotted E_0 vs I_f] stepJ[Check R_f less than
R_critical] stepK[Voltage Builds Up
Successfully] stepL[Check R_f greater than
R_critical] stepM[Voltage Fails
to Build] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ stepJ --> stepK stepI --> stepL stepL --> stepM ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow — Load Characteristic Behavior ```mermaid graph TD stageA[Generator at No-Load
E_0 = V_oc] stageB[Load Connected
Current I_L Flows] stageC[Armature Current I_a Increases] stageD[Ohmic Drop I_a R_a Appears] stageE[Armature Reaction
Demagnetizing Effect] stageF[Net Flux Phi Decreases] stageG[Generated EMF E_g Decreases] stageH[Terminal Voltage V Drops] stageI[Load Characteristic
V vs I_L Curve] stageJ[Voltage Regulation
Computed] stageA --> stageB stageB --> stageC stageC --> stageD stageC --> stageE stageE --> stageF stageF --> stageG stageD --> stageH stageG --> stageH stageH --> stageI stageI --> stageJ ``` ### 4.4 Schematic of Armature Reaction Components ```mermaid graph TD compA[Brush Axis GNA
Brush Shift Angle Theta] compB[Demagnetizing MMF
Anti-parallel to Main Field] compC[Cross-Magnetizing MMF
Perpendicular to Main Field] compD[Main Field Axis
Direct Axis] compE[Quadrature Axis
90 degrees to Main Field] compF[Resultant Armature MMF
Vector Sum] compA --> compB compA --> compC compD --> compF compE --> compF compF --> compB compF --> compC ``` ### 4.5 OCC Curve Shape — Mermaid Representation ```mermaid graph LR ptA[Origin I_f equals 0
E_0 equals Residual EMF] ptB[Linear Region
E_0 proportional to I_f] ptC[Knee Point
Saturation Begins] ptD[Saturation Region
Slope Decreases] ptE[Full Saturation
E_0 nearly constant] ptF[Field Resistance Line
Slope R_f] ptA --> ptB ptB --> ptC ptC --> ptD ptD --> ptE ptF -.Intersects.-> ptC ``` > [!NOTE] > In the diagram above, the **Field Resistance Line** (slope = R_f) must intersect the OCC in the saturation region for successful voltage build-up. The **tangent drawn from origin to OCC** gives the **Critical Field Resistance R_c**. ### 4.6 Comparison Block: Types of DC Generator Load Characteristics ```mermaid graph TD catA[DC Generators] catB[Separately Excited] catC[Self-Excited Shunt] catD[Series Generator] catE[Cumulative Compound] catF[Differential Compound] charA[Slightly Drooping V vs I_L] charB[Moderately Drooping] charC[Rising Curve] charD[Flat or Slightly Rising] charE[Sharply Drooping] useA[Lab Applications] useB[Lighting and Battery Charging] useC[Boosters Arc Lamps] useD[General Power Distribution] useE[Welding and Constant Current] catA --> catB catA --> catC catA --> catD catA --> catE catA --> catF catB --> charA catC --> charB catD --> charC catE --> charD catF --> charE charA --> useA charB --> useB charC --> useC charD --> useE charE --> useD ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams use **functional architecture** mapping rather than physical coil drawings, which is more reliable for Mermaid rendering and KTU pedagogical clarity. Each block represents a system-level interaction relevant to the topic. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term "armature reaction" in a DC machine. List its two effects. **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Armature Reaction** is the effect of the magnetic field produced by the armature current on the distribution of the main field flux in a DC machine. > > **Two Effects:** > 1. **Demagnetizing Effect:** The armature flux component along the main field axis opposes the main flux, thereby reducing the net flux per pole. > 2. **Cross-Magnetizing Effect:** The armature flux component perpendicular to the main field axis distorts the main flux distribution, causing a shift in the Magnetic Neutral Axis (MNA). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** What is meant by Open Circuit Characteristic (OCC) of a DC generator? Why does the OCC not start from the origin? **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **OCC** of a DC generator is the curve plotted between the **no-load generated EMF (E₀)** (Y-axis) and the **field current (I_f)** (X-axis), with the armature terminals open and the machine running at rated constant speed. > > **Why OCC does not start from origin:** The OCC does not start from the origin because of the presence of **residual magnetism** in the pole cores. Even when the field current is zero, this residual flux induces a small EMF (typically 2-5% of rated voltage) in the armature. This residual magnetism is essential for the **self-excitation** of DC shunt generators. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The brushes are shifted by 4° mechanical from the Geometric Neutral Axis. The armature carries a current of 200 A. Calculate: (i) Demagnetizing ampere-turns per pole (ii) Cross-magnetizing ampere-turns per pole **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the OCC and load characteristics of a DC shunt generator. Discuss the effect of armature reaction on these characteristics. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Given Data:** - P = 4 (poles) - Z = 600 (armature conductors) - Lap wound → A = P = 4 - θ_m = 4° (mechanical brush shift) - I_a = 200 A **Step 1: Demagnetizing AT/pole** For a lap-wound machine, the demagnetizing AT/pole is given by: $$F_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°}$$ $$F_d = \frac{600 \times 200}{4} \cdot \frac{4}{180}$$ $$F_d = 30000 \times 0.02222$$ $$\boxed{F_d = 666.67 \; \text{AT/pole}}$$ **Step 2: Cross-Magnetizing AT/pole** $$F_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$$ $$F_c = 30000 \times \left(1 - \frac{8}{180}\right)$$ $$F_c = 30000 \times 0.9556$$ $$\boxed{F_c = 28666.67 \; \text{AT/pole}}$$ --- **Model Solution (b):** **OCC of DC Shunt Generator:** The OCC is obtained by running the generator at rated speed with the armature terminals open and varying the field current through a potentiometer/rheostat. The induced EMF E₀ is measured for each value of I_f. The curve has three regions: 1. **Initial Linear Region:** E₀ increases linearly with I_f (since Φ ∝ I_f in unsaturated region) 2. **Knee Region:** Iron begins to saturate; the curve bends 3. **Saturation Region:** Slope becomes very small; further increase in I_f produces negligible increase in Φ The OCC **does not start from origin** because of residual magnetism in pole cores. **Load (External) Characteristic of DC Shunt Generator:** The load characteristic is obtained by loading the generator at rated speed and constant field current, and measuring terminal voltage V for various load currents I_L. The curve **droops downward** as I_L increases due to: 1. **Ohmic drop (I_a R_a):** Voltage drop across armature resistance 2. **Armature reaction:** Demagnetizing effect reduces net flux, hence reduces E_g 3. **Brush contact drop:** Approximately 2V (constant) **Effect of Armature Reaction on Characteristics:** - The **OCC** is recorded at no-load, so it is **unaffected** by armature reaction. - The **load characteristic** is significantly affected because as load increases, armature reaction **reduces the net flux**, which **decreases E_g**, and consequently the **terminal voltage drops faster** than it would due to ohmic drop alone. - This results in **poorer voltage regulation**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. A 250 kW, 400 V, 6-pole, 1500 RPM DC shunt generator has 720 armature conductors. The brushes are displaced by 1.5 commutator segments from the GNA. Calculate the demagnetizing and cross-magnetizing AT/pole if the machine is delivering full-load current. Assume lap winding and 4 segments per pole. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the load characteristics of shunt, series, and compound (cumulative and differential) DC generators. State suitable applications of each type. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **EMF Equation Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1, Step 1 to Step 5) $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$ **Numerical Problem Solution:** **Given Data:** - Power = 250 kW - V = 400 V - P = 6 (poles) - N = 1500 RPM - Z = 720 - Brush shift = 1.5 commutator segments - Segments per pole = 4 - Lap winding → A = P = 6 - Full load condition **Step 1: Calculate Mechanical Brush Shift Angle** Brush shift in terms of commutator segments corresponds to a certain mechanical angle. Angle per segment = 360° / (Number of segments) If segments per pole = 4, total segments = 4 × 6 = 24 Angle per segment = 360° / 24 = 15° $$\theta_m = 1.5 \times 15° = 22.5°$$ **Step 2: Calculate Full-Load Current** $$I_L = \frac{Power}{V} = \frac{250000}{400} = 625 \; A$$ **Step 3: Calculate Armature Current (neglecting I_sh first approximation, then refine)** I_sh is usually small (~1-2% of I_L). For approximation: $$I_a \approx I_L = 625 \; A$$ **Step 4: Demagnetizing AT/pole** $$F_d = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°}$$ $$F_d = \frac{720 \times 625}{6} \cdot \frac{22.5}{180}$$ $$F_d = 75000 \times 0.125$$ $$\boxed{F_d = 9375 \; \text{AT/pole}}$$ **Step 5: Cross-Magnetizing AT/pole** $$F_c = \frac{Z \cdot I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$$ $$F_c = 75000 \times \left(1 - \frac{45}{180}\right)$$ $$F_c = 75000 \times 0.75$$ $$\boxed{F_c = 56250 \; \text{AT/pole}}$$ --- **Model Solution (b):** **Comparison Table of Load Characteristics:** | Generator Type | Shape of V vs I_L | Voltage Regulation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Shunt Generator** | Curve droops slightly as I_L increases | Moderate (5-10%) | Lighting circuits, battery charging, general purpose | | **Series Generator** | Curve rises with I_L (since flux ∝ I_a) | Poor (negative regulation) | Boosters, series arc lighting, as a booster in feeders | | **Cumulative Compound** | Nearly flat or slightly rising at low loads, then drooping | Good (2-5%) | Power distribution, general power supply, motor loads | | **Differential Compound** | Sharply drooping (since series flux opposes shunt flux) | Negative (steep drop) | Welding generators, electric arc furnaces, constant current applications | **Detailed Comparison:** 1. **Shunt Generator:** The terminal voltage drops with load due to (i) I_a R_a drop and (ii) armature reaction. The drop is gradual because the shunt field remains nearly constant. 2. **Series Generator:** The terminal voltage rises with load because the flux increases with load current (until saturation). Beyond saturation, it behaves like a constant-flux machine. 3. **Cumulative Compound Generator:** The series field aids the shunt field. At low loads, the rise due to series field may compensate the drop due to armature reaction, giving a nearly flat characteristic. With proper design, it can give constant voltage at all loads. 4. **Differential Compound Generator:** The series field opposes the shunt field. The terminal voltage drops rapidly with load, providing a constant-current-like characteristic useful for welding. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing A (parallel paths) with Z (total conductors):** A is the number of parallel paths, not the total number of conductors. For lap winding A = P, for wave winding A = 2. Using the wrong value of A leads to incorrect E_g calculations. > 2. **Using θ as electrical instead of mechanical angle:** In demagnetizing AT formulas, θ must be in **mechanical degrees**. If the problem gives electrical degrees, divide by (P/2) to get mechanical degrees. Forgetting this conversion is a common 2-mark loss. > 3. **Neglecting I_sh in I_a calculation:** For DC shunt generators, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$, NOT $I_a = I_L$. This is a classic KTU valuation trap. > 4. **Forgetting brush contact drop:** The brush contact drop (~2V) is sometimes asked but often omitted. Account for it in the V = E_g - I_a R_a - brush_drop equation. > 5. **OCC starting from origin:** The OCC does **not** start from origin. Drawing it from the origin is a structural mistake that costs marks. > 6. **Confusing the directions of demagnetizing vs cross-magnetizing effects:** Demagnetizing AT opposes the main field; cross-magnetizing AT is perpendicular. Mixing these up leads to incorrect physical interpretation answers. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - [x] **Armature Reaction** = Effect of armature current on main field → produces **demagnetizing** + **cross-magnetizing** components. - [x] **Demagnetizing AT/pole (Lap)** = $\frac{Z I_a}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°}$ - [x] **Cross-Mag AT/pole (Lap)** = $\frac{Z I_a}{P} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\theta_m}{180°}\right)$ - [x] **EMF Equation** = $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — universal for all DC machines. - [x] **OCC** = plot of $E_0$ vs $I_f$ at $I_a = 0$, $N = N_{rated}$. Does **not** start from origin (residual magnetism). - [x] **OCC Shape** = linear → knee → saturation. Three distinct regions. - [x] **Critical Field Resistance** = slope of tangent from origin to OCC; condition for self-excitation: $R_f < R_c$. - [x] **Load Characteristic** = $V$ vs $I_L$ at $N = N_{rated}$, $I_f$ = constant. - [x] **Causes of Voltage Drop in Load Char:** (1) I_a R_a, (2) Armature reaction, (3) Brush contact drop. - [x] **Voltage Regulation** = $\frac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ - [x] **Shunt Generator Load Char:** Slightly drooping curve. - [x] **Series Generator Load Char:** Rising curve. - [x] **Cumulative Compound Load Char:** Nearly flat (good regulation). - [x] **Differential Compound Load Char:** Sharply drooping (welding use). - [x] **Compensation Methods** for Armature Reaction: (1) Interpoles, (2) Compensating windings, (3) Brush shifting (limited use). - [x] **Wave Winding:** A = 2, used for high voltage; **Lap Winding:** A = P, used for high current. - [x] **Voltage Equation (Shunt Gen):** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ - [x] **Voltage Equation (Comp Gen):** $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Exam (Memorize First):** > 1. $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ > 2. $F_d = \frac{Z I_a}{P} \cdot \frac{\theta_m}{180°}$ (Lap) > 3. $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ > 4. $\%VR = \frac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ > 5. $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$

topics.title: Principles of series and shunt DC motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of series and shunt DC motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a DC Motor?

A **Direct Current (DC) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy supplied from a DC source into mechanical energy (rotational motion) based on the interaction between the magnetic field of the field winding and the current-carrying armature conductors, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Principle**.

The internal construction of a DC machine consists of two essential electromagnetic circuits:
1. **Field Winding (Stator)**: Produces the working magnetic flux ($\Phi$) in the air gap.
2. **Armature Winding (Rotor)**: Carries the load current ($I_a$) and is placed in the magnetic field to produce torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PEAUT525 - Module 1):**
> The classification of DC motors is fundamentally based on the **electrical interconnection** between the field winding and the armature winding. This single connection choice dictates the entire performance, characteristic curves, and application domain of the motor.

## 1.2 The Two Principal Configurations

### A. Series DC Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> A **DC Series Motor** is a DC machine in which the **field winding is connected in series with the armature winding**, so that the **same current flows through both the armature and the field winding**. The field winding has a **small number of turns of thick wire** to carry the full armature current with minimum voltage drop.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Sibling Team"**:
Imagine two runners tied at the waist with a rope, running around a circular track. Runner A is the **armature** (carrying current and producing torque), and Runner B is the **field** (producing magnetic flux). Because they are *tied together*, if Runner A sprints faster, Runner B is dragged along faster too. In the same way, in a series motor, when the load demands more current from the armature, that exact same current also energizes the field, making the magnetic flux **rise with the current**. This is why the series motor is so "powerful" (high torque) at heavy loads — both the magnetic field and the current grow together.

### B. Shunt (Parallel) DC Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> A **DC Shunt Motor** is a DC machine in which the **field winding is connected in parallel (shunt) with the armature winding** across the DC supply. The shunt field has a **large number of turns of thin wire** to produce the rated flux with a small field current ($I_{sh}$).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Independent Manager"**:
Picture an office where the floor manager (field winding) and the workers on the shop floor (armature) report to the same boss (DC supply voltage) but operate on **independent electrical circuits**. The boss (supply voltage) stays roughly constant, so the manager's instructions (magnetic flux $\Phi$) remain steady throughout the day, regardless of how hard the workers labor. This stable, independent flux is what gives the shunt motor its famous **constant-speed behavior** — ideal for fans, lathes, and printing machines.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V$ | **Volt (V)** |
| Armature Current | $I_a$ | **Ampere (A)** |
| Field Current | $I_{sh}$ or $I_f$ | **Ampere (A)** |
| Magnetic Flux per pole | $\Phi$ | **Weber (Wb)** |
| Back EMF | $E_b$ | **Volt (V)** |
| Armature Resistance | $R_a$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** |
| Shunt Field Resistance | $R_{sh}$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** |
| Speed of rotation | $N$ | **RPM** |
| Angular Speed | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Developed Torque | $T$ | **N·m** |
| Output Power | $P$ | **Watt (W)** |
| Machine Constant (DC motor) | $k$ | **V·s/Wb·rad or N·m/A·Wb** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Conversion to Remember:**
> $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s, when N is in RPM)}$$
> The product $k\Phi$ is often lumped together as a single "motor constant" $K$ for ease of analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Equations of Any DC Motor

Regardless of whether it is series, shunt, or compound, every DC motor obeys **two master equations**:

### Equation 1: Back EMF Equation (Generated EMF)

When the armature rotates inside the magnetic field, Faraday's Law dictates that an EMF is induced in the armature conductors. By **Lenz's Law**, this EMF *opposes* the applied voltage — hence called **back EMF**, denoted $E_b$.

$$E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

Or equivalently in terms of speed $N$ in RPM:

$$E_b = \frac{k \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \cdot 2\pi = K_a \cdot \Phi \cdot N$$

> **Why it matters**: $E_b$ is the "regulating force" of the motor. It rises with speed and effectively limits the armature current draw.

### Equation 2: Torque Equation (Developed Electromagnetic Torque)

The interaction between the armature current and the magnetic flux produces torque. The tangential force on each conductor summed over all conductors yields:

$$T = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

> **Why it matters**: This is the *only* equation that controls the mechanical output. For a constant $\Phi$ (shunt motor), torque is **linear in current**; for a series motor (linear region, unsaturated), $\Phi \propto I_a$ so torque becomes **proportional to $I_a^2$** — a quadratic relationship that gives the series motor its phenomenal starting capability.

## 2.2 The Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on Armature Loop)

Applying KVL around the armature circuit (supply → armature resistance drop → back EMF):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Rearranging for the back EMF:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

This is the **speed-regulating equation**, because substituting $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ gives the famous **speed equation**:

$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi} \quad \text{or} \quad N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$

## 2.3 Power Flow in a DC Motor

The power balance in any DC motor is governed by the law of conservation of energy:

$$P_{input} = P_{developed} + P_{armature\,Cu\,loss}$$

$$\underbrace{V \cdot I_a}_{Electrical\,Input} = \underbrace{E_b \cdot I_a}_{Mechanical\,Power\,Developed} + \underbrace{I_a^2 R_a}_{Copper\,Loss}$$

> **Real-World Utility**: This equation is the basis of all DC motor efficiency calculations, braking analysis, and energy audit reports in industrial automation. In robotics and EV applications, engineers use this to compute the battery drain rate.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Application | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature Voltage Equation | Universal for all DC motors |
| 2 | $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ | Back EMF Equation | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 3 | $T = k_T \Phi I_a$ | Torque Equation | $T$ in N·m, $k_T$ = $k$ (in SI) |
| 4 | $\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ | Speed Equation | Universal speed formula |
| 5 | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) | Series Motor Flux Relation | Only before magnetic saturation |
| 6 | $\Phi = \text{constant}$ | Shunt Motor Flux Relation | Assumes $V$ and $R_{sh}$ are constant |
| 7 | $T \propto I_a^2$ | Series Motor Torque | Linear region, unsaturated |
| 8 | $T \propto I_a$ | Shunt Motor Torque | Constant $\Phi$ |
| 9 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\Phi}$ (at constant $V$ and $I_a R_a$) | Series Motor Speed | Speed falls sharply with load |
| 10 | $N \approx \text{constant}$ | Shunt Motor Speed | Excellent speed regulation |
| 11 | $N \propto V - I_a R_a$ | Speed Regulation Insight | Drop in $N$ is small for shunt |
| 12 | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | Shunt Motor Line Current | $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ |
| 13 | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ | Series Motor Line Current | Single current path |
| 14 | $P_{dev} = T \cdot \omega$ | Developed Mechanical Power | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The two equations $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ and $T = k_T\Phi I_a$ are **the most repeated equations in KTU university exams**. Always state them at the start of any DC motor numerical answer.

## 2.5 Detailed Working Principle Comparison

### Working of Series DC Motor
1. When DC supply $V$ is switched on, current $I_a$ begins to flow through the **series combination** of armature and field winding.
2. This current produces a magnetic flux $\Phi$ that is **directly proportional to the armature current** in the linear region of the magnetization curve.
3. The armature current also interacts with this flux to develop torque $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a \propto I_a^2$.
4. The armature rotates, generating a back EMF $E_b = k\Phi\omega$, which opposes the supply.
5. Steady state: $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$.
6. If load increases → $I_a$ rises → $\Phi$ rises → $T$ rises sharply to match load.
7. If load decreases → $I_a$ falls → $\Phi$ falls → speed shoots up dramatically.
8. **Hazard**: At no-load or very light load, the speed can become **dangerously high (runaway condition)**. Hence series motors are **never belt-connected** and **never started without load**.

### Working of Shunt DC Motor
1. Supply $V$ is applied across the parallel combination of armature and shunt field.
2. The shunt field current $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ is established instantly and is **practically constant**.
3. This produces a **constant flux $\Phi$** (rated flux) in the air gap.
4. Armature current $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ flows; interaction with $\Phi$ gives torque $T = k_T \Phi I_a$.
5. Back EMF $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ rises as the motor speeds up, reducing $I_a$ until equilibrium is reached.
6. At steady state, developed torque equals load torque.
