7. If load increases → $I_a$ rises (with little change in $\Phi$) → $T$ rises to meet load → speed drops slightly.
8. **Result**: Speed remains **nearly constant** over a wide load range — the hallmark of the shunt motor.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Motor Type | Industry Application | Reason for Choice |
|---|---|---|
| **Series Motor** | Electric locomotives, trams, cranes, hoists, winches, electric vehicles (vintage), starter motors of cars | Very high starting torque, ability to handle heavy overloads |
| **Shunt Motor** | Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, line shafts, printing machines, lathes, drilling machines | Nearly constant speed, good speed regulation |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:**
> - **Series Motor → "S for Strength"** → High torque, variable speed.
> - **Shunt Motor → "S for Stability"** → Stable speed, moderate torque.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation $E_b = k\Phi\omega$

**Starting Point (Faraday's Law):**
Let:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Weber)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in armature (for wave winding $A = 2$; for lap winding $A = P$)
- $N$ = speed in RPM
- $N / 60$ = speed in revolutions per second

**Step 1: Flux cut by one conductor per revolution**
As the armature rotates one full revolution, a single conductor cuts the flux from **all** $P$ poles, so the flux cut per revolution is $P\Phi$ (in Weber).

**Step 2: Flux cut per conductor per second**
Flux cut per second = (flux cut per revolution) × (revolutions per second)

$$
= P\Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{Weber/second} = \text{Volts (average EMF per conductor)}
$$

**Step 3: Total EMF in the armature winding**
The $Z$ armature conductors are distributed in **$A$ parallel paths**, with $Z/A$ conductors in series in each path. EMF adds for conductors in series in a single path:

$$
E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot P\Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 4: Grouping the constants**
Let

$$
k = \frac{Z P}{60 A}
$$

This constant $k$ depends only on the physical construction of the machine (number of poles, conductors, type of winding). Then:

$$
E_b = k \Phi N
$$

**Step 5: Conversion to angular form**
Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, we can write $N = 60\omega / 2\pi$. Substituting:

$$
E_b = k \Phi \cdot \frac{60\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 k}{2\pi} \Phi \omega
$$

If we define a new constant $k' = \dfrac{60 k}{2\pi}$, then:

$$
\boxed{E_b = k' \Phi \omega}
$$

In KTU textbooks and most engineering references, the prime is dropped and the **same symbol $k$** is used with the convention that the unit system dictates whether the input is $N$ (RPM) or $\omega$ (rad/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The examiner typically allows $E_b = k\Phi N$ (with $N$ in RPM) and $T = k\Phi I_a$ (with $T$ in N·m) using the same $k$, provided the unit system is consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation $T = k_T \Phi I_a$

**Starting Point (Force on a current-carrying conductor):**
The force on a single straight conductor of length $L$ carrying current $i$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is:

$$
F = B \cdot i \cdot L
$$

**Step 1: Torque contribution of a single conductor**
The conductor sits at a radius $r$ (half the armature diameter $D$, so $r = D/2$) from the shaft axis. The torque from one conductor is force × radius:

$$
T_1 = F \cdot r = B \cdot i \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Sum the torque over all conductors**
The armature has $Z$ total conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths, so current per conductor is $I_a / A$. The mean flux density $B$ is related to flux per pole $\Phi$ by:

$$
B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{air\,gap}}
$$

where $A_{air\,gap}$ is the area under one pole. Substituting and summing over all $Z$ conductors:

$$
T = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

After substituting $B$ in terms of $\Phi$ and cancelling the geometric area term, the net result is:

$$
T = k_T \Phi I_a
$$

where the machine constant for torque is $k_T$. In SI units, $k_T = k$, giving the clean form:

$$
\boxed{T = k \Phi I_a}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Series vs Shunt — Side-by-Side Derivation

| Feature | Series DC Motor | Shunt DC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit diagram (current path) | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (single loop) | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ (two parallel paths) |
| Flux relation | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) | $\Phi = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} \cdot k_{f}$ = constant |
| Voltage equation | $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$ | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
| Speed equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a+R_{se})}{k\Phi}$ | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ |
| Torque relation | $T \propto I_a^2$ (unsaturated) | $T \propto I_a$ |
| Torque–Current graph | **Parabolic curve** | **Straight line through origin** |
| Speed–Current graph | **Inverse / sharply falling curve** | **Nearly straight, slightly drooping line** |
| Speed–Torque graph | **Hyperbolic-like inverse curve** | **Nearly straight, slightly drooping line** |
| Starting torque | **Very high (2–3 times rated)** | **Moderate (1.2–1.5 times rated)** |
| Speed regulation | **Poor** (large speed change with load) | **Good** (≈ 5–10% from no-load to full-load) |
| No-load behavior | **Dangerous — runaway speed** | **Safe — speed rises only slightly** |
| Field winding wire | Thick, few turns | Thin, many turns |
| Field resistance $R_{se}$ or $R_{sh}$ | Very small (≈ 0.1–1 $\Omega$) | Very high (≈ 50–500 $\Omega$) |

## 3.4 Numerical-Style Worked Derivation (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A 220 V DC series motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and series field resistance $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$. The motor draws 40 A from the supply and runs at 1000 RPM. Calculate:
> (a) The back EMF.
> (b) The developed torque, given that the machine constant for torque is $k_T = 0.05$ V·s/(Wb·rad) and the flux at this load is $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb.
> (c) The developed mechanical power.

**Solution (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step):**

**Part (a): Back EMF**

By KVL on the armature loop:

$$
V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

Substitute $V = 220$ V, $I_a = 40$ A, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$:

$$
220 = E_b + 40 \times (0.5 + 0.3)
$$

$$
220 = E_b + 40 \times 0.8
$$

$$
220 = E_b + 32
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b = 220 - 32 = 188\ \text{V}}
$$

**Part (b): Developed Torque**

Using the torque equation $T = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$:

$$
T = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 40
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 0.04\ \text{N·m}}
$$

**Part (c): Developed Mechanical Power**

First compute angular speed:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Power developed:

$$
P_{dev} = T \cdot \omega = 0.04 \times 104.72
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dev} = 4.189\ \text{W}}
$$

**Cross-Verification (using electrical side):**

$$
P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 188 \times 40 = 7520\ \text{W}
$$

Wait — there's a large discrepancy. This is because the given $k_T$ and $\Phi$ are not consistent with the rest of the problem. In a **real KTU exam**, such a problem is given with **consistent data**, and the discrepancy is resolved by using the **same** $k$ for both EMF and torque equations. The KTU-typical approach uses:

$$
E_b = k\Phi N \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{E_b}{\Phi N} = \frac{188}{0.02 \times 1000} = 9.4
$$

Then $T = k \Phi I_a = 9.4 \times 0.02 \times 40 = 7.52$ N·m, and

$$
P_{dev} = T \omega = 7.52 \times 104.72 \approx 787.5\ \text{W}
$$

Hmm, still not matching. The reason is the **unit conversion factor** between $k$ used in $E_b = k\Phi N$ (with $N$ in RPM) and $k$ used in $T = k\Phi I_a$ (with $T$ in N·m). They are related by:

$$
k_{torque} = k_{EMF} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = k_{EMF} \cdot 9.549
$$

So $k_{torque} = 9.4 \times 9.549 = 89.76$, giving $T = 89.76 \times 0.02 \times 40 = 71.81$ N·m. Now $P_{dev} = 71.81 \times 104.72 = 7520$ W — **matches $E_b I_a$ exactly**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The ratio of constants is **always** $\dfrac{k_{torque}}{k_{EMF}} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \approx 9.549$. If the examiner gives one $k$, derive the other using this ratio. Using mismatched $k$ values is the **#1 most common KTU valuation mistake**.

## 3.5 Speed Regulation Derivation (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Definition:** Speed regulation is the change in speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load speed:

$$
\%\,\text{Speed Regulation} = \frac{N_{no\,load} - N_{full\,load}}{N_{full\,load}} \times 100
$$

**For Shunt Motor:**
- At no-load: $I_a$ is very small, $E_b \approx V$, so $N_{NL} \approx \dfrac{V}{k\Phi}$.
- At full-load: $I_a = I_{FL}$, $E_b = V - I_{FL} R_a$, so $N_{FL} = \dfrac{V - I_{FL} R_a}{k\Phi}$.
- The drop in speed is **proportional to $I_a R_a$**, which is small → excellent regulation.

**For Series Motor:**
- At no-load: $I_a$ → very small → $\Phi$ → very small → $N$ → very large (dangerous).
- At full-load: $I_a$ is large, $\Phi$ is large, $N$ is small.
- The speed varies from **runaway to crawling** → **no defined speed regulation** in the conventional sense.

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram: Series DC Motor (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    supplyP[DC Supply Positive V] --> wire1A
    wire1A --> fieldTerm1[Series Field Winding]
    fieldTerm1 --> fieldTerm2[Field Winding End]
    fieldTerm2 --> wire1B
    wire1B --> armatureP[Armature Positive Brush]
    armatureP --> armRA[Armature Resistance Ra]
    armRA --> armConductor[Armature Conductors in Field]
    armConductor --> armatureN[Armature Negative Brush]
    armatureN --> wire1C
    wire1C --> supplyN[DC Supply Negative 0V]
    fieldTerm1 -. same Ia flows .-> armatureP
    classDef wire fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef coil fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef brush fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class supplyP,supplyN wire
    class fieldTerm1,fieldTerm2,armConductor,armRA coil
    class armatureP,armatureN brush
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows from supply positive go through the series field first, then through the armature, returning to supply negative. This is a **single current loop** — hence the name "series."

## 4.2 Circuit Diagram: Shunt DC Motor (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    supplyP[DC Supply Positive V] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> armBranch[Armature Branch]
    nodeA --> shuntBranch[Shunt Field Branch]
    armBranch --> armRA[Armature Resistance Ra]
    armRA --> armConductor[Armature in Magnetic Field]
    armConductor --> nodeB
    shuntBranch --> shuntR[Shunt Field Resistance Rsh]
    shuntR --> shuntCoil[Shunt Field Coil Many Turns]
    shuntCoil --> nodeB
    nodeB --> supplyN[DC Supply Negative 0V]
    armBranch -. Ia flows .-> armConductor
    shuntBranch -. Ish flows .-> shuntCoil
    classDef node fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef branch fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef supply fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px

    class armRA,armConductor,shuntR,shuntCoil branch

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From supply positive, the current splits into two parallel branches at node A: one through the armature (current $I_a$) and another through the shunt field (current $I_{sh}$). They recombine at node B and return to supply negative. Hence the term "shunt" (= parallel).

```mermaid
graph TD

```

> [!TIP]
> **How to remember the curves (KTU Viva Trick):**
> - **Series Motor Torque Curve:** Looks like a **smile** (parabola opening upward). It rises sharply with current — "**smiles at heavy loads**."
> - **Series Motor Speed Curve:** Looks like a **slide** (steep hyperbolic drop). It falls dramatically with current — "**slides down with load**."
> - **Shunt Motor Torque Curve:** A **straight diagonal line** through the origin.
> - **Shunt Motor Speed Curve:** Almost a **horizontal line** — barely dips.
