# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is a DC series motor never started without load? Justify with the relevant speed equation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A DC series motor is **never started without load** because under no-load (or very light load) conditions, the armature current $I_a$ becomes negligibly small. Since the field winding is in series with the armature, the flux $\Phi$ is also very small (because $\Phi \propto I_a$ in the linear region). 

From the speed equation:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}
$$

as $\Phi \to 0$, the denominator $k\Phi$ becomes extremely small, causing the speed $N$ to rise to **dangerously high values** — a condition known as **"runaway speed."** This can mechanically destroy the motor due to excessive centrifugal forces on the armature. Hence a series motor must always be **mechanically coupled to its load before starting**. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State and explain the significance of the back EMF equation of a DC motor."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **back EMF equation** of a DC motor is given by:

$$
E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega
$$

where $k$ is the machine constant, $\Phi$ is the flux per pole, and $\omega$ is the angular speed.

**Significance:**
1. The back EMF is **induced in the armature conductors** as they rotate through the magnetic field, in accordance with Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.
2. By **Lenz's Law**, it **opposes the applied supply voltage** $V$, hence the name "back" EMF.
3. It acts as a **natural regulator of armature current**: as the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises, reducing the net voltage $(V - E_b)$ across $R_a$, and thus limiting $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$.
4. It is **directly proportional to speed**, making it the basis for **tachogenerators and speed sensing** in modern automation systems (e.g., CNC machines, robotics). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **DC series motor**. Derive the relationship between torque and armature current in the linear (unsaturated) region of the magnetic circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.25 $\Omega$ and a series field resistance of 0.15 $\Omega$. At a certain load, the motor draws 60 A and runs at 1200 RPM. Calculate:
- (i) The back EMF developed.
- (ii) The developed power.
- (iii) The developed torque.
- (iv) The total copper losses.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working of DC Series Motor (7 Marks)

**Construction:** A DC series motor consists of the following essential parts:
1. **Yoke** – Outer cylindrical frame, provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux.
2. **Pole Cores & Pole Shoes** – Made of laminated silicon steel, support the field winding and spread the flux uniformly in the air gap.
3. **Field Winding (Series)** – Wound with **few turns of thick wire** to carry the full armature current. It is connected in **series** with the armature.
4. **Armature Core** – Built of laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy current losses; carries the armature winding in slots.
5. **Armature Winding** – Closed winding of copper conductors placed in the armature slots, terminated at the commutator.
6. **Commutator & Brushes** – The commutator acts as a mechanical rectifier, ensuring unidirectional torque; brushes maintain sliding electrical contact.

**Working Principle:**
When DC supply $V$ is applied, a current $I_a$ flows through the series field winding and the armature. This current establishes a magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the air gap. The armature conductors carrying $I_a$ in this field experience a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$, producing a torque $T = k\Phi I_a$.

The rotating armature cuts the flux, inducing a back EMF $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ which opposes $V$. The motor reaches steady state when developed torque equals load torque.

**Derivation of T–I relation (Linear region):**

In the unsaturated (linear) region of the B–H curve:

$$
\Phi \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = k_f \cdot I_a
$$

Substituting into the torque equation:

$$
T = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = k \cdot (k_f I_a) \cdot I_a
$$

$$
\boxed{T = (k \cdot k_f) \cdot I_a^2 = K' \cdot I_a^2}
$$

Thus, in the linear region, the torque of a series motor is **proportional to the square of the armature current**, which explains its high starting torque. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.25\ \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.15\ \Omega$
- $I_a = 60$ A
- $N = 1200$ RPM

**Step 1 — Back EMF:**

$$
V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

$$
E_b = 250 - 60 \times (0.25 + 0.15) = 250 - 60 \times 0.40 = 250 - 24
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b = 226\ \text{V}} \quad \text{— (1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2 — Developed Power:**

$$
P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 226 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dev} = 13{,}560\ \text{W} = 13.56\ \text{kW}} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Developed Torque:**

First compute angular speed:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Then:

$$
T = \frac{P_{dev}}{\omega} = \frac{13{,}560}{125.66}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 107.9\ \text{N·m}} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Copper Losses:**

$$
P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) = (60)^2 \times (0.25 + 0.15) = 3600 \times 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{cu} = 1440\ \text{W} = 1.44\ \text{kW}} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a **DC shunt motor**. Why is the flux considered practically constant in a shunt motor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has the following specifications: armature resistance $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$, shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$, and full-load armature current $I_a = 25$ A. The no-load speed is 1500 RPM. Calculate:
- (i) The back EMF at full load.
- (ii) The full-load speed, assuming the flux remains constant from no-load to full-load.
- (iii) The speed regulation as a percentage.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working of DC Shunt Motor (7 Marks)

**Construction:** The DC shunt motor has the same physical construction as the series motor (yoke, poles, armature, commutator, brushes) — the **only difference** lies in the field winding.

- The **shunt field winding** is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the armature across the DC supply.
- It is wound with **a large number of turns of thin wire** so that it draws only a small field current $I_{sh}$ while producing the rated flux.

**Working Principle:**
On applying supply voltage $V$, the shunt field draws a small current:

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A (example)}
$$

This $I_{sh}$ produces a magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the air gap. Simultaneously, the armature draws current $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$, which interacts with $\Phi$ to produce torque $T = k\Phi I_a$. The armature rotates, induces back EMF $E_b = k\Phi\omega$, and the motor reaches steady state.

**Why flux is practically constant in a shunt motor:**
The shunt field is connected directly across the (nearly) constant supply voltage $V$. Hence:

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} \approx \text{constant}
$$

Since $\Phi$ depends on $I_{sh}$ (and hence on $V/R_{sh}$), the flux $\Phi$ is **essentially independent of the armature current $I_a$** and the load. This is the fundamental reason a shunt motor has **near-constant speed characteristics** and **good speed regulation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$
- Full-load $I_a = 25$ A
- No-load speed $N_0 = 1500$ RPM

**Step 1 — Shunt Field Current (auxiliary):**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A} \quad \text{— (0.5 mark)}
$$

**Step 2 — Back EMF at Full Load:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 25 \times 0.4 = 220 - 10
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{b,\,FL} = 210\ \text{V}} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Back EMF at No-Load:**
At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so:

$$
E_{b,\,NL} \approx V = 220\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Full-Load Speed:**

Using $E_b = k\Phi N$ and assuming $\Phi$ is constant (so $k\Phi$ is a constant):

$$
\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,\,FL}}{E_{b,\,NL}}
$$

$$
N_{FL} = N_{NL} \times \frac{E_{b,\,FL}}{E_{b,\,NL}} = 1500 \times \frac{210}{220}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{FL} = 1431.82\ \text{RPM}} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}
$$

**Step 5 — Speed Regulation:**

$$
\%\,\text{SR} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1431.82}{1431.82} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,\text{SR} = \frac{68.18}{1431.82} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{SR} \approx 4.76\%} \quad \text{— (2.5 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $R_{se}$**: In a series motor numerical, students often write $E_b = V - I_a R_a$, forgetting the series field drop $I_a R_{se}$. **Always include both resistances** in the armature KVL equation for series motors. Marks lost: up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion in angular speed**: When $N$ is in RPM, use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. **Never use $\omega = 2\pi N$** — this is a very common error. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing $k$ values**: The machine constant $k$ in $E_b = k\Phi N$ (with $N$ in RPM) is different from the one in $T = k\Phi I_a$ (with $T$ in N·m) by a factor of $60/(2\pi) = 9.549$. If the question gives one $k$, **derive the other explicitly** using this factor. Marks lost: 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Sign error in speed regulation**: Some students compute $N_{FL} - N_{NL}$ instead of $N_{NL} - N_{FL}$. The correct formula uses the **no-load speed minus full-load speed** in the numerator.
> 5. **Plotting T–I and N–I curves without labeling axes/units**: In 14-mark descriptive answers, **always** mark the X-axis (armature current $I_a$ in A) and Y-axis (Torque $T$ in N·m or Speed $N$ in RPM), and label each curve clearly as "Series Motor" or "Shunt Motor."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, DC Motors**

- ✅ A **DC series motor** has its field winding **in series** with the armature; a **DC shunt motor** has its field winding **in parallel** with the armature.
- ✅ The **two master equations** are $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ and $T = k\Phi I_a$ — learn them by heart, they appear in **every KTU exam**.
- ✅ The **armature voltage equation** is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ for shunt motors, and $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ for series motors.
- ✅ For series motors in the **linear region**: $\Phi \propto I_a$, hence $T \propto I_a^2$ and $N \propto 1/I_a$.
- ✅ For shunt motors: $\Phi = \text{constant}$ (since $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$), hence $T \propto I_a$ and $N \approx \text{constant}$.
- ✅ Series motors give **very high starting torque** and are used in **traction and hoists**.
- ✅ Shunt motors give **excellent speed regulation** (typically 4–10%) and are used in **fans, pumps, and machine tools**.
- ✅ Series motors must **never be started without load** — they can reach dangerous "runaway" speeds at no-load.
- ✅ Speed regulation formula: $\%\text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$.
- ✅ Angular speed conversion: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ when $N$ is in RPM.
- ✅ Power flow: $VI_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ → Mechanical developed power is $E_b I_a = T\omega$.
- ✅ The relation between torque-constant and EMF-constant in SI units: $k_{torque}/k_{EMF} = 60/(2\pi) \approx 9.549$.
- ✅ Characteristic curves to memorize: (i) Series motor $T$–$I_a$ is **parabolic**, (ii) Series motor $N$–$I_a$ is **hyperbolic (inversely falling)**, (iii) Shunt motor $T$–$I_a$ is a **straight line**, (iv) Shunt motor $N$–$I_a$ is a **nearly flat line**.
- ✅ Magnetic **saturation** breaks the linear $\Phi$–$I_a$ proportionality in a series motor — at high loads, $\Phi$ becomes nearly constant, making the motor behave somewhat like a shunt motor.
- ✅ For a **shunt motor**, line current = $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$; for a **series motor**, line current = $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (single current path).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque and speed equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torque and speed equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Electric Drives and Controls for Automation**, the **torque and speed equations** constitute the foundational mathematical framework that describes the electromechanical energy conversion relationship between the electrical input (voltage, current, flux) and the mechanical output (rotational speed, developed torque) of an electric machine used as a drive.

- The **Speed Equation** defines the steady-state rotational speed (N in rpm or ω in rad/s) of a motor as a function of supply voltage, armature/rotor current, circuit resistance, and magnetic flux.
- The **Torque Equation** expresses the electromagnetic torque developed by the machine as a function of flux, current, and machine constants.

These equations are derived from two fundamental physical laws:
1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** (produces back EMF).
2. **Biot-Savart / Lorentz Force Law** (produces mechanical force/torque).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every motor speed and torque equation must be written in terms of **per-phase equivalent circuit quantities** for AC machines and **armature-circuit quantities** for DC machines. Direct memorization of formulas without understanding the derivation is heavily penalized in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle:
- The **chain** (analogous to the magnetic flux $\Phi$) transmits the force from your legs to the wheel.
- The **pedaling force** (analogous to armature current $I_a$) determines how hard you push.
- The **gear ratio** (analogous to machine construction constant $k$) determines how efficiently your effort translates to wheel rotation.
- The **wheel speed** (analogous to motor speed N) is determined by how fast the chain moves, which in turn depends on your pedaling force minus any resistance (analogous to voltage drop $I_a R_a$).

So:
- **Higher flux** = Stronger magnetic coupling = More torque per ampere of current.
- **Higher voltage** = More "push" available = Higher speed (after subtracting internal drops).
- **Higher load** = More current drawn = More voltage drop = Reduced speed (this is why speed regulation matters!).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Constants Used Throughout:**
> - **Synchronous speed formula constant:** $120$ (conversion from frequency in Hz to rpm with P poles)
> - **$\omega_s$ (synchronous angular velocity):** $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ rad/s
> - **Torque unit:** **N·m** (Newton-meter) in SI, or **kg·m²/s²**
> - **Speed unit:** **rpm** (revolutions per minute) or **rad/s**
> - **Slip $s$:** dimensionless quantity, typically $0 < s < 1$ for motor operation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Machine — Speed and Torque Equations

The DC machine operates on the principle that a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL$. When integrated over the armature periphery, this produces torque.

**Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted):**

1. When supply voltage $V$ is applied to the armature, current $I_a$ flows.
2. As the armature rotates in flux $\Phi$, it generates a **back EMF**: $E_b \propto \Phi N$
3. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
4. Therefore, $E_b = V - I_a R_a$
5. Since $E_b \propto \Phi \omega$: $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ (where $k$ is machine constant)
6. Solving for speed: $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ — this is the **speed equation**
7. The torque developed: $T = k \Phi I_a$ — this is the **torque equation**

### 2.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor — Speed and Torque Equations

The induction motor is the **workhorse of industrial automation**. Unlike DC machines, its rotor current is induced (not directly supplied), making the analysis more subtle.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The stator produces a rotating magnetic field at **synchronous speed**: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
2. The rotor always rotates *slower* than this field — this relative difference is called **slip**: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$
3. The rotor EMF and frequency are slip-dependent: $E_r = s E_{r0}$ and $f_r = s f$
4. The rotor circuit, when referred to stator, gives the classic torque expression.

### 2.3 Synchronous Motor — Speed Equation

A synchronous motor has **no slip** — it rotates exactly at synchronous speed regardless of load (within its pull-out limit).

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \text{ rpm}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU module exam!**

| **Machine Type** | **Speed Equation** | **Torque Equation** | **Key Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | $T = k \Phi I_a$ | $V$ in V, $I_a$ in A, $\Phi$ in Wb, $R_a$ in $\Omega$ |
| **DC Series Motor** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi}$ with $\Phi \propto I_a$ | $T \propto I_a^2$ at low saturation | $\Phi = k_{se} I_a$ before saturation |
| **3-$\phi$ Induction Motor** | $N = \dfrac{120 f}{P}(1-s)$ | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2}$ | $s$ dimensionless, $\omega_s$ in rad/s |
| **Synchronous Motor** | $N = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (constant) | $T = \dfrac{3 V E}{X_s \omega_s} \sin\delta$ | $\delta$ is load/torque angle in rad |
| **Universal Relation** | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Power in W, $T$ in N·m |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- **Slip $s = 0$:** Ideal no-load condition for induction motor (rotor at synchronous speed).
- **Slip $s = 1$:** Standstill (starting) condition for induction motor.
- **Slip $s = s_{max}$:** Condition for **maximum (pull-out) torque**, where $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{X_{ls} + X_{lr}'}$

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Conveyor belts and elevators:** Speed equations determine the steady-state speed under load, critical for process synchronization.
- **CNC machines and robotics:** Precise torque-speed relationships enable trajectory planning and servo-loop design.
- **Electric vehicles (EVs):** The torque equation $T = k \Phi I_a$ (for DC) and induction motor torque-slip curve dictate acceleration profiles.
- **Pump and fan applications:** These are **variable torque loads** where $T_{load} \propto N^2$ — the intersection of motor and load torque-speed curves gives the operating point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: DC Shunt Motor Speed Equation

**Starting Point — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to armature circuit:**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 1: Express back EMF in terms of flux and speed.**

The back EMF $E_b$ is generated because armature conductors cut the magnetic flux $\Phi$. For a machine with $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, and $A$ parallel paths:

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

**Step 2: Define the machine constant.**

Let $k_1 = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$ (a constant for a given machine construction). Then:

$$E_b = k_1 \Phi N$$

**Step 3: Solve the KVL equation for speed $N$.**

$$V = k_1 \Phi N + I_a R_a$$

$$k_1 \Phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 \Phi}}$$

**Step 4: Express in angular velocity form.**

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$, we can rewrite using $k = \dfrac{k_1 \cdot 60}{2\pi}$:

$$\boxed{\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: DC Motor Torque Equation

**Starting Point — Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.**

The force on a single conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I$ in flux density $B$:

$$F = B I L$$

**Step 1: Total force on all armature conductors.**

For $Z$ conductors, total force $F_{total} = B I L Z$ (simplified considering all conductors contribute).

**Step 2: Convert force to torque.**

If the average radius of the armature is $r$ (in meters), then torque:

$$T = F_{total} \cdot r = B I L Z r$$

**Step 3: Express in terms of flux $\Phi$.**

Since flux per pole $\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole}$ (where $A_{pole}$ is the pole face area), and using geometric relationships, we obtain:

$$T = k_1 \Phi I_a$$

where $k_1$ is the same machine constant as in the EMF equation.

**Step 4: Verify dimensional consistency.**

$$[T] = [\Phi] \cdot [I_a] = \text{Wb} \cdot \text{A} = \text{V} \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{A} = \text{N·m} \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Three-Phase Induction Motor Torque Equation

This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU board exams — proceed with care.

**Step 1: Per-phase equivalent circuit (referred to stator).**

The rotor resistance referred to stator is $R_r'$, and rotor leakage reactance referred to stator is $X_{lr}'$. The rotor circuit current at slip $s$ is:

$$I_r' = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2}}$$

**Step 2: Air-gap power (power transferred across air gap).**

$$P_{ag} = 3 I_r'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s}$$

**Step 3: Synchronous angular velocity.**

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \cdot \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$$

**Step 4: Electromagnetic torque developed.**

$$T = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 (R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2}$$

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{3 V^2 (R_r'/s)}{\omega_s \left[\left(\dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2\right]}}$$

**Step 5: Condition for maximum torque — differentiate with respect to $s$.**

Setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{X_{ls} + X_{lr}'}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $s_{max}$ back into torque equation to get maximum torque.**

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')}}$$

**Key Observation:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_r'$** — this is a beautiful engineering result exploited in **rotor resistance control** for starting!

### 3.4 Derivation: Slip and Rotor EMF Frequency

**Slip definition:**

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$

**Solving for actual rotor speed:**

$$N_s - N = s N_s \implies N = N_s - s N_s = N_s(1 - s)$$

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{120 f}{P}(1 - s)}$$

**Rotor frequency (frequency of rotor induced EMF and current):**

The rotor "sees" a relative speed of $N_s - N = s N_s$. Therefore the rotor frequency is:

$$f_r = \dfrac{P \cdot s N_s}{120} = s f$$

This is why rotor resistance and reactance (which depend on frequency) must be scaled by slip when using the per-phase equivalent circuit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DC Drive Speed-Torque System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AC Mains Supply] --> nodeB[Rectifier Unit]
    nodeB --> nodeC[DC Link Filter]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Chopper or Converter]
    nodeD --> nodeE[DC Motor Armature]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mechanical Load]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Torque Feedback Sensor]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Controller PID]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Speed Reference Input]
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeJ[Field Winding Supply] --> nodeE
    nodeK[Back EMF Generator] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeH
    nodeL[Tachogenerator Sensor] --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeL
```

**Explanation of the Architecture:**
- `nodeA` → `nodeB`: AC supply is rectified to DC.
- `nodeD` → `nodeE`: The chopper regulates armature voltage $V$, which directly controls speed per $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$.
- `nodeE` → `nodeK`: The rotating armature generates back EMF $E_b$ which opposes supply voltage.
- `nodeH`: The PID controller continuously adjusts armature voltage to maintain reference speed despite load disturbances.
- `nodeJ`: The field winding controls flux $\Phi$ — weakening flux increases speed but reduces torque capacity.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Torque-Slip Characteristic of Induction Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Slip equals 0: No Load] --> nodeB[Low Slip Region: Stable Operation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Slip equals smax: Maximum Torque Point]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Slip between smax and 1: Unstable Region]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Slip equals 1: Starting Condition]
    nodeE --> nodeF[High Slip Region: Starting Torque Region]
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeG[Torque Axis] --> nodeH[Tmax Maximum Torque]
    nodeI[Slip Axis] --> nodeJ[0 to 1 Range]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Speed Control Methods Overview

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Speed Control Methods for Drives] --> nodeB[DC Motor Methods]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Induction Motor Methods]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Synchronous Motor Methods]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Armature Voltage Control]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Field Flux Control]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Variable Voltage Variable Frequency VVVF]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Rotor Resistance Control]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Stator Voltage Control]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Variable Frequency Control]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Constant V over f Ratio]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define **back EMF** in a DC motor and write its mathematical expression in terms of flux and speed. Why is it called "back" EMF? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Back EMF is the voltage induced in the armature conductors due to their rotation in the magnetic field, which **opposes** the applied supply voltage (by Lenz's law — hence "back"). Its mathematical expression is:

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = k \Phi \omega$$

The "back" prefix emphasizes its opposing polarity to the applied voltage, regulating the net voltage driving current through the armature resistance.

---

**Q2.** What is **slip** in a three-phase induction motor? Write the expression for rotor frequency in terms of supply frequency and slip. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Slip is the relative difference between the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction or percentage of synchronous speed:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$

The rotor frequency (frequency of induced EMF in rotor) is:

$$f_r = s f$$

where $f$ is the supply frequency and $s$ is the slip.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - December 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **speed equation** of a DC shunt motor from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \,\Omega$ and field winding resistance $R_{sh} = 110 \,\Omega$. The motor draws a total current of 20 A at a certain load. Calculate:
   - (i) The armature current $I_a$
   - (ii) The back EMF $E_b$
   - (iii) The speed of the motor if the no-load speed is 1500 rpm and the flux is assumed constant **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of DC Shunt Motor Speed Equation:**

**Assumption:** Magnetic field is constant (steady-state operation, no saturation).

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

The back EMF generated when the armature rotates at speed $N$ in flux $\Phi$ is:

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

where:
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $P$ = number of poles
- $A$ = number of parallel paths
- $N$ = speed in rpm

Substituting back into KVL:

$$V = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} + I_a R_a$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$\dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = V - I_a R_a$$

$$N = \dfrac{60 A (V - I_a R_a)}{\Phi Z P}$$

Defining the machine constant $k = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}}$$

In terms of angular velocity:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k' \Phi}$$

where $k' = \dfrac{Z P}{2 \pi A}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.5 \,\Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110 \,\Omega$
- Total line current $I_L = 20$ A
- No-load speed $N_0 = 1500$ rpm (approximately equal to rated speed in shunt motor since $\Phi$ is nearly constant)

**Step (i): Armature current**

Field current:

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

Armature current by KCL at the input node:

$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} \implies I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 20 - 2 = 18 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 18 \text{ A}}$$

**Step (ii): Back EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (18)(0.5) = 220 - 9 = 211 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 211 \text{ V}}$$

**Step (iii): Speed under load**

Since the motor is shunt-connected, flux $\Phi$ is approximately constant. We use the speed equation:

$$\dfrac{N}{N_0} = \dfrac{E_b}{E_{b0}}$$

where $E_{b0}$ is the no-load back EMF. At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_{b0} \approx V = 220$ V.

$$N = N_0 \cdot \dfrac{E_b}{E_{b0}} = 1500 \cdot \dfrac{211}{220}$$

$$N = 1500 \cdot 0.9591 = 1438.6 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 1439 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Speed Regulation:**

$$\text{Regulation} = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1500 - 1439}{1439} \times 100\% \approx 4.24\%$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **torque equation of a three-phase induction motor** starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A three-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance $R_r' = 0.4 \,\Omega$ and total leakage reactance $(X_{ls} + X_{lr}') = 2 \,\Omega$ per phase referred to stator. Calculate:
   - (i) Synchronous speed and slip at full load when the motor runs at 1440 rpm
   - (ii) The slip at which maximum torque occurs
   - (iii) The ratio of full-load torque to maximum torque if full-load slip is 0.04 **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to the **detailed derivation in Section 3.3** of these notes. The key result is:

$$T = \dfrac{3 V^2 (R_r'/s)}{\omega_s \left[\left(\dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2\right]}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz
- $R_r' = 0.4 \,\Omega$, $X_{total} = X_{ls} + X_{lr}' = 2 \,\Omega$
- $N = 1440$ rpm at full load

**Step (i): Synchronous speed and slip**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad s_{fl} = 0.04}$$

**Step (ii): Slip at maximum torque**

$$s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{X_{ls} + X_{lr}'} = \dfrac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$

$$\boxed{s_{max} = 0.2}$$

**Step (iii): Ratio of full-load torque to maximum torque**

Using the torque ratio formula (derived by dividing the torque equation by $T_{max}$ expression):

$$\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 (a \cdot s_{max})}{s + (s_{max}/s) \cdot (s + a \cdot s_{max})}$$

A more commonly used form (assuming $a = 1$, i.e., stator and rotor referred resistances are equal — a common simplification):

$$\dfrac{T_{fl}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{s/s_{max} + s_{max}/s}$$

Substituting $s = 0.04$ and $s_{max} = 0.2$:

$$\dfrac{T_{fl}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{0.04/0.2 + 0.2/0.04} = \dfrac{2}{0.2 + 5} = \dfrac{2}{5.2}$$

$$\dfrac{T_{fl}}{T_{max}} = 0.3846$$

$$\boxed{T_{fl} \approx 0.385 \times T_{max}}$$

This means the motor operates at about **38.5% of maximum torque** at full load — a typical design choice for stable operation with good starting reserve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion Trap:** Students often forget to convert $N$ from rpm to $\omega$ in rad/s when calculating torque. Always check: $T$ in N·m requires $\omega$ in rad/s.
>
> 2. **Slip Direction Error:** Some students confuse $(1-s)$ vs $s$ in the speed formula. Remember: **rotor speed** uses $(1-s)$; **rotor circuit quantities** use $s$.
>
> 3. **Maximum Torque Independence:** Forgetting that $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** is a 3-mark deduction in board exams.
>
> 4. **Per-Phase vs Total Power:** Induction motor torque equation uses **per-phase voltage** $V$ (phase voltage), not line voltage. Star-connected machines: $V_{phase} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$.
>
> 5. **Field Current Subtraction:** In DC shunt motor problems, students often compute $I_a = I_L$ directly — this **loses 1 mark**. Always subtract field current: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
>
> 6. **Dimensionless Slip Verification:** When $s_{max} > 1$, the motor has unstable characteristics — this is a valuable observation expected in viva voce.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ **DC Motor Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ — flux control and armature voltage control are the two classical speed control methods.
- ✅ **DC Motor Torque Equation:** $T = k \Phi I_a$ — torque is directly proportional to flux and armature current.
- ✅ **Induction Motor Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — depends only on supply frequency and number of poles.
- ✅ **Slip Definition:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$, dimensionless, $0 < s < 1$ for motor operation.
- ✅ **Rotor Frequency:** $f_r = s f$ — rotor circuits always see slip-frequency quantities.
- ✅ **Induction Motor Torque Equation:** $T = \dfrac{3 V^2 (R_r'/s)}{\omega_s [(R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')^2]}$
- ✅ **Maximum Torque Slip:** $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{X_{ls} + X_{lr}'}$
- ✅ **Maximum Torque Value:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (X_{ls} + X_{lr}')}$ — **independent of rotor resistance**!
- ✅ **Synchronous Motor Speed:** Constant at $N_s$ regardless of load (within pull-out limit).
- ✅ **Power Equation:** $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ — always use consistent units (N·m × rad/s = W).
- ✅ **Star-connected stator:** $V_{phase} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$; **Delta-connected:** $V_{phase} = V_L$.
- ✅ **Per-Phase Analysis:** Always work with per-phase equivalent circuit for three-phase machines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque-speed characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torque-speed characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **torque-speed characteristic** of an electric machine is the steady-state graphical/analytical relationship between the electromagnetic torque developed by the machine ($T_e$) and its rotational speed ($N$ or $\omega$) under specified supply, excitation, and load conditions. It is the single most important performance curve used in electric drive engineering because it dictates:

- The **stable operating point** of the motor when coupled to a mechanical load.
- The ability of the drive to handle **transient load perturbations**.
- The **starting, accelerating, and braking capability** of the system.

Mathematically, the characteristic is expressed in two equivalent forms:

$$T_e = f(N) \quad \text{(Speed as the independent variable)}$$

$$\omega = f(T_e) \quad \text{(Torque as the independent variable)}$$

where $\omega$ is in **rad/s** and $T_e$ is in **N·m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme (PEAUT525, Module 1), students must be able to *derive* and *interpret* torque-speed curves for DC (separately excited, series, shunt, compound) and AC (three-phase induction, synchronous) machines, and must apply them to **load matching** problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pedal-Bicycle of a Factory"

Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a hill. The **torque** your legs produce is like the *pulling force* at the wheel, while the **speed** is how fast the wheel turns. When the hill gets steep (high load torque), you instinctively shift to a **low gear** — your pedalling becomes slow but powerful. When the road is flat (light load), you shift to a **high gear** — your legs spin fast but with less effort.

An electric motor behaves identically. Its internal magnetic coupling acts like the gear ratio: the *field flux* ($\Phi$) plays the role of the gear setting. A **stronger field** produces *more torque but lower speed* (low gear), while a **weaker field** gives *less torque but higher speed* (high gear). The **torque-speed curve** is simply the factory floor's version of the gear-shifting rulebook.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ → **Synchronous speed in rad/s** (where $p$ = number of pole pairs).
> - $1 \text{ HP} = 746 \text{ W}$.
> - Slip $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Derivation Framework

The electromagnetic torque in **any** rotating machine is fundamentally given by the power balance:

$$P_{gap} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$$

where $P_{gap}$ is the air-gap power transferred from stator to rotor (or armature to shaft in DC). The torque is the **rate of energy conversion** per radian of mechanical rotation.

For an **idealized lossless machine**, the back-EMF and torque constants are equal:

$$E_b = k_E \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \qquad \text{and} \qquad T_e = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

In SI units, **$k_E = k_T$** (numerically), so the back-EMF and torque equations are dual counterparts.

### 2.2 The Master Equation: Torque-Speed Relationship

Combining the armature voltage equation with the back-EMF gives the universal torque-speed relationship:

$$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a = k_E \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

Substituting $I_a = T_e / (k_T \Phi)$:

$$V_t = k_E \Phi \omega + \frac{T_e R_a}{k_T \Phi}$$

Solving for $\omega$ yields the **canonical torque-speed equation**:

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{k_E \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_E k_T \Phi^2} T_e$$

This is a **straight line** in the $\omega$–$T_e$ plane with:

- **No-load speed** (intercept): $\omega_0 = V_t / (k_E \Phi)$
- **Slope** (stiffness): $m = -R_a / (k_E k_T \Phi^2)$

> [!NOTE]
> The **slope of the torque-speed line** defines the **stiffness** of the motor. A *steep* slope = *soft* motor (large speed drop with load, e.g., DC series). A *shallow* slope = *hard* motor (nearly constant speed, e.g., DC shunt, synchronous).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Machine Type** | **No-Load Speed $\omega_0$** | **Slope / Stiffness** | **Key Equation** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt** | $V / (k_E \Phi)$ | $-R_a / (k_E^2 \Phi^2)$ | $\omega = \omega_0 - m T_e$ | Lathes, fans, line shafts |
| **DC Series** | $\infty$ (theoretically) | $-1/(k_E k_T) \cdot (R_a+R_s)/\Phi^2$ | $\omega \cdot T_e = k$ (constant) | Cranes, traction, hoists |
| **DC Compound (Cumulative)** | Finite, slightly below rated | $-R_a / (k_E^2 \Phi^2)$ but $\Phi \uparrow$ with $I_a$ | $\omega = \omega_0 - m T_e$ | Presses, elevators, shears |
| **3-$\phi$ Induction (Cage)** | $N_s$ (sync) | Curved, non-linear | $T_e = (3/\omega_s) \cdot V^2 R_2/s / [(R_1+R_2/s)^2 + X^2]$ | Pumps, compressors, conveyors |
| **3-$\phi$ Synchronous** | **Constant** $N_s$ | **Zero slope** (vertical line) | $\omega = \omega_s \;\; \forall T_e$ | Constant-speed drives, power-factor correction |

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $\Phi$ | Field flux per pole | **Wb** |
| $k_E, k_T$ | Machine constants (SI) | **V·s/rad**, **N·m/A** |
| $R_a$ | Armature resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $s$ | Per-unit slip ($0$ to $1$) | dimensionless |
| $N_s$ | Synchronous speed | **rpm** |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Traction (Electric Vehicles):** DC series characteristic ($\omega \cdot T_e = \text{const}$) provides *high starting torque* at *low speed* — exactly what a vehicle needs at start-up.
- **Industrial Fans:** DC shunt or induction cage motors have nearly flat curves, ideal for *constant-speed* operation.
- **Steel Rolling Mills:** Cumulative compound DC motors give *high inrush torque* without runaway speed, perfect for *intermittent heavy loading*.
- **Precision Machine Tools:** Synchronous motors lock to $N_s$ regardless of load, enabling *precision machining*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: DC Shunt Motor Torque-Speed Curve

**Step 1: Write the armature voltage equation.**
The applied voltage equals the sum of the back-EMF and the armature IR drop:

$$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2: Express the back-EMF.**
For a constant field current (shunt field is fed from a separate parallel branch), $\Phi = \text{const}$:

$$E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$$

**Step 3: Express torque in terms of armature current.**
By the Lorentz force principle, developed torque is:

$$T_e = k_T \Phi I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \frac{T_e}{k_T \Phi}$$

**Step 4: Substitute (2) and (3) into (1).**

$$V_t = k_E \Phi \omega + \frac{T_e R_a}{k_T \Phi}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\omega$.**

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{k_E \Phi} - \left(\frac{R_a}{k_E k_T \Phi^2}\right) T_e$$

**Step 6: Identify no-load speed and slope.**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_t}{k_E \Phi}, \quad m = -\frac{R_a}{k_E k_T \Phi^2}$$

**Conclusion:** The DC shunt motor torque-speed curve is a **straight line** with negative slope. Since $R_a$ is small, the slope is very small in magnitude → **near-constant speed** (good regulation).

### 3.2 Derivation B: DC Series Motor Torque-Speed Curve

**Step 1: Recognize that in a series motor, the same current $I_a$ flows through the armature AND the field winding.**

$$\Phi = k_{\Phi} I_a \quad \text{(field is unsaturated, linear region)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the back-EMF equation.**

$$E_b = k_E \Phi \omega = k_E k_{\Phi} I_a \omega$$

**Step 3: Write the voltage equation.**

$$V_t = k_E k_{\Phi} I_a \omega + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\omega$.**

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{k_E k_{\Phi} I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k_E k_{\Phi}}$$

**Step 5: Eliminate $I_a$ using the torque expression.**

$$T_e = k_T \Phi I_a = k_T k_{\Phi} I_a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T_e}{k_T k_{\Phi}}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute (5) into (4).**

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{k_E k_{\Phi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_T k_{\Phi}}{T_e}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k_E k_{\Phi}}$$

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{\sqrt{k_E^2 k_{\Phi} / k_T}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_e}} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k_E k_{\Phi}}$$

**Step 7: Identify the inverse relationship.**

$$\omega \cdot \sqrt{T_e} \approx \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_e}}$$

**Conclusion:** The DC series motor has a sharply *falling* (hyperbolic) torque-speed curve. At light loads, $T_e \to 0 \Rightarrow \omega \to \infty$ — this is the **danger of runaway speed** in an unloaded series motor.

### 3.3 Derivation C: Three-Phase Induction Motor Torque-Slip Curve

**Step 1: Start from the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator.** The Thevenin equivalent looking into the rotor branch is:

$$V_{th} = \frac{V_1 \cdot jX_m}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_m)^2}}, \quad Z_{th} = R_{th} + jX_{th}$$

**Step 2: Compute the air-gap power.** Power transferred to the rotor circuit per phase:

$$P_{gap, \phi} = I_2^{'2} \cdot \frac{R_2^{'}}{s}$$

**Step 3: The electromagnetic torque per phase** is the air-gap power divided by the synchronous mechanical speed $\omega_s$:

$$T_{e,\phi} = \frac{P_{gap,\phi}}{\omega_s} = \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2^{'}/s)}{[R_{th} + (R_2^{'}/s)]^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by 3 for a three-phase machine.**

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2^{'}/s)}{[R_{th} + (R_2^{'}/s)]^2 + X_{th}^2}}$$

**Step 5: Find the slip at maximum torque.** Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set $dT_e/ds = 0$:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}}$$

**Step 6: Maximum (pull-out) torque** by substituting $s_{max}$ back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}}$$

**Conclusion:** The induction motor torque-speed curve has two operating regions — the **stable region** ($0 \le s \le s_{max}$) and the **unstable region** ($s_{max} < s \le 1$). The motor normally operates just below $s_{max}$ at rated load.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Characteristics

| **Parameter** | **DC Shunt** | **DC Series** | **DC Compound (Cumulative)** | **Induction (Cage)** | **Synchronous** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shape | Linear | Hyperbolic (inverse) | Slightly drooping | Curved (asymmetric) | Perfectly vertical |
| No-load speed | $V/k_E\Phi$ | Infinity (unsafe) | $0.9\,\omega_0$ to $\omega_0$ | $N_s$ (sync) | $N_s$ exactly |
| Starting torque | Medium ($1.5\times$–$2\times$) | Very high ($5\times$–$10\times$) | High ($2.5\times$–$4\times$) | Medium–High ($1.5\times$–$3\times$) | Low–Medium |
| Speed regulation | Good ($< 10\%$) | Poor | Fair–Good | Moderate | Excellent ($0\%$) |
| Suitability | Constant-speed loads | High-starting-torque loads | Intermittent heavy loads | General-purpose | Constant-speed precision |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Torque-Speed Derivation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates how the **fundamental machine equations** combine to produce the characteristic curves of all common motor types used in industrial drives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Voltage V_t and Frequency f] --> B[Machine Type Selection]
    B --> C1[DC Shunt]
    B --> C2[DC Series]
    B --> C3[DC Compound]
    B --> C4[3-phi Induction]
    B --> C5[3-phi Synchronous]
    C1 --> D1[Phi = const]
    C2 --> D2[Phi proportional to I_a]
    C3 --> D3[Phi = Phi_shunt + k times I_a]
    C4 --> D4[Slip s varies with load]
    C5 --> D5[Phi and omega_s both fixed]
    D1 --> E1[V_t = kE Phi omega + I_a R_a]
    D2 --> E2[V_t = kE kPhi I_a omega + I_a R_total]
    D3 --> E3[Superposition of shunt and series fields]
    D4 --> E4[Thevenin equivalent Rotor circuit]
    D5 --> E5[Synchronous speed lock omega_s = 2 pi f / p]
    E1 --> F1[Linear Curve: omega = omega_0 - m T_e]
    E2 --> F2[Hyperbolic Curve: omega proportional to 1 over sqrt T_e]
    E3 --> F3[Drooping Linear Curve]
    E4 --> F4[Non linear Curve with T_max and s_max]
    E5 --> F5[Vertical Line at omega = omega_s]
    F1 --> G[Load Intersection Point T_e = T_L]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Stable Operating Point omega_op, T_op]
```

### 4.2 Qualitative Family of Torque-Speed Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stable_Region
        A1[DC Shunt: nearly flat] --> A2[Stiff, constant speed]
        A3[DC Compound: drooping] --> A4[Stable for load transients]
        A5[Induction: curved] --> A6[Region 0 to s_max is stable]
        A7[Synchronous: vertical] --> A8[Zero speed drop, locked to N_s]
        A9[DC Series: hyperbolic] --> A10[High starting torque, dangerous at no load]
    end
    A2 --> Z[Select Motor for Application]
    A4 --> Z
    A6 --> Z
    A8 --> Z
    A10 --> Z
```

### 4.3 Load-Matching Concept (Motor Curve vs. Load Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Motor Torque-Speed Curve T_m omega] --> L
    LM[Mechanical Load Curve T_L omega] --> L
    L{Intersection of curves?}
    L -->|Yes| S[Stable Operating Point T_op, N_op]
    L -->|No curves crossing| F[System cannot drive load]
    S --> C{Check s less than s_max?}
    C -->|Yes| OK[Safe continuous operation]
    C -->|No| FAIL[Operation in unstable region: stall risk]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The point of intersection between the **motor torque-speed curve** and the **load torque-speed curve** is the **operating point**. For stable operation, this intersection must lie in the *positive-slope* region of the *net torque* $T_{net} = T_m - T_L$ vs. $\omega$ curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]  
Define the term *speed regulation* of a DC shunt motor. How is it related to the slope of the torque-speed characteristic?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Speed regulation is defined as the change in speed when the load on the motor is reduced from rated value to zero, expressed as a percentage of rated speed:

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$

where $N_{NL}$ = no-load speed, $N_{FL}$ = full-load speed. From the torque-speed equation $\omega = \omega_0 - m T_e$, the slope $m$ is directly proportional to regulation — a steeper (more negative) slope means **poorer** regulation, while a shallower slope means **better** (closer to constant-speed) regulation. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for slope linkage.)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
Why is a DC series motor never started without a mechanical load coupled to its shaft?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a DC series motor, the field flux $\Phi$ is directly proportional to the armature current $I_a$ (in the unsaturated linear region). At no-load, $I_a$ is very small, so $\Phi$ is very small. From the torque-speed equation:

$$\omega = \frac{V_t}{k_E \Phi} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k_E \Phi}$$

as $\Phi \to 0$, $\omega \to \infty$. This causes **mechanical runaway**, which can destroy the armature windings due to excessive centrifugal forces. (1 mark for $\Phi \propto I_a$, 1 mark for runaway condition, 1 mark for mechanical damage consequence.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the torque-speed equation of a separately excited DC motor and sketch its characteristic. Show that the slope of the line is a measure of motor stiffness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A separately excited DC motor has an armature resistance of $0.5 \, \Omega$. Field current is held constant such that the flux per pole is $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb and the machine constant $k_E = 50$. Calculate:  
(i) No-load speed.  
(ii) Speed at full-load torque.  
(iii) Speed regulation in percent. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full step-by-step derivation. Key points to include in the answer sheet:

1. Armature voltage equation: $V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$ — **(1 mark)**
2. Back-EMF: $E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$ — **(1 mark)**
3. Torque: $T_e = k_T \Phi I_a$, hence $I_a = T_e / (k_T \Phi)$ — **(1 mark)**
4. Final equation: $\omega = \dfrac{V_t}{k_E \Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{k_E k_T \Phi^2} T_e$ — **(2 marks)**
5. Sketch showing a straight line from $\omega_0$ on the y-axis down to a point on the x-axis where $T_e = T_{stall}$. Label **slope** $m$ and **stiffness** interpretation. — **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_t = 220$ V, $N = 1500$ rpm, $I_a = 10$ A, $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb, $k_E = 50$.

**Step 1: No-load speed.**  
At no load, $T_e = 0$, so $I_a = 0$, so $E_b = V_t$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_t}{k_E \Phi} = \frac{220}{50 \times 0.02} = \frac{220}{1.0} = 220 \text{ rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm:

$$N_0 = \omega_0 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 220 \times 9.5493 \approx 2101 \text{ rpm}$$

**(i) No-load speed = 2101 rpm** — **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Speed at full-load torque.**  
At full load, $I_a = 10$ A, so back-EMF:

$$E_b = V_t - I_a R_a = 220 - 10 \times 0.5 = 220 - 5 = 215 \text{ V}$$

Speed at full load:

$$\omega_{FL} = \frac{E_b}{k_E \Phi} = \frac{215}{50 \times 0.02} = \frac{215}{1.0} = 215 \text{ rad/s}$$

In rpm:

$$N_{FL} = 215 \times 9.5493 \approx 2053 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Full-load speed = 2053 rpm** — **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Speed regulation.**

$$\text{Reg \%} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{2101 - 2053}{2053} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reg \%} = \frac{48}{2053} \times 100 \approx 2.34\%$$

**(iii) Speed regulation ≈ 2.34 %** — **(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students forget to convert **rad/s → rpm** using $N = \omega \times 60 / (2\pi)$. Losing 1 mark.  
> 2. **Forgetting $I_a R_a$ drop:** At no load $I_a = 0$, but at full load $E_b \neq V_t$. Compute $E_b$ first, then $\omega$.  
> 3. **Stiffness interpretation:** If asked "what is stiffness?", answer *"slope of the $\omega$–$T_e$ curve"*, not "the slope of the $T_e$–$\omega$ curve" (sign convention matters).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the condition for **maximum torque** in a three-phase induction motor and obtain the expression for slip at maximum torque. Show that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2^{'} = 0.4 \, \Omega$ and total standstill reactance referred to stator $X = 1.2 \, \Omega$. The Thevenin equivalent voltage is $V_{th} = 200$ V and Thevenin impedance is $Z_{th} = 0.5 + j1.0 \, \Omega$. Find:  
(i) Slip at maximum torque.  
(ii) Maximum torque.  
(iii) Starting torque. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The torque of a 3-phase induction motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2^{'}/s)}{[R_{th} + (R_2^{'}/s)]^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

Let $x = R_2^{'}/s$. Then:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{th}^2 x}{\omega_s [(R_{th} + x)^2 + X_{th}^2]}$$

Differentiate with respect to $x$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dT_e}{dx} = \frac{3V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{[(R_{th}+x)^2 + X_{th}^2] - x \cdot 2(R_{th}+x)}{[(R_{th}+x)^2 + X_{th}^2]^2} = 0$$

Numerator must vanish:

$$(R_{th}+x)^2 + X_{th}^2 - 2x(R_{th}+x) = 0$$

$$(R_{th}+x)^2 + X_{th}^2 = 2x(R_{th}+x)$$

Let $y = R_{th}+x$:

$$y^2 + X_{th}^2 = 2x y \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^2 - 2xy + X_{th}^2 = 0$$

But $x = y - R_{th}$, so:

$$y^2 - 2(y-R_{th})y + X_{th}^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^2 - 2y^2 + 2R_{th} y + X_{th}^2 = 0$$

$$-y^2 + 2R_{th} y + X_{th}^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^2 - 2R_{th} y - X_{th}^2 = 0$$

$$y = \frac{2R_{th} \pm \sqrt{4R_{th}^2 + 4X_{th}^2}}{2} = R_{th} \pm \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

Taking the positive root and noting that $y = R_{th} + x$:

$$R_{th} + x = R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

$$x = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_2^{'}}{s_{max}} = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}}}$$

Substitute $x = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}$ back into the torque expression. The denominator becomes:

$$[R_{th} + x]^2 + X_{th}^2 = x^2 + 2R_{th} x + R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2 = 2x^2 + 2R_{th} x = 2x(x + R_{th})$$

$$= 2\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}\left(\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2} + R_{th}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2 \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}}{\omega_s \cdot 2\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}\left(\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2} + R_{th}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s \left(R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}\right)}}$$

Since $R_2^{'}$ **does not appear** in this final expression, $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance**. **(Mark allocation: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for $s_{max}$ expression, 2 marks for independence proof.)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2^{'} = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X = 1.2 \, \Omega$, $V_{th} = 200$ V, $Z_{th} = 0.5 + j1.0 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1: Synchronous speed.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Slip at maximum torque:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 1.0^2}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{0.25 + 1.0}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{1.25}}$$

$$\sqrt{1.25} = 1.1180 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{max} = \frac{0.4}{1.1180} \approx 0.358$$

**$s_{max} \approx 0.358$** — **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Maximum torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2})} = \frac{3 \times 200^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times (0.5 + 1.1180)}$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 40000}{2 \times 157.08 \times 1.6180} = \frac{120000}{508.30} \approx 236.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**$T_{max} \approx 236.1$ N·m** — **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Starting torque** ($s = 1$):

$$T_{start} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 R_2^{'}}{[(R_{th}+R_2^{'})]^2 + X_{th}^2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{200^2 \times 0.4}{[0.5 + 0.4]^2 + 1.0^2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{40000 \times 0.4}{0.81 + 1.0}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{16000}{1.81} = \frac{3 \times 8839.78}{157.08} = \frac{26519.3}{157.08} \approx 168.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**$T_{start} \approx 168.8$ N·m** — **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Induction Motor Problems:**  
> 1. **Forgetting $\omega_s$ vs $N_s$:** $T_{max}$ formula requires $\omega_s$ in **rad/s**, not rpm. Convert first.  
> 2. **Confusing $X$ and $X_{th}$:** If the problem gives total standstill reactance $X = X_1 + X_2$ directly, then $X_{th}$ may be approximated as $X$ for deep-bar/squirrel-cage simplified analysis. Always state this assumption.  
> 3. **Missing units in $V_{th}$:** Use line-to-line or phase voltage **consistently** with the formula (which uses per-phase). Convert if needed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Torque-Speed Characteristics**

- ✅ **DC Shunt:** Linear characteristic, $\omega_0 = V_t / (k_E \Phi)$, slope $m = -R_a / (k_E^2 \Phi^2)$. **Application:** Constant-speed drives.
- ✅ **DC Series:** Hyperbolic characteristic $\omega \propto 1/\sqrt{T_e}$. **Never run unloaded** (runaway). **Application:** Traction, cranes.
- ✅ **DC Compound (Cumulative):** Combination of shunt stability + series starting torque. **Application:** Presses, elevators.
- ✅ **Induction Motor:** $T_e = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2 (R_2^{'}/s)}{\omega_s [(R_{th} + R_2^{'}/s)^2 + X_{th}^2]}$. **Stable only for $0 < s < s_{max}$.**
- ✅ **Slip at Max Torque:** $s_{max} = R_2^{'} / \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2}$ — **depends on $R_2^{'}$**, so **changing rotor resistance shifts the operating point but NOT the magnitude of $T_{max}$**.
- ✅ **Max Torque:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2\omega_s (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2})}$ — **independent of $R_2^{'}$**.
- ✅ **Synchronous Motor:** Strictly constant speed $\omega = \omega_s$ for all torques up to pull-out. Used where **exact speed synchronization** is required (clocks, precision textile machinery, power-factor correction).
- ✅ **Stiffness:** A measure of speed drop per unit torque change. Stiff motors = small slope magnitude = good regulation.
- ✅ **Load Matching:** Stable operation requires that at the intersection of motor and load curves, $\dfrac{dT_m}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{\omega}$ (motor curve steeper in the negative sense).
- ✅ **Starting Torque order (typical):** DC Series > DC Compound > DC Shunt ≈ Induction (cage) > Synchronous.
- ✅ **KTU Numerical Trick:** When asked for *speed regulation*, always compute both $N_{NL}$ and $N_{FL}$ explicitly — do NOT approximate.
- ✅ **Conversion constant to memorize:** $1 \text{ rpm} = 2\pi/60 = 0.10472$ rad/s; $1$ rad/s $= 9.5493$ rpm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of DC shunt, series and compound motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of DC shunt, series and compound motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts Direct Current electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational motion) based on the interaction between the armature current-carrying conductors and the main field flux, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Principle**.

The three principal categories of DC motors are classified based on the **interconnection between the field winding and the armature winding**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions — Field Winding Topology**
>
> 1. **DC Shunt Motor:** The field winding (high resistance, many turns of fine wire) is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the armature winding across the DC supply.
>
> 2. **DC Series Motor:** The field winding (low resistance, few turns of thick wire) is connected in **series** with the armature winding, hence carrying the full armature current.
>
> 3. **DC Compound Motor:** A hybrid motor carrying **two field windings** — one connected in series and another in parallel with the armature. It is further classified as:
>    - **Cumulative Compound** (series field flux *aids* shunt field flux)
>    - **Differential Compound** (series field flux *opposes* shunt field flux)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine three workers pulling a heavy cart up a hill:

- **Shunt Motor (The Steady Pedestrian):** Has a steady, constant "internal motivation" (constant field flux). Walks at a fixed speed regardless of the small bumps on the road. Best for smooth, predictable journeys.
- **Series Motor (The Sprinting Horse):** The harder the load (more current demanded), the more it "kicks in" with extra strength (flux increases). Cannot run unloaded — like a horse that bolts if no cart is attached.
- **Compound Motor (The Hybrid Vehicle):** Combines a cruise-control system (shunt field) with a turbocharger (series field) — giving both stable cruising *and* extra boost when climbing hills.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold Constants)**
> - Standard DC supply in India: **220 V DC** (traction) and **240 V DC**
> - Industrial DC drives: **400 V / 440 V / 550 V / 600 V DC**
> - Back EMF constant: $k = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{60 A}$ (V/rpm·Wb)
> - Armature reaction demagnetizing effect is compensated using **interpoles** and **compensating windings**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Characteristics Breakdown

#### A. DC Shunt Motor — Operational Logic

The shunt field is connected across a **constant DC supply voltage**. Therefore, the shunt field current $I_{sh}$ remains approximately **constant** because the field winding resistance $R_{sh}$ is very high and does not change significantly.

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} \approx \text{constant}
$$

- **Flux ($\Phi$):** Practically constant (only minor drop due to armature reaction).
- **Speed:** Nearly constant from no-load to full-load (excellent speed regulation).
- **Starting Torque:** Moderate (1.5 to 2 times full-load torque).
- **Speed-Torque Curve:** A nearly straight line with a very gentle negative slope.

**Why constant speed?** Because $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\Phi}$ and $\Phi$ is constant. The small drop in speed with load is because the back EMF $E_b$ decreases as armature drop $I_a R_a$ increases.

#### B. DC Series Motor — Operational Logic

The field winding is in series with the armature, so $I_{se} = I_a$. Before magnetic saturation, $\Phi \propto I_a$.

- **Flux:** Directly proportional to armature current (until saturation).
- **Speed:** Inversely proportional to load → varies widely.
- **Starting Torque:** Very high (up to 4–5 times full-load torque).
- **Never run on no-load** — flux becomes very small, speed becomes dangerously infinite (mechanical destruction).

#### C. DC Compound Motor — Operational Logic

Two fluxes: $\Phi_{sh}$ (constant) and $\Phi_{se}$ (variable with load).

$$
\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}
$$

- **Cumulative:** $\Phi_{net}$ increases with load → speed decreases more rapidly than shunt; high starting torque.
- **Differential:** $\Phi_{net}$ decreases with load → speed increases with load (unstable, rarely used).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Shunt Motor** | **Series Motor** | **Compound Motor** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field Connection | Parallel to Armature | Series with Armature | Both (Parallel + Series) |
| Field Current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | $I_{se} = I_a$ | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}},\; I_{se} = I_a$ |
| Flux $\Phi$ | Approximately **constant** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) | $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}$ |
| Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | $E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})$ | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ |
| Speed Equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi}$ | Same general form |
| Speed Regulation | **Good** ($\approx$ 5–15%) | **Poor** (very wide variation) | **Moderate** (cumulative) |
| Starting Torque | Moderate (1.5–2 $\times$ FLT) | Very High (4–5 $\times$ FLT) | High (2.5–3.5 $\times$ FLT) |
| No-Load Operation | Safe | **Dangerous — must avoid** | Safe |
| Cost | Low | Lowest | Highest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> The choice of DC motor is dictated by the **load characteristic** of the driven machine. For instance, traction requires high starting torque → series motor. Machine tools require constant speed → shunt motor. Presses and elevators (high torque + stable speed) → cumulative compound motor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Speed Equation of a DC Motor

**Starting Point — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) applied to the armature circuit:**

The supply voltage $V$ is dropped across two elements:
1. The armature resistance drop $I_a R_a$
2. The back EMF $E_b$ (induced, opposing $V$ by Lenz's Law)

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Rearranging to express $E_b$:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Faraday's Law Expression for Back EMF:**

The back EMF is proportional to the speed $N$ and the flux per pole $\Phi$:

$$
E_b = k \Phi N
$$

where $k = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A}$ is the machine constant (P = poles, Z = total conductors, A = parallel paths).

**Equating both expressions of $E_b$:**

$$
k \Phi N = V - I_a R_a
$$

**Solving for the speed $N$:**

$$
N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

This is the **fundamental speed equation** of any DC motor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Mechanical power developed in the armature:**

$$
P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

where $T$ is the torque in N·m.

**Substituting $E_b = k \Phi N$:**

$$
k \Phi N \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

**Cancelling $N$ from both sides:**

$$
k \Phi I_a = \dfrac{2 \pi T}{60}
$$

**Solving for torque $T$:**

$$
T = \dfrac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot k \Phi I_a = K \Phi I_a
$$

where $K = \dfrac{60 k}{2 \pi}$ is the torque constant.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Speed-Torque Behaviour

For a **Shunt Motor** with $\Phi \approx \text{constant}$:

$$
N = N_0 - \dfrac{R_a}{k \Phi} \cdot I_a
$$

Substituting $I_a = \dfrac{T}{K \Phi}$:

$$
N = N_0 - \dfrac{R_a}{k (K \Phi^2)} \cdot T
$$

This is a **linear equation** in $T$ → the speed-torque characteristic is a straight line.

For a **Series Motor** with $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region):

Let $\Phi = c \cdot I_a$. Then:

$$
T = K (c I_a) I_a = K c I_a^2 \implies I_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{K c}}
$$

And:

$$
N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi} = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k c I_a}
$$

This is an **inverse-square type** relationship → speed varies as $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ approximately, giving a hyperbolic curve.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark Type)

> **Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \; \Omega$ and field resistance $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$. At no-load, the armature takes 3 A at 1500 rpm. At full load, armature current is 50 A. Assuming flux is constant, calculate: (a) speed at full load, (b) developed torque at full load.

**Solution:**

**(a) Shunt field current (constant):**

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{220}{110} = 2 \; \text{A}
$$

**Back EMF at no-load:**

$$
E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (3)(0.3) = 220 - 0.9 = 219.1 \; \text{V}
$$

**Back EMF at full load:**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.3) = 220 - 15 = 205 \; \text{V}
$$

**Speed ratio (since $\Phi$ constant):**

$$
\dfrac{N_1}{N_0} = \dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b0}}
$$

$$
N_1 = 1500 \times \dfrac{205}{219.1} = 1500 \times 0.9357 = 1403.5 \; \text{rpm}
$$

**(b) Developed torque at full load:**

$$
T = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1}}{2 \pi N_1} = \dfrac{60 \times 205 \times 50}{2 \pi \times 1403.5}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{615000}{8819.5} = 69.73 \; \text{N·m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
