<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> Why is a DC series motor never started on no-load? What happens if it is accidentally run on no-load?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A DC series motor carries the entire armature current through its field winding. On no-load, the armature current $I_a$ drops to a very small value (only to supply friction and windage losses). This causes the flux $\Phi$ to become extremely small. Since speed $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$, as $\Phi \to 0$, $N \to \infty$. The motor thus reaches a dangerously high "runaway" speed that can mechanically destroy the armature due to excessive centrifugal forces. Hence, a series motor must always be mechanically coupled to its load before starting — typically through a **load-bearing gear or pinion** that cannot be decoupled.

*(Keywords for full marks: no-load flux → zero, speed → infinite, centrifugal force → mechanical damage, always coupled.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> Mention any three applications each of DC shunt and DC series motors and justify one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**DC Shunt Motor Applications:**
1. **Lathe machines** — require constant cutting speed regardless of depth of cut.
2. **Centrifugal pumps and fans** — speed must remain steady for consistent flow.
3. **Printing machines** — uniform speed ensures consistent print quality.

*Justification for Lathe:* Speed regulation is critical because cutting speed determines surface finish; shunt motor's nearly constant speed ensures this.

**DC Series Motor Applications:**
1. **Electric locomotives and traction systems.**
2. **Cranes, hoists, and winches.**
3. **Electric vehicles (older designs).**

*Justification for Traction:* A locomotive needs extremely high starting torque to pull a heavy train from rest; series motors provide 4–5 times full-load torque at start, which is essential.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the emf equation and torque equation of a DC motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance of $0.5 \; \Omega$ and field resistance of $250 \; \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws 5 A armature current at 1000 rpm. At full load, armature current is 40 A. Assuming the flux is reduced by 4% due to armature reaction at full load, calculate: (i) full-load speed, (ii) full-load torque, (iii) armature power developed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EMF and Torque Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

Apply KVL across the armature circuit:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a \implies E_b = V - I_a R_a
$$

From Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction, the back EMF induced in a DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total conductors, $\Phi$ flux per pole, and $A$ parallel paths is:

$$
E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}
$$

Let $k = \dfrac{P Z}{60 A}$, hence:

$$
E_b = k \Phi N
$$

Therefore the **EMF equation** is:

$$
E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}
$$

The mechanical power developed equals electrical power converted:

$$
P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

Substituting $E_b = k\Phi N$:

$$
k \Phi N I_a = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

Cancelling $N$:

$$
T = \dfrac{60 \, k \Phi I_a}{2 \pi} = K \Phi I_a
$$

This is the **torque equation** of a DC motor.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.5 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250 \; \Omega$, $I_{a0} = 5$ A, $N_0 = 1000$ rpm, $I_{a1} = 40$ A, $\Phi_1 = 0.96 \Phi_0$.

**Step 1 — No-load back EMF:**

$$
E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 250 - (5)(0.5) = 247.5 \; \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-load back EMF:**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 250 - (40)(0.5) = 230 \; \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio equation (with flux change):**

$$
\dfrac{N_1}{N_0} = \dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b0}} \times \dfrac{\Phi_0}{\Phi_1}
$$

$$
N_1 = 1000 \times \dfrac{230}{247.5} \times \dfrac{1}{0.96}
$$

$$
N_1 = 1000 \times 0.9293 \times 1.0417 = 968.0 \; \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Full-load torque:**

$$
T_1 = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1}}{2 \pi N_1} = \dfrac{60 \times 230 \times 40}{2 \pi \times 968.0}
$$

$$
T_1 = \dfrac{552000}{6081.7} = 90.76 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Power developed in armature:**

$$
P_m = E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1} = 230 \times 40 = 9200 \; \text{W} = 9.2 \; \text{kW}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** Compare DC shunt, series and compound motors with respect to (i) speed regulation, (ii) starting torque, (iii) no-load safety, and (iv) applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a 4-pole DC compound motor. Discuss cumulative and differential compounding. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | DC Shunt | DC Series | DC Cumulative Compound |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed Regulation | Good | Poor | Moderate |
| Starting Torque | Moderate | Very High | High |
| No-Load Safety | Safe | Unsafe | Safe |
| Speed Characteristic | Approximately Constant | Inversely proportional to load | Slight decrease with load |
| Applications | Lathes, fans, pumps | Traction, hoists | Rolling mills, elevators |

**(b) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

- **Yoke (Frame):** Provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux.
- **Pole Cores & Pole Shoes:** Made of laminated silicon steel; support field windings and spread flux uniformly across the air gap.
- **Field Windings:** Two distinct windings — *shunt* (fine wire, many turns, high resistance) connected across supply; *series* (thick wire, few turns, low resistance) in series with armature.
- **Armature Core:** Laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy current losses; slots house the armature winding.
- **Armature Winding:** Lap or wave wound; closed winding delivering current through commutator.
- **Commutator & Brushes:** Mechanical rectifier converting AC induced in armature to DC at the brushes.

**Cumulative vs Differential:**
- **Cumulative:** Series field MMF *aids* shunt field MMF → net flux increases with load → high starting torque, stable operation.
- **Differential:** Series field MMF *opposes* shunt field MMF → net flux decreases with load → speed rises dangerously with load → unstable; rarely used except in specific arc-welding generators.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert shunt motor's flux reduction due to armature reaction** when computing speed at full load → wrong numerical answer.
> 2. **Mixing up $E_b$ (back EMF)** with supply voltage $V$ in torque calculations — always use $E_b$, not $V$.
> 3. **Writing "compound motor is used everywhere"** in Part A — examiners expect *specific* applications with *justification*.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions on motor construction — a neatly labeled sketch carries 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing cumulative vs differential** — examiners frequently ask this; remember "cumulative = aiding = stable, differential = opposing = unstable".

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- DC motors are classified by field winding topology: **Shunt, Series, Compound**.
- **Shunt Motor:** Field in parallel; **constant flux → constant speed**; used in lathes, fans, pumps.
- **Series Motor:** Field in series; flux $\propto I_a$; **high starting torque but no-load runaway**; used in traction, cranes.
- **Compound Motor:** Both windings; **cumulative** (stable, high torque) is preferred; used in rolling mills, elevators, presses.
- **Differential compound:** Opposing fields; flux reduces with load; speed rises dangerously → rarely used.
- Key equations: $E_b = k\Phi N$, $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$, $T = K \Phi I_a$.
- Speed regulation order: **Shunt > Cumulative Compound > Differential Compound > Series**.
- Starting torque order: **Series > Compound (Cumulative) > Shunt**.
- A series motor **must never be belt-coupled** to its load (no-load danger); always gear or direct-coupled.
- Standard Indian traction voltages: **600 V DC** (trams), **750 V DC** (metros), **1500 V DC / 25 kV AC** (Indian Railways mainline).
- Always mention **armature reaction** in long-answer questions on DC motors — KTU examiners reward this awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of starting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of starting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Electric Drives and Automation (PEAUT525)**, the **Principles of Starting** refer to the engineering methodology, control strategies, and power-electronic switching techniques employed to safely accelerate an electric motor from standstill (n = 0) to its operating speed while simultaneously constraining the **inrush (starting) current**, the **starting torque**, and the **mechanical stress** on the rotor and coupling within permissible design limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 1):** The student must analyze, compare, and justify the selection of starting methods for **DC Motors (Shunt, Series, Compound)** and **Three-Phase Induction Motors (Squirrel-Cage and Slip-Ring)** with mathematical rigor.

The generalized starting problem is governed by the fundamental motor equation at n = 0:

$$\text{Back-EMF } (E_b) = 0 \quad \text{at standstill}$$

This single physical condition is the *root cause* of every starting problem in DC and AC machines. Because the counter-EMF (which normally opposes the supply and limits current) is zero at rest, the motor draws a dangerously high current limited only by the winding's ohmic resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why starters are mandatory in industrial drives:**
> - Prevent **mechanical shock** to belts, gears, and couplings.
> - Limit **locked-rotor current** to safe values (typically 5×–7× Full-Load Current in induction motors).
> - Protect the **insulation** of stator/rotor windings from thermal overload.
> - Maintain **supply voltage stability** for neighboring loads on the same grid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a **fully loaded, stationary railway wagon** (mass = 50 tonnes) by hand:
- If you apply your **full strength immediately**, you will injure yourself and the wagon may jerk violently, breaking the coupling hook.
- A skilled worker instead applies a **graduated, controlled force** — first a small push, then incrementally greater force as the wagon begins to roll.

This is **exactly** what an electric motor starter does. At rest, the rotor has zero back-EMF acting as a "natural brake" against current flow. The starter acts as the **intelligent intermediary** that limits the initial electrical "push" (voltage/current) and progressively allows full power as the motor gains speed and develops its own opposing back-EMF.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum permissible starting current** | **1.5 × to 2.0 × Full-Load Current (FLC)** |
| **DOL inrush current (Induction motor)** | **5× to 7× FLC** |
| **DOL inrush current (DC shunt motor)** | **10× to 20× FLC** |
| **Minimum starting torque required** | **1.1 × to 1.5 × Load Torque** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of "Why Starting is Hard"

When a motor is connected directly across rated supply voltage with no starter, two catastrophic conditions arise simultaneously:

**Step 1 — Electrical Catastrophe (Current):**
At n = 0, the back-EMF E_b = 0. Therefore, the armature/stator current is limited only by the very small winding resistance (R_a or R_s). This causes a current surge of enormous magnitude.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Catastrophe (Torque):**
In induction motors, the starting torque under DOL is often **only 0.5× to 1.5× of full-load torque** — which is insufficient to break the static friction of heavy industrial loads (crushers, conveyors, elevators).

This creates a paradox: **High current but low torque.** The starter must simultaneously reduce current *and* enhance torque.

### 2.2 Starting Methods — Taxonomy and Logic

#### A. For DC Motors
1. **Face-plate / Conventional Starter** (3-point and 4-point starters)
2. **Thyristor-controlled soft starter**
3. **Ward-Leonard starting (voltage control)**

#### B. For Three-Phase Induction Motors (Squirrel-Cage Rotor)
1. **Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter** — full voltage, simplest
2. **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter** — voltage reduced to 1/√3
3. **Autotransformer Starter** — adjustable voltage tap
4. **Soft Starter (SCR-based phase-angle control)**
5. **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** — true soft start with frequency reduction

#### C. For Slip-Ring (Wound-Rotor) Induction Motors
1. **Rotor Resistance Starting** — external rheostat in rotor circuit

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Starting Method | Starting Current (I_st) | Starting Torque (T_st) | Application / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** | $I_{st} = 5 \text{ to } 7 \times I_{fl}$ | $T_{st} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \times T_{fl}$ | Small motors < 5 HP |
| 2 | **Star-Delta (Y-Δ)** | $I_{st,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot I_{st,DOL}$ | $T_{st,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st,DOL}$ | Medium loads, centrifugal pumps |
| 3 | **Autotransformer (tap x)** | $I_{st,line} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$ | $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}$ | High-inertia loads, compressors |
| 4 | **Rotor Resistance (Slip-Ring)** | $I_{st} \propto \dfrac{1}{R_{rotor}}$ | $T_{st} \uparrow$ significantly (max possible) | Cranes, hoists, elevators |
| 5 | **DC Shunt — 3-Point Starter** | $I_{a} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{ext}}$ | $T_{st} \propto I_a$ | Constant-speed applications |
| 6 | **VFD Starting** | $I_{st} \approx I_{fl}$ | $T_{st}$ adjustable to 1.5× T_fl | Premium applications |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of a starting method directly influences the **Energy Bill**, **Power Quality**, and **Equipment Life-Cycle**:
- **Cement Industry**: Uses **Slip-Ring motors with rotor resistance** starting for ball mills (high inertia).
- **HVAC and Pumps**: **Star-Delta** dominates due to light starting load and cost-effectiveness.
- **Oil & Gas Compressors**: **Autotransformer** or **VFD** starting is mandated.
- **Electric Vehicles (EV)**: Uses **PMSM** motors with **VFD/inverter starting** for instant high torque at zero speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Starting Current of a Three-Phase Induction Motor at Standstill

**Given:** Three-phase induction motor with per-phase standstill impedance $Z_s = R_s + jX_s$ connected to a line-to-line supply voltage $V_L$.

**Step 1:** At standstill (s = 1), the rotor circuit impedance as referred to stator is added to the stator impedance. For a simplified equivalent, the per-phase Thevenin voltage drives the standstill impedance.

**Step 2:** The per-phase starting current magnitude is given by Ohm's law for AC circuits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{phase}} &= \frac{V_{ph}}{Z_s} \\
&= \frac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a star-connected stator, the line current equals phase current. The line-to-line voltage is related to phase voltage by $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ for a star connection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{line}} &= I_{st,\text{phase}} \\
&= \frac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2}} \\
&= \frac{V_L \,/\, \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator is divided by $\sqrt{3}$ to convert the line-to-line supply voltage (which is the industrial standard, e.g., 415 V) into the per-phase voltage required for the wye-connected stator.

**Step 4:** Express the starting current as a multiple of full-load current. Typically $I_{st} \approx 5 \text{ to } 7 \times I_{fl}$ because $X_s \gg R_s$ in industrial motors.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Star-Delta Starting: Reduction in Current and Torque

**Given:** A motor draws starting current $I_{st,DOL}$ and produces starting torque $T_{st,DOL}$ under Direct-On-Line conditions.

**Step 1:** When the stator is reconfigured in STAR (Y) during starting, the phase voltage becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ph,Y} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** In star connection, each winding receives only $1/\sqrt{3}$ (≈ 57.7%) of the line voltage because the windings are connected between a line and the neutral point.

**Step 2:** The starting current in star mode per phase is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,Y,\text{phase}} &= \frac{V_{ph,Y}}{Z_s} = \frac{V_L \,/\, \sqrt{3}}{Z_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a star connection, the line current equals the phase current. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,Y,\text{line}} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_s} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_{st,DOL,\text{line}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Starting torque is proportional to the **square of the applied voltage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st,Y} &= T_{st,DOL} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{ph,Y}}{V_{ph,DOL}}\right)^2 \\
&= T_{st,DOL} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \\
T_{st,Y} &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot T_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Result for KTU Board Exam:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,Y} &= \frac{I_{st,DOL}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577 \cdot I_{st,DOL} \\
T_{st,Y} &= \frac{T_{st,DOL}}{3} \approx 0.333 \cdot T_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autotransformer Starting with Tap Ratio x

**Given:** An autotransformer with a voltage tap ratio $x$ (where $x = \frac{V_{secondary}}{V_{primary}}$, typically 0.5, 0.65, or 0.8).

**Step 1:** The motor terminal voltage during start is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{motor} &= x \cdot V_{supply}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The current drawn by the motor from the autotransformer secondary is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{motor} &= \frac{x \cdot V_{supply}}{Z_s} = x \cdot I_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Applying transformer action, the line current (drawn from the supply) is reduced by the transformation ratio $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{line}} &= x \cdot I_{motor} \\
&= x \cdot (x \cdot I_{st,DOL}) \\
I_{st,\text{line}} &= x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Starting torque scales with the square of the motor terminal voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= T_{st,DOL} \cdot x^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{line}} &= x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL} \\
T_{st} &= x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Unlike Star-Delta, autotransformer starting allows **independent tap selection** (50%, 65%, 80%). For a 65% tap, $I_{st} = 0.65^2 = 0.4225 \times I_{DOL}$ and $T_{st} = 0.4225 \times T_{DOL}$ — a **better torque profile** than star-delta for the same current reduction.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — DC Shunt Motor Starting Resistance Calculation

**Given:** DC shunt motor with armature resistance $R_a$, supply voltage $V$, and desired maximum armature current $I_1$ during the first starting step.

**Step 1:** Total circuit resistance required to limit current to $I_1$ at start:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total,1} &= \frac{V}{I_1} = R_a + R_{ext,1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the external starting resistance for the first step is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ext,1} &= \frac{V}{I_1} - R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** As the motor accelerates, back-EMF $E_b$ builds up. When current drops to a lower threshold $I_2$, the first resistance section is cut out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total,2} &= \frac{V - E_{b,2}}{I_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The process is repeated through $n$ steps until all external resistance is removed and the motor runs on its natural $V - I_a R_a$ characteristic.

**Final Standard Formula for a Step-1 Starter:**

$$
\boxed{
R_{ext} = \frac{V}{I_{max}} - R_a
}
$$

---

### 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix (Analytical Summary)

| Method | Current Reduction | Torque Reduction | Cost | Smoothness | KTU Exam Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DOL** | 1× (baseline) | 1× (baseline) | Very Low | Poor (jerk) | **High** |
| **Star-Delta** | 1/√3 ≈ 0.577× | 1/3 ≈ 0.333× | Low | Discontinuous (transient) | **Very High** |
| **Autotransformer** | $x^2$ (selectable) | $x^2$ (selectable) | Medium | Smooth (tap selection) | **Very High** |
| **Rotor Resistance** | Significantly reduced | **Increased** | Medium-High | Excellent & smooth | **High** |
| **VFD** | ≈ 1× (no surge) | Adjustable | High | Excellent (continuous) | **High (modern)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Motor Starting Methods

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Why is a starter necessary for a DC shunt motor? What will happen if it is started without a starter?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A starter is required to limit the **armature current** during starting. At standstill, the back-EMF $E_b = 0$, so the armature current is given by $I_a = V / R_a$. Since $R_a$ is very small (0.1 Ω to 1 Ω), the current becomes dangerously high (10× to 20× of full-load current).

**Consequences of starting without a starter:**
- The commutator and brushes will be damaged due to heavy sparking.
- The windings may burn out due to $I^2R$ heating.
- The protective fuses/MCBs will trip, disconnecting the supply.
- Mechanical shock may damage the coupling and the driven load.

Hence, a starter with external resistance (3-point/4-point) is essential to limit $I_a$ to a safe value of **1.5 to 2 times full-load current** during starting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Compare star-delta starting with autotransformer starting of a 3-phase induction motor. Mention two advantages of autotransformer starting over star-delta.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Star-Delta | Autotransformer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voltage reduction factor | Fixed $1/\sqrt{3}$ | Adjustable by tap (0.5, 0.65, 0.8) |
| Starting current | $I_{st} = I_{DOL}/\sqrt{3}$ | $I_{st} = x^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$ |
| Starting torque | $T_{st} = T_{DOL}/3$ | $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$ |

**Two Advantages of Autotransformer Starting:**
1. **Tap Selection Flexibility:** The starting voltage can be optimized to match the load requirement, whereas star-delta offers only one fixed reduction factor.
2. **Better Torque-to-Current Ratio:** For the same current reduction, the autotransformer delivers higher starting torque because the torque reduction equals current reduction ($x^2$), whereas in star-delta the torque is reduced by $1/3$ — three times the current reduction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from the choice provided. Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[a] [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Explain with neat diagrams the working of a 3-point starter used for starting a DC shunt motor. Why is a no-volt release (NVR) coil provided in the starter?*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A 3-point starter is a conventional face-plate-type starter used to limit the starting current of a DC shunt motor by inserting external resistance in series with the armature circuit. The resistance is cut out gradually as the motor accelerates.

**Constructional Features (2 Marks):**
The starter consists of:
- A **resistance bank** divided into several sections (R₁, R₂, R₃ ... R_n) connected to brass studs on a face plate.
- A **movable brass lever (handle)** that slides over the studs to cut out resistance step by step.
- A **No-Volt Release (NVR) coil** connected between the first stud and the negative line, in series with the shunt field winding.
- A **Hold-on electromagnet** (soft iron piece) to keep the lever in the running position against spring tension.
- An **Overload Release (OLR)** mechanism for protection against excessive armature current.

**Working (3 Marks):**
When the starter lever is moved to stud 1, the full starting resistance is inserted in the armature circuit. The starting current is limited to a safe value: $I_a = V / (R_a + R_{ext})$. As the motor accelerates, back-EMF $E_b$ builds up, and the lever is progressively moved to studs 2, 3, ... until the final stud, where the entire external resistance is cut out and the armature is directly connected to the supply.

**Role of NVR (1 Mark):**
The NVR coil is energized by the supply voltage via the field winding. If the supply fails, the NVR coil de-energizes, the hold-on electromagnet releases the lever, and the spring returns the lever to the OFF position. This prevents the motor from restarting automatically when the supply resumes (which could endanger an operator who had switched it off for maintenance).

---

**[b] [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has a standstill impedance per phase of (1.5 + j3.0) Ω. The motor draws a full-load current of 20 A at 0.85 power factor lagging. Calculate:*
*(i) The starting current at direct-on-line (DOL) starting.*
*(ii) The starting current and starting torque when started using a star-delta starter.*
*(iii) The starting current and starting torque when started using a 65% tap autotransformer.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Line voltage $V_L = 400$ V (Y-connected motor assumed, since star-delta is specified)
- Per-phase impedance at standstill: $Z_s = (1.5 + j3.0)$ Ω
- Full-load current $I_{fl} = 20$ A

**Calculation of Impedance Magnitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_s &= \sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1.5)^2 + (3.0)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2.25 + 9.00} \\
&= \sqrt{11.25} \\
&\approx 3.354 \;\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase voltage (star-connected):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ph} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 230.94 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(i) DOL Starting Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,DOL} &= \frac{V_{ph}}{Z_s} = \frac{230.94}{3.354} \\
&\approx 68.85 \text{ A (per phase)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the motor is star-connected, $I_{st,line} = I_{st,phase} = \mathbf{68.85 \text{ A}}$.

---

**(ii) Star-Delta Starting:**

In star mode, $V_{ph,Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V (which is the same as the rated phase voltage here, since the motor runs in delta normally).

*Wait — Correction:* When a motor is rated for **delta running**, its per-phase winding is designed for line voltage. The given impedance is per phase. Let us redo the DOL calculation correctly:

For a **delta-connected motor**, the per-phase voltage at DOL equals the line voltage: $V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L = 400$ V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,DOL,phase} &= \frac{400}{3.354} \approx 119.26 \text{ A} \\
I_{st,DOL,line} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{st,phase} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 119.26 \approx 206.6 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

In star mode: $V_{ph,Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V, and $I_{line,Y} = I_{phase,Y}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,Y,line} &= \frac{230.94}{3.354} \approx 68.85 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Torque ratio:** $T_{st,Y} / T_{st,DOL} = (1/\sqrt{3})^2 = 1/3$.

**Assuming DOL starting torque = 2 × full-load torque (standard estimate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st,DOL} &= 2 \times T_{fl} \\
T_{st,Y} &= \frac{1}{3} \times 2 \times T_{fl} = \frac{2}{3} T_{fl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Results:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,DOL,line} &\approx 206.6 \text{ A} \\
I_{st,Y,line} &\approx 68.85 \text{ A} = \frac{I_{st,DOL}}{\sqrt{3}} \\
T_{st,Y} &\approx \frac{2}{3} T_{fl}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

**(iii) 65% Tap Autotransformer:**

With $x = 0.65$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,line} &= x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL,line} \\
&= (0.65)^2 \cdot 206.6 \\
&= 0.4225 \cdot 206.6 \\
&\approx 87.29 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL} \\
&= 0.4225 \cdot 2 \cdot T_{fl} \\
&\approx 0.845 \, T_{fl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Results:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{auto,line}} &\approx 87.29 \text{ A} \\
T_{st,\text{auto}} &\approx 0.845 \, T_{fl}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 Marks):** Confusing phase and line voltage for a delta-connected motor. Always re-read the problem to identify whether the motor runs in star or delta.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 Mark):** Forgetting to multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ when converting $I_{phase}$ to $I_{line}$ in a delta connection.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 Mark):** Writing $I_{st,Y} = I_{st,DOL}/3$ instead of $I_{st,DOL}/\sqrt{3}$. The factor is $\sqrt{3}$ for current, **not 3** — the factor 3 applies to torque reduction only.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[a] [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021, May 2024 Expected]**
*With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the principle of operation of an autotransformer starter for a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Derive the expressions for starting current and starting torque in terms of the tap ratio x.*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**
A 3-phase autotransformer has its primary windings connected to the 3-phase supply (415 V) and its secondary windings connected to the motor terminals through a set of contactors. Taps are provided on the autotransformer at **50%, 65%, and 80%** of the primary voltage. The starter consists of:
- A **tap-changing switch** to select the desired starting voltage.
- **Contactors** to disconnect the autotransformer and connect the motor directly to the supply once it accelerates.
- **Overload and short-circuit protection** relays.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
During starting, the motor is connected to the secondary of the autotransformer. The motor receives a reduced voltage $V_{motor} = x \cdot V_{supply}$, where $x$ is the tap ratio. This reduced voltage limits the starting current. As the motor accelerates and develops back-EMF, the autotransformer is disconnected and the motor is connected directly to the full supply voltage through a run contactor.

**Derivations (2 Marks):** The mathematical derivations are as derived in Section 3.3 above:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,\text{line}} &= x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL} \\
T_{st} &= x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

**[b] [7 Marks]**
*Explain the rotor resistance starting method for a slip-ring induction motor. Why is this method preferred for high-inertia loads like cranes and hoists? Compare it with squirrel-cage induction motor starting.*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
A slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor has three slip rings on the rotor shaft connected to the three-phase rotor windings. External resistances are connected to these slip rings via brushes. The resistance can be varied using a **liquid rheostat** or a **graded metallic resistance bank** with a controller.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
At starting, full external resistance $R_{ext}$ is added in the rotor circuit. This:
- **Increases the starting torque** significantly (often up to 2× to 2.5× of full-load torque).
- **Reduces the starting current** simultaneously.
As the motor accelerates, the external resistance is progressively cut out in steps, and the motor finally runs with the slip rings short-circuited.

**Why Preferred for High-Inertia Loads (1 Mark):**
Cranes and hoists require **high starting torque** to lift heavy loads from rest, **without drawing excessive current** from the supply. Rotor resistance starting uniquely provides both: maximum torque at minimum current. The torque-speed curve becomes "stretchable" — the starting slip can be placed where the torque is maximum.

**Comparison with Squirrel-Cage (1 Mark):**
| Feature | Slip-Ring (Rotor Resistance) | Squirrel-Cage (DOL/Star-Delta) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Starting Torque | **High (up to 2.5× T_fl)** | **Low to Moderate (0.5× to 1.5× T_fl)** |
| Starting Current | **Low (controlled by R_ext)** | **High (5× to 7× I_fl)** |
| Cost & Maintenance | Higher (slip rings, brushes) | Lower (rugged, brushless) |
| Speed Control | Possible (by varying R_ext) | Not possible |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 Marks):** Not drawing the circuit diagram for autotransformer/3-point starter — KTU examiners award dedicated marks for neat labeled diagrams.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 Marks):** Confusing the relationship between starting current and tap ratio. **Both** $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ scale with $x^2$ for autotransformer, but for star-delta the current scales with $1/\sqrt{3}$ and torque with $1/3$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 Mark):** Writing "slip-ring motor" without explaining what slip rings are and how they allow rotor resistance to be varied externally.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision!**

- ☐ At standstill, **back-EMF = 0**, so $I_{st} = V / R$ (very high current).
- ☐ DC shunt motor starting requires a **3-point or 4-point starter** to limit $I_a$ to **1.5×–2× I_fl**.
- ☐ **4-point starter** is used when the shunt field current is very small (the NVR coil would otherwise fail to hold the lever in a 3-point starter).
- ☐ **DOL starting**: Simplest, cheapest, but inrush = **5×–7× FLC** — only for small motors.
- ☐ **Star-Delta starting**: $I_{st}$ reduced by $1/\sqrt{3}$, $T_{st}$ reduced by $1/3$ — suitable for light-to-medium starting loads.
- ☐ **Autotransformer starting**: $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ both reduced by $x^2$ — flexible tap selection.
- ☐ **Rotor resistance starting** (slip-ring motor): **High starting torque** with **low starting current** — best for cranes, hoists, elevators.
- ☐ **VFD starting**: Modern premium method — current ≈ FLC, smooth torque control, but high cost.
- ☐ **Overload Release (OLR)** protects the motor against sustained overcurrent.
- ☐ **No-Volt Release (NVR)** ensures the motor does not restart automatically after a power failure.
- ☐ **Motor terminal voltage** in any reduced-voltage starting method determines both $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ through the $V^2$ relationship.
- ☐ For KTU numericals: Always identify whether the motor is **star or delta connected** before computing $I_{st,line}$.
- ☐ Industrial rule of thumb: **$I_{st} \leq 1.5 \times I_{fl}$** is the target after the starter action.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transformers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Transformer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **transformer** is a **static electrical machine** that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current circuit to another through **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency, while its voltage and current levels are transformed according to the **turns ratio** of its windings. In the context of **Electric Drives and Controls for Automation**, transformers are not merely step-up/step-down power devices. They serve as **instrument transformers** (Current Transformers — CTs, and Potential Transformers — PTs) for feedback signals, as **isolation transformers** for breaking ground loops in PLC/Controller panels, as **pulse transformers** for gate-drive circuits of IGBTs/thyristors in variable-frequency drives (VFDs), and as **drive input/output transformers** for matching voltage levels and providing harmonic mitigation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Water-Pressure Booster Pump" Analogy:** > Imagine water flowing through a pipe. If you want to deliver the *same quantity* of water but at a *higher pressure* to a distant floor, you use a booster pump. The booster takes in low-pressure water and outputs high-pressure water — sacrificing flow for pressure. > > A transformer is the *electrical twin* of this pump. It takes in **low-voltage, high-current** AC power and outputs **high-voltage, low-current** power (step-up), or the reverse (step-down). The *amount of energy* (roughly) stays the same; only the *form* (pressure ↔ flow) changes. The two pipe sections are physically separated but hydraulically coupled through the pump — exactly like the two coils being electrically isolated but magnetically coupled through the iron core. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Frequency of operation (f)** in **Hz**: For India, the standard grid frequency is **50 Hz**; in North America, it is **60 Hz**. - **Maximum flux density (B_m)** in core: typically between **1.1 T to 1.4 T** for CRGO (Cold Rolled Grain Oriented) silicon-steel laminations used in power transformers. - **Lamination thickness**: typically **0.23 mm to 0.35 mm** to reduce eddy-current losses. - **Standard transformer oil** (for oil-cooled units): mineral oil with dielectric strength ≥ **30 kV / 2.5 mm gap**. - **Efficiency benchmark**: large power transformers can reach **98% to 99.5%**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Breakdown A transformer physically consists of: 1. **Magnetic Core** — Made of laminated silicon-steel sheets (CRGO/M4/M5 grade). Two structural configurations exist: - **Core-type**: Windings surround a part of the core. The core has a rectangular cross-section. - **Shell-type**: Core surrounds a major portion of the windings. Better mechanical protection and reduced leakage flux. 2. **Primary Winding (N₁)** — The input winding, connected to the AC source. 3. **Secondary Winding (N₂)** — The output winding, connected to the load. 4. **Tank, Conservator, Breather, Bushings** (for oil-immersed units) — Provide insulation, cooling, and protection against moisture. 5. **Tap Changer** (off-load or on-load) — Allows ±5% or ±10% variation in turns ratio for voltage regulation. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!NOTE] > The transformer operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. **Step 1 — Excitation:** When AC voltage **V₁** is applied to the primary, an alternating current **I₁** flows and sets up an alternating **mutual flux Φ** in the core. **Step 2 — Induced EMF (Primary side):** The alternating flux links with the primary itself and induces a self-induced EMF **E₁** that opposes **V₁** (per **Lenz's Law**). At steady state, **V₁ ≈ E₁**. **Step 3 — Flux linkage (Secondary side):** The same alternating flux also links the secondary winding, inducing an EMF **E₂** across its terminals. **Step 4 — Load current:** If a load is connected, current **I₂** flows in the secondary, delivering power **V₂ × I₂** to the load. The current **I₂** itself creates a demagnetizing MMF, which is instantly neutralized by the primary drawing extra current from the source — this is **MMF balance (N₁I₁ ≈ N₂I₂)**. **Step 5 — Energy transfer:** The link between the two sides is purely **magnetic**, so there is no electrical connection. Power is transferred via the **core flux**. ## 2.3 EMF Equation — The Heart of Transformer Theory > [!IMPORTANT] > **EMF Equation (RMS value per phase, sinusoidal flux):** > > $$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$ > > where $\Phi_{m}$ is the **maximum (peak) flux** in Webers. The constant **4.44** comes from: $4.44 = \pi \sqrt{2} / \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2} = 4.44$ (derived as form factor × shape factor for a pure sine wave). For square-wave flux (as in inverter-fed drive isolation transformers), the constant becomes **4.0**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | EMF per turn | $E_t = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m$ | Volts per turn | | 2 | Total EMF (primary) | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$ | Volts | | 3 | Total EMF (secondary) | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$ | Volts | | 4 | Turns ratio | $a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Transformation ratio (K) | $K = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Voltage regulation | $VR\% = \dfrac{V_{2,NL} - V_{2,FL}}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_c} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 8 | Iron loss | $P_i = P_h + P_e = k_h f B_m^{1.6} + k_e f^2 B_m^2$ | Watts | | 9 | Copper loss | $P_c = I_1^2 R_{1e} + I_2^2 R_{2e} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | Watts | | 10 | Condition for max efficiency | $P_i = P_c$ (variable loss = constant loss) | — | | 11 | kVA at max η | $x \, \text{kVA} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{c,FL}}}$ | kVA | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In automation, the EMF equation is the **sizing tool** — it dictates how many turns a drive's gate-drive pulse transformer needs, or the core cross-section $A_i = \Phi_m / B_m$ for a CT used in motor current feedback. ## 2.5 Equivalent Circuit & Phasor Summary The exact equivalent circuit (referred to primary) contains: - $R_1, X_1$: primary resistance & leakage reactance - $R_2', X_2'$: secondary resistance & leakage reactance (referred to primary) - $R_0, X_0$: magnetizing branch (no-load branch, in parallel) - Load: $R_L', X_L'$ in parallel (or impedance $Z_L' = V_1 / I_2'$) **Approximate equivalent circuit** (used in KTU problems for simplicity): drop the shunt branch while computing voltage regulation, and approximate $R_{01} = R_1 + R_2'$, $X_{01} = X_1 + X_2'$. ## 2.6 Instrument Transformers — Automation's Best Friend > [!IMPORTANT] > **Current Transformer (CT):** > A CT is a *step-down* transformer with the *primary* in **series** with the line carrying the current to be measured (e.g., the AC motor feeder), and the *secondary* connected to the **ammeter / protection relay / PLC analog input card**. > - Primary turns $N_1$ are very few (often just 1). > - Secondary turns $N_2$ are many. > - Secondary is **always kept short-circuited** (never open-circuited) — opening it causes dangerously high voltages and core saturation. > - Standard secondary current: **5 A** (or **1 A** for metering class). **Potential Transformer (PT):** > A PT is essentially a *step-down* transformer (e.g., 11 kV / 110 V) used to scale down voltage for voltmeters, relays, and PLC analog inputs. Standard secondary: **110 V** or **110/√3 V**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation ### Given: - $N$ = number of turns - $\Phi(t)$ = instantaneous core flux - $f$ = supply frequency (Hz) - Flux is sinusoidal: $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $\omega = 2\pi f$ ### Required to prove: $$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$$ ### Step 1 — Instantaneous EMF per turn (Faraday's Law) By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the EMF induced in a single turn is the negative rate of change of flux linkage. $$e_{turn} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ ### Step 2 — Substituting sinusoidal flux Substitute $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ into the equation. $$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \Phi_m \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$ Therefore: $$e_{turn} = -\Phi_m \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$ ### Step 3 — EMF across N turns For $N$ turns connected in series (the entire coil), total EMF is: $$e = -N \, \Phi_m \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$ The negative sign simply indicates the opposing direction per Lenz's Law. For magnitude analysis, we write: $$e = N \, \Phi_m \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$ ### Step 4 — Peak (maximum) value of EMF The peak occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$. Hence: $$E_m = N \, \Phi_m \, \omega = N \, \Phi_m \, (2\pi f)$$ ### Step 5 — RMS value of EMF The RMS value is the peak divided by $\sqrt{2}$: $$E_{rms} = \dfrac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$ ### Step 6 — Simplify using $\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$ Compute the numerical coefficient: $$\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416}{1.4142} = \dfrac{6.2832}{1.4142} \approx 4.443$$ Therefore, the **final expression** is: $$\boxed{E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m}$$ This is the **KTU textbook EMF equation** of a transformer, applicable to both primary and secondary sides individually. ## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Regulation (Approximate Formula) For an **inductive load** with power factor $\cos\phi_2$ lagging, the approximate regulation is given by the expression below. **Derivation starting from the approximate equivalent circuit:** The full-load secondary terminal voltage (referred to primary) is: $$V_2' = V_1 - I_2'(R_{02}' \cos\phi_2 + X_{02}' \sin\phi_2)$$ Therefore, the regulation referred to primary is: $$VR = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2'}{V_2'} = \dfrac{I_2(R_{02} \cos\phi_2 + X_{02} \sin\phi_2)}{V_2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **The four canonical PF cases:** > - **Unity PF** $(\cos\phi = 1, \sin\phi = 0)$: $\; VR = \dfrac{I (R \cos\phi)}{V} \times 100$ > - **Lagging PF** $(\cos\phi > 0, \sin\phi > 0)$: regulation **increases** with lag. > - **Leading PF** $(\cos\phi > 0, \sin\phi < 0)$: regulation can become **negative**. > - **Zero PF** (purely inductive): $\; VR = \dfrac{I \, X_{02}}{V_2} \times 100$ — the largest regulation for a given current. ## 3.3 Derivation of Condition for Maximum Efficiency Total losses $P_L = P_i + P_c$ where $P_c = I^2 R_{eq}$, and $I = x \cdot I_{FL}$, with $x$ being the fractional loading. So: $$P_L = P_i + x^2 P_{c,FL}$$ Output power is: $$P_{out} = x \cdot S_{FL} \cos\phi$$ Efficiency is: $$\eta = \dfrac{x \, S_{FL} \cos\phi}{x \, S_{FL} \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{c,FL}}$$ To maximize $\eta$, differentiate with respect to $x$ and set to zero. $$\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_i = x^2 P_{c,FL}$$ Therefore, **maximum efficiency occurs when the iron loss equals the copper loss at the operating load**: $$\boxed{P_i = x^2 \, P_{c,FL}}$$ Loading fraction at max efficiency: $$x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{c,FL}}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU-style numerical (full solution):** > > A **5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz**, single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the LV side. Compute: > (a) Turns on HV side > (b) Maximum core flux $\Phi_m$ > (c) Full-load current on each side > > **Solution:** > > **(a)** Turns on HV side: > > $$\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = 80 \cdot \dfrac{400}{200} = 160 \text{ turns}$$ > > **(b)** Maximum core flux — apply the EMF equation on the LV side (V₁ = 200 V): > > $$\Phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{200}{4.44 \times 50 \times 80} = \dfrac{200}{17760} \approx 0.01126 \text{ Wb} = 11.26 \text{ mWb}$$ > > **(c)** Full-load currents: > > $$I_{FL,LV} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{5000}{200} = 25 \text{ A}$$ > > $$I_{FL,HV} = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \text{ A}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Power-Flow Through a Transformer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Mains Supply
V1, f Hz] --> B[Primary Winding
N1 turns] B --> C[Laminated Iron Core
Flux Phi_m] C --> D[Secondary Winding
N2 turns] D --> E[Load Z_L
Industrial Motor Drive] E --> F[Output Voltage V2
Scaled by N1 or N2] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 style D fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Primary) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Input Terminal V1] --> R1[R1 primary resistance] R1 --> X1[X1 primary leakage reactance] X1 --> N1a{Node A} N1a --> R0[R0 core loss resistance] N1a --> X0[X0 magnetizing reactance] N1a --> X2[X2 secondary leakage reactance referred] X2 --> R2[R2 secondary resistance referred] R2 --> P2[Load Terminal V2 prime] P2 --> ZL[Load ZL prime] ZL --> P1 style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style R1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style X1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style R0 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style X0 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style R2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style X2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style ZL fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Instrument Transformers in an Automation Drive ```mermaid flowchart TD GRID[3 Phase AC Mains 415 V 50 Hz] --> CB[Circuit Breaker MCCB] CB --> CT[Current Transformer CT
Ratio 100 to 5 A] CB --> PT[Potential Transformer PT
Ratio 415 V to 110 V] CT --> PLC[PLC Analog Input Card
or VFD Feedback] PT --> PLC PLC --> CTRL[Drive Controller
VFD or Soft Starter] CTRL --> MOTOR[3 Phase AC Induction Motor] MOTOR --> LOAD[Industrial Load
Pump Compressor Conveyor] style GRID fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style CB fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style CT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style PT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style PLC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 style CTRL fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 style MOTOR fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000 style LOAD fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Step-Up vs Step-Down vs Isolation ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{Need to change
voltage level?} Q -- Yes Step Up --> SU[Step Up Transformer
N2 greater than N1
V2 greater than V1] Q -- Yes Step Down --> SD[Step Down Transformer
N2 less than N1
V2 less than V1] Q -- No Same Level Needed --> ISO[Isolation Transformer
1 to 1 turns ratio
Galvanic isolation only] SU --> USE1[Long distance transmission
HV substation stepping] SD --> USE2[Drive input transformer
CT PT for metering] ISO --> USE3[PLC panel isolation
Gate drive pulse transformer
Medical equipment] style Q fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style SU fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 style SD fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 style ISO fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > > **Q1.** Define the term **voltage regulation** of a transformer. Why is it preferred to be small for power transformers? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Voltage regulation of a transformer is the change in secondary terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage: > > $$VR\% = \dfrac{V_{2,NL} - V_{2,FL}}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$$ > > For power transformers, regulation is preferred to be **small (typically < 5%)** because it ensures that the load voltage remains stable irrespective of load variations. Large regulation would cause voltage dips during peak loads, leading to poor performance of connected equipment such as induction motors and lighting loads. --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** > > **Q2.** List the major **no-load losses** and **load losses** in a transformer and state two methods to reduce each. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Loss Type | Component | Reduction Method | > |---|---|---| > | **No-load (Iron) losses** | Hysteresis loss $P_h \propto f B_m^{1.6}$ | Use CRGO silicon-steel laminations with low coercivity | > | | Eddy-current loss $P_e \propto f^2 B_m^2 t^2$ | Use thin laminations (0.23 to 0.35 mm) insulated from each other | > | **Load (Copper) losses** | $I^2 R$ in primary and secondary windings | Use large conductor cross-section (low-resistance Cu) and Litz wire for HF applications | > | | Stray load loss due to leakage flux | Use interleaved windings and proper winding placement | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > > [!WARNING] > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the EMF equation constant as **4.44** for *square-wave flux*. Drive-fed transformers use high-frequency switching, where flux is often trapezoidal or square. **For a square wave, the constant is 4.0, not 4.44.** > > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **refer all secondary quantities to the primary side** (multiply by $a^2$) before drawing the equivalent circuit or solving regulation problems. > > - **Common Mistake #3:** Computing maximum efficiency at **full-load**. Max efficiency occurs at a load fraction $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{c,FL}}$, which is **rarely at full-load** unless the transformer is specifically designed so. > > - **Common Mistake #4:** In CT problems, assuming the secondary can be left open. **Never open-circuit a CT secondary** — it develops dangerous voltages and can saturate the core. > > - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing regulation formula without specifying **lagging/leading/unity** power factor — examiner deducts 1–2 marks if PF context is missing. --- ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > > **(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer and explain the significance of each term. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A **10 kVA, 500 V / 250 V, 50 Hz**, single-phase transformer has 100 turns on the HV winding. Find: > (i) Number of turns on LV winding > (ii) Maximum core flux > (iii) Full-load currents on both sides > (iv) The kVA rating at which it operates at maximum efficiency if the iron loss is 80 W and full-load copper loss is 120 W. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A: **Part (a) — EMF Equation Derivation (7 marks):** Starting with Faraday's Law, instantaneous flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(2\pi f t)$, EMF in $N$ turns is: $$e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \Phi_m (2\pi f) \cos(2\pi f t)$$ The peak value: $$E_m = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$ The RMS value: $$E_{rms} = \dfrac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$$ **Significance of each term:** - **4.44**: combined form-factor and shape-factor for sinusoidal flux - **f**: supply frequency (Hz) — higher frequency means smaller transformer for same voltage - **N**: number of turns — determines the voltage ratio - **$\Phi_m$**: maximum flux in the core (Wb) — limits are imposed by core saturation (typically $B_m \leq 1.4$ T) **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Turns on LV winding:** Given: HV = 500 V, $N_1$ = 100 turns; LV = 250 V. $$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = 100 \times \dfrac{250}{500} = 50 \text{ turns}$$ **(ii) Maximum core flux:** Apply EMF equation on HV side ($E_1 \approx V_1 = 500$ V): $$\Phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{500}{4.44 \times 50 \times 100} = \dfrac{500}{22200} \approx 22.52 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 22.52 \text{ mWb}$$ **(iii) Full-load currents:** $$I_{1,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{10000}{500} = 20 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{2,FL} = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{10000}{250} = 40 \text{ A}$$ **(iv) kVA at max efficiency:** Loading fraction: $$x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{c,FL}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{80}{120}} = \sqrt{0.667} \approx 0.816$$ kVA at maximum efficiency: $$S_{max\eta} = x \times S_{rated} = 0.816 \times 10 = 8.16 \text{ kVA}$$ --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** > > **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Current Transformer (CT)** with a neat diagram. State **two precautions** while using a CT. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A **100 kVA, 11000 V / 400 V, 50 Hz**, 3-phase transformer has an iron loss of 900 W at rated voltage. The full-load copper loss is 1500 W. Calculate: > (i) The kVA load at which maximum efficiency occurs > (ii) Maximum efficiency at **0.8 power factor lagging** **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B: **Part (a) — Current Transformer (7 marks):** A **CT** is a *step-down* instrument transformer used to measure high AC currents safely. Its primary winding has very few turns (often just one — the line conductor itself passing through the core window), while the secondary has many turns and is connected to an ammeter or relay coil. **Working principle:** The primary current $I_1$ produces an MMF $N_1 I_1$, which sets up a small flux in the core. This flux induces an EMF in the secondary, driving current $I_2$ through the ammeter. The secondary MMF $N_2 I_2$ opposes the primary, and the small difference $N_1 I_1 - N_2 I_2$ produces just enough flux to induce the secondary EMF. Hence, $I_1 / I_2 \approx N_2 / N_1$. **Two Precautions:** 1. **The secondary must never be open-circuited** while the primary is energized. If open, the entire primary MMF is used to produce flux, leading to dangerous high voltage across the secondary terminals, severe core saturation, overheating, and possible insulation failure. 2. **The secondary should always be earthed** to prevent the meter or relay from floating at a dangerous potential in case of insulation breakdown between primary and secondary. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) kVA at maximum efficiency:** $$x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{c,FL}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{900}{1500}} = \sqrt{0.6} \approx 0.7746$$ $$S_{max\eta} = x \times S_{rated} = 0.7746 \times 100 \approx 77.46 \text{ kVA}$$ **(ii) Maximum efficiency at 0.8 PF lagging:** Output power at max-η load: $$P_{out} = S_{max\eta} \times \cos\phi = 77.46 \times 0.8 = 61.97 \text{ kW}$$ Total losses at this loading: $$P_L = P_i + x^2 P_{c,FL} = 900 + 900 = 1800 \text{ W} = 1.8 \text{ kW}$$ Maximum efficiency: $$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_L} = \dfrac{61.97}{61.97 + 1.8} = \dfrac{61.97}{63.77} \approx 0.9718 = 97.18\%$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** > > - **EMF equation:** $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$ (sinusoidal) ; $E = 4.0 f N \Phi_m$ (square wave — important for drive transformers). > - **Turns ratio = Voltage ratio = Inverse current ratio:** $a = N_1 / N_2 = V_1 / V_2 = I_2 / I_1$. > - **Ideal transformer:** $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ (no losses). Real transformer has iron + copper losses. > - **Iron losses** = Hysteresis loss + Eddy-current loss. Both are **constant** (independent of load) at fixed voltage and frequency. > - **Copper losses** are **variable** (proportional to $I^2$). At full load, $P_c = I_{FL}^2 R_{eq}$. > - **Max efficiency** occurs when $P_i = P_c$ at that loading. This is **not at full load** unless the transformer is *specifically designed* to peak at rated load. > - **Voltage regulation** depends on load power factor: > - Unity PF → only resistance drop matters. > - Lagging PF → worst regulation. > - Leading PF → can be negative. > - **CT:** Secondary must be **short-circuited and earthed**. Standard secondary: **5 A or 1 A**. > - **PT:** Step-down for voltage measurement. Standard secondary: **110 V or 110/√3 V**. Acts like a normal transformer but with very high accuracy class (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 3.0). > - **Isolation transformer (1:1):** No voltage change; only galvanic isolation. Critical for breaking ground loops in PLC/DCS systems and for gate-drive pulse transformers in VFDs. > - **Approximate equivalent circuit:** Used when solving regulation and efficiency for small ratings. Drop the magnetizing branch ($R_0, X_0$) when calculating voltage drops. > - **Always** refer secondary quantities to primary side before calculation: > - $R_2' = a^2 R_2$ and $X_2' = a^2 X_2$ and $V_2' = a V_2$ and $I_2' = I_2 / a$. > - **Drive-relevance:** Pulse transformers provide galvanic isolation between low-voltage control circuits and the high-voltage power devices (IGBT/MOSFET gates). CT/PT feedback is essential for closed-loop control of VFDs and soft starters. > - **Always specify the operating power factor** in any regulation/efficiency problem. Examiner awards full marks only when PF context is clearly stated.

topics.title: Principles of operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Principle of Electric Drives

An **electric drive** is a sophisticated electromechanical system designed to convert electrical energy into mechanical energy (or vice versa) with precise control over speed, torque, and position. The "Principles of Operation" studied in this module form the bedrock upon which all automation—ranging from CNC machines to robotic arms—is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The *principle of operation* of an electric machine is the physical mechanism by which electrical energy is converted to mechanical energy (motor action) or mechanical to electrical (generator action). This conversion is governed fundamentally by three electromagnetic laws: **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, **Lorentz Force Law**, and **Lenz's Law**.

### 1.2 The Three Universal Laws Governing All Electric Machines

| Law | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Faraday's Law** | $e = -\dfrac{d\Psi}{dt}$ | A changing magnetic flux linkage induces an EMF | Pushing water through a pipe—the faster you push, the more pressure (voltage) builds up |
| **Lorentz Force** | $\vec{F} = I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$ | A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force | Two magnets pushing each other—the strength depends on how strong the magnets are and how much current flows |
| **Lenz's Law** | Induced EMF opposes the cause | Conservation of energy in electromagnetic systems | A boat's oar pushing backward pushes the boat forward—the reaction is always opposite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Dynamo:**
> Imagine a bicycle dynamo (a small generator). When the wheel spins, a magnet inside rotates near a coil of wire. The *changing magnetic field* (Faraday's law) generates a voltage that lights your bulb. Now reverse the concept: if you *feed* current into that coil placed in a magnetic field, the *Lorentz force* makes the magnet (and hence the wheel) rotate. This reversible energy conversion is the **principle of operation** of every electric machine.

### 1.3 Classification of Electric Machines Covered in Module 2

For automation, the KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes machines into three families:

1. **DC Machines** — DC shunt, series, separately excited, and compound motors.
2. **AC Machines** — Three-phase induction motors (squirrel cage and slip ring) and synchronous motors.
3. **Special Machines** — Stepper motors, servo motors, and BLDC (Brushless DC) motors — the workhorses of modern automation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in automation:** Industrial robots, conveyor belts, CNC spindles, and AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) all rely on these machines. Understanding the *principle of operation* is what enables an engineer to select, size, and control the right motor for the right application.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation of a DC Motor

### 2.1.1 The Underlying Physics

When a current-carrying armature conductor is placed in the magnetic field produced by the field winding, it experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$. The collective force on all armature conductors produces a **torque** that rotates the armature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (Motor Rule):**
> Stretch the thumb, forefinger, and middle finger of your left hand mutually perpendicular. The **forefinger** points to the **Field (B)**, the **middle finger** points to the **Current (I)**, and the **thumb** gives the direction of **Motion (Force F)**. This rule is the foundation of all DC motor analysis.

### 2.1.2 The Back-EMF Phenomenon

As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, the conductors *also* cut the field flux. By **Faraday's law**, this induces a voltage in the armature winding called the **Back-EMF** (Counter-EMF). The Back-EMF always **opposes** the applied voltage (Lenz's law), thereby limiting the armature current and providing inherent self-regulation.

### 2.1.3 Armature Reaction

The current flowing in the armature conductors produces its own magnetic field (armature flux), which distorts the main field flux. This phenomenon, called **armature reaction**, weakens the main field and shifts the neutral plane. It is mitigated using **interpoles** and **compensating windings**.

## 2.2 Principle of Operation of a Three-Phase Induction Motor

### 2.2.1 The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — The Heart of AC Machines

When a balanced **three-phase supply** is given to a three-phase winding spatially displaced by **120°**, a **rotating magnetic field** of constant magnitude is produced in the air gap. This RMF rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s$.

### 2.2.2 How the Rotor "Follows" the RMF

The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's law) and hence a current in the short-circuited rotor bars. This induced current, in the presence of the RMF, experiences a Lorentz force that drags the rotor in the direction of the RMF. **The rotor can never reach synchronous speed** — if it did, there would be no relative motion, no induced EMF, and no torque.

### 2.2.3 The Concept of Slip

Slip $s$ is the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed. It is the most critical parameter in induction motor analysis.

## 2.3 Principle of Operation of a Synchronous Motor

A three-phase stator winding produces an RMF as in the induction motor. The rotor carries a **DC-excited field winding** (or permanent magnets). The rotor locks with the RMF and rotates at **exactly synchronous speed**. There is **no slip** in steady state — the rotor and RMF rotate as a single unit.

## 2.4 Principle of Operation of Special Machines

### 2.4.1 Stepper Motor
A **brushless DC motor** that rotates in **discrete angular steps** in response to electrical pulses. Position is precisely determined by the number of pulses, making it ideal for **open-loop position control** in 3D printers, CNC machines, and robotics.

### 2.4.2 Servo Motor
A high-performance **closed-loop** motor (typically BLDC or DC) with integrated feedback (encoder/resolver). It provides precise control of position, velocity, and acceleration — the heart of industrial robotics.

### 2.4.3 BLDC Motor
Electrically commutated: the rotor has **permanent magnets**, and the stator has **three-phase windings**. Electronic commutation (via inverter + position sensors) replaces mechanical brushes. High efficiency, long life, and excellent dynamic response.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** A common exam question asks: *"Why does a DC motor not burn at starting despite the absence of back-EMF?"* The correct answer is: starting resistors or soft-start electronic controllers (choppers/VFDs) limit the inrush current. This is a direct application of Kirchhoff's voltage law applied to the armature circuit.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Master Formula Table — Memorize This for the Exam!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | EMF Equation (DC Machine) | $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 \, A}$ | Volts (V) | $\Phi$: flux per pole, $Z$: conductors, $N$: speed (rpm), $P$: poles, $A$: parallel paths |
| 2 | Torque (DC Machine) | $T = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ | N·m | Developed electromagnetic torque |
| 3 | Armature Voltage Eqn. | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | Steady-state, neglecting brush drop |
| 4 | Back-EMF (DC) | $E_b = K_b \, \Phi \, \omega$ | V | $K_b = \dfrac{P Z}{2\pi A}$ |
| 5 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$: supply frequency (Hz) |
| 6 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $0 < s < 1$ for motoring |
| 7 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \, f$ | Hz | Frequency of induced rotor current |
| 8 | Rotor EMF (per phase) | $E_2 = s \, E_2^{\text{stand}}$ | V | Proportional to slip |
| 9 | Rotor Resistance (ref.) | $R_2' = R_2 + \dfrac{R_{2,\text{ext}}}{s}$ | $\Omega$ | Referred to stator |
| 10 | Torque (3-ph Induction) | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_2^2 R_2 / s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | N·m | Per-phase equivalent circuit based |
| 11 | Starting Torque Ratio | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{s_m / s_{st} + s_{st} / s_m}$ | — | $s_m$ = slip at max torque |
| 12 | Pull-out Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | N·m | Independent of rotor resistance |
| 13 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N \, m}$ | degrees | $N$: stator teeth, $m$: phases |
| 14 | Servo Bandwidth | $f_{BW} = \dfrac{\omega_{BW}}{2\pi}$ | Hz | Determines dynamic response |
| 15 | Power Developed | $P_{dev} = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical power developed |
| 16 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Includes all losses |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake Alert:** Students often confuse **electromagnetic torque** (developed) with **shaft torque** (delivered to load). They differ by the rotational losses. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Equation of a DC Machine (Board-Exam Standard)

### Statement
Derive the EMF equation for a DC machine: $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$

### Given Data
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = speed of armature (rpm)
- $P$ = number of poles
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert rotational speed from rpm to revolutions per second.

$$\text{Speed in rps} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the flux cut by one conductor in one revolution.

In one revolution, a conductor cuts all the flux emanating from the $P$ poles. The flux cut per revolution is $P \Phi$ webers.

**Step 3:** Calculate the average EMF induced in one conductor.

By Faraday's law, average EMF per conductor:

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{d\Psi}{dt} = \dfrac{\text{Flux cut per revolution}}{\text{Time for one revolution}}$$

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P \Phi}{1 / (N/60)} = \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

**Step 4:** Account for total conductors connected in series per parallel path.

Total number of conductors = $Z$. These are divided into $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in **series per path** = $Z/A$.

**Step 5:** Total EMF across the brushes (sum of all parallel paths).

Since all parallel paths have the same EMF, the terminal EMF is the EMF of one path:

$$\boxed{E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \text{ volts}}$$

### Physical Interpretation

- $E_b \propto \Phi$ — More flux (stronger field) → higher EMF.
- $E_b \propto N$ — Faster rotation → higher EMF (the dynamo principle).
- $E_b \propto Z P / A$ — More conductors and poles increase EMF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A 4-pole DC shunt motor has 600 armature conductors, flux per pole of 0.05 Wb, and runs at 1000 rpm. The armature is wave-wound. Find the back-EMF.
>
> Solution: For wave winding, $A = 2$.
> $$E_b = \dfrac{0.05 \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{120000}{120} = 1000 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Synchronous Speed and Slip Relation

### Given
- Supply frequency $f$ (Hz)
- Number of poles $P$

### Derivation

**Step 1:** The stator winding creates a rotating magnetic field. The poles of this RMF alternate at the same frequency as the supply.

**Step 2:** In one full cycle of the supply (period $T = 1/f$), the RMF moves across one pair of poles (one North and one South).

**Step 3:** In one second, the total RMF "pole-pairs traversed" = $f$.

**Step 4:** Number of revolutions per second = $f / (\text{number of pole pairs}) = f / (P/2)$.

**Step 5:** Convert to rpm by multiplying by 60:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 6:** Define slip — the relative speed between RMF and rotor, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Step 7:** Rearranging for actual rotor speed:

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why must slip be positive for motoring?**
> If $N_r < N_s$, the RMF moves faster than the rotor → relative motion exists → EMF is induced → current flows → torque is developed. If $N_r = N_s$ (slip = 0), no relative motion → no EMF → no torque → rotor slows down. The system is **self-balancing**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque Equation of a DC Motor from First Principles

### Starting Point: Mechanical Power Developed

$$P_{\text{dev}} = T \cdot \omega_m$$

where $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

**Step 1:** Electrical power converted to mechanical = $E_b \cdot I_a$ (this is the air-gap power).

**Step 2:** Equate the two (energy conservation):

$$T \cdot \omega_m = E_b \cdot I_a$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ and $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \cdot I_a$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $N$ and $60$ from both sides:

$$T \cdot 2\pi = \dfrac{\Phi Z P \, I_a}{A}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $T$:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A} = K_a \, \Phi \, I_a}$$

where $K_a = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ is the **armature constant**.

### Key Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque is directly proportional to flux and armature current.** This is the single most important equation in DC motor control — it tells us that to control torque, we can either vary $\Phi$ (field control) or $I_a$ (armature control). This duality forms the basis of all DC drive schemes (e.g., Ward-Leonard, chopper drives, and dual-converter drives covered later in the course).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Operating Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **The "At a Glance" Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU Exams**

| Feature | DC Motor | Induction Motor | Synchronous Motor | Stepper Motor | BLDC Motor |
|---------|----------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Lorentz force on armature | Induced current in rotor from RMF | Magnetic locking with RMF | Discrete-step electromagnetic alignment | Electronically commutated Lorentz force |
| **Rotor Type** | Armature winding (commutated) | Squirrel cage / wound | DC field or PM | PM or variable reluctance | Permanent magnet |
| **Speed** | Variable, depends on $V$ and $\Phi$ | $N < N_s$ (sub-synchronous) | $N = N_s$ (synchronous) | Stepped / synchronized to pulses | Variable via electronic commutation |
| **Self-Starting?** | Yes (with field energized) | Yes (cage) / No (wound rotor, needs aux) | No (needs auxiliary drive) | Yes (in steps) | Yes (with electronic commutation) |
| **Slip** | N/A | Yes ($0 < s < 1$) | Zero | N/A | N/A |
| **Commutation** | Mechanical (brushes) | Not required | Not required | Electronic | Electronic |
| **Efficiency** | Medium (75-85%) | High (85-92%) | Very High (90-95%) | Medium | Very High (85-95%) |
| **Control Complexity** | Simple | Moderate (V/f control) | Moderate (separate excitation) | Simple (open-loop) | High (sensor-based) |
| **Typical Automation Use** | Small drives, legacy systems | Pumps, conveyors, fans | High-power compressors | 3D printers, CNC feed drives | Robotics, drones, EV traction |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Stepper Motor Step Angle (for Variable Reluctance Type)

### Given
- Number of stator teeth = $N_s$
- Number of rotor teeth = $N_r$
- Number of phases = $m$

### Step Angle Derivation

**Step 1:** For the rotor to move from one stable position to the next, the rotor must rotate by the angular distance between two consecutive rotor teeth as seen from the stator.

**Step 2:** A full revolution of 360° corresponds to $N_r$ rotor teeth pitch. So one rotor tooth pitch (mechanical) is:

$$\theta_{\text{tooth}} = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 3:** In one full step (one phase excitation change), the rotor moves by:

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N \, m}$$

where $N$ is the effective number of rotor/stator teeth pairs (lesser of $N_s$ and $N_r$).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Numerical:** A 4-phase stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth. Find the step angle.
> $$\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{50 \times 4} = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8°/step$$
> This is the standard step angle of widely used NEMA 17 stepper motors in 3D printers and small CNC machines.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Three-Phase Induction Motor Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Three-Phase AC Supply] --> Q[Stator winding creates RMF rotating at Ns]
    Q --> R[RMF cuts stationary rotor conductors]
    R --> S[Faraday's law: EMF induced in rotor bars]
    S --> T[EMF drives short-circuit current in rotor]
    T --> U[Current in magnetic field produces Lorentz force]
    U --> V[Force creates torque on rotor]
    V --> W{Rotor speed N}
    W -->|N less than Ns| X[Slip exists, induction continues]
    W -->|N equals Ns| Y[No slip, no EMF, rotor decelerates]
    X --> Z[Rotor accelerates toward Ns but cannot reach]
```
