**Statement:** A single-phase alternating flux $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$, which is purely pulsating along a stationary axis, can be mathematically resolved into **two equal constant-amplitude fluxes**, each of magnitude $\Phi_m/2$, rotating in **opposite directions** at synchronous angular velocity $\omega$.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
\phi(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t} + \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t} = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Significance:**
- Each rotating field induces its own rotor current and produces its own torque.
- Forward field → motoring torque $T_f$.
- Backward field → braking torque $T_b$.
- At $s = 1$ (start): $T_f = T_b$ → net torque = 0 → not self-starting.
- The motor runs in whichever direction it is initially pushed. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Double Revolving Field Theory** as applied to single-phase induction motors. Show that a pulsating field can be resolved into two counter-rotating fields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase induction motor has the following parameters (referred to stator): $R_1 = 2 \,\Omega$, $X_1 = 3 \,\Omega$, $R_2 = 4 \,\Omega$, $X_2 = 3 \,\Omega$, $X_m = 50 \,\Omega$. For a slip of $s = 0.05$, calculate the **forward** and **backward** field rotor branch impedances and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Double Revolving Field Theory (7 marks)**

**Diagram (2 marks):**
[A Mermaid block or schematic showing the pulsating field phasor splitting into two equal counter-rotating phasors, similar to Section 4.2 above.]

**Mathematical Derivation (3 marks):**

Let the air-gap flux be:

$$
\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)
$$

Using Euler's identity:

$$
\phi(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\left(e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right)
$$

Define two rotating phasors:

- Forward: $\bar{\phi}_f = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t}$ — rotates CCW at $\omega$ rad/s.
- Backward: $\bar{\phi}_b = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$ — rotates CW at $\omega$ rad/s.

The sum of these two counter-rotating phasors produces a **pulsating field** of amplitude $\Phi_m$ along the stator axis.

**Physical Interpretation (2 marks):**
- Forward field induces rotor current at slip $s_f = s$, producing motoring torque $T_f$.
- Backward field induces rotor current at slip $s_b = 2 - s$, producing braking torque $T_b$.
- Net torque $T = T_f - T_b = 0$ at $s = 1$ → no self-starting.

**Conclusion:** A single-phase pulsating field is mathematically equivalent to two equal counter-rotating fields, and this equivalence forms the foundation for analyzing single-phase induction motor performance.

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $R_1 = 2 \,\Omega$, $X_1 = 3 \,\Omega$, $R_2 = 4 \,\Omega$, $X_2 = 3 \,\Omega$, $X_m = 50 \,\Omega$
- Slip $s = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Forward field slip**

$$
s_f = s = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Forward field rotor branch impedance**

$$
Z_{2f}' = \frac{R_2}{s_f} + jX_2 = \frac{4}{0.05} + j3 = 80 + j3 \,\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert Z_{2f}' \vert = \sqrt{80^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 9} = \sqrt{6409} \approx 80.06 \,\Omega}
$$

**Step 3 — Backward field slip**

$$
s_b = 2 - s = 2 - 0.05 = 1.95
$$

**Step 4 — Backward field rotor branch impedance**

$$
Z_{2b}' = \frac{R_2}{s_b} + jX_2 = \frac{4}{1.95} + j3 = 2.051 + j3 \,\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert Z_{2b}' \vert = \sqrt{(2.051)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{4.207 + 9} = \sqrt{13.207} \approx 3.63 \,\Omega}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (2 marks):**

- The **forward field branch impedance is very large (80 $\Omega$)** because $R_2/s_f = 80 \,\Omega$ dominates — this represents near-synchronous operation with low rotor losses.
- The **backward field branch impedance is small (3.63 $\Omega$)** because $R_2/s_b$ is small (rotor sees the backward field as nearly twice-synchronous, but with reduced effective resistance).
- The forward field produces a strong motoring torque, while the backward field produces a small (but non-zero) braking torque.
- At normal running slip ($s \approx 0.05$), the net torque is positive and the motor continues to rotate in the forward direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Numerical Problems:** Students often confuse the formula for $s_b$. It is **not** $\dfrac{1+s}{1}$ or similar — it is precisely $s_b = 2 - s$. Forgetting this leads to incorrect backward impedance and a wrong answer. Always verify with $s = 1$ giving $s_b = 1$ (symmetry at start).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **torque-slip characteristics** of a single-phase induction motor (with and without starting winding energized) with a three-phase induction motor. Explain why a single-phase IM has zero starting torque without auxiliary means. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Capacitor-Start Motor (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A capacitor-start single-phase induction motor has the following components:

1. **Stator:** Laminated core with two windings:
   - **Main (running) winding** — heavy gauge, low resistance, placed in the bottom of stator slots.
   - **Auxiliary (starting) winding** — thin gauge, higher resistance, displaced spatially by 90° electrical from the main winding.
2. **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage rotor (same as three-phase).
3. **Capacitor:** A high-value AC electrolytic capacitor (typically 80–300 µF for FHP motors) connected in **series with the auxiliary winding**.
4. **Centrifugal switch:** A mechanical (or solid-state) switch mounted on the rotor shaft. At rest, it is **closed**, connecting the auxiliary winding to the supply. When rotor speed reaches ~70–80% of synchronous speed, centrifugal force **opens** the switch, **disconnecting** the auxiliary winding and capacitor from the circuit.

**Working (3 marks):**

- At start, both windings are energized. The main winding current $I_m$ lags the supply voltage by some angle $\theta_m$. The capacitor in the auxiliary branch causes $I_a$ to **lead** the supply voltage by a different angle $\theta_a$.
- The **phase displacement between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is large (≈ 80°–90°)**, producing a **near-circular rotating magnetic field** at start.
- The motor develops **high starting torque** (typically 3–4.5 times full-load torque), sufficient for heavy starting loads like compressors and pumps.
- Once the rotor reaches ~75% of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch opens, the auxiliary circuit is disconnected, and the motor continues to run on the main winding alone (single-phase operation).
- The capacitor and starting winding are designed for **intermittent duty** (a few seconds only) — not for continuous running.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch (1 mark):**
The centrifugal switch protects the capacitor and auxiliary winding from overheating during continuous operation by disconnecting them once the motor has accelerated to running speed.

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Torque-Slip Comparison (7 marks)**

**Three-Phase IM Torque-Slip Curve (2 marks):**

- A three-phase induction motor produces a **rotating magnetic field** directly from the balanced three-phase supply.
- Starting torque is **high** (typically 1.5–2× full-load torque) without any auxiliary winding.
- The torque-slip curve is smooth: zero at $s = 0$ (synchronous), maximum at $s = R_2/X_2$, decreases monotonically on either side.

**Single-Phase IM Torque-Slip Curve (3 marks):**

- **With auxiliary winding energized (during start):** A weak rotating field exists, producing a **non-zero starting torque**. The curve is similar in shape to a 3-phase curve but with reduced magnitude. This is the *transient* operating region.
- **Without auxiliary winding (steady-state running):** The motor operates on a single pulsating field. Per DRFT, $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$, and the net torque is **zero at $s = 0$ AND at $s = 1$**, with a **peak** somewhere in between. The curve is roughly bell-shaped between $s = 0$ and $s = 1$.
- The **stable operating slip is small** ($s \approx 0.03$–$0.08$), and the motor cannot reach true synchronous speed.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | 3-Phase IM | Single-Phase IM (no start) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting torque | High (1.5–2× FL) | Zero |
| Self-starting | Yes | No |
| Auxiliary winding | Not required | Required (or shaded pole) |
| Torque at $s = 0$ | Zero | Zero |
| Torque at $s = 1$ | High | Zero |
| Stable region | $s = 0$ to $s_{max}$ | $s = 0$ to small $s$ only |

**Why Zero Starting Torque Without Auxiliary (1 mark):**
At standstill ($s = 1$), the forward and backward rotating fields have equal magnitudes ($s_f = s_b = 1$) and produce equal and opposite torques. Thus, $T_{net} = 0$. The motor needs an external push or auxiliary starting mechanism to develop a net torque in a preferred direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — B(7-marker):** Students often draw the torque-slip curve **without clearly labeling the three regions** — forward field torque, backward field torque, and net torque. Always draw **all three curves on the same graph** and label them. Also, mention that the *backward* field torque is plotted as a **negative quantity** on the slip axis. A neat, well-labeled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Single-Phase Induction Motor: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **single-phase induction motor** has a single stator winding that produces a **pulsating (not rotating) magnetic field**.

- ✅ It is **NOT self-starting** on a single-phase supply — the Double Revolving Field Theory explains this mathematically.

- ✅ **Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT):** A pulsating field $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ is equivalent to **two equal counter-rotating fields** of magnitude $\Phi_m/2$ each, rotating at synchronous speed $\omega$.

- ✅ **Slip values:** $s_f = s$ (forward) and $s_b = 2 - s$ (backward). At $s = 1$: $s_f = s_b = 1$ → equal and opposite torques → no start.

- ✅ **Net torque:** $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$ where $T_f$ and $T_b$ follow the standard induction motor torque formula with $s$ and $(2-s)$ respectively.

- ✅ **Cross-Field Theory (Lamme):** Alternative explanation — once rotor is spinning, induced rotor current interacts with cross-axis stator flux to sustain rotation. Useful for understanding running condition, not starting.

- ✅ **Starting methods** (memorize the table in §2.4):
   - **Split-phase (resistance start)** → small phase shift → low starting torque
   - **Capacitor-start** → large phase shift (≈ 90°) → high starting torque
   - **Capacitor-run (PSC)** → permanent capacitor → smooth running
   - **Capacitor-start capacitor-run** → best of both worlds
   - **Shaded-pole** → simplest construction → very low starting torque, used in small fans

- ✅ **Centrifugal switch** is essential in capacitor-start motors to disconnect the auxiliary winding at ~75% of synchronous speed.

- ✅ **Equivalent circuit (per-phase):** Two parallel rotor branches — $R_2/s + jX_2$ (forward) and $R_2/(2-s) + jX_2$ (backward), both in parallel with the magnetizing branch $jX_m$.

- ✅ **Applications:** Fractional-horsepower (FHP) motors — ceiling fans, table fans, washing machines, refrigerators, blowers, small pumps, mixers, hair dryers.

- ✅ **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. For $f = 50$ Hz and $P = 4$, $N_s = 1500$ rpm.

- ✅ **Key phrase to remember:** *"Pulsating field = two counter-rotating fields of half the magnitude each."* — this is the **single most important line** for any KTU question on this topic.

- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Students often write $s_b = (1+s)$ — the **correct** relation is $s_b = (2 - s)$.

- ✅ **Numerical problem format:** Always (i) find $s_f$ and $s_b$, (ii) compute forward and backward rotor branch impedances, (iii) comment on relative magnitudes, (iv) state net torque direction and stability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Split phase motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Split phase motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SPLIT PHASE MOTOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Split-Phase Induction Motor (SPIM)** is a single-phase AC induction motor that employs two stator windings—a **Main Winding (Running Winding)** and an **Auxiliary Winding (Starting Winding)**—displaced spatially by 90 electrical degrees, to produce a rotating magnetic field during the starting period. A **centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding from the supply once the rotor reaches approximately **75% to 80%** of synchronous speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Split-phase motors belong to the family of *single-phase induction motors with auxiliary winding starters*. They are the most common fractional-horsepower (FHP) motors used in domestic and light-industrial automation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy, stationary merry-go-round by yourself. No matter how hard you push from one side, it will not rotate smoothly—it just rocks back and forth. Now, suppose a friend stands on the opposite side and pushes simultaneously, but with a slight time delay (like a quarter of a second later). The combination of two staggered pushes from different positions will cause the merry-go-round to **start spinning smoothly in one direction**. The split-phase motor works on exactly this principle: the **main winding** and the **auxiliary winding** push the rotor's magnetic field from two different locations, with a phase difference, creating a rotating magnetic field that "drags" the rotor into motion.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Supply Frequency (f):** Typically **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (US standard).
- **Synchronous Speed (Ns):** Calculated as $N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ where $P$ is the number of poles.
- **Standard Power Range:** **1/20 HP to 1/2 HP** (approximately **40 W to 375 W**).
- **Standard Voltage Ratings:** **230 V AC** (single-phase, India).
- **Phase Displacement Between Windings:** Ideally **90°**, but practically **25° to 30°** in resistance-split designs.

> [!NOTE]
> The split-phase motor is a **non-condenser** single-phase induction motor; it does **not** use a capacitor for phase splitting. This is the key distinction from capacitor-start and capacitor-run motors (covered in subsequent topics).

### 1.4 Classification Context

Within KTU Module 3 (Single-Phase Induction Motors), the Split-Phase Motor is the **simplest and most economical** member of the auxiliary-winding family. It is the baseline from which more sophisticated motors (Capacitor-Start, Capacitor-Run, Shaded-Pole) are derived.

![Figure: Classification Context Placeholder]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details

A split-phase motor consists of the following major components:

**A. Stator:**
- Laminated silicon-steel core with semi-enclosed slots.
- Carries **two windings** embedded in the slots:
  - **Main (Running) Winding (M):** Made of thick, low-resistance copper wire; placed deep in the slots; has **low resistance and high inductive reactance** ($X_M$ is high).
  - **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding (A):** Made of thin, high-resistance wire (or fewer turns of thicker wire in some designs); placed on top of the slots; has **high resistance and low inductive reactance** ($X_A$ is low).

**B. Rotor:**
- **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to a three-phase induction motor rotor.
- Constructed of laminated steel with copper or aluminum bars short-circuited by end rings.
- No brushes, no slip rings — hence highly robust and maintenance-free.

**C. Centrifugal Switch:**
- Mounted on the rotor shaft (or sometimes on a stationary part with mechanical linkage).
- **Normally Closed (NC)** type — connects the auxiliary winding to the supply at standstill.
- **Opens at ~75–80% of synchronous speed**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding to prevent insulation overheating and to reduce losses.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **At Standstill:** Both windings are connected in parallel across the single-phase AC supply. The main winding current $I_M$ lags the supply voltage by a large angle (since $X_M \gg R_M$). The auxiliary winding current $I_A$ lags the voltage by a smaller angle (since $R_A$ is purposely made high relative to $X_A$).

2. **Phase Splitting:** The two currents are displaced in time phase by approximately **25° to 30°** (ideally 90°). This time-phase difference, combined with the **90° spatial displacement** of the windings, produces a **weak rotating magnetic field**.

3. **Rotor Acceleration:** This rotating field cuts the rotor bars, inducing EMF and current. By Lenz's Law, the rotor follows the rotating field, accelerating towards synchronous speed.

4. **Cut-off by Centrifugal Switch:** Once rotor speed exceeds **~75–80%** of $N_s$, the centrifugal switch opens. The auxiliary winding is disconnected.

5. **Single-Winding Operation:** The motor continues to run on the main winding alone. The pulsating single-phase field is resolved into two counter-rotating fields, and the forward field component maintains rotation (cross-field theory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the centrifugal switch mandatory?** The auxiliary winding is designed for short-duration duty only (typically 3 seconds). Continuous operation would cause the winding to overheat and burn out due to its high $I^2R$ losses.

### 2.3 Why Two Windings Instead of One?

A single-phase winding produces only a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field**, not a rotating one. By Fourier analysis, this pulsating field equals two equal counter-rotating fields of half amplitude. At standstill, these produce **equal and opposite torques**, resulting in **zero net starting torque**. Splitting the phase by introducing a second winding breaks this symmetry and produces a net starting torque.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Speed of rotating magnetic field | RPM |
| Slip | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Fractional difference between synchronous and rotor speeds | dimensionless |
| Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | Actual mechanical speed | RPM |
| Main Winding Impedance | $Z_M = \sqrt{R_M^2 + X_M^2}$ | Total opposition in main branch | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| Aux Winding Impedance | $Z_A = \sqrt{R_A^2 + X_A^2}$ | Total opposition in auxiliary branch | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| Main Winding Current Angle | $\theta_M = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_M}{R_M}\right)$ | Phase lag of $I_M$ behind $V$ | degrees ($^\circ$) |
| Aux Winding Current Angle | $\theta_A = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_A}{R_A}\right)$ | Phase lag of $I_A$ behind $V$ | degrees ($^\circ$) |
| Phase Displacement | $\alpha = \theta_M - \theta_A$ | Time-phase difference between $I_M$ and $I_A$ | degrees ($^\circ$) |
| Starting Torque | $T_{st} \propto I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Proportional to current product and sine of phase angle | N·m |
| Full-Load Torque | $T_{fl} = \frac{P_{out} \times 60}{2 \pi N_r}$ | Mechanical output torque | N·m |
| Power Input | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Single-phase apparent power × power factor | Watts (W) |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Output-to-input power ratio | percent (%) |
| Torque Ratio (Starting) | $\frac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ | Typical range for split-phase motors | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The starting torque formula $T_{st} \propto I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ is **THE most tested equation** in this module. Maximize it by maximizing $I_M$, $I_A$, and $\alpha$ (towards 90°). Split-phase achieves $\alpha \approx 25°-30°$ only — hence **low starting torque** is the motor's main drawback.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Split-phase motors are the workhorses of low-cost automation:
- **Domestic:** Washing machines (small), ceiling fans (in some older designs), refrigerators (small compressors).
- **Industrial:** Small pumps, blowers, machine tool auxiliaries, conveyors (light duty), office equipment.
- **HVAC:** Fan and blower motors in air-handling units.

The key advantages — **low cost, no capacitor, simple construction, high reliability** — make them ideal for mass-produced fractional-HP applications. The key disadvantage — **low starting torque (~1.5–2× full-load torque)** — limits them to applications requiring smooth, no-load starts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Phase Displacement is Limited (~30°) in Resistance Split

**Given:**
- Main winding: $R_M$ small, $X_M$ large.
- Auxiliary winding: $R_A$ large, $X_A$ small.
- Both windings connected in parallel across supply voltage $V$.

**To Find:** Maximum possible phase angle $\alpha$ between $I_M$ and $I_A$.

**Step 1:** Express the current phase angles.

For the main winding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_M &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_M}{R_M}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $X_M \gg R_M$, we get $\theta_M \rightarrow 90°$ (highly inductive).

For the auxiliary winding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_A}{R_A}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_A \gg X_A$, we get $\theta_A \rightarrow 0°$ (highly resistive).

**Step 2:** Compute the phase displacement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \theta_M - \theta_A \\
       &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_M}{R_M}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_A}{R_A}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting typical motor values (e.g., $R_M = 2\,\Omega$, $X_M = 8\,\Omega$, $R_A = 8\,\Omega$, $X_A = 2\,\Omega$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_M &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(4) = 75.96° \\
\theta_A &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04° \\
\alpha    &= 75.96° - 14.04° = 61.92° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** In practice, due to magnetic coupling, saturation, and design compromises, the achievable $\alpha$ is further reduced to **25°–30°**.

**Conclusion:** The resistance-split method cannot achieve the ideal 90° displacement. This is the **fundamental physical limitation** that necessitates capacitor-based phase splitting for higher starting torque.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem — Typical KTU Pattern

**Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole split-phase induction motor has the following parameters:
- Main winding: $R_M = 4\,\Omega$, $X_M = 12\,\Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_A = 15\,\Omega$, $X_A = 5\,\Omega$
- Full-load slip $s_{fl} = 0.04$

**Calculate:**
1. Synchronous speed $N_s$
2. Full-load rotor speed $N_r$
3. Phase angle of main winding current $\theta_M$
4. Phase angle of auxiliary winding current $\theta_A$
5. Phase displacement $\alpha$
6. Main winding current magnitude $I_M$
7. Auxiliary winding current magnitude $I_A$

---

**Solution:**

**(1) Synchronous Speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \\
    &= \frac{120 \times 50}{4} \\
    &= 1500 \text{ RPM} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Full-Load Rotor Speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_r &= N_s (1 - s) \\
    &= 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) \\
    &= 1500 \times 0.96 \\
    &= 1440 \text{ RPM} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Phase Angle of Main Winding Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_M &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_M}{R_M}\right) \\
         &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{4}\right) \\
         &= \tan^{-1}(3) \\
         &= 71.57° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4) Phase Angle of Auxiliary Winding Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_A}{R_A}\right) \\
         &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{15}\right) \\
         &= \tan^{-1}(0.3333) \\
         &= 18.43° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(5) Phase Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \theta_M - \theta_A \\
       &= 71.57° - 18.43° \\
       &= 53.14° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(6) Main Winding Current Magnitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_M &= \sqrt{R_M^2 + X_M^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{4^2 + 12^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{16 + 144} \\
    &= \sqrt{160} \\
    &= 12.649 \, \Omega \\
I_M &= \frac{V}{Z_M} \\
    &= \frac{230}{12.649} \\
    &= 18.18 \text{ A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7) Auxiliary Winding Current Magnitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_A &= \sqrt{R_A^2 + X_A^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{15^2 + 5^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{225 + 25} \\
    &= \sqrt{250} \\
    &= 15.811 \, \Omega \\
I_A &= \frac{V}{Z_A} \\
    &= \frac{230}{15.811} \\
    &= 14.55 \text{ A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Split-Phase** | **Capacitor-Start** | **Capacitor-Run** | **Shaded-Pole** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starting Torque (× FL) | 1.5 – 2.0 | 3.0 – 4.5 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| Starting Current | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | High | Lowest |
| Power Factor (running) | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.8 – 0.95 | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| Efficiency | 55 – 65% | 60 – 70% | 70 – 80% | 30 – 40% |
| Power Range | 40 – 375 W | 100 W – 7.5 kW | 100 W – 1.5 kW | < 50 W |
| Auxiliary Winding | Disconnected at start | Disconnected at start | Always connected | Copper ring only |
| Reversibility | Easy (swap M-winding leads) | Easy | Difficult | Not practical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is **frequently tested** in KTU Module 3 short-answer questions. Memorize the starting-torque ranges and cost rankings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAC[AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> nodeSW[SPST Switch]
    nodeSW --> nodeMainW[Main Winding M High X Low R]
    nodeSW --> nodeCent[Centrifugal Switch NC]
    nodeCent --> nodeAuxW[Auxiliary Winding A Low X High R]
    nodeMainW --> nodeCommon[Common Junction]
    nodeAuxW --> nodeCommon
    nodeCommon --> nodeReturn[Return to Supply]
    nodeMainW -.induces.-> nodeField[Rotating Magnetic Field 25 to 30 deg displacement]
    nodeAuxW -.induces.-> nodeField
    nodeField -.cuts.-> nodeRotor[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    nodeRotor --> nodeMotion[Mechanical Rotation N_r]
    nodeMotion --> nodeShaft[Shaft Output]
    nodeShaft --> nodeSense[Speed Sensor 75 to 80 percent Ns]
    nodeSense --> nodeCent
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Torque-Speed Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[Torque T] --> nodeStart[Starting Torque T_st 1.5 to 2x FL]
    nodeStart --> nodeAccel[Acceleration Region 0 to 75 percent Ns]
    nodeAccel --> nodeCut[Centrifugal Switch Opens at 75 to 80 percent Ns]
    nodeCut --> nodeStable[Stable Operating Region]
    nodeStable --> nodePullout[Pullout Torque 1.5 to 3x FL]
    nodePullout --> nodeSync[Synchronous Speed N_s]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Phasor Diagram — Current Phase Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeV[Reference V along x-axis] --> nodeIM[Main Current I_M lags V by theta_M 70 to 80 deg]
    nodeV --> nodeIA[Aux Current I_A lags V by theta_A 15 to 25 deg]
    nodeIM --> nodeAlpha[Phase Difference alpha 25 to 30 deg]
    nodeIA --> nodeAlpha
    nodeAlpha --> nodeField[Resultant Rotating Magnetic Field]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Layout (Textual Schematic)

```
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │       STATOR CORE           │
   │  ┌───┐         ┌───┐       │
   │  │ M │  90°    │ A │       │   M = Main Winding
   │  │   │ ←─────→ │   │       │   A = Auxiliary Winding
   │  └───┘  spatial└───┘       │
   │         displacement       │
   │  ┌─────────────────────┐   │
   │  │  SQUIRREL CAGE      │   │
   │  │  ROTOR (Bars +      │   │   ┌──────────────┐
   │  │  End Rings)         │◄──┼───│ Centrifugal  │
   │  └─────────────────────┘   │   │ Switch on    │
   │                            │   │ Shaft        │
   └────────────────────────────┘   └──────────────┘
              ▲
              │
          SHAFT OUTPUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The main winding (M) and auxiliary winding (A) are placed 90° apart spatially around the stator periphery. The centrifugal switch is mechanically coupled to the rotor shaft and opens at ~75–80% of $N_s$.

### 4.5 Mermaid Constructional Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeMotor[Split Phase Induction Motor] --> nodeStatorPart[Stator Assembly]
    nodeMotor --> nodeRotorPart[Rotor Assembly]
    nodeMotor --> nodeSwitchPart[Centrifugal Switch]
    nodeStatorPart --> nodeCore[Laminated Si Steel Core]
    nodeStatorPart --> nodeMain[Main Winding Thick Cu Wire Deep Slots]
    nodeStatorPart --> nodeAux[Aux Winding Thin Cu Wire Top Slots]
    nodeRotorPart --> nodeBars[Aluminium or Copper Bars]
    nodeRotorPart --> nodeEndRings[Short Circuit End Rings]
    nodeRotorPart --> nodeShaftRotor[Steel Shaft]
    nodeSwitchPart --> nodeMount[Mounted on Shaft Internal]
    nodeSwitchPart --> nodeSpring[Spring Loaded Weights]
    nodeSwitchPart --> nodeContact[NC Contact Pair]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of a split-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Low cost and simple construction** — no capacitor, no brushes, no commutator; only a squirrel-cage rotor and a centrifugal switch.
2. **High reliability and long life** — robust rotor, minimal moving parts, suitable for continuous-duty applications.
3. **Easy reversal** — interchanging the leads of either the main or auxiliary winding reverses the rotation direction.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Low starting torque** (typically 1.5–2× full-load torque) — unsuitable for high-inertia loads.
2. **Auxiliary winding cannot remain in circuit** continuously — requires a centrifugal switch, which is a wear-prone component.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a centrifugal switch necessary in a split-phase motor? What happens if it fails to open?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **centrifugal switch** is necessary to disconnect the auxiliary winding once the motor reaches approximately **75–80% of synchronous speed**. The auxiliary winding is designed with high resistance and small conductor cross-section, making it suitable only for **short-duration starting duty** (typically 3–5 seconds). If the centrifugal switch **fails to open**:

- The auxiliary winding will carry continuous current and overheat rapidly.
- The high $I^2R$ losses in the thin-wire auxiliary winding will cause **insulation failure and burnout** within minutes.
- Reduced motor efficiency and possible fire hazard.

---

### PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a split-phase induction motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The split-phase induction motor consists of:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core carrying two windings displaced by 90° in space:
  - **Main winding (M):** Thick copper wire, low resistance, high inductance. Placed deep in the slots.
  - **Auxiliary winding (A):** Thin copper wire, high resistance, low inductance. Placed on top of the slots.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type with copper/aluminum bars short-circuited by end rings.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Spring-loaded, normally-closed, mounted on the shaft. Disconnects the auxiliary winding at ~75–80% of $N_s$.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. At **startup**, both windings are energized in parallel from the single-phase supply.
2. Due to the high $X_M/R_M$ ratio, the main winding current $I_M$ lags the voltage by a large angle (~70–80°).
3. Due to the high $R_A/X_A$ ratio, the auxiliary current $I_A$ lags the voltage by a small angle (~15–25°).
4. The two currents are **displaced in time by 25°–30°** and **in space by 90°**, producing a **weak elliptical rotating magnetic field**.
5. This rotating field cuts the rotor bars, inducing current; the rotor begins to accelerate.
6. At **75–80% of $N_s$**, the centrifugal switch opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding.
7. The motor continues to run on the main winding alone under the principle of **cross-field theory** (single-phase running operation).

**Q. (b)** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole split-phase induction motor has $R_M = 2\,\Omega$, $X_M = 6\,\Omega$, $R_A = 8\,\Omega$, $X_A = 2\,\Omega$. Calculate the magnitude and phase of currents in both windings, and the starting torque in terms of the torque constant $K = 0.5$ N·m/A². **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Main Winding Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_M &= \sqrt{R_M^2 + X_M^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{2^2 + 6^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{4 + 36} \\
    &= \sqrt{40} \\
    &= 6.324 \, \Omega \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Main Winding Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_M &= \frac{V}{Z_M} = \frac{230}{6.324} = 36.37 \text{ A} \\
\theta_M &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_M}{R_M}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3) = 71.57° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Auxiliary Winding Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_A &= \sqrt{R_A^2 + X_A^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{8^2 + 2^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{64 + 4} \\
    &= \sqrt{68} \\
    &= 8.246 \, \Omega \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Auxiliary Winding Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= \frac{V}{Z_A} = \frac{230}{8.246} = 27.89 \text{ A} \\
\theta_A &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_A}{R_A}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \theta_M - \theta_A \\
       &= 71.57° - 14.04° \\
       &= 57.53° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Starting Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= K \cdot I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
       &= 0.5 \times 36.37 \times 27.89 \times \sin(57.53°) \\
       &= 0.5 \times 36.37 \times 27.89 \times 0.844 \\
       &= 427.96 \text{ N·m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert degrees to use sine function correctly. Always use degrees in the calculator mode.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing the formula for starting torque. The correct form is $T_{st} \propto I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \sin(\alpha)$, NOT $I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \alpha$ (which would be a linear approximation, only valid for small $\alpha$ in radians).
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state the **direction** of the torque or to identify which winding leads/lags.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Not mentioning the role of the centrifugal switch in the working principle — this is a **must-have** for full marks in the 7-mark question.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**Q. (a)** Explain the torque-speed characteristic of a split-phase induction motor with a suitable sketch. Discuss the role of the centrifugal switch in determining the operating region. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Torque-Speed Characteristic (5 marks):**

The torque-speed curve of a split-phase motor has three distinct regions:

1. **At Standstill (s = 1):** Starting torque $T_{st}$ is **1.5 to 2.0 times** the full-load torque. This is produced by the rotating field due to the time-phase displacement between $I_M$ and $I_A$.

2. **Acceleration Region (0 < s < 1):** As rotor speed increases, the torque first **rises to a maximum (pull-out torque)** of about 1.5–3× full-load torque at around **80% of $N_s$**.

3. **Normal Operating Region (s ≈ 0.04–0.06):** The motor settles at a low slip value where motor torque equals load torque. At this point, the auxiliary winding has been disconnected by the centrifugal switch, and the motor runs on the main winding alone.

4. **Near Synchronous Speed (s → 0):** Torque drops to zero as the relative speed between field and rotor vanishes.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch (2 marks):**

- The centrifugal switch is designed to **open at 75–80% of $N_s$** (corresponding to slip s ≈ 0.20–0.25).
- This cut-off is **positioned on the rising portion of the torque-speed curve**, just before the maximum torque point.
- Disconnecting the auxiliary winding at this point ensures:
  - The motor has already gained sufficient momentum to continue running on the main winding.
  - The auxiliary winding does not overheat from continuous operation.
  - The motor avoids the unstable negative-slope region of the curve.

**Q. (b)** Compare split-phase, capacitor-start, and shaded-pole motors on the basis of starting torque, cost, applications, and starting current. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Split-Phase** | **Capacitor-Start** | **Shaded-Pole** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting Torque** | 1.5–2× FL torque (Low) | 3–4.5× FL torque (High) | 0.5–1× FL torque (Very Low) |
| **Cost** | Low (no capacitor) | Medium (capacitor + switch) | Lowest (no winding, no switch) |
| **Starting Current** | High (5–7× FL) | Moderate (4–5× FL) | Low (2–3× FL) |
| **Applications** | Fans, blowers, washing machines, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors, machine tools, large pumps | Small fans, hair dryers, toys, clock motors |
| **Power Range** | 40–375 W | 100 W – 7.5 kW | < 50 W |
| **Reversibility** | Easy | Easy | Not practical |
| **Construction** | Two windings + centrifugal switch | Two windings + capacitor + centrifugal switch | Single winding + copper shading rings |
| **Efficiency** | 55–65% | 60–70% | 30–40% |
| **Power Factor** | 0.6–0.7 lagging | 0.6–0.7 lagging | 0.5–0.6 lagging |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Listing comparison points without proper **values/ranges**. The KTU marking scheme explicitly awards marks for **quantitative values** wherever possible.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Confusing "capacitor-start" with "capacitor-run" — these are **two distinct motor types** and must not be merged.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Omitting the torque-speed **sketch** in part (a) — KTU mandates a diagram for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Failing to identify the **pull-out (maximum) torque** point and the **stable operating region** on the torque-speed curve.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition:** A split-phase motor is a single-phase induction motor that uses **two spatially-90°-displaced windings** with different R/X ratios to create a starting rotating field.
- **Phase Splitting Method:** **Resistance split** (no capacitor) — main winding is inductive, auxiliary winding is resistive.
- **Typical Phase Displacement:** **25° to 30°** (practically achievable); ideally 90° but not attainable by pure resistance-inductance split.
- **Starting Torque Range:** **1.5 to 2.0 × full-load torque** — this is the motor's **main limitation**.
- **Centrifugal Switch Cut-off Speed:** **75% to 80% of synchronous speed** (slip s ≈ 0.20–0.25).
- **Rotor Type:** Squirrel-cage (identical to 3-phase induction motors).
- **Auxiliary Winding Duty:** **Short-time duty only** (3–5 seconds); cannot run continuously.
- **Standard Synchronous Speeds (50 Hz):** 3000 RPM (2P), 1500 RPM (4P), 1000 RPM (6P).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$
  - $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
  - $T_{st} \propto I_M \cdot I_A \cdot \sin(\alpha)$
  - $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$
  - $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right)$
- **Reversal Method:** Interchange the leads of **either** the main winding **or** the auxiliary winding (not both).
- **Cost Position:** **Second-lowest** (cheaper than capacitor motors, slightly costlier than shaded-pole).
- **Typical Applications:** Domestic fans, washing machines, small pumps, blowers, office machinery.
- **Not Suitable For:** High-inertia loads, frequent-start applications, loads requiring high starting torque (use capacitor-start instead).
- **Always remember:** The main winding has **high impedance (inductive)**, and the auxiliary winding has **low impedance with high resistance**.
- **Cross-field theory** explains the single-winding running operation (after centrifugal switch opens).
- **Comparison trick:** Split-phase < Capacitor-Start in starting torque; Split-phase > Shaded-pole in efficiency and starting torque.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitor start motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacitor start motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Capacitor Start Motor (CSM)** is a modified single-phase induction motor in which a **capacitor** is connected in series with the auxiliary (starting) winding to create a phase displacement between the currents of the main (running) winding and the auxiliary winding. This phase shift is designed to approach **90°** during the starting period, producing a high starting torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The capacitor start motor is classified as a *split-phase motor variant* under the family of **Single-Phase Induction Motors**. Its primary engineering distinction is the use of a **capacitor in the starting circuit only**, disconnected after the rotor reaches approximately **70–80 %** of synchronous speed by a centrifugal switch.

### Key Constructional Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Main (Running) Winding** | Low-resistance, high-inductance winding that carries current continuously during operation. |
| **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding** | High-resistance winding, physically displaced by 90° electrical from the main winding. |
| **AC Capacitor (Start)** | Electrolytic-type, short-duty rated. Connected **only during starting**. Provides leading current to auxiliary winding. |
| **Centrifugal Switch (or Potential Relay)** | Disconnects the auxiliary winding + capacitor branch when rotor speed reaches ~75 % of synchronous speed. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rowing Boat" Intuition

Imagine two rowers on opposite ends of a small boat trying to push it forward:

- **Rower A (Main Winding)** pulls the oar *just a little late* after the command — he is **slow** (lagging current due to high inductance).
- **Rower B (Auxiliary Winding)** pulls the oar almost *exactly when the command is given* — he is **fast** (leading current due to capacitor).
- Because of the **time-gap between their strokes**, the boat twists and surges forward. The bigger the time-gap (closer to 90° phase shift), the harder the boat pushes forward.

In an induction motor, this "twist" between the two windings produces a **rotating magnetic field** during starting, which the rotor chases, generating **high starting torque**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A well-designed capacitor start motor delivers a starting torque of approximately **3 to 4.5 times** the full-load torque — significantly higher than a plain split-phase motor (which only achieves ~1.5× FLT).

## 1.3 Why Use a Capacitor at All?

In a pure **resistance split-phase motor**, the phase angle between the two winding currents is limited to ~25°–30° because the auxiliary winding is only made *resistive* (high resistance). This gives poor starting torque.

By inserting a **capacitor** in the auxiliary branch, the current in that winding becomes **leading** instead of lagging, while the main winding current remains lagging. This effectively *doubles* the phase angle and pushes it toward the ideal **90°**, dramatically improving the starting torque.

$$T_{st} \propto I_a \cdot I_m \cdot \sin\alpha$$

where the phase angle $\alpha$ is the angle between $I_a$ and $I_m$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Single-phase AC supply** is applied across both windings simultaneously.
2. The **main winding** has high inductance → its current $I_m$ **lags** the supply voltage by a large angle $\phi_m$.
3. The **auxiliary winding** is in series with a **capacitor** → its current $I_a$ **leads** the supply voltage by angle $\phi_a$.
4. The **net phase displacement** $\alpha$ between $I_a$ and $I_m$ is:

$$\alpha = \phi_a + \phi_m$$

5. This $\alpha$ is engineered to be **close to 90°**, generating a quasi-rotating magnetic field.
6. The rotating magnetic field induces currents in the **squirrel-cage rotor**, producing starting torque.
7. As rotor accelerates to ~75 % of $N_s$, the **centrifugal switch opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor.
8. The motor continues to run on the **main winding alone**, sustained by the forward and backward rotating fields of a single-phase winding.

## 2.2 Phasor Analysis

Let supply voltage $V$ be the reference phasor.

### Main Winding Branch (Inductive)
$$Z_m = R_m + jX_m$$
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) \quad \text{(lagging)}$$
$$I_m = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^{2} + X_m^{2}}}$$

### Auxiliary Winding Branch (Capacitive)
$$Z_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_C)$$
$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) \quad \text{(leading if } X_C > X_a\text{)}$$
$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^{2} + (X_a - X_C)^{2}}}$$

### Phase Displacement Between Currents
$$\alpha = \phi_a + \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** For maximum starting torque, $\alpha$ must equal **90°**, which means:
> $$\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = 90°$$
> Solving the tangent of both sides:
> $$\frac{X_m}{R_m} \cdot \frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a} = 1$$
> $$\boxed{\;X_C = X_a + \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m}\;}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | $N_s$ in rpm; $f$ in Hz; $P$ = poles |
| Starting Torque | $T_{st} \propto I_a \cdot I_m \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Maximised when $\alpha = 90°$ |
| Starting Torque (Ratio) | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{FL}} \approx 3 \text{ to } 4.5$ | Typical CSM ratio |
| Main Winding Impedance | $Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^{2} + X_m^{2}}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Auxiliary Branch Impedance | $Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^{2} + (X_a - X_C)^{2}}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$); $C$ in Farads |
| Phase Angle (Auxiliary) | $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right)$ | Radians or Degrees |
| Phase Angle (Main) | $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$ | Radians or Degrees |
| Optimum Capacitance | $C_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}\right)}$ | Farads |
| Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Per-unit |
| Switch-off Speed | $N_{sw} \approx 0.75 \, N_s$ | rpm |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility & Applications

The capacitor start motor is the **workhorse** of single-phase industrial and commercial drives because of its **high starting torque**:

- Air compressors and refrigeration compressors
- Water pumps (submersible and centrifugal)
- Conveyor belts and industrial fans
- Washing machines, large air-conditioner compressors
- Workshop machinery (lathes, drill presses, grinders)
- Any load requiring high breakaway torque under direct-on-line starting

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** The starting capacitor is *electrolytic* and short-duty rated (typically **3 seconds ON, 70 seconds OFF**). It must NEVER remain in the circuit continuously, or it will overheat and explode. This is why the centrifugal switch is a **mandatory safety device**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Starting Torque Expression for Two-Phase Operation

Consider two windings displaced by 90° in space, carrying currents $I_m$ and $I_a$ with phase difference $\alpha$ in time.

### Step 1 — Instantaneous MMF of Each Winding

Let the main winding lie along the x-axis and the auxiliary winding along the y-axis.

$$\theta_m(t) = N_m I_m \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(MMF along x-axis)}$$
$$\theta_a(t) = N_a I_a \cos(\omega t - \alpha) \quad \text{(MMF along y-axis)}$$

### Step 2 — Resultant MMF Components

$$\theta_x(t) = \theta_m(t) = N_m I_m \cos(\omega t)$$
$$\theta_y(t) = \theta_a(t) = N_a I_a \cos(\omega t - \alpha)$$

### Step 3 — Resolve into Forward and Backward Fields

For a balanced two-phase system, the resultant field has constant magnitude rotating at synchronous speed. For an **unbalanced** system (which is our case), the field splits into two counter-rotating components.

Using trigonometric expansion:
$$\cos(\omega t - \alpha) = \cos\alpha\cos(\omega t) + \sin\alpha\sin(\omega t)$$

$$\theta_y(t) = N_a I_a \left[\cos\alpha\cos(\omega t) + \sin\alpha\sin(\omega t)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Magnitude of Forward Rotating Component

The forward (positive-sequence) rotating MMF has magnitude:
$$\theta_f = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(N_m I_m)^{2} + (N_a I_a)^{2} + 2(N_m I_m)(N_a I_a)\sin\alpha}$$

The backward (negative-sequence) rotating MMF has magnitude:
$$\theta_b = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(N_m I_m)^{2} + (N_a I_a)^{2} - 2(N_m I_m)(N_a I_a)\sin\alpha}$$

### Step 5 — Starting Torque Proportionality

The net torque is the difference between forward and backward torques:

$$T_{st} = k \cdot (\theta_f^{2} - \theta_b^{2})$$

After algebraic expansion, the cross term survives:

$$\boxed{\;T_{st} \;\propto\; I_a \, I_m \, \sin\alpha\;}$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on rotor resistance, rotor reactance, slip, and machine dimensions.

### Step 6 — Condition for Maximum Starting Torque

Differentiate $T_{st}$ with respect to $\alpha$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d(\sin\alpha)}{d\alpha} = \cos\alpha = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha = 90°\;}$$

This confirms that the **ideal phase displacement is 90°** for maximum starting torque — the entire engineering rationale behind adding the capacitor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Capacitor Value

Starting from the design condition $\alpha = 90°$:

$$\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = 90°$$

**Step 1:** Take the tangent of both sides. Since $\tan(90° + \beta) = -\cot(\beta)$, we can rewrite:

$$\tan^{-1}(A) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(B)$$
$$\Rightarrow \tan^{-1}(A) + \tan^{-1}(B) = 90°$$
$$\Rightarrow A \cdot B = 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply to our variables:

$$\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a} \cdot \frac{X_m}{R_m} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $X_C$:

$$X_a - X_C = \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m}$$

$$X_C = X_a + \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi f C} = X_a + \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_{opt} = \frac{1}{2\pi f \left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}\right)}\;}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor Start Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary Winding Phase Shift Method | High Resistance | Capacitor in series |
| Phase Angle $\alpha$ | ~25° to 30° | ~80° to 90° |
| Starting Torque (× FLT) | 1.5 to 2 | 3 to 4.5 |
| Starting Current (× FLC) | 6 to 8 | 4 to 6 |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| Capacitor Type | None | Electrolytic AC (Start duty) |
| Disconnect Device | Centrifugal Switch | Centrifugal Switch / Potential Relay |
| Typical Applications | Fans, blowers, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors, machine tools |
| Power Range | 60 W – 250 W | 250 W – 7.5 kW |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole single-phase capacitor start induction motor has the following winding parameters:
- Main winding: $R_m = 4\,\Omega$, $X_m = 8\,\Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_a = 7\,\Omega$, $X_a = 4\,\Omega$

Find: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Value of capacitor for maximum starting torque, (iii) Magnitudes of $I_m$ and $I_a$, (iv) Verify phase angle.

**Solution:**

### Part (i) Synchronous Speed
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

### Part (ii) Optimum Capacitance
$$X_C = X_a + \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m} = 4 + \frac{7 \times 4}{8} = 4 + 3.5 = 7.5\,\Omega$$

$$C_{opt} = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 7.5}$$

$$\boxed{C_{opt} = 4.244 \times 10^{-4} \text{ F} \approx 424\,\mu\text{F}}$$

### Part (iii) Current Magnitudes
$$I_m = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^{2} + X_m^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{4^{2} + 8^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{80}} = \frac{230}{8.944}$$

$$\boxed{I_m \approx 25.71 \text{ A}}$$

$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^{2} + (X_a - X_C)^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{7^{2} + (4 - 7.5)^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{49 + 12.25}}$$

$$I_a = \frac{230}{\sqrt{61.25}} = \frac{230}{7.826}$$

$$\boxed{I_a \approx 29.39 \text{ A}}$$

### Part (iv) Phase Angle Verification
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2) = 63.43°$$

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4 - 7.5}{7}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.5) = -26.57°$$

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 63.43° - (-26.57°) = 90° \;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign on $\phi_a$ confirms that the auxiliary current **leads** the supply voltage (capacitive branch), while $I_m$ **lags** (inductive branch). The sum gives the ideal 90° displacement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Capacitor Start Motor)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUP[230V 50Hz Single Phase AC Supply] --> MAIN[Main Running Winding Rm Xm]
    SUP --> AUX[Auxiliary Starting Winding Ra Xa]
    CAP[AC Start Capacitor C] --> AUX
    SW[Centrifugal Switch or Potential Relay] --> CAP
    SW --> AUX
    MAIN --> ROTOR[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    AUX --> ROTOR
    ROTOR --> SHAFT[Mechanical Output Shaft]
    ROTOR -. at 75 percent Ns .-> SW
    style MAIN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style AUX fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style CAP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style SW fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style ROTOR fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style SUP fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram Showing 90° Displacement

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[V Supply Reference Horizontal] --> IM[Im Lagging by 63.43 deg Inductive]
    V --> IA[Ia Leading by 26.57 deg Capacitive]
    IM -. angle 90 deg .-> IA
    style V fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000
    style IM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style IA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Torque-Speed Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start at s = 1: High Starting Torque 3 to 4.5 FLT] --> B[Rotor Accelerates Switch Opens near 75 percent Ns]
    B --> C[Auxiliary Branch Disconnected]
    C --> D[Runs on Main Winding Only Single Phase Torque Slope]
    D --> E[Operates near Synchronous Speed at Rated Load Point]
    style A fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit of Capacitor Start Motor (At Starting)

```mermaid
graph LR
    TERM_A[Terminal A] --> RM[Main Winding Branch Rm series jXm]
    RM --> NEUTRAL_N[Neutral N]
    TERM_A --> SWNODE[Switch Node Closed during start]
    SWNODE --> RA[Auxiliary Branch Ra]
    RA --> XA[Leakage Reactance jXa]
    XA --> CAP[Capacitor reactance minus jXC]
    CAP --> NEUTRAL_N
    SWNODE -. centrifugal switch .-> SWNODE
    style TERM_A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style NEUTRAL_N fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style CAP fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style SWNODE fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** Each schematic above is a *functional block diagram* representing the topology of the capacitor start motor's electrical architecture. The dual arrows between rotor and switch represent the centrifugal feedback that opens the auxiliary branch automatically at high speed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a capacitor connected in the auxiliary winding of a single-phase induction motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-phase induction motor has no inherent starting torque because a single winding produces only a **pulsating magnetic field** (no rotation). To produce a rotating magnetic field during starting, we need **two currents displaced in time phase**.

The auxiliary winding alone (high resistance) gives only ~25°–30° phase shift — insufficient for high starting torque.

By connecting a **capacitor** in series with the auxiliary winding, the current in that branch becomes **leading**, while the main winding current remains **lagging**. The total phase angle $\alpha$ approaches **90°**, which:

1. Generates a quasi-rotating magnetic field during starting.
2. Produces a **high starting torque** (3 to 4.5 times full-load torque).
3. Improves starting performance compared to a plain split-phase motor.

**Mark Split:** (1) Pulsating field issue – 1 mark; (2) Capacitor makes Ia leading – 1 mark; (3) 90° shift → high torque – 1 mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the function of the centrifugal switch in a capacitor start motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The centrifugal switch is a **mechanical or electromechanical device** mounted on the motor shaft. Its functions are:

1. **To disconnect the auxiliary winding and capacitor** from the supply once the rotor reaches approximately **70–80 % of synchronous speed** (typically 75 % of $N_s$).
2. **To protect the electrolytic start capacitor** from overheating and explosion — these capacitors are designed only for short-duration duty (3 seconds ON, 70 seconds OFF).
3. **To allow the motor to run on the main winding alone**, as continuous running on two windings is unnecessary and inefficient.

**Mark Split:** (1) Disconnect function – 1 mark; (2) Speed threshold – 1 mark; (3) Capacitor protection – 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **capacitor start single-phase induction motor**. Discuss why a **starting capacitor** (not a running capacitor) is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole capacitor start induction motor has:
- Main winding: $R_m = 5\,\Omega$, $X_m = 7.5\,\Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_a = 8\,\Omega$, $X_a = 5\,\Omega$

Calculate:
1. The value of the starting capacitor for maximum starting torque.
2. The currents in the main and auxiliary windings.
3. The phase angle between the two currents. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Has two windings — main (running) winding with low resistance and high inductance; auxiliary (starting) winding with high resistance, displaced 90° electrical in space.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase motor rotors.
- **Capacitor:** Electrolytic AC type, connected in series with auxiliary winding.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Mounted on shaft, in series with auxiliary winding; opens at ~75 % of synchronous speed.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. On supply, $I_m$ lags by $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(X_m/R_m)$ due to inductive main winding.
2. $I_a$ leads by $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}((X_a - X_C)/R_a)$ due to capacitive auxiliary branch.
3. Phase difference $\alpha = \phi_m + \phi_a$ approaches 90°, producing rotating magnetic field.
4. Rotor accelerates; centrifugal switch opens at ~75 % $N_s$, disconnecting auxiliary branch.
5. Motor continues on main winding only.

**Why starting capacitor, not running capacitor (1 mark):**
- Starting capacitor is **electrolytic AC type** with high capacitance (50–400 µF) but **short-duty rated** — gives high starting torque but cannot run continuously.
- A running capacitor is **oil-filled, low-capacitance (5–50 µF), continuous-duty rated** — used in capacitor-run motors for better running performance, but gives lower starting torque.
- For applications needing **high starting torque only**, starting capacitor suffices, saving cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$
- $R_m = 5\,\Omega$, $X_m = 7.5\,\Omega$
- $R_a = 8\,\Omega$, $X_a = 5\,\Omega$

**Step 1 — Optimum Capacitance (3 marks):**

$$X_C = X_a + \frac{R_a R_m}{X_m} = 5 + \frac{8 \times 5}{7.5} = 5 + 5.333 = 10.333\,\Omega$$

$$C_{opt} = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 10.333} = \frac{1}{3246.18}$$

$$\boxed{C_{opt} \approx 308.05\,\mu\text{F}}$$

**Step 2 — Current Magnitudes (2 marks):**

$$I_m = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^{2} + X_m^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{25 + 56.25}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{81.25}} = \frac{230}{9.014}$$

$$\boxed{I_m \approx 25.52 \text{ A}}$$

$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^{2} + (X_a - X_C)^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{64 + (5 - 10.333)^{2}}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{64 + 28.44}}$$

$$I_a = \frac{230}{\sqrt{92.44}} = \frac{230}{9.614}$$

$$\boxed{I_a \approx 23.92 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3 — Phase Angle (2 marks):**

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{7.5}{5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{5 - 10.333}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.667) = -33.69°$$

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 56.31° - (-33.69°) = 90° \;\checkmark$$

**Verification confirms design for maximum starting torque.**

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` — **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **starting torque** of a capacitor start motor and show that it is **maximum when the phase angle between the two winding currents is 90°**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **performance characteristics** of a **capacitor start motor** with those of a **split-phase motor** and a **capacitor-run motor** in terms of starting torque, starting current, power factor, efficiency, and applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For two windings displaced by 90° in space carrying currents $I_m$ and $I_a$ with phase difference $\alpha$:

$$\text{MMF}_x(t) = N_m I_m \cos(\omega t)$$
$$\text{MMF}_y(t) = N_a I_a \cos(\omega t - \alpha)$$

Using the identity $\cos(\omega t - \alpha) = \cos\alpha\cos(\omega t) + \sin\alpha\sin(\omega t)$, the resultant MMF decomposes into two counter-rotating components:

$$\theta_f = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(N_m I_m)^2 + (N_a I_a)^2 + 2 N_m I_m N_a I_a \sin\alpha}$$

$$\theta_b = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(N_m I_m)^2 + (N_a I_a)^2 - 2 N_m I_m N_a I_a \sin\alpha}$$

Torque is proportional to the difference:

$$T \propto \theta_f^2 - \theta_b^2 = 2 N_m N_a I_m I_a \sin\alpha$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} = k \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha}$$

where $k$ is a machine constant.

**Maximum condition (2 marks):**

For $T_{st}$ to be maximum, $\sin\alpha$ must be maximum:

$$\frac{d(T_{st})}{d\alpha} = k I_m I_a \cos\alpha = 0$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 90° \;\Rightarrow\; T_{st,max} = k \cdot I_m \cdot I_a}$$

Hence, **maximum starting torque occurs when the phase displacement between main and auxiliary winding currents is exactly 90°**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor Start | Capacitor Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase Angle** | 25°–30° | 80°–90° | 80°–90° continuous |
| **Starting Torque (×FLT)** | 1.5–2 | 3–4.5 | 1.5–2 |
| **Starting Current** | High (6–8×) | Moderate (4–6×) | Moderate (3–5×) |
| **Running Power Factor** | Poor (0.5–0.6) | Poor (same as split-phase) | Good (0.8–0.95) |
| **Running Efficiency** | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Capacitor Type** | None | Electrolytic (short duty) | Oil-filled (continuous duty) |
| **Switch / Relay** | Centrifugal | Centrifugal / Potential | None (permanent connection) |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Higher |
| **Noise Level** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Fans, blowers | Compressors, conveyors, pumps | Fans, blowers needing quiet operation, HVAC |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Capacitor start motors offer the best balance of **high starting torque** and **moderate cost** for heavy-load starting applications, while capacitor-run motors are preferred for **continuous-duty quiet operation** with lower starting torque needs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Students often forget the negative sign in $\phi_a$ when capacitor reactance exceeds inductive reactance. Always remember: leading current → negative angle (relative to inductive reference).
> 2. **Phase angle formula confusion:** The angle between the two currents is $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$, NOT $\phi_m + \phi_a$ (the latter is only true when both angles are defined as positive magnitudes).
> 3. **Switch speed assumption:** Many students write "centrifugal switch opens at synchronous speed" — incorrect. It opens at **~75 % of $N_s$**, after rotor has gained sufficient mechanical momentum to continue running on the main winding alone.
> 4. **Capacitor duty cycle:** Examiners deduct marks if students don't mention that the start capacitor is **short-duty rated (3 sec ON / 70 sec OFF)**.
> 5. **Missing units:** Always specify capacitance in µF (not F) for readability; KTU scripts often lose ½ mark for unit inconsistency.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A capacitor start motor is a single-phase induction motor with a **capacitor in the auxiliary winding branch** to achieve ~90° phase shift during starting.
- **Core Formulas:**
  - $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$
  - $X_C = X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}$ (for $\alpha = 90°$)
  - $C_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}\right)}$
  - $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
- **Key Components:** Main winding, auxiliary winding, AC electrolytic start capacitor, centrifugal switch.
- **Capacitor Duty:** Short-duty only — must be disconnected by centrifugal switch at ~75 % $N_s$.
- **Phase Angle:** Ideally **90°** for maximum starting torque.
- **Starting Torque:** Typically **3 to 4.5 × full-load torque** — significantly higher than split-phase (1.5–2 ×) and capacitor-run motors (1.5–2 ×).
- **Applications:** Compressors, pumps, conveyors, refrigeration units, washing machines, machine tools.
- **KTU Module Context:** This topic belongs to **Module 3** of PEAUT525 — *Electric Drives and Controls for Automation* — and typically forms 2-mark to 14-mark questions depending on the question paper pattern.
- **Common Comparison:** Always be ready to write a comparison table with **split-phase** and **capacitor-run motors**.
- **Numerical Pattern:** Board problems typically give $R_m$, $X_m$, $R_a$, $X_a$, $V$, $f$, $P$ and ask for $C_{opt}$, $I_m$, $I_a$, $\alpha$.
- **Critical Safety:** Never connect a start capacitor continuously — it will explode due to overheating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous machines types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous machines types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Synchronous Machine

A **Synchronous Machine** is a rotating AC electrical machine in which the **rotor rotates at exactly the synchronous speed**, which is rigidly locked to the supply frequency. The synchronous speed in revolutions per minute (rpm) is governed by the fundamental relationship:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$

where $f$ is the supply frequency in Hertz (**50 Hz** in India as per Indian Standards) and $P$ is the total number of poles on the machine.

The defining electrical feature is that the rotor either carries a **DC-excited winding** (creating fixed north-south magnetic poles) or is constructed from materials with high magnetic anisotropy (creating saliency or permanent magnetization). The stator (armature) is fed with balanced three-phase AC, producing a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at $N_s$, and the rotor locks into synchronism with this RMF — hence the name *synchronous*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A synchronous machine may operate as a **motor, generator (alternator), or synchronous condenser**. In automation drives, the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) and Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM) are the most relevant topologies for high-performance servo and vector-controlled drives.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two bar magnets mounted on a turntable (the rotor), with a clock hand pointing to "12 o'clock" (the rotor's magnetic north pole). Now imagine a large rotating compass needle on a turntable above (the stator's RMF). The clock hand will **always** try to follow the compass needle's position because of the magnetic attraction — if the compass needle rotates at exactly 1 revolution per second, the clock hand is forced to rotate at exactly 1 revolution per second, no matter how much load tries to slow it down (up to a breaking point called the *pull-out torque*).

This is the essence of a synchronous machine: **the rotor speed is mathematically locked to the supply frequency**, not to the load. This is why synchronous motors are preferred in precision drives (CNC machines, robotics, elevators, textile mills) where **exact speed proportional to frequency** is mandatory.

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Laws Governing Synchronous Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Law 1 — Synchronous Speed Law:** Rotor mechanical speed is mathematically tied to supply frequency and pole count. There is **no slip** under steady-state operation.
>
> **Law 2 — Constant Air-Gap Flux Law:** In the steady state, the average air-gap flux per pole is held approximately constant by the field (rotor) excitation, very similar to the field-flux control philosophy of separately excited DC machines.

## 1.4 Key Synchronous Speed Values (for reference in KTU problems)

For a **50 Hz** Indian supply system:

| Poles (P) | $N_s$ (rpm) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 3000 | Turbo-alternators, high-speed PMSM servos |
| 4 | 1500 | Industrial drives, alternators |
| 6 | 1000 | Hydro-generators, large motors |
| 8 | 750 | Hydro-generators, low-speed mills |
| 10 | 600 | Hydro-generators |
| 12 | 500 | Hydro-generators |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Synchronous Machines (Hierarchical Breakdown)

Synchronous machines are classified under KTU 2024 scheme according to the following structural and functional criteria:

### A. Classification Based on **Rotor Construction**
This is the most important classification for the KTU exam.

1. **Salient-Pole Synchronous Machine**
   - Rotor has **projecting (salient) poles** bolted or dovetailed onto a solid yoke.
   - Has **non-uniform air gap** (small at pole centre, large at pole shoes' edges).
   - Pole shoes carry **damper (amortisseur) windings** — short-circuited copper bars embedded in the pole face, similar to a squirrel-cage rotor.
   - **Operating speed:** Low to medium (typically 100–1500 rpm).
   - **Rotor diameter:** Large, **axial length:** short.
   - **Applications:** Hydro-electric generators, large synchronous motors driving compressors, mills, crushers.

2. **Cylindrical-Rotor (Non-Salient Pole) Synchronous Machine**
   - Rotor is a **solid, smooth, cylindrical forging** of high-grade silicon steel or alloy steel.
   - Slots are milled on the rotor surface and the field winding is **distributed** in these slots (typically 2/3rd short-pitched).
   - Has a **uniform air gap** → produces nearly **sinusoidal EMF**.
   - **Operating speed:** High (typically 1500–3000 rpm for 50 Hz).
   - **Rotor diameter:** Small, **axial length:** long.
   - **Mechanical strength:** Very high, capable of withstanding centrifugal forces at 3000 rpm.
   - **Applications:** Turbo-alternators (steam turbine, gas turbine coupled), high-speed PMSM.

### B. Classification Based on **Excitation Method**

1. **DC-Excited (Electromagnetic) Synchronous Machine** — Separate DC source via slip rings and brushes.
2. **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine (PMSM)** — Rotor flux created by high-energy permanent magnets (NdFeB, SmCo).
3. **Synchronous Reluctance Machine (SynRM)** — No rotor excitation; torque is produced by the tendency of a salient anisotropic rotor to align with the minimum reluctance path of the stator RMF.
4. **Hysteresis Synchronous Motor** — Rotor is a smooth cylinder of hardened steel with high hysteresis loop area; torque arises from hysteresis lag of magnetisation.
5. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Excited Machine** — Rotating rectifier on rotor shaft feeds the field winding; no slip rings or brushes.

### C. Classification Based on **Mode of Operation**

1. **Synchronous Generator (Alternator)** — Converts mechanical → electrical. Backbone of power systems.
2. **Synchronous Motor** — Converts electrical → mechanical. Constant-speed drive.
3. **Synchronous Condenser** — Operates without shaft load, used purely for **reactive power (VAr) compensation / power factor correction** in grids.

### D. Classification Based on **Prime Mover Coupling**

1. **Turbo-Alternator** — Coupled to steam/gas turbine → Cylindrical rotor, 2/4 poles.
2. **Hydro-Alternator** — Coupled to water turbine → Salient-pole rotor, multi-pole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *"Salient = Slow & Sturdy (Large Dia, Short Length) for Hydro, Smooth = Fast & Furious (Small Dia, Long Length) for Turbo."*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part-B synchronous-machine question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N_s = 120f/P$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (\text{pole pairs})$ or $\omega_s = 4\pi f / P$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s |
| 3 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | RMS EMF per phase (transformer EMF equation adapted) | Volts |
| 4 | $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ | Winding factor (pitch × distribution) | dimensionless, $\vert K_w \vert < 1$ |
| 5 | $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ | Pitch factor; $\alpha$ is chording angle in electrical degrees | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_d = \sin(m\beta/2) / [m \sin(\beta/2)]$ | Distribution factor; $m$ slots/pole/phase, $\beta$ slot angle | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_m = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \cos\phi$ | Three-phase input/output mechanical power | Watts |
| 8 | $P_d = (3 V E_f / X_s) \sin\delta$ | Power developed (power-angle equation) | Watts |
| 9 | $T_e = (3/\omega_s) \cdot (V E_f / X_s) \sin\delta$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 10 | $\delta$ | Power / Torque angle (between $V$ and $E_f$) | degrees or rad |

**Where:**
- $f$ = supply frequency (Hz)
- $P$ = total number of poles
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $T_{ph}$ = turns per phase
- $K_w$ = winding factor
- $\delta$ = load angle (KTU favourite variable!)
- $X_s$ = synchronous reactance
- $V$ = terminal voltage; $E_f$ = induced EMF (excitation EMF)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Synchronous Machine Classification

- **Power Generation Sector:** Over 95 % of global electricity is generated by **synchronous alternators** (turbo + hydro).
- **Industrial Automation:** PMSM and SynRM dominate the **servo drive** market due to high power density, precise speed control, and zero rotor copper losses.
- **Power Quality:** Synchronous condensers (no-load over-excited synchronous motors) provide dynamic VAr support, critical for renewable-rich grids (solar/wind).
- **Traction & EV:** PMSMs are the leading motor in modern electric vehicles (e.g., Tesla Model 3, BMW i3) because of their high efficiency (95–97 %).
- **High-Speed Spindles:** Cylindrical rotor PMSMs run up to 100,000 rpm in CNC machining centres.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed

The synchronous speed is the most fundamental parameter of any synchronous machine. It is derived as follows.

> **Statement:** Prove that the synchronous speed in rpm is $N_s = 120 f / P$.

**Step 1 — Definition of frequency.**
Let the supply AC have frequency $f$ Hz. By definition, this means the stator current completes $f$ full electrical cycles per second.

**Step 2 — Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution.**
A full mechanical revolution of the rotor corresponds to traversing $P/2$ North-South pole pairs. For each pole pair, the rotating magnetic field advances by $360^\circ$ electrical. Hence, one mechanical revolution corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Electrical cycles per mechanical revolution} &= \frac{P}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical revolutions per second.**
Since the rotor must keep pace with the electrical cycles (one electrical cycle per pole pair, per stator rotation step), the number of mechanical revolutions per second is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revolutions/sec} &= \frac{f}{\text{cycles/rev}} = \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rpm.**
Multiply by 60 to convert from revolutions per second to revolutions per minute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence Proved.**

### Numerical Illustration (KTU-Standard Problem Type)
A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 8-pole synchronous motor is supplied. Calculate $N_s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = \frac{6000}{8} = 750 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the supply frequency is varied (variable frequency drive, VFD) to 40 Hz:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \times 40}{8} = 600 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This demonstrates the core VFD principle: **speed is strictly proportional to frequency** in a synchronous machine.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — EMF Equation of a Synchronous Machine (Per Phase, RMS)

> **Statement:** Derive the EMF induced per phase in a 3-phase synchronous machine winding.

**Step 1 — Average EMF in a single conductor.**
A single conductor cuts the rotating air-gap flux. The flux cut per revolution = $P \phi$ (since there are $P$ poles and $\phi$ Wb per pole). The time for one revolution is $60/N_s$ seconds. Hence the average rate of change of flux linkage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{avg} &= \frac{P \phi}{60/N_s} = \frac{P \phi N_s}{60} \text{ Volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute synchronous speed expression.**
Substitute $N_s = 120 f / P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{avg} &= \frac{P \phi}{60} \times \frac{120 f}{P} = 2 f \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert average to RMS (form factor).**
For a sinusoidal flux distribution, the form factor $K_f = \text{RMS}/\text{Average} = 1.11$ (the standard sine-wave value $\pi/2\sqrt{2}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{rms,\,per\,cond} &= 1.11 \times 2 f \phi = 2.22 \, f \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by turns per phase $T_{ph}$.**
For a full-pitch, concentrated winding of $T_{ph}$ turns per phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph,\,concentrated} &= 2.22 \, f \phi \, T_{ph}
\end{aligned}
$$
