**Step 5 — Introduce winding factor $K_w$ for practical windings.**
In practice, the winding is short-pitched (chorded) and distributed across multiple slots per pole per phase. Both effects reduce the induced EMF, captured by the winding factor $K_w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w \quad \text{Volts (RMS, per phase)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $4.44 = 2 \times 1.11 \times 2$ accounts for two sides per turn and form factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the EMF equation appears in KTU:** Usually in alternator problems — "A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole alternator has 60 slots with 4 conductors/slot, single-layer, full-pitch winding. Flux per pole = 0.05 Wb. Find the EMF per phase."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power-Angle Equation $P_d = (3 V E_f / X_s) \sin\delta$

> **Statement:** For a cylindrical-rotor synchronous machine, derive the power-angle relationship.

**Step 1 — Phasor diagram construction.**
Let $V$ = terminal voltage (reference phasor). Let $E_f$ = excitation EMF (internal EMF induced by rotor field), lagging $V$ by the load angle $\delta$. The synchronous reactance $X_s$ drops the voltage $j I_a X_s$ perpendicular to $I_a$.

**Step 2 — Voltage equation from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.**
For a synchronous motor (current entering):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_f + I_a (R_a + j X_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

Neglecting armature resistance $R_a$ (standard KTU assumption for cylindrical rotor analysis):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &\approx E_f + j I_a X_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= \frac{V - E_f}{j X_s} = \frac{V \angle 0^\circ - E_f \angle -\delta}{j X_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $E_f$ in rectangular form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= E_f (\cos\delta - j \sin\delta) \\
V - E_f &= (V - E_f \cos\delta) + j E_f \sin\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute complex power $S = 3 V I_a^*$.**
The real power transferred across the air gap (developed power) is the real part of $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 3 \cdot \text{Re}\left[V \cdot I_a^*\right] = \frac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **power-angle equation** — one of the most important equations in synchronous machine theory.

**Step 6 — Derive the torque equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{P_d}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The maximum (pull-out) torque occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$:
> $$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s}$$
> This is a direct favourite for "calculate maximum torque" sub-parts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Salient-Pole vs Cylindrical-Rotor

| Feature | Salient-Pole Rotor | Cylindrical (Non-Salient) Rotor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Air gap | Non-uniform (small d-axis, large q-axis) | Uniform |
| Rotor shape | Projecting poles on yoke | Smooth cylindrical forging |
| Field winding | Concentrated on pole bodies | Distributed in rotor slots |
| Damper winding | Always present | May or may not be present |
| Mechanical strength | Moderate | Very high (centrifugal safe) |
| Operating speed | Low–Medium (≤1500 rpm) | High (1500–3000 rpm) |
| Typical prime mover | Water turbine | Steam/Gas turbine |
| EMF waveform | Approximately sinusoidal | Sinusoidal |
| Synchronous reactance | Two-reaction model ($X_d, X_q$) | Single $X_s$ |
| Construction cost | Higher (assembly) | Lower (machined forging) |
| Rotational inertia (GD²) | High | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics
