# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 3, KTU University Exam Pattern) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting? List any two methods used to start it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A synchronous motor is **not self-starting** because, at standstill, the rotating magnetic field produced by the 3-phase stator winding alternates rapidly (at supply frequency) past the stationary rotor poles. Due to the high inertia of the rotor, it cannot accelerate from rest to follow the rotating field. The average starting torque over one complete cycle is theoretically **zero**.

**Two methods of starting** (any two, 1 mark each):

1. **Damper winding (amortisseur winding) starting:** Copper or brass bars are embedded in the rotor pole faces and short-circuited at the ends. On applying 3-phase AC to the stator, the rotor accelerates by induction-motor action (like a squirrel-cage motor) up to near-synchronous speed. Then DC excitation is applied and the rotor pulls into synchronism.

2. **Pony motor (auxiliary motor) starting:** A small auxiliary motor (DC shunt or induction) mechanically coupled to the synchronous motor shaft drives the rotor up to synchronous speed. DC excitation is then switched on, and the pony motor is disconnected.

*(Alternative acceptable methods: variable frequency starting, slip-ring induction motor starting, reduced-voltage starting.)*

### Question 2 (Module 3, KTU University Exam Pattern) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define synchronous speed. A 3-phase, 6-pole synchronous motor is supplied from a 50 Hz, 3-phase AC source. Calculate its synchronous speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Synchronous speed is the speed of the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator winding, given by $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$. It is the **only speed at which a synchronous motor rotor can run in steady state** (synchronous operation), independent of the mechanical load.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}
$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3 Carry-Over]`

**(a)** Explain with neat phasor diagrams the working of a synchronous motor when **(i) under-excited** and **(ii) over-excited**. Comment on the power factor in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor has a synchronous reactance of $X_s = 5$ $\Omega$/phase (Y-connected) and negligible armature resistance. The excitation EMF per phase is $E_f = 280$ V. The motor draws an armature current of 30 A at a load angle of $\delta = 30°$ (mechanical). Calculate: **(i)** the synchronous speed, **(ii)** the power factor, and **(iii)** the developed torque. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Working of synchronous motor — under-excited and over-excited (7 marks):**

The per-phase voltage equation of a synchronous motor is:

$$
\vec{V_t} = \vec{E_f} + \vec{I_a} R_a + j \vec{I_a} X_s
$$

Assuming $R_a \approx 0$ (typical for KTU problems unless otherwise stated):

$$
\vec{V_t} \approx \vec{E_f} + j \vec{I_a} X_s
$$

**Case (i) — Under-excited ($E_f < V_t$):**

Taking $V_t$ as reference along the +x axis and $E_f$ lagging by $\delta$:

The current $I_a$ must satisfy $j I_a X_s = V_t - E_f$, with $I_a$ perpendicular to $X_s$ drop. The resulting phasor shows $I_a$ **lagging** $V_t$ → **lagging power factor**. The motor draws reactive power from the supply (acts as inductor).

**Case (ii) — Over-excited ($E_f > V_t$):**

With $E_f$ now larger, the construction of the phasor diagram shows $I_a$ **leading** $V_t$ → **leading power factor**. The motor **delivers reactive power** to the supply (acts as capacitor / synchronous condenser).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** By varying DC excitation, a synchronous motor can operate at lagging, unity, or leading power factor at the same mechanical load. This dual capability is its unique advantage for **power-factor correction** in industrial plants.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{L} = 400$ V (line), Y-connected → $V_t = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94$ V/phase; $P = 4$; $f = 50$ Hz; $X_s = 5$ $\Omega$/phase; $E_f = 280$ V/phase; $I_a = 30$ A; $\delta = 30°$ (electrical, since $P = 4$, electrical $\delta$ = mechanical $\delta$ for this pole count — assume electrical).

**(i) Synchronous speed (2 marks):**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

Angular synchronous speed:

$$
\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Power factor (2 marks):**

From the phasor relation, using the law of cosines on the voltage triangle:

$$
V_t^2 = E_f^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 - 2 E_f (I_a X_s) \cos(90° + \delta)
$$

$$
V_t^2 = E_f^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 + 2 E_f (I_a X_s) \sin\delta
$$

Wait — for the **motor convention** with $E_f$ lagging $V_t$ by $\delta$ and $R_a = 0$:

$$
V_t = \sqrt{E_f^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 + 2 E_f (I_a X_s) \sin\delta}
$$

Substituting:

$$
I_a X_s = 30 \times 5 = 150 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_t = \sqrt{280^2 + 150^2 + 2 \times 280 \times 150 \times \sin 30°}
$$

$$
V_t = \sqrt{78400 + 22500 + 2 \times 280 \times 150 \times 0.5}
$$

$$
V_t = \sqrt{78400 + 22500 + 42000} = \sqrt{142900} = 378.02 \text{ V/phase}
$$

Hmm, this exceeds the given 230.94 V. The given 30 A is likely **not** consistent with $E_f = 280$ V and the line voltage. The correct KTU approach: **use the given values directly** with the power formula.

**Revised approach using mechanical power (2 marks):**

For $R_a = 0$, developed (air-gap) power per phase × 3:

$$
P_d = 3 \cdot \dfrac{V_t E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

$$
P_d = 3 \times \dfrac{230.94 \times 280}{5} \times \sin 30°
$$

$$
P_d = 3 \times 46.188 \times 280 \times 0.5
$$

$$
P_d = 3 \times 6466.32 = 19398.96 \text{ W} \approx 19.4 \text{ kW}
$$

Now compute the apparent power input to determine p.f.:

$$
S_{\text{in}} = 3 V_t I_a = 3 \times 230.94 \times 30 = 20784.6 \text{ VA} \approx 20.78 \text{ kVA}
$$

Assuming armature current corresponds to this input:

$$
\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_d}{S_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{19398.96}{20784.6} = 0.9334
$$

Since the motor must deliver mechanical power $P_d$ and this is the input real power (neglecting stator copper loss as $R_a = 0$):

$$
\boxed{\text{Power factor} \approx 0.933 \text{ lagging}}
$$

*(Note: Whether leading or lagging depends on whether $E_f$ lags or leads the result of the phasor construction. With $E_f$ lagging $V_t$ by $\delta$ in the under-excited case, $\cos\phi$ is lagging. With the given numerical magnitudes here, the construction yields a lagging p.f.)*

**(iii) Developed torque (3 marks):**

$$
T_d = \dfrac{P_d}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{19398.96}{157.08} = 123.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** confuse electrical load angle with mechanical load angle. For a $P$-pole machine, **mechanical $\delta$ = electrical $\delta \times (2/P)$**. For $P = 4$, mechanical $\delta$ = electrical $\delta / 2$. Read the question carefully!
> - **Do not** forget to convert line voltage to phase voltage for Y-connected machines.
> - **Do not** forget to convert rpm to rad/s before dividing power by $\omega_s$.
> - **Common error:** Using $P_d = 3 V_t I_a \cos\phi$ without first finding $\phi$ — this creates circular dependency. Use $P_d = 3 V_t E_f \sin\delta / X_s$ instead.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a 3-phase synchronous motor. Derive an expression for the **synchronous speed** and explain why the motor is **not self-starting**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with diagrams any **two methods of starting** a synchronous motor. Compare their starting current, starting torque, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Principle of operation, synchronous speed derivation, and non-self-starting nature (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):** When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is applied to the distributed 3-phase stator winding, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** of constant magnitude is produced. This RMF rotates at a speed determined by the supply frequency and number of poles. The rotor is separately excited by a DC source through slip rings, producing a constant-magnitude magnetic field. When the rotor is brought up to the synchronous speed (by an auxiliary means), the rotor poles lock with the stator field poles of opposite polarity, producing a steady unidirectional torque. The rotor then continues to run at synchronous speed, with the angle between rotor and stator field axes (the **load angle $\delta$**) varying with load.

**Derivation of synchronous speed (2 marks):**

For a $P$-pole machine, the rotating field completes $\dfrac{P}{2}$ pole-pairs per mechanical revolution, and one electrical cycle corresponds to $\dfrac{2}{P}$ mechanical revolutions:

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \text{ rpm}
$$

**Why not self-starting (2 marks):** At standstill, the rotor is at rest while the stator RMF rotates at $N_s$. The relative velocity between the two fields is high (alternating at supply frequency $f$). The instantaneous torque alternates rapidly between motoring and braking values, with **average torque over a cycle = 0**. The rotor inertia prevents any net acceleration, hence external assistance is mandatory.

**(b) Two starting methods with comparison (7 marks):**

**Method 1: Damper Winding (Asynchronous) Starting (3.5 marks):**

- Copper/brass bars are embedded in slots cut into the rotor pole faces and short-circuued at both ends by end rings, forming a squirrel-cage-like structure.
- When 3-phase AC is applied to the stator, the rotating field cuts these shorted bars. By **induction-motor action**, torque is produced and the rotor accelerates toward $N_s$.
- Near synchronous speed, DC excitation is applied to the rotor field winding. The rotor pulls into synchronism due to the synchronizing torque.
- **Starting current:** 5–7 × full load current.
- **Starting torque:** ~50–100% of full load torque.
- **Application:** Most common method for standard industrial synchronous motors.

**Method 2: Pony Motor Starting (3.5 marks):**

- A small auxiliary motor (DC shunt motor or small induction motor) is mechanically coupled to the synchronous motor shaft.
- The pony motor brings the rotor up to (or slightly above) synchronous speed.
- DC excitation is then applied to the rotor. The rotor synchronizes with the stator field.
- The pony motor is then disconnected (or for DC pony, the field is weakened to lose its driving capability).
- **Starting current:** Practically **zero** from the synchronous motor stator (the auxiliary motor draws the starting current).
- **Starting torque:** Smooth, controlled, and free from transients.
- **Application:** Large synchronous motors, alternators in power stations, where inrush current from the main supply is unacceptable.

**Comparison table (embedded in the 7 marks):**

| Feature | Damper Winding | Pony Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary equipment | Damper bars on rotor | Separate small motor |
| Starting current (from main) | 5–7 × FLC | ~0 |
| Starting torque | 0.5–1.0 × FLT | Smooth, controllable |
| Cost | Low (built-in) | Higher (extra motor) |
| Use | General industry | Large machines / power station auxiliaries |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (for Question B):**
> - **Always** draw a labelled phasor diagram or cross-section sketch wherever the question says "with diagrams." Sketches carry 1–2 marks.
> - **Numerical errors** in the synchronous speed derivation cost 1 mark; the relation $N_s = 120 f / P$ must be derived, not just stated.
> - **Avoid vague phrases** like "starting methods are used" — name the specific method and its principle.
> - Students often confuse the role of **damper winding** with the role of the **rotor field winding**. The damper winding is a **starting aid**; once synchronized, it carries no current (or only that induced by hunting oscillations).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Synchronous Motor Principles & Starting**

- ✅ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — speed of stator RMF, in rpm.
- ✅ **Slip at synchronism:** $s = 0$; rotor runs at exactly $N_s$ in steady state.
- ✅ **Rotor is DC-excited** through slip rings; stator is 3-phase AC excited.
- ✅ **EMF equation:** $E_f = 4.44 f \phi T_{ph} K_w$ V/phase.
- ✅ **Developed power:** $P_d = 3 V_t E_f \sin\delta / X_s$ (with $R_a = 0$).
- ✅ **Torque equation:** $T_d = T_{max} \sin\delta$, with $T_{max} = 3 V_t E_f / (\omega_s X_s)$.
- ✅ **Pull-out torque** at $\delta = 90°$; operation must remain in $0° < \delta < 90°$ for stability.
- ✅ **Power factor is controllable** by varying DC excitation — lagging when under-excited, leading when over-excited.
- ✅ **Synchronous motor is NOT self-starting** because the average starting torque is zero due to rotor inertia and rapid alternation of the stator RMF.
- ✅ **Five starting methods** (must know at least 3 with diagrams):
  1. Pony motor (auxiliary motor)
  2. Damper / amortisseur winding (most common, DOL)
  3. Slip-ring induction motor type starting
  4. Variable frequency (VFD) starting
  5. Reduced voltage (autotransformer / star-delta) starting
- ✅ **Synchronous condenser:** An over-excited synchronous motor running on no-load, used purely for **reactive power compensation** (power-factor correction).
- ✅ **V-curves:** Plot of $I_a$ vs $E_f$ for constant load — U-shaped, minimum at unity p.f.
- ✅ **Hunting:** Periodic oscillation of rotor about its mean position due to sudden load changes; suppressed by damper windings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU exam survival mantra:**
> *"Synchronous = locked, not self-starting, controllable power factor, runs at $120f/P$, started with damper or pony, V-curves and inverted-V-curve (Λ-curve) describe its excitation behavior."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Operation of Stepper Motors:**
> A stepper motor is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a series of electrical pulses applied to its stator windings into discrete angular displacements (steps) of its rotor shaft. The rotor rotates in exact, fixed angular increments (steps) for each input pulse, making it an **open-loop** positioning device widely used in digital control systems and industrial automation.

In the context of **Electric Drives and Controls for Automation**, the "Principle of Operation" refers to the fundamental electromagnetic mechanism by which a stepper motor produces **discrete mechanical motion** in response to **sequentially energized stator phases**, without requiring feedback sensors (open-loop control). The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically expects students to understand:

- The role of **magnetic reluctance torque** in variable reluctance (VR) stepper motors.
- The role of **permanent magnet excitation** in permanent magnet (PM) stepper motors.
- The combined **hybrid principle** in hybrid stepper motors.
- The relationship between **stator phase excitation sequence** and **rotor step angle**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Compass and the Electromagnet:**
> Imagine a compass needle (rotor) placed inside a room whose walls contain several electromagnets (stator poles). When you switch ON a particular electromagnet, the compass needle instantly aligns itself to point toward that magnet. Now, if you switch OFF the first magnet and switch ON the next magnet in sequence (say, to the right), the needle "jumps" to point to the new magnet. By continuously cycling the ON-states of the magnets in a circular sequence, the needle rotates in **discrete jumps** — each jump being one *step*.

This is exactly how a stepper motor works:

- **Stator** = The room with electromagnets.
- **Rotor** = The compass needle (or a toothed iron piece / permanent magnet).
- **Pulse sequence** = Switching the electromagnets ON/OFF one after another.
- **One pulse = One step** = A fixed angular movement (e.g., **1.8°** or **0.9°**).

**Key distinguishing feature compared to a normal DC/AC motor:**

| Feature | Conventional Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation | Continuous | Discrete steps |
| Feedback needed | Often yes (closed-loop) | No (open-loop) |
| Position accuracy | Limited by sensors | Defined by step angle |
| Torque at standstill | Zero (DC) | Holding torque |

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Step angle** $\theta_s$ — typically **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.9°** (400 steps/rev) for hybrid motors.
- **Number of phases** — commonly **2-phase** or **5-phase**.
- **Supply voltage** — typically **5 V DC to 48 V DC** for small/medium motors.
- **Step accuracy** — typically **±3% to ±5%** of one step (non-cumulative).
- **Holding torque** — torque required to deflect the rotor by one full step angle from its rest position.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis: Students should clearly distinguish between **reluctance torque** (no permanent magnet) and **permanent magnet torque** (PM present). This distinction carries direct marks in conceptual questions.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physics Behind Stepper Motor Operation

The principle of operation of any stepper motor rests on **two fundamental electromagnetic laws**:

1. **Law of Minimum Reluctance:** Magnetic flux always prefers to flow through the path of *lowest magnetic reluctance*. The rotor moves to minimize the reluctance of the magnetic circuit.

2. **Torque Generation Law:** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL \sin\theta$, and the corresponding torque aligns the magnetic axes of stator and rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept — Why does the rotor move?**
> The rotor moves to align itself to a position where the **total magnetic reluctance** of the flux path is **minimum**, OR where the **permanent magnet flux** is most strongly **reinforced** by the stator field. This creates a *restoring torque* (reluctance torque) or an *alignment torque* (PM torque) that locks the rotor to a stable equilibrium position.

### 2.2 Three Operating Principles (KTU Syllabus Coverage)

#### A. Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor — *Reluctance Torque Principle*

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Salient-pole structure with concentrated windings (commonly 8 poles in a 4-phase configuration).
- **Rotor:** Made of soft iron, with **salient teeth** (typically 50 teeth) and **no permanent magnet**.

**Principle:**
When phase A is energized, the nearest rotor tooth pair aligns with the energized stator poles (minimum reluctance). When phase A is de-energized and phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by **one step angle** $\theta_s$ so that a new pair of rotor teeth aligns with phase B poles.

**Step angle formula:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}
$$

Where:
- $N_r$ = Number of rotor teeth
- $m$ = Number of stator phases

**Direction of rotation** depends on the **order in which phases are energized**:
- $A \to B \to C \to D$ → Clockwise
- $A \to D \to C \to B$ → Counter-clockwise

#### B. Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor — *Magnetic Alignment Principle*

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Salient poles with windings.
- **Rotor:** Axially or radially magnetized **permanent magnet** (cylindrical rotor with alternating N-S poles).

**Principle:**
When a stator phase is energized, it creates a magnetic field of definite polarity (N or S). The PM rotor aligns itself such that its **N pole faces the S pole of the stator** and vice versa, producing *permanent magnet alignment torque*. Stepping is achieved by sequentially reversing the polarity of stator excitation.

**Step angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \cdot N_p}
$$

Where $N_p$ = number of pole pairs on the rotor.

Typical values: **7.5°, 15°, 30°, 45°** (much coarser than VR or hybrid types).

#### C. Hybrid Stepper Motor — *Combined Principle*

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Multi-toothed structure (similar to VR motor).
- **Rotor:** Two rotor cups (north and south cups), each with **50 teeth**, separated by a permanent magnet.

**Principle:**
The PM magnetizes one cup as N and the other as S. The toothed structure on the rotor creates a **small angular offset** between the two cups (typically ½ tooth pitch), which produces a combined reluctance and PM torque. This delivers:
- **Small step angles** (0.9° to 3.6°)
- **High holding torque**
- **Excellent damping**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Step angle of VR stepper motor | degrees |
| 2 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{2 \cdot N_p}$ | Step angle of PM stepper motor | degrees |
| 3 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Step angle of hybrid stepper motor (effective) | degrees |
| 4 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Total steps per revolution | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{N \cdot \theta_s}{360°}$ | Pulse frequency for rotation speed $N$ (rev/s) | pulses/sec (Hz) |
| 6 | $T \propto \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Reluctance torque proportional to inductance gradient | N·m |
| 7 | $\tau_{elec} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant of phase winding | seconds |
| 8 | $\theta_m = N_p \cdot \theta_s$ | Mechanical rotation for $N_p$ input pulses | degrees |
| 9 | $T_h = k \cdot I^2$ | Holding torque (proportional to $I^2$) | N·m |
| 10 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi f_{step} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_s}{360°}$ | Mechanical output power | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The principle of operation of stepper motors is the foundation of **CNC machines, 3D printers, robotic arms, XY plotters, disk drives, and pick-and-place machines** — all of which require precise open-loop positioning. The relationship $f_{step} = N \cdot \theta_s / 360°$ is **critical** for KTU numerical problems on motor speed selection.

### 2.4 Stepping Modes (Directly tied to Principle of Operation)

| Mode | Sequence | Pulses per cycle | Torque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave (one-phase-on)** | A → B → C → D | 1 phase energized | Low |
| **Full-step (two-phase-on)** | AB → BC → CD → DA | 2 phases energized | High |
| **Half-step** | A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA | 1 or 2 phases | Medium, smoother |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle of a Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-stack, 12-pole variable reluctance stepper motor has **30 rotor teeth** per stack. The stator is wound with a 3-phase winding. Calculate:
1. The full-step angle.
2. The total number of steps per revolution.
3. The pulse frequency required to rotate the shaft at **240 rpm**.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{poles,\,stator} &= 12 \\
N_{teeth,\,rotor} &= 30 \\
m &= 3 \;\text{(number of phases)} \\
N_{speed} &= 240 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Apply the VR Stepper Step Angle Formula**

The standard formula for a VR stepper motor is:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}
$$

But this assumes the rotor moves by **one tooth pitch** for each phase change. In a multi-stack VR motor, the rotor has $N_r$ teeth per stack, and with $m$ phases, the tooth pitch divided by $m$ gives the step angle:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}
$$

Substituting $m = 3$ and $N_r = 30$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s &= \frac{360°}{3 \times 30} \\
&= \frac{360°}{90} \\
\theta_s &= 4° \;\text{per step}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** Each input pulse advances the rotor by **4°**.

---

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Steps per Revolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{steps} &= \frac{360°}{\theta_s} \\
&= \frac{360°}{4°} \\
N_{steps} &= 90 \;\text{steps/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Calculate the Required Pulse Frequency for 240 rpm**

First, convert the speed to revolutions per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{240 \;\text{rev/min}}{60 \;\text{s/min}} \\
&= 4 \;\text{rev/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Number of pulses per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{step} &= N \times N_{steps} \\
&= 4 \;\text{rev/s} \times 90 \;\text{steps/rev} \\
f_{step} &= 360 \;\text{Hz (pulses per second)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** The controller must output **360 pulses per second** to drive the motor at 240 rpm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Holding Torque and Inductance Gradient Relation

**Theoretical Foundation:**
The instantaneous electromagnetic torque developed in a VR stepper motor phase is given by:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

Where:
- $i$ = Phase current (A)
- $L(\theta)$ = Phase inductance as a function of rotor position $\theta$
- $dL/d\theta$ = Rate of change of inductance with rotor angle

**Derivation of Holding Torque:**

At the **aligned position** (rotor tooth exactly under stator pole), the inductance is **maximum** ($L_{max}$), and $dL/d\theta = 0$. Hence, **holding torque at exact alignment is zero** for a single phase — this is the *unstable* equilibrium.

The **maximum holding torque** occurs at the **detent position**, halfway between two aligned positions, where $dL/d\theta$ is maximum.

If the inductance varies sinusoidally:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(2\theta)
$$

Then:

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -2L_1 \sin(2\theta)
$$

Maximum gradient occurs at $\theta = 45°$:

$$
\left\vert \frac{dL}{d\theta} \right\vert_{max} = 2L_1
$$

So the **maximum holding torque** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{h,\,max} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot I^2 \cdot 2L_1 \\
T_{h,\,max} &= I^2 \cdot L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the KTU high-yield formula entry: **$T_h \propto I^2$** (quadratic dependence on current).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 Exam-Worthy)

| Parameter | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor material** | Soft iron, toothed | Permanent magnet (axially/radially magnetized) | Toothed iron + PM |
| **Source of torque** | Reluctance variation (variable air-gap) | Permanent magnet alignment | Combined reluctance + PM |
| **Step angle range** | 1.8° – 15° | 7.5° – 90° | 0.9° – 3.6° |
| **Holding torque** | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| **Detent torque (no excitation)** | Zero | Present | Present |
| **Power consumption (un-energized)** | Zero | Small (PM field) | Small |
| **Step accuracy** | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Typical applications** | Industrial automation, valves | Clocks, low-cost printers | CNC, robotics, 3D printers |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Step Resolution in Half-Step Mode

For a 2-phase, 200-step/rev (1.8°/step) hybrid stepper:

- **Full-step resolution** = 1.8° (one tooth pitch moved per pulse)
- **Half-step resolution** = 0.9° (achieved by alternating 1-phase-on and 2-phase-on states)

Derivation: In a hybrid stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth, one full step = $\theta_s = 360°/N_r$. When alternating excitation is used, the effective step becomes:

$$
\theta_{s,\,half} = \frac{\theta_s}{2} = \frac{180°}{N_r}
$$

For $N_r = 50$:

$$
\theta_{s,\,half} = \frac{180°}{50} = 3.6° \;\text{(full step)} \quad \text{and} \quad 1.8° \;\text{(half step)}
$$

This matches the standard 1.8° half-step resolution of common NEMA 17 hybrid stepper motors.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Working Sequence of a 4-Phase VR Stepper

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CTL as Controller
    participant DRV as Driver Circuit
    participant STA as Stator Phases
    participant ROT as Rotor (Toothed Iron)

    Note over CTL,ROT: Initial State: All phases OFF, rotor at rest

    CTL->>DRV: Pulse 1: Energize Phase A
    DRV->>STA: Current in Phase A winding
    STA->>ROT: Rotor tooth 1 aligns with Phase A poles
    Note over ROT: Step 1: Rotor moved 0° (rest position)

    CTL->>DRV: Pulse 2: Energize Phase B (A OFF)
    DRV->>STA: Current in Phase B winding
    STA->>ROT: Rotor tooth moves to align with Phase B
    Note over ROT: Step 2: Rotor advanced by θs

    CTL->>DRV: Pulse 3: Energize Phase C (B OFF)
    STA->>ROT: Rotor advances by θs
    Note over ROT: Step 3: Rotor advanced by 2θs

    CTL->>DRV: Pulse 4: Energize Phase D (C OFF)
    STA->>ROT: Rotor advances by θs
    Note over ROT: Step 4: Rotor advanced by 3θs

    CTL->>DRV: Pulse 5: Back to Phase A (D OFF)
    STA->>ROT: Cycle continues, rotor turns
    Note over ROT: Continuous stepping achieved
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Explain the principle of operation of a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor with a neat diagram. State the step angle formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A variable reluctance stepper motor consists of a **stator with salient poles** wound with phase windings and a **rotor made of soft iron with salient teeth** but no permanent magnet.

**Principle of Operation:**
The rotor always aligns itself to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** with respect to the energized stator phase. When phase A is energized, the nearest pair of rotor teeth aligns with phase A poles (lowest reluctance path). When phase A is switched OFF and phase B is energized, the rotor moves by **one step angle** $\theta_s$ so that another tooth pair aligns with phase B. Sequential switching of phases causes the rotor to advance in discrete steps.

**Step angle formula:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}
$$

where $m$ = number of phases and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.

**(1 mark – Diagram, 1 mark – Principle, 1 mark – Formula)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**

**Differentiate between the principle of operation of a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor and a Hybrid stepper motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Alignment torque from **permanent magnet rotor** with stator field | Combined **reluctance torque + PM alignment torque** |
| **Rotor** | Axially magnetized cylindrical PM | Two toothed iron cups + axially magnetized PM |
| **Step angle** | Coarse (7.5° to 90°) | Fine (0.9° to 3.6°) |
| **Detent torque** | Present (PM always exerts torque) | Present |
| **Holding torque** | Medium | High |
| **Applications** | Printers, clocks, low-cost drives | CNC, robotics, 3D printers |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a hybrid stepper motor**. Discuss the role of the permanent magnet and the toothed rotor structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-phase, 200-step/revolution hybrid stepper motor is driven by a pulse train of **1.2 kHz**. Calculate:
   1. The step angle.
   2. The shaft speed in **rpm**.
   3. The number of pulses required to rotate the shaft through **54°**.
   4. The time taken for this rotation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A hybrid stepper motor combines the high-resolution feature of a VR motor with the holding-torque feature of a PM motor.

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Multi-toothed with concentrated windings (typically 8 or 16 poles in 2 phases).
- **Rotor:** Two soft-iron cups (North and South), each with $N_r$ teeth (commonly 50), separated by a cylindrical **permanent magnet** magnetizing one cup as N and the other as S. The two cups are mechanically offset by **½ tooth pitch**.

**Principle of Operation:**

When a stator phase is energized, it produces a magnetic field that interacts with both the PM flux and the variable air-gap reluctance. The rotor moves to a position that simultaneously:
1. **Minimizes magnetic reluctance** of the stator flux path (reluctance torque).
2. **Reinforces the PM alignment** between the N cup and the South stator pole, and S cup with the North stator pole (alignment torque).

Sequential excitation of phases causes the rotor to advance in fine steps (typically 0.9° or 1.8°).

**Role of the Permanent Magnet:**
- Provides the **holding torque** even when stator is de-energized (detent torque).
- Polarizes the two rotor cups in opposite directions, doubling the effective number of torque-producing interactions per step.

**Role of the Toothed Structure:**
- Creates the **small angular offset** between the two cups, allowing finer stepping.
- Produces **variable air-gap reluctance** — essential for the reluctance torque component.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.4 for the logical cross-section schematic)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 4$ phases
- $N_{steps} = 200$ steps/revolution
- $f_{step} = 1.2 \;\text{kHz} = 1200 \;\text{Hz}$

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s &= \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} \\
&= \frac{360°}{200} \\
\theta_s &= 1.8° \;\text{per step}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shaft speed in rpm:**

Number of revolutions per second:

$$
N = \frac{f_{step}}{N_{steps}} = \frac{1200}{200} = 6 \;\text{rev/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N_{rpm} = 6 \times 60 = 360 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**(iii) Pulses required for 54° rotation:**

$$
N_{pulses} = \frac{54°}{\theta_s} = \frac{54°}{1.8°} = 30 \;\text{pulses}
$$

**(iv) Time taken for 54° rotation:**

$$
t = \frac{N_{pulses}}{f_{step}} = \frac{30}{1200} = 0.025 \;\text{s} = 25 \;\text{ms}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice — Alternative]* [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the **construction and principle of operation of a permanent magnet (PM) stepper motor**. Derive the step angle formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-phase, 4-pole PM stepper motor is required to drive a load at **120 rpm**. Calculate:
   1. The step angle.
   2. The required pulse frequency.
   3. The number of pulses needed to produce a shaft rotation of **180°**.
   4. Comment on the suitability of this motor for a **CNC feed drive** application. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction of a PM Stepper Motor:**

- **Stator:** Comprises salient poles (usually 4, 6, or 8) on which the phase windings are wound. When a phase is energized, the poles become magnetized with definite polarities (alternating N and S).
- **Rotor:** A **cylindrical permanent magnet**, magnetized radially or axially, with alternating N and S poles around its circumference. The rotor does not have teeth (unlike VR and hybrid types).

**Principle of Operation:**

When a stator phase is energized, the energized poles develop definite magnetic polarities (say, N-S-N-S around the stator). The PM rotor aligns such that its **N poles face the S poles of the stator** and its **S poles face the N poles of the stator**, producing a **permanent magnet alignment torque**.

When the phase excitation is reversed (current direction reversed), the stator polarities reverse, and the rotor rotates by one step to a new alignment position. By sequentially energizing the phases in a particular order, the rotor can be made to rotate in **discrete angular steps**.

**Step Angle Derivation:**

For a PM stepper motor with $N_p$ pole pairs on the rotor:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \cdot N_p}
$$

> *Derivation logic:* One full mechanical revolution = 360° = $2N_p$ magnetic pole-pitches (each N-S pair). Each step moves the rotor by one pole-pitch. Hence $\theta_s = 360°/(2N_p)$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 2$ phases
- Number of poles $P = 4$ (hence pole pairs $N_p = 2$)
- Desired speed $N_{rpm} = 120$ rpm

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \cdot N_p} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 2} = \frac{360°}{4} = 90° \;\text{per step}
$$

**(ii) Required pulse frequency:**

Convert speed to rev/s:

$$
N = \frac{120}{60} = 2 \;\text{rev/s}
$$

Total steps per revolution:

$$
N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{90°} = 4 \;\text{steps/rev}
$$

Required pulse frequency:

$$
f_{step} = N \times N_{steps} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**(iii) Pulses for 180° rotation:**

$$
N_{pulses} = \frac{180°}{90°} = 2 \;\text{pulses}
$$

**(iv) Suitability for CNC feed drive:**

A 2-phase, 4-pole PM stepper motor with a **90° step angle** is **NOT suitable** for a CNC feed drive application. Reasons:
- **Step angle is too coarse** — CNC applications typically require fine step angles (0.9° to 1.8°) for smooth motion and high positional accuracy.
- **PM type has detent torque and lower resolution** — modern CNC systems use **hybrid stepper motors** or **servo motors** for superior performance.
- **Limited to 4 steps/rev** — cannot achieve the smooth interpolation required for complex tool paths.

A more suitable choice would be a **2-phase, 200-step/rev (1.8°) hybrid stepper motor**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing step angle formulas:** Many students incorrectly use the VR stepper formula for PM motors or vice versa. **Remember:**
>    - VR: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$
>    - PM: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{2 N_p}$
>    - Hybrid: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_{r,\,effective}}$ (depends on tooth count and excitation sequence)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** before computing pulse frequency. This is the #1 reason for a wrong numerical answer in KTU valuation.
>
> 3. **Drawing incorrect diagrams:** Stator and rotor must be clearly distinguished with proper labels (e.g., "Stator Phase A", "Rotor tooth 1"). **Unlabeled diagrams fetch zero marks** in KTU 2024 scheme.
>
> 4. **Confusing "phases" with "poles":** A 4-phase motor does **not** necessarily have 4 poles. State the number of poles and phases separately in derivations.
>
> 5. **Missing the role of the permanent magnet** in hybrid motors — students often explain only the reluctance torque and skip the PM torque component, losing up to 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Sign errors in step angle derivation:** A step angle of 90° (PM, 4-pole) vs. 45° (PM, 8-pole) — verify using the formula before writing the final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Principle of Operation**

- ✅ A **stepper motor** is a **brushless, synchronous** motor that moves in **discrete steps** controlled by digital pulses.
- ✅ **Three types:** VR, PM, and Hybrid — each based on a different operating principle.
- ✅ **VR stepper:** Rotor has **no magnet**; torque from **variable reluctance** (minimization of magnetic path reluctance).
- ✅ **PM stepper:** Rotor is a **permanent magnet**; torque from **magnetic alignment** between stator and rotor poles.
- ✅ **Hybrid stepper:** Combines **PM + toothed rotor**; produces **both reluctance and PM torque**; finest step angles.
- ✅ **VR step angle formula:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$
- ✅ **PM step angle formula:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{2 N_p}$
- ✅ **Direction of rotation** is determined by the **sequence of phase energization** (forward vs. reverse order).
- ✅ **Stepping modes:** Wave (1-phase-on), Full-step (2-phase-on), and Half-step (alternating) — half-step gives **double resolution**.
- ✅ **Pulse frequency** $f_{step} = N_{rev/s} \times N_{steps/rev}$ — convert rpm to rev/s first.
- ✅ **Holding torque** is proportional to **$I^2$** (quadratic dependence on phase current).
- ✅ **Detent torque** is the residual torque in PM/hybrid motors when stator is de-energized; absent in VR motors.
- ✅ **Open-loop operation:** No feedback sensor required — pulse count directly gives shaft position.
- ✅ **Key applications:** CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics, plotters, conveyor control, valve actuators, disk drives.
- ✅ **KTU exam focus areas:** Step angle derivation, distinguishing operating principles, pulse frequency calculation, suitability analysis for automation applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable reluctance motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Variable reluctance motors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variable Reluctance Motor (VRM):** A salient-pole synchronous electric machine in which **rotor torque is produced purely by the tendency of a ferromagnetic rotor to align with a magnetic field such that the magnetic circuit's reluctance is minimized**. The stator carries concentrated phase windings while the rotor is a passive, magnet-less, winding-less laminated steel structure. There are three recognized members of this family, all sharing the same fundamental physics but differing in excitation and control: | Sub-Family | Excitation Source | Operation Mode | |---|---|---| | **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)** | DC pulses fed sequentially | Open-loop step-by-step rotation | | **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** | DC link inverter, closed-loop | Continuous-speed industrial drive | | **Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynR)** | Sinusoidal stator supply | Synchronous AC drive (vector controlled) | In the KTU 2024 scheme, **SRM and VRSM** are emphasized in this module, as they are the true "switched" reluctance drives controlled by power-electronic converters. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Filing Seeker" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine two iron bars separated by an air gap in a magnetic field produced by an electromagnet. The iron bars always try to **slide together to shorten the air gap**, because that path offers the least magnetic "resistance" (reluctance). The rotor of a VRM behaves exactly like that pair of iron bars — it physically *chases* the position where its iron teeth line up with the energized stator teeth, because that is the path of minimum reluctance. **No magnets are needed**; the *steel itself* is the actuator of motion. ### 1.3 Physical Construction Highlights - **Stator**: Salient poles (projecting teeth) carrying concentrated coils, typically 6, 8, or 12 poles for industrial SRMs. - **Rotor**: A simple stack of laminated silicon-steel salient poles — **no windings, no magnets, no cage, no brushes**. - **Power converter**: An asymmetric H-bridge per phase because torque direction depends on **current magnitude only** (not polarity). - **Rotor Position Sensor (RPS)**: An optical or Hall-effect encoder is **mandatory** because the converter must know rotor angle to energize the right phase. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constant/Metric:** The **co-energy derivative relation** is the heart of all VRM analysis — the torque at any instant is: > > $$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i=\text{const}} = \frac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ > > This **quadratic dependence on current** and **independence from current polarity** are the two most-tested facts in KTU examinations. ### 1.4 Why a VRM is Inherently Salient Any machine that produces reluctance torque **must** have a *non-uniform* air gap. If the air gap were perfectly cylindrical, $L(\theta)$ would be constant, $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}=0$, and no torque could exist. Therefore: > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule:** The number of *rotor poles* $N_r$ and *stator poles* $N_s$ must satisfy $N_s \neq N_r$ and $N_r \neq N_s/2$ for stable rotation. Typical combinations: $8/6$, $6/4$, $12/8$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physics of Torque Production — Stepwise Logic 1. **Apply DC excitation to a stator phase.** Flux $\Phi$ crosses the air gap and returns through the rotor body. 2. **The flux path reluctance is minimum** when a rotor pole is exactly aligned under the energized stator pole (called the *aligned position*, $\theta_a$). 3. **It is maximum** when the rotor pole is between two stator poles (*unaligned position*, $\theta_u$). 4. As the rotor rotates, the air-gap permeance — and hence the phase inductance — **varies periodically** with rotor angle. 5. If a current $i$ flows through a phase whose inductance is *increasing* with $\theta$, the magnetic co-energy grows; the system minimizes co-energy by rotating the rotor toward higher $L$. 6. The instantaneous torque is the partial derivative of co-energy w.r.t. angle at constant current — leading to the canonical torque formula. ### 2.2 Co-Energy Based Derivation (Compact Form) For a **linear** magnetic system (no saturation), flux linkage $\lambda = L(\theta)\,i$ and: $$W_m = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta)\,i'\,di' = \frac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^2$$ Because the magnetic system is conservative, co-energy equals stored energy: $$W'_{co} = W_m = \frac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^2$$ Torque in the direction of increasing $\theta$: $$\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,i^2\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight — Why polarity doesn't matter:** > Since $T_e \propto i^2$ (and $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ carries the sign of the *torque direction*), the rotor always turns to *increase* inductance regardless of whether current is positive or negative. **This is why an asymmetric (unipolar) H-bridge is sufficient** for each SRM phase — you never need to reverse current to reverse torque direction; you simply move the excitation to a different phase. ### 2.3 Inductance Profile — Idealized Model A standard idealized inductance profile for one phase (ignoring mutual coupling) is: $$L(\theta) = L_{min} + \frac{L_{max}-L_{min}}{2}\Big(1+\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)\big)\Big)$$ where: - $L_{min}$ = inductance at the **unaligned** rotor position - $L_{max}$ = inductance at the **aligned** rotor position - $N_r$ = number of rotor poles - $\theta_a$ = aligned rotor angle for this phase The derivative needed for torque: $$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -\frac{N_r(L_{max}-L_{min})}{2}\sin\!\big(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)\big)$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity / Equation | Symbol | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Co-energy in linear magnetics | $W'_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2}L(\theta)i^2$ | Joules | Assumes unsaturated iron | | 2 | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2}i^{2}\,\dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | N·m | The **master equation** of VRM | | 3 | Idealized inductance profile | $L(\theta)=L_{min}+\tfrac{\Delta L}{2}\bigl(1+\cos(N_r(\theta-\theta_a))\bigr)$ | H | $\Delta L = L_{max}-L_{min}$ | | 4 | Derivative of inductance | $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = -\tfrac{N_r\Delta L}{2}\sin(N_r(\theta-\theta_a))$ | H/rad | Used in torque computation | | 5 | Mechanical speed relation | $\omega_m = \dfrac{\omega_e}{N_r/2}$ for steppers | rad/s | $\omega_e$ is electrical frequency | | 6 | Step angle of VRSM | $\alpha_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_s\cdot m}$ | degrees/step | $m$ = phases | | 7 | Average torque approximation | $T_{avg}\approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,i_{rms}^{2}\,(L_{max}-L_{min})\,\dfrac{N_r}{\theta_{cyc}}$ | N·m | Useful for design | | 8 | Stored magnetic energy ratio | $\dfrac{L_{max}}{L_{min}} \approx 6\text{–}10$ | — | Typical SRM design | | 9 | Pull-out torque (stepper) | $T_{po} = \tfrac{1}{2}i^{2}\,\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}\big\vert_{mid}$ | N·m | At mid-step position | | 10 | Output power | $P_{out} = T_{avg}\cdot\omega_m$ | Watts | Useful mechanical power | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Inside the table above, all absolute-value-like bars have been written using the explicit `\tfrac`, `\dfrac`, and `\big\vert` LaTeX commands to **never collide with the markdown pipe `|` symbol** that delimits table cells. Always follow this convention in KTU answer sheets when rendering tables in markdown. ### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Washing machines** and **dishwashers**: Use 4-phase, 8/6 SRM direct drives for high efficiency (>85%) and silent operation. - **Vacuum cleaners & cordless power tools**: Use 2-phase miniature VRM for high speed (>100,000 rpm). - **Electric vehicles (EVs)**: Some traction drives (e.g., Nidec/Tesla SR concept) leverage SRM for **fault tolerance** — a short-circuit in one phase does not propagate because each phase is magnetically and physically independent. - **Aerospace actuators**: SRMs are favoured because they can operate in extreme temperatures and survive single-phase failures. - **Industrial servo-replacement drives**: Modern SRMs with closed-loop control now compete with PMSMs in variable-speed applications due to the absence of rare-earth magnets (cost advantage). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Fundamental Torque Equation **Goal:** Starting from first principles (energy in a magnetic field), derive $T_e=\tfrac{1}{2}i^2\,\dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$. **Step 1 — Define the system energy.** The total electrical input energy into a single phase winding (from $t=0$ to $t$) is: $$W_e = \int_{0}^{t} v\cdot i\,dt$$ Using $v = \dfrac{d\lambda}{dt}$, we get: $$W_e = \int_{0}^{t} i\,\frac{d\lambda}{dt}\,dt = \int_{0}^{\lambda} i\,d\lambda$$ **Step 2 — Express energy in terms of flux linkage and current.** For a *linear* magnetics system (no saturation, no hysteresis), flux linkage and current are linearly related: $$\lambda(\theta,i) = L(\theta)\cdot i$$ Therefore: $$W_e = \int_{0}^{\lambda} i\,d\lambda = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta)\,i'\,di' = \frac{1}{2}L(\theta)\,i^2$$ **Step 3 — Identify stored magnetic energy.** In a lossless (ideal) magnetic system, all electrical input energy becomes stored magnetic field energy: $$W_f = W_e = \frac{1}{2}L(\theta)\,i^2$$ **Step 4 — Define co-energy.** The magnetic **co-energy** $W'_{co}$ is defined as the complementary function: $$W'_{co}(i,\theta) = i\lambda - W_f$$ For the linear case: $$W'_{co} = i\,L(\theta)i - \frac{1}{2}L(\theta)i^{2} = \frac{1}{2}L(\theta)\,i^{2} = W_f$$ So in the linear case, **stored energy equals co-energy**. **Step 5 — Apply the principle of virtual work.** The electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular displacement, holding current constant (because torque must oppose the source's reaction): $$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i=\text{const}}$$ Substituting $W'_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2}L(\theta)i^{2}$: $$\boxed{T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}L(\theta)\,i^{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$ **Step 6 — Sign convention check.** The minus sign convention is defined such that positive $T_e$ produces rotation that *increases* $\theta$. Since $L(\theta)$ is a maximum at the aligned position, $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ is negative when moving *away* from alignment and positive when moving *toward* it. Hence, with current flowing during the rising-inductance region, **torque is always in the direction that pulls the rotor toward alignment** — the classical VRM principle. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Step Angle (Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor) **Goal:** Prove that for a $N_s$-stator-pole, $N_r$-rotor-pole VR stepper excited by sequential phase pulses, the step angle is $\alpha_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m\cdot q}$ where $m$ is the number of phases and $q = \text{lcm}(N_s, N_r)$ logical increment. For a **standard $m$-phase, two-pole-per-phase VRSM**, in one full electrical cycle the rotor advances by one rotor-pitch: $$\text{Rotor pitch} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$ There are $m$ switching events per electrical cycle (one per phase pulse), and each event advances the rotor by $\tfrac{1}{m}$ of the rotor pitch: $$\alpha_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m\cdot N_r}$$ **Example:** For an 8/6 SRM ($N_s=8$, $N_r=6$, $m=4$): $$\alpha_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{4 \times 6} = 15^{\circ}$$ This is the most commonly quoted textbook number for a hybrid VR stepper, and is a guaranteed KTU problem. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Average Torque for a Single Phase Pulse **Goal:** Compute the average torque over one excitation pulse from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$, assuming constant current $I$ (flat-topped pulse). **Step 1 — Instantaneous torque:** $$T_e(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\,I^{2}\,\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -\frac{1}{2}\,I^{2}\,\frac{N_r\Delta L}{2}\sin\!\big(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)\big)$$ **Step 2 — Integrate over the conduction interval $[\theta_1,\theta_2]$:** $$W_{pulse} = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} T_e(\theta)\,d\theta = -\frac{1}{4}\,I^{2}\,N_r\Delta L \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \sin\!\big(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)\big)\,d\theta$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$\int \sin(N_r\theta)\,d\theta = -\frac{\cos(N_r\theta)}{N_r}$$ Therefore: $$W_{pulse} = -\frac{1}{4}\,I^{2}\,N_r\Delta L \left[-\frac{\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_2-\theta_a)\big)-\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_1-\theta_a)\big)}{N_r}\right]$$ $$\boxed{W_{pulse} = \frac{1}{4}\,I^{2}\,\Delta L\Big[\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_2-\theta_a)\big)-\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_1-\theta_a)\big)\Big]}$$ **Step 4 — Average torque = energy per pulse divided by electrical angle swept:** $$T_{avg} = \frac{W_{pulse}}{\theta_2-\theta_1} = \frac{I^{2}\Delta L}{4(\theta_2-\theta_1)}\Big[\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_2-\theta_a)\big)-\cos\!\big(N_r(\theta_1-\theta_a)\big)\Big]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design takeaway:** To maximize average torque, the current pulse must be centred on the **rising-inductance region** where $dL/d\theta > 0$ (i.e., before alignment). This is precisely why the rotor-position sensor in an SRM fires the *turn-on* angle $\theta_{on}$ slightly before alignment and the *turn-off* angle $\theta_{off}$ slightly after the peak — a critical concept for KTU Part-B problems. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Style Numerical:** A 3-phase, 6/4 SRM has $L_{max} = 60$ mH, $L_{min} = 10$ mH, and operates with a constant phase current of $I=10$ A. The rotor is at the mid-inductance-rise position where $\theta = \theta_a - 15^{\circ}$. Compute the instantaneous torque. **Given:** - $N_r = 4$, $N_s = 6$, $\Delta L = L_{max}-L_{min} = 60-10 = 50$ mH $= 0.05$ H - $I = 10$ A - At mid-rise, $N_r(\theta-\theta_a) = -60^{\circ}$ → $\sin(-60^{\circ}) = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ **Solution:** $$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -\frac{N_r\Delta L}{2}\sin(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)) = -\frac{4 \times 0.05}{2}\times\sin(-60^{\circ})$$ $$= -0.1 \times \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = +0.0866 \text{ H/rad}$$ $$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,I^{2}\,\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2}\times(10)^{2}\times 0.0866$$ $$\boxed{T_e \approx 4.33\ \text{N·m}}$$ (Positive ⇒ torque is in direction of rotation, as expected.) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Policy:** Physical cross-sections of salient-pole motors are notoriously difficult to render cleanly in Mermaid (overlapping arrows, rotor/stator lamination hatching). Instead, the following two figures present the **functional block architecture of a complete SRM drive** and a **phase-current vs. rotor-angle timing diagram** using labelled node-link logic and ASCII schematic — both of which are acceptable in KTU answer scripts. ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an SRM Drive System ```mermaid flowchart LR DC_BUS[(DC Link Bus
Capacitor Bank)] INV[Asymmetric H-Bridge
Power Converter] PHASE_A[Phase A Winding] PHASE_B[Phase B Winding] PHASE_C[Phase C Winding] ROTOR[Salient Rotor
8/6 Laminated Steel] RPS[Rotor Position Sensor
Optical Encoder or Hall] CTRL[Digital Controller
DSP or Microcontroller] REF[Speed / Torque
Reference Input] LOGIC[Commutation
Lookup Table] GATE[Gate Driver
Isolated IGBT/MOSFET] MECH[Mech Load
Coupling and Shaft] DC_BUS --- INV INV --- PHASE_A INV --- PHASE_B INV --- PHASE_C PHASE_A --- ROTOR PHASE_B --- ROTOR PHASE_C --- ROTOR ROTOR --- MECH ROTOR --- RPS RPS --> CTRL REF --> CTRL CTRL --> LOGIC LOGIC --> GATE GATE --> INV CTRL -.feedback.-> LOGIC ``` **Block-Level Reading Guide:** | Block | Function | |---|---| | `DC_BUS` | Provides a stiff DC link (typically 300–800 V) from rectifier or battery | | `INV` | Per-phase asymmetric H-bridge; switches are unipolar (one direction current) | | `PHASE_A/B/C` | Concentrated stator coils on salient poles | | `ROTOR` | Passive steel rotor with salient teeth — produces reluctance torque | | `RPS` | Provides absolute rotor angle to controller (essential for commutation) | | `CTRL` | Implements current, speed, and torque control loops (e.g., PI) | | `LOGIC` | Converts angle to firing command: turns on/off the right IGBTs | | `GATE` | Level shifts and isolates the low-voltage logic from the high-voltage DC link | --- ### 4.2 Phase Excitation Timing Diagram (ASCII Schematic) This figure depicts the **alignment of three phase currents with the rotor's inductance profile** for a 3-phase, 6/4 SRM. Read horizontally: rotor angle advances from left to right. ``` Inductance L(theta) Lmax | ____ ____ ____ | / \ / \ / \ | / \ / \ / \ Lmin |_______/ \____/ \____/ \____ +--------------------------------------------------> theta 0 60 120 180 240 300 360 420 deg A ^ B ^ C ^ Phase A current ___|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|__ turn-on turn-off Phase B current __________|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|_______ turn-on turn-off Phase C current ________________|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|__ turn-on turn-off ``` **Interpretation of the Timing Diagram:** 1. Each phase is energised **during the rising-inductance slope** — the only region where $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}>0$ and positive torque is produced. 2. The "commutation angle" $\theta_{comm}$ separates the regions of positive torque (useful work) and negative torque (regenerative braking — avoided by proper turn-off timing). 3. The overlap of phase currents is what generates a smooth *total* torque, but residual torque ripple is the principal design challenge of SRMs. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]** *(3 Marks)* **Q.** Why does the rotor of a Variable Reluctance Motor (VRM) always rotate in a direction that *increases* the phase inductance, regardless of the polarity of current flowing in the stator winding? Use the torque equation in your answer. **Model Answer:** The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in a VRM is given by: $$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ Since $T_e$ depends on $i^2$, the sign of torque is **independent of current polarity** — only the sign of $\dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ matters. The magnetic co-energy is minimised when $L$ is maximum, so the system always develops torque in the direction of *increasing* inductance, irrespective of whether $i$ is positive or negative. This is why the rotor always moves toward alignment with the excited stator pole. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students wrongly write "torque depends on $i$" and then conclude direction depends on polarity. Always emphasise $i^2$ explicitly. --- **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]** *(3 Marks)* **Q.** Mention any **four advantages** of Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM) over conventional induction motors in variable-speed drives. **Model Answer:** 1. **Rotor has no windings or magnets** → no rotor copper losses, very high efficiency, and excellent cooling (heat is on stator only). 2. **Fault tolerant** — each phase is magnetically and electrically independent; a faulted phase is isolated without disabling the motor. 3. **Wide constant-power speed range** — speed can be varied from a few rpm to over 100,000 rpm with the same converter. 4. **Simple, low-cost, rugged construction** — rotor is just a stack of laminations; no permanent magnets (no rare-earth dependency). 5. *(Optional extra)* Bidirectional operation requires only sequencing of firing, not polarity reversal. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM). Discuss the role of the rotor position sensor. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Derive, from first principles using magnetic co-energy, the expression for electromagnetic torque in a VRM: $$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Model Answer — Part (a):** *(7 Marks)* **Construction (3 Marks):** - **Stator**: $N_s$ salient poles (commonly 6 or 8) carrying concentrated single-phase windings connected in series pairs. - **Rotor**: A simple stack of $N_r$ salient poles (e.g., 4 or 6) of laminated silicon steel — **no windings, no magnets, no cage**. - **Power Converter**: An asymmetric H-bridge per phase; each phase is fed through two power switches and two freewheeling diodes. - **Rotor Position Sensor (RPS)**: An incremental encoder or Hall-effect sensor mounted on the non-drive end of the shaft. **Working (3 Marks):** 1. The controller reads the rotor angle $\theta$ from the RPS. 2. Based on a lookup table, it determines which phase should produce positive torque (i.e., whose $dL/d\theta>0$). 3. The corresponding pair of IGBTs in that phase's H-bridge is switched ON; current builds up in the winding and produces flux. 4. The rotor salient pole aligns with the energized stator pole, minimising reluctance and producing torque in the direction of rotation. 5. As alignment is approached, the controller switches OFF; the magnetic energy is returned to the DC bus through the freewheeling diodes (soft turn-off). 6. The next phase in the firing sequence is energised, and the cycle repeats. **Role of RPS (1 Mark):** The RPS is essential because the torque production depends entirely on the *correct phase being energised at the correct rotor angle*. Without precise position feedback, torque would be erratic, inefficient, or even negative. It also enables **closed-loop speed and current control**. --- #### **Model Answer — Part (b):** *(7 Marks)* **Assumptions (2 Marks):** 1. Magnetic circuit is **linear** (no saturation), so $\lambda = L(\theta)i$. 2. Magnetic system is **lossless** (no hysteresis, no eddy current). 3. Single phase considered in isolation; mutual coupling neglected. **Derivation (5 Marks):** For an isolated phase winding supplied with current $i$: - Flux linkage: $\lambda(\theta,i) = L(\theta)\,i$. - Stored magnetic energy: $$W_f = \int_{0}^{\lambda} i\,d\lambda = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta)\,i'\,di' = \frac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^{2}$$ - For a linear system, **co-energy = stored energy**: $$W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2}\,L(\theta)\,i^{2}$$ - Applying the principle of virtual work at constant current: $$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i=\text{const}} = \frac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ Hence the required expression is obtained. ∎ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **linearity assumption** — without it, $\lambda \neq Li$ and the derivation is no longer valid. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if assumptions are not stated. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** A 6/4, 3-phase SRM has $L_{max}=80$ mH and $L_{min}=15$ mH. When a constant current of $I = 8$ A flows in phase A at a rotor position $\theta$ such that $N_r(\theta-\theta_a) = -45^{\circ}$, calculate: - (i) The phase inductance $L(\theta)$ at this position. - (ii) The instantaneous electromagnetic torque $T_e$. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Compare the Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) with the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) under the heads: construction, rotor losses, efficiency at high speed, torque ripple, fault tolerance, and cost. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Model Answer — Part (a):** *(7 Marks)* **Given:** - $N_r = 4$, $L_{max}=80$ mH $=0.08$ H, $L_{min}=15$ mH $=0.015$ H - $I = 8$ A, $\Delta L = 0.080 - 0.015 = 0.065$ H - $N_r(\theta-\theta_a) = -45^{\circ}$ **(i) Phase Inductance** *(3 Marks)* $$L(\theta) = L_{min} + \frac{\Delta L}{2}\Big(1+\cos(N_r(\theta-\theta_a))\Big)$$ $$= 0.015 + \frac{0.065}{2}\Big(1+\cos(-45^{\circ})\Big)$$ $$= 0.015 + 0.0325 \times \Big(1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\Big)$$ $$= 0.015 + 0.0325 \times 1.7071$$ $$\boxed{L(\theta) \approx 0.015 + 0.0555 = 0.0705\ \text{H} = 70.5\ \text{mH}}$$ **(ii) Instantaneous Torque** *(4 Marks)* $$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -\frac{N_r\Delta L}{2}\sin(N_r(\theta-\theta_a)) = -\frac{4 \times 0.065}{2}\sin(-45^{\circ})$$ $$= -0.13 \times \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = +0.0919\ \text{H/rad}$$ $$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\,I^{2}\,\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2}\times(8)^{2}\times 0.0919$$ $$\boxed{T_e \approx 2.94\ \text{N·m}}$$ The positive sign indicates torque is in the *direction of rotation* — the rotor is being pulled toward the aligned position. --- #### **Model Answer — Part (b):** *(7 Marks)* | Head | SRM | PMSM | |---|---|---| | **Construction** | Salient stator + salient rotor (no magnets/windings) | Salient/surface rotor carrying rare-earth magnets | | **Rotor losses** | Negligible — passive steel only | Eddy-current losses in magnets, no copper loss | | **Efficiency at high speed** | Very high — robust rotor handles >100,000 rpm | Limited — magnet retention issues, eddy losses | | **Torque ripple** | High (major drawback) | Low (smooth sinusoidal back-EMF) | | **Fault tolerance** | Excellent (phase-independent, short-circuit safe) | Poor (short-circuit in winding can demagnetise magnets) | | **Cost** | Low (no rare-earth magnets) | High (NdFeB magnet dependency) | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (memorise before entering the exam hall):** - ✅ Master equation: $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2}i^2\,\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ — derive it cold. - ✅ $T_e$ depends on $i^2$, not $i$ → polarity **does not** set torque direction. - ✅ Co-energy = $\tfrac{1}{2}L(\theta)i^2$ under the linear magnetics assumption. - ✅ Torque is *positive* only during the **rising-inductance slope** (region where $dL/d\theta > 0$). - ✅ Step angle for a VRSM = $\dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m\cdot N_r}$ — classic formula tested every year. - ✅ 8/6, 6/4, 12/8 are the standard pole combinations. - ✅ Asymmetric H-bridge per phase — *unipolar* current is sufficient. - ✅ Rotor Position Sensor is **mandatory** — there is no sensorless startup mode in classical SRMs. - ✅ Major *advantages*: rotor simplicity, fault tolerance, no magnets, high speed. - ✅ Major *drawbacks*: torque ripple, acoustic noise, complex control. - ✅ Applications: washing machines, EVs, aerospace, cordless tools. - ✅ Inductance ratio $L_{max}/L_{min}\approx 6\text{–}10$ for a well-designed SRM. - ✅ Energy conversion per stroke $W_{pulse} = \tfrac{1}{4}I^2\Delta L\big[\cos(N_r\theta_2)-\cos(N_r\theta_1)\big]$.

topics.title: Hybrid stepper motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid stepper motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Hybrid Stepper Motors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical actuator that converts a train of discrete digital input pulses into precise, discrete angular shaft displacements (steps). It is termed "hybrid" because its operational principle and construction actively merge the **high torque-density features of the Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** with the **fine angular resolution and detent (cogging) torque characteristics of the Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):** A hybrid stepper motor is a salient-pole, multi-toothed machine in which the rotor carries a permanent magnet (typically axially magnetized rare-earth material such as NdFeB) sandwiched between two toothed soft-iron end-caps. The stator possesses a multi-tooth structure with concentrated phase windings. Stepping action is achieved by sequential excitation of stator phases, with torque produced by the **simultaneous interaction of permanent-magnet excitation AND variable-reluctance alignment of rotor and stator teeth**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Compass on a Ratchet Wheel":**
> Imagine a magnetic compass needle (representing the rotor's permanent magnet) mounted in the middle of a toothed gear. Around it, imagine two crown-like toothed wheels (the stator poles). When you twist the outer crown clockwise by one tooth, the inner magnet-and-gear tries to align its nearest tooth to the stator tooth that is now closer — producing an audible, precise "click". Now, by reversing the magnetic polarity of the compass mid-step, the gear rotates the *opposite* way. Every pulse of current creates one such magnetic "click", and each click is exactly one step. Thousands of clicks per second = smooth rotation. This is essentially what a hybrid stepper does — it is the **most precise, repeatable, open-loop incremental actuator ever mass-produced**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard step angles:** **0.9°, 1.8°** (most common), 3.6°, 7.5°
- **Standard supply voltages:** **5 V DC, 12 V DC, 24 V DC, 48 V DC**
- **Phase count:** **2-phase** (bipolar drive) or **5-phase** (high-resolution variants)
- **Holding torque range:** **0.1 N·m to 50 N·m** (industrial NEMA 17, 23, 34 frame sizes)
- **Detent torque (unenergized):** Typically **1–5 %** of holding torque (very low → smooth microstepping)
- **Rotor material:** NdFeB (rare-earth), SmCo, or Ferrite axial-magnetized disc
- **Stator core:** High-grade silicon steel laminations (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm thickness)

### 1.4 Why Hybrid? — The Design Justification

| Feature | PM Stepper | VR Stepper | **Hybrid Stepper** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detent torque at zero current | High | None | **Low (1–5 %)** |
| Holding torque | High | Low | **Very High** |
| Step resolution (per revolution) | Low (48–100 steps) | High (200 steps) | **Very High (200–400 steps typical, up to 50,000 with microstepping)** |
| Power consumption (holding) | High | None | **Low (current decay controllable)** |
| Rotor inertia | Medium | Low | **Low (slim disc magnet)** |
| Industrial adoption | Low | Low | **Dominant (>70 % market)** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** Hybrid = **H**olding torque of PM + **R**esolution of VR + **I**ntegrated toothed structure + **D**igital pulse compatibility + **I**nherent detent + **D**amping of rotor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Constructional Breakdown

The hybrid stepper is built from **three concentric sub-assemblies**:

1. **Stator Assembly (Outer)**
   - Comprises a stack of silicon-steel laminations forming a **multi-toothed cylindrical core**.
   - Carries **two (or five) phase windings**, typically bifilar-wound or bipolar-wound.
   - Each stator pole has **multiple small teeth** (usually 5, 8, or 10) on its inner face.

2. **Rotor Assembly (Inner) — Two End-Caps + Axial Magnet**
   - Two identical **toothed soft-iron end-caps** are mounted on a common shaft, separated axially by a **cylindrical permanent magnet**.
   - The magnet is **axially magnetized** (N on one end-cap side, S on the other).
   - Therefore, the two end-caps carry **opposite magnetic polarities** — one is a magnetic North pole, the other a South pole, from the magnet's perspective.
   - The end-cap teeth are **mechanically offset by ½ tooth-pitch** (one-half tooth pitch) between the two ends.
   - For a 2-phase stator, the rotor typically has **50 teeth per end-cap** (NEMA 17 standard: 50 teeth → 1.8° step).

3. **Bearing & Housing Assembly**
   - Provides axial preload via wave spring to maintain end-cap alignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical design insight:** The rotor end-caps are **rotationally displaced by exactly half a tooth-pitch** relative to each other. This mechanical offset is the **single most important geometric parameter** that gives the hybrid stepper its high detent-torque-free fine resolution.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Two-Phase ON Excitation Sequence

Assume the stator has **two phases (A and A')** wound on diametrically opposite pole pairs. Energization follows the sequence: **A ON → B ON → A OFF → B OFF → A ON (reverse)** etc. The actual magnetic action proceeds as follows:

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Phase A energized (positive polarity):**
   - Stator pole pair 1 becomes N–S (say, N on left pole tooth, S on right pole tooth).
   - The rotor's North-polarity end-cap tooth aligns with the South stator pole (because N–S attraction), and the rotor's South-polarity end-cap tooth aligns with the North stator pole.
   - All teeth on the rotor's N-end and S-end simultaneously lock into stator pole teeth (with a ½-pitch phase shift between the two ends due to the offset).

2. **Phase B energized (Phase A still ON — *two-phase ON mode*):**
   - The energized B phase adds its own N–S poles 90° electrical offset from A.
   - The resultant stator field vector rotates by **one full step-angle θ_s**.
   - The rotor follows the resultant vector and locks at the new position.

3. **Phase A de-energized (Phase B only):**
   - Rotor locks into Phase B's preferred teeth configuration.

4. **Phase B reverse-polarity:**
   - The stator field rotates by another θ_s, and the rotor follows.

5. **Sequence repeats**, generating a complete revolution in $N_s$ steps where $N_s$ is the **steps-per-revolution**.

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundation — Step Angle Derivation Logic

Let:
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth on **one end-cap** (typically 50)
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth under one pole (typically 5)
- The full tooth pitch of the rotor is:

$$\tau = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

Because the two end-caps are offset by half a tooth-pitch, **one full excitation cycle causes the rotor to advance by exactly one rotor tooth pitch**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r}}$$

For an $m$-phase stator, the electrical cycle advances the field by $360°/m$ per step, but the rotor advances only one tooth pitch $\tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This formula is the basis for **selecting a hybrid stepper for CNC, 3D-printer, and robotics applications**. A NEMA 17 with 50 rotor teeth gives 200 steps/revolution (1.8°/step) — the de-facto industry standard.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Standard Value / Unit | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$ | Step angle | degrees per step | $N_r$ = rotor teeth on one end-cap |
| 2 | $N_{\text{steps/rev}} = N_r \cdot m$ | Steps per revolution (full-step) | dimensionless | $m$ = number of stator phases |
| 3 | $\theta_e = m \cdot \theta_s$ | Electrical angle per step | degrees (electrical) | Also $= 360°/N_r \cdot m$ |
| 4 | $N_r = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Rotor teeth from step angle | dimensionless | Inverse of (1) |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{\pi D_r}{N_r}$ | Tooth pitch on rotor surface | metres (m) | $D_r$ = rotor diameter |
| 6 | $T_h = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Holding torque | N·m | $K_t$ = torque constant, $I_a$ = phase current |
| 7 | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{V}{L \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dt}}$ | Maximum pull-out frequency | Hz | $V$ = supply, $L$ = phase inductance |
| 8 | $N_{\text{micro}} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s \cdot \mu}$ | Microstepping resolution | steps/rev | $\mu$ = microstep divisor (e.g., 1/16 → $\mu = 16$) |
| 9 | $T(\theta) = -T_h \sin\!\left(\dfrac{N_r \cdot \theta}{2}\right)$ | Static torque-position curve | N·m | $\theta$ = angular displacement from detent |
| 10 | $P_{\text{mech}} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | Watts | $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s) |
| 11 | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta_s \cdot f_p}{2\pi}$ | Shaft speed from pulse rate | rad/s | $f_p$ = pulse frequency (Hz) |
| 12 | $K_t = \dfrac{T_h}{I_a}$ | Torque constant | N·m / A | Equals back-EMF constant $K_e$ in SI units |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units pitfall:** In SI, $K_t$ (N·m/A) numerically equals $K_e$ (V·s/rad). Always verify units in exam problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC machines:** X/Y/Z axis feed drives in low-to-medium power mills.
- **3D printers:** Extruder and bed positioning (NEMA 17, 1.8°/step).
- **Textile machinery:** Cam-less loom shedding mechanisms.
- **Disk drives & robotics:** Head positioning, pick-and-place.
- **Medical devices:** Syringe pumps, blood analyzers (precision dosing).
- **Aerospace (small actuators):** Valve positioning, antenna pointing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Step-Angle Formula

We begin from **first principles of geometry and electromagnetic alignment**.

### Derivation — Step Angle of a 2-Phase Hybrid Stepper

**Given:**
- Rotor has $N_r$ teeth on each end-cap.
- Two rotor end-caps are mechanically displaced by **½ tooth pitch** ($\tau/2$).
- Stator has $m$ phases (for our case, $m = 2$).
- Each excitation sequence advances the stator field by $360°/m$ electrical.

**Step 1 — Geometric definition of one rotor tooth pitch:**

When the rotor rotates by one full tooth pitch $\tau$, every tooth on the rotor is replaced by its neighbour at the same stator location. The angular advance of the shaft corresponding to one tooth pitch is:

$$\theta_{\text{tooth}} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of the ½-pitch offset between the two end-caps:**

Because end-cap 1 has a tooth exactly where end-cap 2 has a slot (and vice-versa), one electrical phase change produces a net rotor advance equal to the **shift that simultaneously minimizes reluctance on BOTH ends**. The combined optimization resolves to a rotation of exactly one rotor tooth pitch per full electrical switching cycle.

$$\theta_s = \theta_{\text{tooth}} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 3 — Convert degrees to radians (for use in torque equations):**

$$\theta_s \text{ [rad]} = \frac{2\pi}{N_r}$$

**Step 4 — Compute steps per revolution for an $m$-phase machine:**

The rotor needs to traverse $N_r$ tooth pitches to complete one mechanical revolution. Per excitation cycle the rotor moves by one tooth pitch. The number of steps to complete $360°$ is therefore:

$$N_{\text{steps}} = N_r$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: For a 2-phase machine, $N_{\text{steps}} = N_r$ (NOT $N_r \cdot m$). This is because the ½-pitch offset already factors in the phase count geometrically. For a 5-phase machine, each electrical step is $\tau/m = \tau/5$, and one full revolution takes $N_r \cdot m = N_r \cdot 5$ steps. **This is a frequently tested nuance.**

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU standard NEMA 17):**

For $N_r = 50$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50} = 7.2° \text{ per phase electrical step}$$

Wait — this is the **electrical step**. For a 2-phase full-step drive, the **mechanical step** is half this:

$$\theta_s^{\text{mech}} = \frac{7.2°}{2} = 3.6° \quad \text{(half-step mode)}$$

For **full-step, two-phase ON** drive (standard NEMA 17):

$$\theta_s^{\text{full}} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

Therefore, the **effective mechanical tooth count per revolution is $N_r^{\text{eff}} = 200$**, even though the physical rotor has only 50 teeth. The doubling occurs because the ½-pitch offset combined with 2-phase alternation creates **4 stable detent positions per tooth pitch**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 1.8°?** Because $1.8° \times 200 = 360°$. With microstepping at $\mu = 16$, you get $200 \times 16 = 3200$ microsteps/rev → $0.1125°$/microstep.
