## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Holding Torque from Magnetic Co-energy

The electromagnetic torque in a doubly-salient (stator teeth + rotor teeth) machine with PM excitation is obtained by differentiating the **magnetic co-energy** $W_{co}$ with respect to angular displacement $\theta$ at constant current and constant PM flux:

$$T(\theta) = \left.\frac{\partial W_{co}(i, \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}}$$

For a sinusoidal approximation (valid for small air-gap harmonics), the co-energy decomposes into:

$$W_{co} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2}_{\text{VR component}} + \underbrace{\psi_m(\theta) \cdot i}_{\text{PM component}}$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the position-dependent phase inductance and $\psi_m(\theta)$ is the PM flux linkage.

**Step 1 — Position-dependent inductance (VR part):**

Because the rotor has $N_r$ teeth, the air-gap permeance varies with period $\tau = 360°/N_r$:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right)$$

(The factor of 2 arises because of the ½-pitch offset between end-caps — both ends contribute to permeance variation.)

**Step 2 — PM flux linkage (PM part):**

$$\psi_m(\theta) = \psi_{m0} \sin\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the co-energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\theta) &= \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + i \frac{d\psi_m(\theta)}{d\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the derivatives:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} &= -\frac{N_r}{2} L_1 \sin\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right) \\
\frac{d\psi_m(\theta)}{d\theta} &= \frac{N_r}{2} \psi_{m0} \cos\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine both contributions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\theta) &= -\frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{N_r}{2} L_1 \sin\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right) + i \cdot \frac{N_r}{2} \psi_{m0} \cos\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right) \\
&= -\frac{N_r}{4} i^2 L_1 \sin\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right) + \frac{N_r}{2} i \, \psi_{m0} \cos\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Holding torque at the aligned detent ($\theta = 0$):**

At the detent position, $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\cos(0) = 1$. The holding torque (when current $i$ is applied to hold the rotor against an external load) simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{T_h = \frac{N_r}{2} \, \psi_{m0} \, I_a = K_t \, I_a}
$$

This is the **defining torque equation** of the hybrid stepper and is the one most frequently tested in KTU examinations.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

| Problem Type | Given Quantities | Goal | Governing Equations | Solution Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step resolution | $N_r$, $m$ | Find $\theta_s$, $N_{\text{steps/rev}}$ | $\theta_s = 360°/N_r$, $N_{\text{steps}} = N_r$ for 2-phase | Direct substitution |
| Shaft speed | $f_p$ (pulse rate), $\theta_s$ | Find $\omega$ or RPM | $\omega = \theta_s f_p / 2\pi$ | Convert, multiply |
| Holding torque | $K_t$, $I_a$ | Find $T_h$ | $T_h = K_t I_a$ | Direct multiplication |
| Microstep resolution | $\theta_s$, $\mu$ | Find $\theta_{\text{micro}}$ | $\theta_{\text{micro}} = \theta_s / \mu$ | Division |
| Position after N pulses | $N$ pulses, $\theta_s$ | Find total angle | $\theta_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \theta_s$ | Multiplication |
| Required rotor teeth | Target step angle $\theta_s$ | Find $N_r$ | $N_r = 360° / \theta_s$ | Division |
| Tooth pitch | $D_r$ (rotor diameter), $N_r$ | Find $\tau$ | $\tau = \pi D_r / N_r$ | Direct substitution |
| Pull-out torque curve | Speed vs torque graph | Find safe operating region | Manufacturer datasheet | Graphical |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Exploded Cross-Sectional View of a 2-Phase Hybrid Stepper Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Stator Laminations Stack"] --> B["Phase A Winding North Pole Teeth"]
    A2["Stator Laminations Stack"] --> B2["Phase A Winding South Pole Teeth"]
    A3["Stator Laminations Stack"] --> C["Phase B Winding North Pole Teeth"]
    A4["Stator Laminations Stack"] --> C2["Phase B Winding South Pole Teeth"]
    B --> D["Air Gap to Rotor"]
    B2 --> D
    C --> D
    C2 --> D
    D --> E["Rotor End Cap North 50 Teeth Front Section"]
    E --> F["Axially Magnetised Permanent Magnet NdFeB Disc"]
    F --> G["Rotor End Cap South 50 Teeth Back Section Offset by Half Tooth Pitch"]
    G --> H["Shaft Common Mechanical Axis"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Ball Bearings Front and Rear"]
    I --> J["External Mechanical Load Coupling"]
    K["Bipolar Drive Electronics H Bridge"] -.energises.-> B
    K -.energises.-> C
    L["Microstepping Controller Pulse Train Input"] -.drives.-> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Two-Phase Excitation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Pulse Generator Step and Direction"] --> Q["Translator Ring Counter Logic"]
    Q --> R["Current Chopper Bipolar Chopper Driver"]
    R --> S["Phase A Winding"]
    R --> T["Phase B Winding"]
    S --> U["Stator Magnetic Field Vector"]
    T --> U
    U --> V["Rotor Tooth Alignment to Minimum Reluctance"]
    V --> W["Half Tooth Pitch Offset Torque Boost"]
    W --> X["Output Shaft Incremental Rotation Step"]
    Y["DC Bus Supply 12V to 48V"] --> R
    Z["Feedback Encoder Optional Closed Loop"] -.monitors.-> X
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Excitation Sequence State Diagram (Full-Step Two-Phase ON)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1["Step 1 Phase A Positive Phase B Zero"] --> State2["Step 2 Phase A Positive Phase B Positive"]
    State2 --> State3["Step 3 Phase A Zero Phase B Positive"]
    State3 --> State4["Step 4 Phase A Negative Phase B Positive"]
    State4 --> State5["Step 5 Phase A Negative Phase B Zero"]
    State5 --> State6["Step 6 Phase A Negative Phase B Negative"]
    State6 --> State7["Step 7 Phase A Zero Phase B Negative"]
    State7 --> State8["Step 8 Phase A Positive Phase B Negative"]
    State8 --> State1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Torque vs Speed Characteristic (Pull-Out Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Low Speed Region 0 to 1000 Steps per Second"] --> B["Holding Torque Region Flat Plateau"]
    B --> C["Mid Speed Region 1000 to 5000 Steps per Second"]
    C --> D["Torque Roll Off Due to Back EMF and Winding Inductance"]
    D --> E["Pull Out Torque Curve Concave Downward"]
    E --> F["Maximum Slew Frequency Pull Out Limit"]
    F --> G["No Torque Available Region Above Pull Out"]
    H["Continuous Duty Envelope"] -.within.-> E
    I["Start Stop Region without Ramp Profile"] -.requires.-> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the KTU exam:** When asked to "draw the cross-section", always label the **PM magnet location**, the **two end-caps with offset arrows**, the **stator teeth count per pole**, and the **winding polarities**. Examiner reward: +2 marks for clear labelling.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor and a Hybrid stepper motor in terms of construction and torque production mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor construction | Cylindrical magnet with smooth surface | Magnet sandwiched between two **toothed** end-caps |
| Stator construction | Smooth-bore salient poles | **Multi-toothed** salient poles |
| Number of rotor poles | Equal to step count × 2 | Equal to **number of rotor teeth** (typically 50) |
| Torque production mechanism | **Only PM torque** (interaction of stator field with rotor magnet) | **Combined PM + Variable Reluctance (VR)** torque |
| Step angle range | Larger (e.g., 7.5°, 15°) | Much smaller (e.g., 0.9°, 1.8°) |
| Holding torque | Moderate | **Highest** among all stepper types |
| Detent torque | Significant | Very low (1–5 %) |

> **Valuation Tip:** Drawing the rotor shape (smooth vs toothed) earns 1 mark. Writing "both PM and VR torque contribute" earns 2 marks.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: Why are the two end-caps of a hybrid stepper rotor offset by exactly half a tooth pitch? What is the consequence of not doing so?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Why offset by ½ tooth pitch (2 marks):**
   - The two rotor end-caps carry **opposite magnetic polarities** (N and S).
   - The ½-pitch offset ensures that **when a stator tooth aligns with a North-end rotor tooth, it simultaneously aligns with a slot (not tooth) of the South-end rotor cap**, and vice-versa.
   - This produces a **strong, unidirectional torque** on the rotor from both ends simultaneously, rather than cancelling.
   - It also reduces **cogging (detent) torque** because the reluctance peaks of the two ends are phase-shifted by 180°, partially cancelling the air-gap harmonic content.
   - The offset enables **fine step resolution** — one electrical cycle advances the rotor by exactly **one tooth pitch**.

2. **Consequence of no offset (1 mark):**
   - Net electromagnetic torque on the rotor averages to **near zero**, because the N-end and S-end forces would be in opposite rotational directions at every rotor angle.
   - The motor would either refuse to step or rotate with extremely weak, lumpy motion and very high cogging torque.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in torque equation:** Forgetting the negative sign in $T(\theta) = -\frac{N_r}{4} i^2 L_1 \sin(\cdot)$ — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing electrical vs mechanical step angle:** In 2-phase machines, $\theta_e = 2\theta_s$ (electrical angle = twice mechanical step angle). Mixing these gives **wrong shaft RPM** and loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units of $K_t$:** Writing $K_t$ in N·m without per-ampere loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not labelling the MAF diagram:** Examiner allocates 2 marks purely for labels — never submit a blank diagram.
> 5. **Treating hybrid as identical to VR:** Wastes 3 marks because the PM-torque contribution term $i \cdot d\psi_m/d\theta$ is completely missed.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Internal Choice Option A]`

**A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has a rotor with 50 teeth on each end-cap. The end-caps are offset by half a tooth pitch. The stator has 8 poles, each pole having 5 teeth.**

**(a)** Derive the step angle and find the total number of steps per revolution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the shaft speed in RPM if the motor is driven at 2000 pulses/second in full-step mode. If the motor has a torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A and is supplied with a phase current of 2 A, determine the developed holding torque. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities:**

- $N_r = 50$ (rotor teeth per end-cap)
- $m = 2$ (number of phases)
- Stator pole teeth = 5 (not directly required for step-angle formula, but required for torque quality)

**Step 2 — Apply the step-angle formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s &= \frac{360°}{N_r} \quad \text{(mechanical step angle)} \\
&= \frac{360°}{50} \\
&= 7.2° \text{ per electrical cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For a 2-phase full-step drive, mechanical step is half the electrical:**

Wait — careful. Let us re-derive rigorously using the ½-pitch offset logic:

The rotor has 50 teeth → 50 tooth pitches around the circumference → each pitch = 7.2°. With 2-phase bipolar excitation (4 switching states per electrical cycle: +A, +A+B, +B, -A+B, -A, -A-B, -B, +A-B — i.e., 8 states or 4 full steps depending on convention), the **effective mechanical advance per pulse** is:

$$\theta_s^{\text{mech}} = \frac{7.2°}{4} = 1.8°$$

Therefore, **steps per revolution**:

$$N_{\text{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the practical industrial formula:**

For a NEMA 17 standard 2-phase hybrid stepper with 50 rotor teeth, the industry-standard resolution is exactly **200 steps/rev (1.8°/step)**. ✓

> **Answer (a):** $\theta_s = 1.8°$/step, $N_{\text{steps/rev}} = 200$ steps.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert pulse frequency to angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \theta_s \cdot f_p \\
&= 1.8° \times 2000 \text{ pulses/sec} \\
&= 3600° \text{ per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to revolutions per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPS} &= \frac{3600°/\text{s}}{360°/\text{rev}} = 10 \text{ rev/s} \\
\text{RPM} &= 10 \times 60 = 600 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify using the rad/s formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \text{ [rad/s]} &= \frac{\theta_s \text{ [rad]} \cdot f_p}{1} \\
&= \frac{(1.8 \times \pi/180) \cdot 2000}{1} \\
&= 0.03142 \times 2000 = 62.83 \text{ rad/s} \\
\text{RPM} &= \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 62.83 \times 9.549 = 600 \text{ RPM} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Holding torque calculation:**

Given $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $I_a = 2$ A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_h &= K_t \cdot I_a \\
&= 0.05 \text{ N·m/A} \times 2 \text{ A} \\
&= 0.1 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** Speed = **600 RPM**, Holding torque = **0.1 N·m**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Internal Choice Option B]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional sketch, explain the construction and operating principle of a 2-phase hybrid stepper motor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has $N_r = 100$ rotor teeth on each end-cap. Determine: (i) the step angle, (ii) the number of steps per revolution in full-step mode, (iii) the microstep size when driven with 1/32 microstepping, and (iv) the electrical step angle per phase. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block for the schematic.)*

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with **8 salient poles** (for 2-phase) carrying concentrated phase windings. Each pole has multiple small teeth (typically 5). Two phases A and B are wound on alternate pole pairs.
- **Rotor:** Consists of a **permanent magnet disc** (axially magnetized) sandwiched between **two toothed soft-iron end-caps**. The end-caps are mounted on the same shaft but **offset by ½ tooth pitch** rotationally. One end-cap is magnetically North, the other South.
- **Bearings & housing:** Standard ball-bearing arrangement.

**Operating Principle (3.5 Marks):**

- When Phase A is energized with positive polarity, the stator pole pair develops N–S magnetic polarity.
- The rotor's N-end-cap teeth align with the stator S pole teeth (attraction); the rotor's S-end-cap teeth align with the stator N pole teeth (attraction).
- The ½-pitch offset ensures simultaneous alignment at both ends, maximizing torque.
- When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the stator field vector rotates by **one rotor tooth pitch**.
- The rotor follows this vector, advancing by one mechanical step $\theta_s$.
- Reversing the polarity of Phase B and repeating causes continuous rotation in the opposite direction.
- The complete 4-state sequence (A+, B+, A–, B–) constitutes one electrical cycle and advances the rotor by 4 tooth pitches.

> **Award marks:** 3.5 for labelled sketch + 3.5 for the sequence explanation with direction control.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N_r = 100$ rotor teeth per end-cap.

**(i) Step angle (mechanical):**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°/\text{tooth pitch}
$$

Wait — for full-step 2-phase mode, the **mechanical step per pulse** is:

$$\theta_s^{\text{full}} = \frac{3.6°}{2} = 1.8°$$

> **Actually for a 2-phase HSM with 100 teeth, the standard full step is:** $360°/200 = 1.8°$. ✓

**(ii) Steps per revolution (full-step mode):**

$$N_{\text{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps}$$

**(iii) Microstep size with 1/32 microstepping:**

$$\theta_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\theta_s^{\text{full}}}{\mu} = \frac{1.8°}{32} = 0.05625°/\text{microstep}$$

Total microsteps per revolution:

$$N_{\text{micro}} = 200 \times 32 = 6400 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

**(iv) Electrical step angle per phase:**

For a 2-phase machine ($m = 2$):

$$\theta_e = m \cdot \theta_s = 2 \times 1.8° = 3.6°$$

> **Answer (b):** (i) 1.8°/step, (ii) 200 steps/rev, (iii) 0.05625°/µstep (6400 µsteps/rev), (iv) 3.6° electrical/step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Re-read 24 hours before KTU exam):**

- [ ] **Hybrid stepper = PM stepper + VR stepper in one machine.** The rotor has both a permanent magnet AND toothed end-caps.
- [ ] **Rotor end-cap tooth count ($N_r$) is the key design parameter.** For NEMA 17: $N_r = 50$ → 1.8°/step; for NEMA 23: also typically 50 teeth (1.8°) but larger frame.
- [ ] **End-cap offset = ½ tooth pitch (mandatory).** Without it, the motor produces zero net torque.
- [ ] **Two-phase ON drive is the default industrial drive mode** (better torque than single-phase ON).
- [ ] **Step angle formula (2-phase full-step):** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{2 N_r}$ for mechanical step per pulse. **Simplified:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_{\text{steps}}}$.
- [ ] **Torque has TWO sources:** PM torque $\propto i \psi_{m0}$ AND VR torque $\propto i^2 L_1$ — both MUST appear in derivation answers.
- [ ] **Holding torque:** $T_h = K_t I_a$ where $K_t = (N_r/2) \psi_{m0}$.
- [ ] **Detent torque is very low** (1–5 % of $T_h$) — a major advantage over PM-only steppers.
- [ ] **Microstepping divisor $\mu$** multiplies resolution: 1/16, 1/32, 1/256 are common.
- [ ] **Shaft speed:** $\omega = \theta_s f_p$ (rad/s) or $\text{RPM} = 60 \cdot \theta_s \cdot f_p / 360°$.
- [ ] **Phase current equals torque constant times holding torque** (not the rated current directly).
- [ ] **Rotor material:** NdFeB (rare-earth) preferred over ferrite for higher energy density.
- [ ] **Standard KTU exam keywords** that MUST appear in answers: "doubly-salient", "axially magnetized", "half tooth pitch offset", "co-energy derivative", "coercivity", "detent torque", "back-EMF", "pull-out torque".
- [ ] **Examiner love-affair:** Always draw the cross-section AND write the torque equation. Doing both = guaranteed 12+ out of 14.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** $K_t$ in N·m/A, $K_e$ in V·s/rad (numerically equal in SI), $T_h$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s for power $P = T\omega$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second mnemonic — "HSM-DOTS":**
> **H**ybrid = **S**andwich (PM + toothed end-caps).
> **M**echanical offset = **½ tooth pitch**.
> **D**etent torque = **O**nly 1–5 %.
> **O**ne pulse = **1 step** = $\theta_s$ angular advance.
> **T**orque = **PM + VR** (two sources).
> **S**tandard step angle = **1.8°** (NEMA 17, $N_r = 50$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear stepper motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear stepper motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Stepper Motor (LSM):** A linear stepper motor is a electromagnetic actuator that converts a sequence of digital electrical pulses into discrete, open-loop linear displacement along a straight axis, without the need for intermediate rotary-to-linear mechanical conversion (such as lead screws, rack-and-pinion, or belt drives).

The device consists of two primary parts:
- **Forcer (Stator):** The stationary member carrying the polyphase winding and salient poles that produce a travelling magnetic field.
- **Plunger (Rotor / Slider):** The moving member that travels in a straight line, carrying either salient iron teeth (Variable Reluctance type), permanent magnets (Permanent Magnet type), or both (Hybrid type).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** The linear stepper is an open-loop positioning device — feedback sensors (encoders, LVDTs) are *not* required for normal operation, which is one of its chief industrial advantages.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Magnetic Ratchet Wrench":**
> Imagine you have a long iron bar lying on a wooden table, and above it sits a multi-pronged electromagnet. Every time you *click* an electrical switch (a single pulse), the electromagnet grabs the bar at the closest tooth, pulls it forward by exactly one tooth-width, and releases. Each "click" = one **step**. If you click 1,000 times at 500 clicks/second, the bar slides 1,000 tooth-widths at a constant 500 steps/s.
>
> - The **tooth-width** = the step size.
> - The **click-rate** = the pulse frequency.
> - The **number of teeth it skips per click** = the resolution.
> - The **synchronised clicks** across multiple electromagnets = the commutation sequence (full-step / half-step / microstepping).

This direct, gearless, no-backlash motion is *exactly* what makes linear stepper motors prized in **3D printers, CNC routers, pick-and-place machines, and laboratory automation**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard step sizes (commercial LSMs):** **10 µm, 25 µm, 50 µm, 100 µm, 254 µm (0.01 inch)**
- **Force range:** **5 N – 2,500 N** (depending on forcer length and frame size)
- **Maximum linear velocity:** typically **≤ 5 m/s** (limited by back-EMF and mechanical resonance)
- **Holding force (static stiffness):** proportional to **i²** (square of phase current)
- **Standard supply voltages:** **12 V, 24 V, 48 V DC** (bipolar chopper drives common)
- **Ambient operating range:** **0 °C to +50 °C** (industrial grade)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Linear Stepper Motors

Linear stepper motors are categorised analogously to rotary stepper motors, but adapted to linear geometry:

| **Type** | **Plunger Construction** | **Force Density** | **Step Resolution** | **Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR-LSM)** | Soft iron with salient poles; no PM | Low – Medium | Coarse (mm range) | Lowest |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM-LSM)** | Axially-magnetised PM bars | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **Hybrid Linear Stepper (H-LSM)** | Toothed iron + axially-magnetised PM | **Highest** | **Finest (µm range)** | Highest |

---

## 2.2 Construction of a Variable-Reluctance Linear Stepper Motor (VR-LSM)

The VR-LSM is the simplest and most frequently tested in KTU board exams. Its construction has two sub-assemblies:

1. **Forcer (stator):**
   - Built from laminated silicon-steel stamping stacks.
   - Contains **m pole-pairs**, each wound with a phase winding.
   - Polyphase windings are excited in a prescribed sequence.

2. **Plunger (moving part):**
   - A plain bar of soft iron (or silicon-steel laminations) with **regularly spaced salient teeth** (or a smooth bar that gives variable air-gap reluctance).
   - No permanent magnets, no windings — purely a passive, magnetically-soft member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it called "Variable Reluctance"?** Because the *reluctance* of the magnetic circuit (forcer–air-gap–plunger) *changes* as the plunger moves — minimum reluctance occurs when the teeth of the forcer are *aligned* with the teeth of the plunger, and maximum reluctance occurs when they are *misaligned*. The motor therefore tries to *minimise* stored magnetic energy, and this tendency is what produces the linear force.

---

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

The VR-LSM operates on the fundamental electromechanical principle:

> **A current-carrying coil always tries to minimise the reluctance of its magnetic path, thereby producing a force that pulls the nearest high-permeability member into alignment.**

The detailed sequence is:

1. **Phase-A energised:** Phase-A produces flux that flows through the aligned tooth of the plunger, minimising reluctance. The plunger locks at this position (holding torque equivalent = *holding force*).
2. **Phase-A switched OFF, Phase-B energised:** The reluctance minimum shifts to the Phase-B pole. The plunger is pulled by **one pole-pitch** to align with Phase-B.
3. **Phase-C energised, Phase-B OFF:** Plunger advances by another pole-pitch.
4. **Cycle repeats** in a cyclic ABCD–A… sequence.
5. **Reversal** is achieved by reversing the commutation sequence (e.g., A→D→C→B→A).

> [!NOTE]
> **The direction of motion is therefore a function of the commutation sequence**, *not* the magnitude of current. This is a *purely digital* control — ideal for microcontroller-based drives.

---

## 2.4 Step Size Derivation — The KTU-Standard Result

Let:
- $\tau_p$ = pole pitch of the plunger (distance between two consecutive plunger teeth)
- $N_{ph}$ = number of phases on the forcer

For a multi-stack or multi-tooth-per-pole forcer with $m$ pole-pairs per phase:

$$
s = \frac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}}
$$

For a **two-phase** forcer ($N_{ph} = 2$):

$$
s = \frac{\tau_p}{2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Example (KTU-style):** A VR-LSM has a plunger tooth pitch of **$\tau_p = 2.4$ mm** and a 4-phase forcer. The step size is $s = 2.4 / 4 = \mathbf{0.6\ mm}$. A pulse rate of **2,000 pulses/s** yields a linear velocity of $v = f \times s = 2000 \times 0.6\text{ mm} = \mathbf{1.2\ m/s}$.

---

## 2.5 Force and Energy Equations

The instantaneous developed force in a linear stepper is given by the co-energy principle:

$$
F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^{2} \cdot \frac{dL}{dx}
$$

where:
- $i$ = phase current (A)
- $L$ = phase inductance (H) — a function of plunger position $x$
- $\frac{dL}{dx}$ = rate of change of inductance with respect to displacement (H/m)

The co-energy stored in the magnetic field is:

$$
W_{co} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot i^{2}
$$

Therefore, the developed mechanical force is:

$$
F = \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^{2} \cdot \frac{dL}{dx}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because the force is proportional to $i^{2}$, the force is *always attractive* regardless of current polarity. This is why VR-LSMs are *inherently unipolar* and *non-reversing in force direction per phase* — direction reversal is achieved purely by phase sequencing.

---

## 2.6 Linear Velocity and Acceleration

For a stepping frequency $f$ (pulses per second) and step size $s$:

$$
v = f \cdot s \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

The maximum achievable stepping frequency is limited by:
- **Mechanical:** natural frequency of the plunger-bearing system (resonance).
- **Electrical:** $L/R$ time-constant of the phase winding (must settle within one step period).
- **Thermal:** $I^{2}R$ heating in the winding.

Maximum slew rate (no-load):

$$
f_{max} = \frac{v_{max}}{s}
$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the following table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU board problem on linear stepper motors.**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Legend** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step size (VR-LSM) | $s = \dfrac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}}$ | $\tau_p$ = pole pitch, $N_{ph}$ = phases | m |
| 2 | Step size (Hybrid LSM) | $s = \dfrac{\text{tooth pitch}}{2}$ | — | m |
| 3 | Linear velocity | $v = f \cdot s$ | $f$ = pulse frequency, $s$ = step size | m/s |
| 4 | Developed force | $F = \dfrac{1}{2} i^{2} \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dx}$ | $i$ = current, $L$ = inductance | N |
| 5 | Co-energy | $W_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} L \cdot i^{2}$ | — | J |
| 6 | Holding force | $F_h \propto i^{2}$ | Proportionality constant from geometry | N |
| 7 | Pulse-to-distance | $x = N \cdot s$ | $N$ = number of pulses | m |
| 8 | Resolution (Hybrid) | $R = \dfrac{\text{tooth pitch}}{N_{ph} \times N_{teeth/pole}}$ | — | m/step |
| 9 | Force ripple (full step) | $\Delta F \approx 15\%$ of $F_{avg}$ | (typical) | N |
| 10 | Step accuracy | $\pm 5\%$ per step, non-cumulative | open-loop | — |

> **Real-world Engineering Use:** Hybrid linear steppers with 1.8° equivalent rotary stepping (giving **5 µm – 25 µm linear steps**) are the de-facto standard in **semiconductor lithography stages, optical alignment benches, and bio-medical infusion pumps**, where sub-micron positional repeatability is mandatory.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Size of an m-Phase, Multi-Tooth Variable Reluctance LSM

**Statement:** A variable reluctance linear stepper has a forcer with **$m$ phase pole-pairs** and a plunger with regularly-spaced teeth of pitch $\tau_p$. Derive the linear step size $s$.

---

### Step 1 — Define the geometry

Let the plunger be considered as a long, passive iron bar with teeth spaced at regular intervals $\tau_p$. The forcer, when energised in a single phase, produces a localised intense magnetic field at one set of teeth (one *pole-pair* of the forcer).

The forcer moves along the plunger, but in the canonical LSM, the **plunger moves with respect to a fixed forcer**. We analyse the *relative* motion between forcer pole and plunger tooth.

### Step 2 — Establish the alignment conditions

The minimum reluctance condition (stable holding position) occurs when a forcer tooth and a plunger tooth are **fully aligned**.

When the next phase is energised (with the previous phase de-energised), the new minimum-relaxance position is **shifted by one tooth-pitch of the forcer**.

### Step 3 — Determine the forcer's effective tooth pitch

For a multi-tooth-per-pole forcer, the effective pole pitch of the *forcer's magnetic field pattern* along the direction of motion is:

$$
\tau_{forcer} = \frac{\tau_p}{m}
$$

This is because there are $m$ pole-pairs of the forcer distributed across one full plunger-tooth period $\tau_p$.

### Step 4 — Compute the step size

The plunger moves by the *distance between two consecutive minimum-relaxance positions of the same phase*, which is one full plunger-tooth pitch $\tau_p$, divided by the number of phases $N_{ph}$ (since the forcer's pattern repeats $N_{ph}$ times per plunger-tooth).

$$
\boxed{\,s = \dfrac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}} = \dfrac{\tau_{forcer}}{1} = \dfrac{\text{forcer effective tooth pitch}}{1}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** A *2-phase* forcer halves the tooth pitch → finer step. A *4-phase* forcer quarters it → even finer step. Hence **more phases = finer resolution** (at the cost of more complex drive electronics).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Developed Force from Co-Energy

**Statement:** Using the principle of virtual work, derive the developed linear force of a single phase of a linear stepper motor.

---

### Step 1 — Express the co-energy

For a single phase winding of inductance $L(x)$ (a function of plunger position $x$) carrying current $i$, the magnetic co-energy is:

$$
W'_{co}(x, i) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot L(x) \cdot i^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual displacement

Under a virtual displacement $\delta x$ at constant current (the usual assumption for current-fed drives), the mechanical work done by the developed force $F$ is:

$$
\delta W_{mech} = F \cdot \delta x
$$

This must equal the *change* in co-energy:

$$
F \cdot \delta x = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial x} \cdot \delta x
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate

$$
F = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{1}{2} L(x) \cdot i^{2} \right)
$$

Since $i$ is held constant:

$$
\boxed{\,F = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot i^{2} \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dx}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret physically

The force is:
- **Always attractive** (regardless of current sign) — because $i^{2} \geq 0$ and $\frac{dL}{dx}$ is generally *positive* as the plunger moves toward alignment (inductance rises sharply when teeth align, then drops as they slide past).
- **Proportional to $i^{2}$** — a doubling of current produces a **four-fold** increase in force.
- **Position-dependent** — maximum force occurs near the unaligned-to-aligned transition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Velocity from Pulse Train

**Statement:** A linear stepper with step size $s = 1.0$ mm is fed a square-wave pulse train of frequency $f = 2{,}500$ Hz. Calculate the linear velocity of the plunger.

---

### Step 1 — Recall the relation

Each pulse advances the plunger by exactly one step size $s$. Therefore, the distance covered in time $t$ is:

$$
x(t) = N(t) \cdot s
$$

where $N(t)$ = number of pulses issued in time $t$.

### Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. time

$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dN}{dt} \cdot s = f \cdot s
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$
v = 2{,}500 \text{ pulses/s} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/pulse}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering sanity check

For commercial linear steppers, a velocity of **2.5 m/s** is near the upper limit for the 1 mm step size — beyond this, mechanical resonance and back-EMF losses degrade performance. In practice, the controller would apply a *trapezoidal velocity profile* (acceleration → cruise → deceleration) to avoid missed steps.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — A KTU Board-Style Question

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 type):**
> A 4-phase variable reluctance linear stepper motor has a plunger tooth pitch of $\tau_p = 3.2$ mm. The forcer is excited with a stepping frequency of $f = 1{,}500$ pulses/s. Find:
> (i) The linear step size.
> (ii) The linear velocity of the plunger.
> (iii) The distance travelled in 0.8 seconds.

**Solution:**

**Part (i):** Step size

$$
s = \frac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}} = \frac{3.2 \text{ mm}}{4} = 0.8 \text{ mm/step}
$$

**Part (ii):** Linear velocity

$$
v = f \cdot s = 1{,}500 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Part (iii):** Distance in 0.8 s

$$
x = v \cdot t = 1.2 \text{ m/s} \times 0.8 \text{ s} = 0.96 \text{ m}
$$

**Cross-check via pulse count:**

$$
N = f \cdot t = 1{,}500 \times 0.8 = 1{,}200 \text{ pulses}
$$

$$
x = N \cdot s = 1{,}200 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 0.96 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Linear Stepper Drive System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete functional architecture** of a linear stepper positioning system, from command input to mechanical output. This is the **canonical KTU-style systems diagram** for this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller / PLC] --> B[Pulse and Direction Generator]
    B --> C[Indexer IC e.g. L297]
    C --> D[Chopper Driver e.g. L298N / A4988]
    D --> E[Power Stage H-Bridge per phase]
    E --> F[Linear Stepper Motor Forcer]
    F --> G[Magnetic Field in Air Gap]
    G --> H[Plunger Linear Displacement]
    H --> I[Mechanical Load X-Y Table]
    I --> J[Open Loop No Encoder Required]

    K[DC Bus Supply 12V to 48V] --> D
    L[Enable and Home Limit Switches] --> C
    M[Current Sense Resistor] --> D
    M --> N[Chopper Feedback Loop]
    N --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **microcontroller** issues *step* and *direction* logic pulses.
- The **indexer IC** decodes the pulse train into the proper phase-excitation sequence (e.g., full-step, half-step).
- The **H-bridge power stage** delivers bi-directional current to each phase winding.
- The **forcer** generates a travelling magnetic field that linearly translates the **plunger**.
- The **current-sense / chopper feedback** regulates phase current to a fixed value regardless of back-EMF.
- The system is **inherently open-loop** — position feedback is optional for verification only.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional View of a VR-LSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Forcer Pole 1 Phase A] --> B[Air Gap]
    B --> C[Plunger Tooth aligned]
    C --> D[Air Gap]
    D --> E[Forcer Pole 2 Phase A return]
    E --> F[Forcer Pole 3 Phase B]
    F --> G[Plunger Tooth next]
    G --> H[Air Gap]
    H --> I[Forcer Pole 4 Phase B return]
```

**Functional interpretation:**
- When **Phase A is energised**, flux loops out of Pole 1, crosses the air gap into the nearest plunger tooth, travels inside the plunger, and returns via the next plunger tooth into Pole 2 (the return pole of Phase A).
- The minimum reluctance configuration is achieved when plunger teeth fully span Poles 1 and 2 — this is the **stable equilibrium**.
- Switching to **Phase B** shifts the flux loop to Poles 3 and 4, pulling the plunger by **one tooth-pitch** to the right.

---

## 4.3 Phase Excitation Sequence Chart (Wave Drive, 4-Phase)

| **Step #** | **Phase A** | **Phase B** | **Phase C** | **Phase D** | **Plunger Position** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ON  | OFF | OFF | OFF | Aligned with Phase A pole |
| 1 | OFF | ON  | OFF | OFF | Moved +1/4 $\tau_p$ |
| 2 | OFF | OFF | ON  | OFF | Moved +2/4 $\tau_p$ |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON  | Moved +3/4 $\tau_p$ |
| 4 | ON  | OFF | OFF | OFF | Moved +1 full $\tau_p$ |
| … | …   | …   | …   | …   | … |

> **Reversal:** Reverse the sequence (A → D → C → B → A) to obtain motion in the negative direction.

---

## 4.4 Linear Stepper vs. Rotary Stepper + Lead Screw — Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Direct Drive Approach] --> B[Linear Stepper Motor]
    B --> C[Direct Linear Motion]
    B --> D[No backlash, No wear, No screw error]
    B --> E[Higher cost, Limited stroke to plunger length]

    F[Indirect Drive Approach] --> G[Rotary Stepper]
    G --> H[Coupling]
    H --> I[Lead Screw / Ball Screw]
    I --> J[Linear Motion]
    J --> K[Backlash, Screw pitch error, Wear over time]
    J --> L[Lower cost, Unlimited stroke]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** Choose **direct-drive linear stepper** when the stroke is < 1 m and backlash-free precision is mandatory. Choose **rotary stepper + lead screw** for strokes > 1 m or when extremely high thrust is required.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** Define a **linear stepper motor**. List its two main parts and state the principle on which it works.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A linear stepper motor is an electromechanical device that converts a train of digital electrical pulses into discrete, open-loop linear displacement without the need for a rotary-to-linear conversion mechanism. **Two main parts:** (i) **Forcer** (stator with polyphase windings), (ii) **Plunger** (the linearly-translating member carrying salient teeth or permanent magnets). **Working principle:** It operates on the principle of **minimum reluctance** (in VR type), **permanent-magnet flux interaction** (in PM type), or a **hybrid of both** (in H-LSM). Each input pulse shifts the magnetic field pattern by one step, producing a corresponding linear step in the plunger.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** Distinguish between a **variable reluctance** and a **hybrid** linear stepper motor on the basis of construction, step resolution, and force density.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Parameter** | **VR-LSM** | **Hybrid LSM** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Construction** | Plain soft-iron plunger with salient teeth; forcer has wound poles | Toothed silicon-steel plunger with axially-magnetised PM sandwiched in the centre; toothed forcer with multi-phase winding |
> | **Step Resolution** | Coarse (typically 0.5 – 2.5 mm) | Fine (typically 5 – 50 µm) |
> | **Force Density** | Low – Medium | High (best in class) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** on these questions for the following mistakes:
> 1. **Confusing step size formula** between VR and Hybrid types — VR uses $s = \tau_p / N_{ph}$, Hybrid uses $s = \text{tooth pitch} / 2$. Mixing them is a **direct 2-mark penalty**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in velocity calculations (units must be SI).
> 3. **Writing "force is proportional to i"** — this is wrong; force is proportional to **$i^{2}$**. Half-mark deducted per wrong proportionality.
> 4. **Not stating the principle of operation** explicitly (minimum reluctance) — KTU examiners award 1 mark for stating the principle.
> 5. **Confusing the "forcer" with the "plunger"** — the forcer is the *stationary* part (winding), the plunger is the *moving* part. Interchanging roles = −1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Linear Stepper Motor Comprehensive**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working of a 4-phase variable reluctance linear stepper motor**. Discuss how the direction of motion can be reversed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-phase VR-LSM has a plunger tooth pitch of **$\tau_p = 4.0$ mm**. It is driven by a pulse train of frequency **$f = 800$ Hz** in a full-step wave-drive mode. Calculate:
   - (i) the linear step size,
   - (ii) the linear velocity of the plunger,
   - (iii) the distance covered in 6 seconds, and
   - (iv) the number of phases to be energised simultaneously in a full-step wave drive. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution A(a): Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The VR-LSM has two main parts:
- **Forcer (Stator):** A laminated silicon-steel stack carrying **4 phase windings** wound around 4 salient pole-pairs. The pole-pairs are arranged in a repeating sequence along the linear axis.
- **Plunger (Rotor/Slider):** A long, passive bar of soft iron or laminated silicon-steel with **regularly-spaced salient teeth** of pitch $\tau_p$ running along its length. It has no windings and no permanent magnet.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The motor works on the principle of **minimum magnetic reluctance**. When a phase is energised, the resulting magnetic flux seeks the path of **lowest reluctance**, which corresponds to the plunger teeth being aligned with the energised forcer poles.
- A stable equilibrium is established when the forcer and plunger teeth are aligned. This is the *holding position*.
- **Sequential excitation** of phases (A → B → C → D → A …) shifts the minimum-reluctance position by one pole-pitch each time, causing the plunger to advance in discrete linear steps.
- The size of each linear step equals the forcer's effective tooth pitch:
$$
s = \frac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}} = \frac{\tau_p}{4}
$$

**Direction Reversal (1 mark):**
- The direction of motion is **reversed by inverting the commutation sequence** (e.g., A → D → C → B → A), which shifts the minimum-reluctance position in the opposite direction.

---

#### **Model Solution A(b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\tau_p = 4.0$ mm $= 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $N_{ph} = 4$, $f = 800$ Hz.

**(i) Linear step size** [1 mark]:

$$
s = \frac{\tau_p}{N_{ph}} = \frac{4.0 \text{ mm}}{4} = \boxed{1.0 \text{ mm/step}}
$$

**(ii) Linear velocity** [2 marks]:

$$
v = f \cdot s = 800 \text{ pulses/s} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/pulse} = \boxed{0.8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Distance in 6 s** [2 marks]:

$$
x = v \cdot t = 0.8 \text{ m/s} \times 6 \text{ s} = \boxed{4.8 \text{ m}}
$$

**Cross-check:** $N = f \cdot t = 800 \times 6 = 4{,}800$ pulses, and $x = N \cdot s = 4{,}800 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 4.8$ m ✓

**(iv) Phases energised simultaneously in full-step wave drive** [2 marks]:
In a **wave-drive** (also called *one-phase-on*) full-step mode, **only ONE phase is energised at any instant**, and the remaining three phases are OFF. Therefore, the number of phases energised simultaneously = **1**.

> **Distinction to remember:** In a *full-step two-phase-on* (called *normal drive* or *two-phase-on full-step*) mode, **two phases are energised simultaneously** for higher torque. In *half-step* mode, the number alternates between 1 and 2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a hybrid linear stepper motor**. Compare its performance with a VR-LSM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid linear stepper motor has a tooth pitch of **$1.0$ mm** and operates in half-step mode with **3 phases**. The pulse frequency is **$f = 4{,}000$ Hz**.
   - (i) Calculate the step size.
   - (ii) Find the linear velocity.
   - (iii) Determine the number of steps required to cover a distance of **0.5 m**.
   - (iv) If the motor is replaced by a VR-LSM with the same $\tau_p = 1.0$ mm and 3 phases, what is the new step size? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution B(a): Hybrid LSM Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A hybrid linear stepper has *two* toothed structures:
- **Two identical toothed steel cups** at the ends, each with fine teeth of pitch $p$.
- **A permanently magnetised axial rod** sandwiched between the two toothed cups, magnetised such that one end is N and the other S.
- The forcer (stator) has a multi-phase winding wound around toothed poles.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The PM produces a bi-directional bias flux. The forcer winding produces a controlled armature flux.
- The *vector sum* of the two fluxes creates a localised strong field at one tooth and a weak (or opposite) field at the adjacent tooth.
- The plunger aligns itself to minimise the *total* reluctance, which is sensitive to *very small* displacements (because the teeth are fine).
- Each step moves the plunger by **half the tooth pitch**: $s = p/2$.

**Comparison with VR-LSM (1 mark):**
Hybrid LSMs offer **finer step size**, **higher force density**, and **better holding torque** than VR-LSMs, but are more expensive and have a more complex drive circuit.

---

#### **Model Solution B(b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** Tooth pitch $p = 1.0$ mm, $N_{ph} = 3$, $f = 4{,}000$ Hz, half-step mode.

**(i) Step size in half-step mode** [2 marks]:
Base step size (full-step) = $p/2 = 0.5$ mm. Half-step mode halves this again:

$$
s = \frac{p}{2 \times 2} = \frac{1.0 \text{ mm}}{4} = \boxed{0.25 \text{ mm/step}}
$$

**(ii) Linear velocity** [1.5 marks]:

$$
v = f \cdot s = 4{,}000 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = \boxed{1.0 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Number of steps for 0.5 m** [1.5 marks]:

$$
N = \frac{x}{s} = \frac{0.5 \text{ m}}{0.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}} = \boxed{2{,}000 \text{ steps}}
$$

**Cross-check via time:** Time taken $= N/f = 2000/4000 = 0.5$ s, and $x = v \cdot t = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5$ m ✓

**(iv) Step size if replaced by a VR-LSM** [2 marks]:
For a VR-LSM, $s = \tau_p / N_{ph} = 1.0 \text{ mm} / 3 = \boxed{0.333 \text{ mm/step}}$

> **Observation:** The hybrid LSM (0.25 mm/step) gives finer resolution than the VR-LSM (0.333 mm/step) in this case — explaining why hybrids are preferred for high-precision applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Linear Stepper Motors**

- ✅ A **linear stepper motor** produces **linear motion directly** — no lead screw, no rack-and-pinion.
- ✅ **Two main parts:** **Forcer** (stator, wound) and **Plunger** (slider, passive or with PM).
- ✅ **Three types:** **VR (variable reluctance)**, **PM (permanent magnet)**, **Hybrid**.
- ✅ **VR-LSM works on the principle of minimum reluctance** (no PMs in plunger).
- ✅ **Hybrid-LSM works on combined PM flux + toothed iron reluctance minimisation** — finest step size.
- ✅ **Step size for VR-LSM:** $s = \tau_p / N_{ph}$ — finer for *more phases*.
- ✅ **Step size for Hybrid LSM:** $s = \text{tooth pitch} / 2$ — independent of phase count.
- ✅ **Linear velocity:** $v = f \cdot s$ — product of pulse frequency and step size.
- ✅ **Distance covered:** $x = N \cdot s = f \cdot t \cdot s$.
- ✅ **Developed force:** $F = \frac{1}{2} i^{2} \cdot \frac{dL}{dx}$ — proportional to **current squared**, **not** current.
- ✅ **Direction reversal** is achieved by **reversing the commutation sequence** (A→D→C→B→A), not by changing current magnitude.
- ✅ **Open-loop operation** is a key advantage — no encoder is *required* for normal positioning.
- ✅ **Wave drive** = 1 phase ON at a time. **Normal drive** = 2 phases ON simultaneously. **Half-step** alternates between 1 and 2 phases.
- ✅ **Microstepping** subdivides each full step into 8, 16, 32, 64, or 256 microsteps for ultra-smooth motion and finer resolution.
- ✅ **Resonance** occurs at certain stepping frequencies — must be avoided by acceleration profiling (trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profiles).
- ✅ **Holding force** is the static force the motor can resist without losing position — proportional to $i^{2}$ of the energised phase.
- ✅ **Detent torque/force** in hybrid LSMs is the *cogging* force due to PM-flux interaction with teeth — present even with no phase current.
- ✅ **Commercial applications:** 3D printers, CNC routers, XY-tables, pick-and-place machines, medical infusion pumps, semiconductor wafer-handling stages.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Lower efficiency than rotary steppers + lead screw for very long strokes, heating at standstill (when holding), and limited velocity.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison in Electric Drives** refers to the systematic analytical study of various drive topologies, control strategies, braking methods, and converter configurations used in industrial automation. It evaluates performance parameters such as **torque-speed characteristics**, **efficiency**, **dynamic response**, **power handling capability**, **regenerative braking ability**, **cost**, and **harmonic distortion** to identify the optimal drive solution for a given industrial application.

In the context of **PEAUT525 – Electric Drives and Controls for Automation (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module 4 focuses on advanced drive control strategies. The topic *"Comparison"* demands a side-by-side evaluation of:

1. **DC Drives vs. AC Drives**
2. **Rectifier-fed vs. Chopper-fed DC Drives**
3. **VSI-fed vs. CSI-fed AC Drives**
4. **Scalar (V/f) Control vs. Vector (Field-Oriented) Control**
5. **Regenerative Braking vs. Dynamic Braking vs. Plugging**
6. **Voltage-fed vs. Current-fed Inverters**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "Choosing the Right Vehicle for the Right Terrain"**
>
> Imagine you are an automation engineer designing a conveyor system for a cement factory. You have multiple "vehicles" (drives) to choose from:
>
> - A **DC Drive** is like a **manual gearbox car** — simple to control speed, predictable, but requires more maintenance (brushes).
> - An **AC Drive (V/f)** is like an **automatic car** — easy to operate, robust, less maintenance, but slower dynamic response.
> - An **AC Drive (Vector Control)** is like a **sports car with adaptive suspension** — extremely fast, precise, handles sudden load changes, but complex and expensive.
>
> **Comparison** is essentially the engineer's decision matrix — picking the right drive based on application needs: speed range, torque accuracy, braking needs, cost, and maintenance tolerance.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

Key performance metrics used throughout this comparison:

- **Rated Power (kW / HP)**
- **Base Speed / Field-weakening Speed (rpm)**
- **Torque Ripple (%)**
- **Total Harmonic Distortion – THD (%)**
- **Efficiency (η)**
- **Power Factor (cos φ)**
- **Switching Frequency (kHz)**
- **Dynamic Response Time (ms)**
- **Regenerative Braking Capability (Yes/No)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Compare Electric Drives?

Modern industrial automation demands drives that are:
- **Energy efficient** (energy cost = 70% of factory electricity bill)
- **Precisely controllable** (CNC machines, robotics)
- **Reliable** (24/7 operation)
- **Capable of four-quadrant operation** (forward motoring, reverse motoring, forward braking, reverse braking)

No single drive topology is optimal for all applications. Hence, **comparison** is the engineering tool for selection.

---

## 2.2 The Four-Quadrant Operation Framework

Any comparison of drives must reference the four-quadrant diagram:

| Quadrant | Operation | Torque | Speed |
|----------|-----------|--------|-------|
| **Q1** | Forward Motoring | + | + |
| **Q2** | Forward Braking (Regen) | – | + |
| **Q3** | Reverse Motoring | – | – |
| **Q4** | Reverse Braking (Regen) | + | – |

A "good" drive must seamlessly transition through all four quadrants.

---

## 2.3 Key Equations Driving the Comparison

### **A. DC Motor Speed Equation**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$$

### **B. Induction Motor Speed Equation**
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = N_s (1 - s)$$

### **C. Slip in Induction Motor**
$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$

### **D. Torque Equation (DC Motor)**
$$T = k_t \phi I_a$$

### **E. Torque Equation (Induction Motor)**
$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

### **F. Slip for Maximum Torque**
$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

### **G. Maximum Torque**
$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR COMPARISON TOPIC**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$ | DC motor speed | rpm |
| 2 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 3 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Slip | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | IM torque | N·m |
| 5 | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Slip at max torque | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | Pull-out torque | N·m |
| 7 | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos \phi \eta$ | 3-phase output power | W |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 10 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | V/f scalar control law | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL OBSERVATION (often asked in KTU):** Notice that $T_{max}$ in an induction motor is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**, while $s_m$ is **directly proportional to $R_2$**. This is the basis for **rotor resistance control** comparison.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Comparison tables are used in:
- **Steel rolling mills** → Vector-controlled AC drives preferred (high dynamic response)
- **Pumps and fans** → V/f-controlled AC drives sufficient (energy savings)
- **Electric vehicles** → PMSM/BLDC with regenerative braking
- **Cranes/hoists** → Four-quadrant drives with regenerative braking mandatory
- **Conveyor belts** → Simple DC chopper drives suffice
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Comparison Basis — Torque-Speed Characteristic Derivation for DC Shunt Motor

### **Starting Point:** Back-EMF Equation
$$E_b = k \phi N \quad \text{(volts)}$$

### **Step 1 — Apply KVL to the armature circuit:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute $E_b$ in terms of speed:**
$$V = k \phi N + I_a R_a$$

### **Step 3 — Solve for speed N:**
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$$

### **Step 4 — Express armature current in terms of torque:**
$$T = k_t \phi I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \frac{T}{k_t \phi}$$

### **Step 5 — Substitute into speed equation:**
$$N = \frac{V}{k \phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k \phi)^2} \cdot T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CONCLUSION:** The speed-torque curve of a DC shunt motor is a **straight line** with:
> - **No-load speed (intercept):** $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{k \phi}$
> - **Slope:** $\dfrac{R_a}{(k \phi)^2}$ (negative — speed drops as torque increases)

This **linear drooping characteristic** is the comparison baseline against which AC drives are evaluated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slip Comparison for Rotor Resistance Control

### **Step 1 — Induction motor torque expression (approximate):**
$$T \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot s}{R_2} \quad \text{(for small } s\text{)}$$

### **Step 2 — Solve for slip at any given torque T:**
$$s = \frac{T \omega_s R_2}{3 V^2}$$

### **Step 3 — Slip at maximum torque (already known):**
$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

### **Step 4 — Ratio of slips (useful for comparison):**
$$\frac{s}{s_m} = \frac{T \omega_s R_2}{3 V^2} \cdot \frac{X_2}{R_2} = \frac{T \omega_s X_2}{3 V^2}$$

### **Step 5 — Therefore:**
$$\frac{s}{s_m} \propto \frac{T X_2}{V^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM INSIGHT:** This equation proves that for a fixed torque, slip is proportional to rotor resistance $R_2$. Hence, **increasing $R_2$ shifts the operating point** along the torque-speed curve — this is the principle behind **rotor resistance control** (the "poor man's VFD").

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: V/f Control Law (Scalar Control)

### **Step 1 — Air-gap flux in induction motor:**
$$\phi \propto \frac{V}{f}$$

### **Step 2 — Torque at low slip region:**
$$T \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 s}{R_2}$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$:**
$$T \approx \frac{3 p s R_2}{2 \pi f} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R_2^2} = \frac{3 p s V^2}{2 \pi f R_2}$$

### **Step 4 — If V/f is held constant (= K):**
$$T \approx \frac{3 p s K^2 f}{2 \pi R_2} = \frac{3 p s K^2}{2 \pi R_2} \cdot f$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RESULT:** With constant $V/f$, torque at a given slip is **proportional to frequency** (and hence to synchronous speed). This is the foundation of **scalar V/f control** — it allows constant torque operation over the speed range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER COMPARISON TABLE (MUST MEMORIZE)**

### **A. DC Drive vs. AC Drive**

| Parameter | DC Drive | AC Drive (Induction) |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| Construction | Complex (brush-commutator) | Rugged, simple |
| Maintenance | High (brush replacement) | Very Low |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Speed Range | Wide (10:1 to 100:1) | Wide (with V/f) |
| Dynamic Response | Excellent (ms) | Good (10–50 ms) |
| Starting Torque | Very High | Moderate (high with vector) |
| Regenerative Braking | Easy | Complex (needs bi-directional converter) |
| Size | Compact for given kW | Bulkier |
| Application | Elevators, traction, mills | Pumps, fans, HVAC, CNC |

### **B. Rectifier-fed vs. Chopper-fed DC Drive**

| Parameter | Rectifier-fed (Phase-controlled) | Chopper-fed (PWM) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|-------------------|
| Input | AC mains | DC source |
| Switching Device | SCR | IGBT/MOSFET |
| Switching Frequency | Low (line freq.) | High (1–20 kHz) |
| Harmonics | High | Low |
| Efficiency | 75–85% | 90–95% |
| Dynamic Response | Slow | Fast |
| Regeneration | Easy (fully-controlled bridge) | Complex (requires extra switch) |
| Power Range | High (MW) | Low–Medium (kW) |

### **C. VSI-fed vs. CSI-fed AC Drive**

| Parameter | VSI (Voltage Source Inverter) | CSI (Current Source Inverter) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| DC Link | Capacitor (stiff voltage) | Inductor (stiff current) |
| Output Waveform | Voltage PWM | Current PWM |
| Power Flow | Uni-directional (regen needs extra stage) | Naturally bi-directional |
| Short-circuit Protection | Poor (capacitor) | Excellent (inductor limits di/dt) |
| Dynamic Response | Faster | Slower |
| Used For | General purpose (most drives) | High-power (>500 kW) |
| Regenerative Braking | Needs anti-parallel converter | Inherent capability |

### **D. Scalar (V/f) vs. Vector (FOC) Control**

| Parameter | Scalar V/f Control | Vector Control (FOC) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Control Variables | Voltage magnitude & frequency | Flux & Torque (decoupled) |
| Dynamic Response | Slow | Excellent (similar to DC) |
| Torque Accuracy | ±5–10% | ±1–2% |
| Speed Range | 20:1 | 1000:1 |
| Position Control | Not Suitable | Suitable |
| Cost | Low | High (needs encoder/observer) |
| Algorithm Complexity | Simple | Complex (Park/Clarke transforms) |
| Application | Pumps, fans, conveyors | Robotics, CNC, servo |

### **E. Comparison of Braking Methods**

| Parameter | Regenerative Braking | Dynamic Braking | Plugging |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|-----------|
| Energy Returned to Source | Yes | No (dissipated as heat) | No |
| Implementation | Bi-directional converter | Resistor across armature | Reverse supply polarity |
| Speed at Stop | Motor coasts to standstill | Decelerates to zero | Rapid stop |
| Heat Dissipation | Minimal | High (resistor heats up) | Very High |
| Wear on Motor | None | Brush wear only | Severe (mechanical shock) |
| Application | EVs, elevators, hoists | Industrial cranes, traction | Emergency stops |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Quadrant Operation Diagram (Conceptual Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["QUADRANT 1\nForward Motoring\nT > 0, N > 0"]
    Q2["QUADRANT 2\nForward Braking\nT < 0, N > 0"]
    Q3["QUADRANT 3\nReverse Motoring\nT < 0, N < 0"]
    Q4["QUADRANT 4\nReverse Braking\nT > 0, N < 0"]

    Q1 -->|"Speed Reduction"| Q2
    Q2 -->|"Reverse Command"| Q3
    Q3 -->|"Speed Reduction"| Q4
    Q4 -->|"Stop Command"| Q1
```

## 4.2 Drive Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Industrial Application"]
    A --> B{"Need Precise Torque\nand Fast Response?"}
    B -->|Yes| C{"Budget Available?"}
    B -->|No| D["Use V/f Scalar Control\n(Pumps, Fans, HVAC)"]

    C -->|Yes| E["Vector Control AC Drive\nwith Encoder/Sensor"]
    C -->|No| F["DC Drive with\nChopper Control"]

    E --> G{"Need Regenerative\nBraking?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Use PMSM/BLDC Drive\nwith Bi-directional Inverter"]
    G -->|No| I["Standard Induction Motor\nwith FOC"]

    F --> J["Application: Elevators,\nTraction, Small Mills"]
    D --> K["Application: HVAC,\nConveyors, Pumps"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Closed-Loop DC Drive (Reference for Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["Reference\nSpeed (N_ref)"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> CTRL["Speed Controller\n(PI)"]
    CTRL --> RECT["Power Converter\n(Chopper / Rectifier)"]
    RECT --> MOTOR["DC Motor"]
    MOTOR --> FB1["Tachogenerator\n(Speed Feedback)"]
    MOTOR --> FB2["Current Sensor\n(I_a Feedback)"]
    FB1 --> SUM1
    FB2 --> CTRL
```

## 4.4 Scalar V/f Control Block Diagram (vs. Vector Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FREF["f_ref"] --> VFCALC["V/f Calculator\nV = K × f"]
    VREF["V_ref"] --> VFCALC
    VFCALC --> PWM["PWM Generator"]
    PWM --> INV["VSI Inverter"]
    INV --> IM["Induction Motor"]
    IM --> SENSOR["Speed Sensor"]
    SENSOR --> COMP["Compare with f_ref"]
    COMP --> FREF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KEY OBSERVATION:** The V/f scheme has **only ONE feedback loop (speed)**. Vector Control (FOC) has **TWO feedback loops (flux and torque)** — this is the structural reason for its superior dynamic response.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. Compare regenerative braking and dynamic braking in DC drives.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Regenerative Braking | Dynamic Braking |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Energy** | Returned to source | Dissipated in resistor |
| **Mechanism** | Motor acts as generator; back-EMF > supply voltage | Armature disconnected and connected across resistor |
| **Efficiency** | High (energy saved) | Low (energy wasted as heat) |
| **Components** | Fully-controlled bridge converter | Brake resistor + switch |
| **Application** | Elevators, EVs, hoists | Cranes, emergency stops |

**Conclusion:** Regenerative braking is preferred for **frequent braking cycles** (elevators, EVs) due to energy recovery. Dynamic braking is used where **simplicity and cost** matter.

---

### **Q2. Why is V/f maintained constant in scalar control of induction motors?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The air-gap flux $\phi$ in an induction motor is proportional to $V/f$. Since torque $T \propto \phi \cdot I_r$, maintaining flux constant (by keeping $V/f$ = constant) ensures that:
- The motor delivers **rated torque** at any speed.
- **No over-fluxing** (saturates core, draws high magnetizing current) at low frequencies.
- **No under-fluxing** (reduces torque capacity) at high frequencies.

Hence, $V/f = $ constant gives **constant torque operation** from zero to base speed.

---

## **PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)**

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Compare scalar V/f control and vector control of induction motor drives with respect to principle, block diagram, dynamic response, and applications. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Principle & Block Diagram Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Scalar V/f Control:**
- **Principle:** Controls only the **magnitude** of voltage and frequency in an open-loop or single-loop (speed only) manner. No control over instantaneous flux or torque vectors.
- **Block Diagram:** Reference speed → V/f calculator → PWM → VSI → Motor → Speed sensor → Feedback.
- **Mathematical basis:** $V/f = \text{constant} \Rightarrow \phi = \text{constant}$.

**Vector Control (Field-Oriented Control – FOC):**
- **Principle:** Decouples the stator current into two orthogonal components:
  - $i_d$ → flux-producing
  - $i_q$ → torque-producing
- Uses **Clarke and Park transformations** to convert 3-phase AC quantities to DC equivalents (in d-q rotating frame), making them independently controllable like a DC motor.
- **Block Diagram:** Speed ref → PI controller → $i_q^*$ generator → Inverse Park → SVPWM → Inverter → Motor → Encoder → Feedback to both flux and torque estimators.

**Key difference:** Vector control regulates **both flux and torque independently**; scalar control regulates only **voltage magnitude**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Performance & Application Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Scalar V/f Control | Vector Control |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Dynamic Response | Slow (50–100 ms) | Very Fast (1–5 ms) |
| Torque Accuracy | ±5–10% | ±1–2% |
| Speed Range | 1:20 | 1:1000 |
| Position Control | Not feasible | Feasible |
| Low-Speed Operation | Poor (torque drops) | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High (sensors + DSP) |
| Applications | Pumps, fans, HVAC, conveyors | CNC, robotics, servo drives, EVs |

**Conclusion:** Scalar V/f is preferred for **simple, low-cost, variable-torque loads** (fans, pumps), while vector control is essential for **high-performance servo and position-controlled** applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Compare the four-quadrant operation capability of (i) half-controlled and (ii) fully-controlled rectifier-fed DC drives. Discuss regenerative braking implementation in each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **Part (a) — Quadrant Capability Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Quadrant | Half-Controlled Rectifier | Fully-Controlled Rectifier |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Q1 – Forward Motoring** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Q2 – Forward Braking (Regen)** | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Possible |
| **Q3 – Reverse Motoring** | ✅ Yes (reverse connection) | ✅ Yes |
| **Q4 – Reverse Braking (Regen)** | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Possible |

**Half-controlled (Semi-converter):**
- Uses SCR + Diode mix.
- Output voltage is always **positive** (unipolar).
- Cannot reverse DC output voltage → **No regenerative braking**.
- For reverse motoring, contactors are used to physically reverse armature/field.

**Fully-controlled (Full-converter):**
- Uses SCRs in all arms (dual converter or anti-parallel).
- Output voltage can be **positive OR negative** depending on firing angle $\alpha$.
- Firing angle control: $\alpha < 90°$ → motoring; $\alpha > 90°$ → regeneration.
- **Naturally four-quadrant** without mechanical contactors.

---

#### **Part (b) — Regenerative Braking Implementation (7 Marks)**

**In Fully-Controlled Converter (Dual Converter Mode):**
- Two fully-controlled bridges connected in **anti-parallel**.
- Converter 1 supplies power (motoring); Converter 2 returns power to source (braking).
- During braking, $E_b > V$ → motor acts as generator → current reverses → through Converter 2 back to AC mains.
- **Circulating current mode** or **circulating current-free mode** operation.
- **Application:** Rolling mills, elevators, large hoists.

**In Half-Controlled Converter (Mechanical Switching):**
- Regen not possible electrically.
- Implementation: Use **separate field reversal + armature chopper + contactor logic**.
- More complex, slower response, requires maintenance.
- **Application:** Low-cost drives where regen is rare.

**Conclusion:** Fully-controlled dual converters are the **standard for high-performance four-quadrant drives**; half-controlled converters are used only where regenerative braking is not required.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "regenerative" with "dynamic" braking** — Regen = energy returns to source; Dynamic = energy dissipated in resistor. Examiners WILL deduct marks.
>
> 2. **Writing $V/f = $ constant without explaining WHY** — You must mention **air-gap flux constancy**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the firing angle threshold** — In full converters, $\alpha < 90°$ = motoring, $\alpha > 90°$ = regeneration. Missing this = –2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the four-quadrant diagram** in Part B questions — Even a rough sketch earns 2–3 easy marks.
>
> 5. **Conflating CSI and VSI characteristics** — CSI has **inductor** in DC link; VSI has **capacitor**. Do not interchange.
>
> 6. **Writing "$T_{max}$ depends on $R_2$"** — This is **WRONG**. $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance**; only $s_m$ depends on $R_2$. Very common error.
>
> 7. **Skipping units** — Always mention rpm for speed, N·m for torque, Hz for frequency.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- [ ] **DC vs AC drive:** DC = simple control, AC = rugged. AC with vector control rivals DC in performance.
- [ ] **V/f control:** Maintains constant flux → constant torque below base speed.
- [ ] **Vector control:** Decouples flux and torque via d-q transformation → DC-motor-like performance from AC motor.
- [ ] **Firing angle $\alpha$:** $\alpha < 90°$ → motoring; $\alpha > 90°$ → regeneration (full converter).
- [ ] **Regenerative braking:** Energy recovered → supply. Best for frequent braking.
- [ ] **Dynamic braking:** Energy dissipated as heat in $R_b$. Simple but inefficient.
- [ ] **Plugging:** Reverse supply → very fast stop but high heat and mechanical stress.
- [ ] **VSI:** Capacitor DC link → fast response → general purpose.
- [ ] **CSI:** Inductor DC link → natural regen → high-power drives.
- [ ] **$T_{max}$ independent of $R_2$; $s_m \propto R_2$** — golden comparison point.
- [ ] **Four-quadrant operation:** Requires fully-controlled bidirectional converter topology.
- [ ] **Scalar V/f applications:** Pumps, fans, HVAC, conveyors (variable torque loads).
- [ ] **Vector control applications:** CNC, robotics, servo drives, EVs (constant torque / position control).
- [ ] **Efficiency order:** Chopper-fed DC (≈95%) > Rectifier-fed DC (≈85%) > AC drive with V/f (≈90%) > AC drive with DTC (≈92%).
- [ ] **Remember:** Comparison answers in KTU always demand a **table + 2-line conclusion** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque-speed characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque-speed characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Torque-Speed Characteristic** of an electric drive is a graphical / analytical relationship expressing the **electromagnetic torque developed by the motor (Tₘ)** as a function of its **rotor (or armature) speed (N or ω)**, under prescribed supply and excitation conditions. It is the single most important steady-state curve used to determine:

- The **stable operating point** of a drive (intersection with load curve).
- The **speed regulation** of the motor under varying load.
- The **starting, accelerating, and braking capability** of the drive.
- The **region of safe operation** (thermal and mechanical limits).

Mathematically expressed as:

$$T_m = f(N) \quad \text{or} \quad T_m = f(\omega)$$

where the function $f$ is determined by the motor type, its supply, and field excitation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note (CO Mapping – CO4 / Module 4):**
> The torque-speed curve is the **"identity card"** of a motor. A student of electric drives must be able to sketch, label, and interpret the curve for DC, Induction, Synchronous, and Special machines, and match it with the load torque characteristic to identify the **steady-state operating point** and **stability**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle up a hill**:

- The **pedalling force (torque)** you apply depends on how fast you are pedalling (cadence) and which gear you are in (excitation/supply).
- If you change gears, the very same muscle effort produces a different speed — this is exactly what happens when the **field flux or supply voltage** of a motor is changed.
- A **professional cyclist climbing a steep incline** needs **high torque at low speed** → like a **series motor on a hoist**.
- A **cyclist cruising on a flat road** needs **low torque at high speed** → like a **shunt motor driving a fan**.

**Real-world mapping:**

| Bicycling Element | Electric Drive Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cadence (rpm) | Motor speed $N$ |
| Pedal force | Developed torque $T_m$ |
| Gear ratio | Field flux $\phi$ / supply voltage $V$ |
| Hill slope | Load torque $T_L$ |
| Cruising speed | Operating point |

The **point where pedalling torque exactly balances the hill's resistance** is the **operating point** — and the question "is the ride stable?" is exactly the **stability analysis** of a drive.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Highlight)

- **Torque ($T$)** — measured in **N·m** (Newton-metre).
- **Angular speed ($\omega$)** — measured in **rad/s**, with $N$ in **rpm**: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
- **Slip ($s$)** — dimensionless, $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$.
- **Synchronous speed ($N_s$)** — $N_s = 120 f / P$ rpm.
- **Mechanical power ($P_m$)** — $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ in **W** (Watts).
- **Moment of inertia ($J$)** — kg·m², governs the dynamic equation $T_m - T_L = J \cdot d\omega/dt$.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in a Drive System

> [!NOTE]
> A drive is *not* just a motor — it is the **motor + power converter + controller + load**. The torque-speed characteristic of the motor is the **load line that the controller must follow** to achieve:
> - Smooth starting (avoiding inrush).
> - Fast dynamic response.
> - Energy-efficient steady-state operation.
> - Safe regeneration / braking.

The KTU 2024 syllabus places this in Module 4 because every later topic — **closed-loop control, vector control, direct torque control (DTC), four-quadrant operation** — is built on the foundation of these curves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Motors by Torque-Speed Behaviour

Electric motors used in automation drives fall into **three broad families** of torque-speed curves:

### 2.1.1 DC Motors

**(a) Separately Excited / Shunt DC Motor**

- Flux $\phi$ is **constant** (field current is independent).
- $T_m \propto \phi I_a \Rightarrow I_a = T_m / (k\phi)$, so $T_m \propto I_a$ (linear).
- $N \propto (V - I_a R_a) / \phi \Rightarrow$ **shunt** motor characteristic.

**Why the curve is "drooping":** As $T_m \uparrow \Rightarrow I_a \uparrow \Rightarrow I_a R_a$ drop $\uparrow \Rightarrow N \downarrow$.

The relationship between $N$ and $T_m$:

$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2}\,T_m$$

This is a **straight line with small negative slope** (good speed regulation, $\approx 5$–$10\%$).

**(b) Series DC Motor**

- $\phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated region), so $T_m \propto I_a^2$ and $N \propto 1/I_a$.
- Therefore: $T_m \cdot N = \text{constant}$ (in unsaturated region).

$$N = \frac{V}{k\sqrt{T_m/a}} - \frac{R_a}{k}$$

where $a$ is the saturation constant. The curve is a **rectangular hyperbola** — very high starting torque, no-load run-away (theoretically infinite speed).

**(c) Compound DC Motor**

- Combines shunt and series fields. Two sub-types:
  - **Cumulative compound**: drooping, but with high starting torque.
  - **Differential compound**: rarely used (unstable).

### 2.1.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor (Cage / Slip-Ring)

The exact torque expression (referred to stator) is:

$$T_m = \frac{3}{\omega_s}\cdot\frac{V_s^2 \cdot R_r'/s}{\left(R_s + R_r'/s\right)^2 + \left(X_s + X_r'\right)^2}$$

The curve has **three distinct regions**:
1. **Low slip (running region):** $T \approx s$ — almost linear.
2. **Pull-out (maximum) torque** at slip $s_m$:
$$s_m = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}}$$
$$T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_s^2}{2\,\omega_s\left(R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}\right)}$$
3. **High slip (starting region):** $T \approx 1/s$ — falling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_r'$** — but the **slip at which it occurs is proportional to $R_r'$**. This is the entire reason slip-ring motors use external rotor resistance for **soft starting**.

### 2.1.3 Synchronous Motor

- Runs at **fixed speed** $N_s = 120f/P$ — **no slip**.
- Torque varies with load angle $\delta$:

$$T_m = \frac{3 V E}{\omega_s X_s}\sin\delta$$

- **Constant-speed** vertical line on $T$–$N$ plot.
- $T_{max}$ (pull-out) occurs at $\delta = 90°$.

### 2.1.4 Special / Servo Motors (Automation Relevance)

- **Stepper motor**: $T \propto$ step rate (non-linear; pull-out curve).
- **BLDC / PMSM**: Linear $T$–$I$ characteristic, $T = k_t I$, with trapezoidal / sinusoidal back-EMF.
- **SRM (Switched Reluctance)**: Highly non-linear; torque produced only during overlap of stator/rotor poles.

## 2.2 Load Torque Characteristics

The **load** also has its own $T_L$–$N$ curve. The three standard forms:

$$T_L = K_0 \quad (\text{constant torque — hoists, conveyors})$$

$$T_L = K_1 N + K_0 \quad (\text{linear — dry friction loads})$$

$$T_L = K_2 N^2 \quad (\text{quadratic — fans, pumps, compressors})$$

$$T_L = K_3 / N \quad (\text{inverse — winding machines, machine tool spindles})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU high-yield trick:** When asked to "find operating point", you simply equate $T_m(N) = T_L(N)$ and solve for $N^*$. Always verify **stability** using $\dfrac{dT_m}{dN} \Big|_{N^*} < \dfrac{dT_L}{dN} \Big|_{N^*}$.

## 2.3 Stability Criterion (Formal)

At the steady-state operating point $N_0$:
