**Step 4:** Apply the standard Z-transform pairs.

Using $\mathcal{Z}\{1/s\} = z/(z-1) = 1/(1-z^{-1})$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{1/(s+a)\} = z/(z - e^{-aT_s})$:

$$
\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{ \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} \right\} \;=\; K\!\left( \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by the $(1 - z^{-1})$ factor from the ZOH.

$$
G(z) \;=\; K\!\left( 1 - \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the expression.

Common denominator is $(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1})$:

$$
G(z) \;=\; K \cdot \frac{(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}) - (1 - z^{-1})}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}}
$$

Cancel the constant terms $1 - 1 = 0$:

$$
G(z) \;=\; K \cdot \frac{z^{-1} - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau} z^{-1}}
$$

Factor $z^{-1}$ in the numerator:

$$
\boxed{\,G(z) \;=\; \frac{K\,(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau})\, z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau}\, z^{-1}}\,}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify dimensionally and at $T_s \to 0$.

For very small $T_s$, $e^{-T_s/\tau} \approx 1 - T_s/\tau$, so the numerator becomes $K \cdot (T_s/\tau) z^{-1}$ and the denominator $1 - (1 - T_s/\tau) z^{-1} \approx T_s/\tau$ in the limit, recovering the integrator-like low-frequency behaviour — consistent with a first-order plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This boxed result is the **canonical form** that examiners expect in Part (b) of 14-mark digital-controller questions. **Memorize it!**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Digital PID Controller (Position Form)

**Step 1:** Start from the analog PID law.

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_p\, e(t) \;+\; K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\sigma)\, d\sigma \;+\; K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Discretize the integral using the rectangular (forward Euler) rule.

$$
\int_{0}^{kT_s} e(\sigma)\, d\sigma \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)
$$

**Step 3:** Discretize the derivative using the backward-difference rule.

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and assemble the discrete PID output.

$$
u(k) \;=\; K_p\, e(k) \;+\; K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) \;+\; \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]
$$

This is the **position form**: $u(k)$ is the absolute control word sent to the PWM.

**Step 5:** Take the Z-transform to obtain the controller transfer function.

Using $\mathcal{Z}\{\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)\} = E(z)/(1 - z^{-1})$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{e(k-1)\} = z^{-1} E(z)$:

$$
D(z) \;=\; \frac{U(z)}{E(z)} \;=\; K_p \;+\; \frac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} \;+\; \frac{K_d}{T_s}\,(1 - z^{-1})
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - z^{-1})$ to expose the polynomial form.

$$
D(z) \;=\; \frac{K_p(1 - z^{-1}) + K_i T_s + (K_d/T_s)(1 - z^{-1})^2}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

Expanding $(1 - z^{-1})^2 = 1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}$:

$$
\boxed{\,D(z) \;=\; \frac{(K_p + K_i T_s + K_d/T_s) \;-\; (2K_d/T_s + K_p)\, z^{-1} \;+\; (K_d/T_s)\, z^{-2}}{1 - z^{-1}}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical discrete PID** as implemented in industrial microcontrollers (TI C2000, STM32, Renesas RX).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Incremental / Velocity PID Form

**Step 1:** Compute $u(k) - u(k-1)$ from the position form. The summation term becomes a telescoping difference:

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) \;-\; \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) \;=\; e(k)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute and simplify.

$$
\Delta u(k) \;=\; K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] \;+\; K_i T_s\, e(k) \;+\; \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3:** The actual output is recovered as $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why velocity form?** – It is **bumpless** (no sudden jumps on set-point changes) and naturally incorporates **anti-windup** because $\Delta u$ is bounded even when $e$ saturates.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Implementation Platforms

| Parameter | 8-bit μC (e.g., 8051, PIC) | 32-bit μC (STM32, TI C2000) | DSP (TMS320F28x) | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clock | 12–50 MHz | 100–200 MHz | 150–300 MHz | 50–500 MHz |
| ADC | 10-bit | 12–16-bit | 12–16-bit | External |
| PWM channels | 1–2 | 6–24 | 6–24 | Any number |
| PID loop time | 100 μs | 1–10 μs | 1 μs | <0.5 μs |
| Cost | Very low | Medium | Medium-high | High |
| Best for | Small DC drives, fans | VFDs, servo drives | High-perf. servo | Multi-axis, ultrafast |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Digital Speed Control of an Electric Drive

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete signal flow of a microprocessor-controlled drive, from the reference command down to the mechanical output and back through the feedback sensor.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REF[Reference n_ref k] --> SUM1((+))
    TAC[Tacho Encoder Feedback n_act k] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> AAF[Anti Alias Low Pass Filter]
    AAF --> ADC[ADC Sampler Quantizer]
    ADC --> PROC[Digital Controller PID on MCU DSP or FPGA]
    PROC --> REG[Output Register PWM Compare]
    REG --> ZOH[Zero Order Hold DAC Stage]
    ZOH --> PWR[Power Converter IGBT VSI or CSI]
    PWR --> MOT[Electric Motor IM PMSM BLDC SRM]
    MOT --> LOAD[Mech Load Shaft]
    MOT -.-> TAC
    PROC -.->|Anti Windup| SAT[Saturate Output]
    SAT -.-> REG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **forward path** is REF → AAF → ADC → Processor → ZOH → Converter → Motor → Load.
- The **feedback path** is Motor → Tacho → Summing junction.
- The **dashed line** from Processor to Saturation block represents the **software anti-windup** scheme, which clamps the integrator term when the PWM duty saturates at 100 %.

## 4.2 Flowchart — Software Implementation of the Digital PID in the Processor

The following flowchart shows the periodic control task executed by the processor every sampling interval $T_s$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Power On Reset]) --> INIT[Initialise GPIO ADC PWM Timers and Variables]
    INIT --> WAIT{Is Sampling Timer Triggered?}
    WAIT -- No --> WAIT
    WAIT -- Yes --> READ[Read ADC Channel n_act k]
    READ --> ERR[Compute Error e k = n_ref - n_act k]
    ERR --> PTERM[P term = Kp times e k]
    PTERM --> ITERM[I term = Kp times Ki times Ts times e k plus old I]
    ITERM --> DTERM[D term = Kp times Kd over Ts times e k minus e k minus 1]
    DTERM --> SUM[Output u k = P plus I plus D]
    SUM --> CHK{Absolute u k exceeds Umax?}
    CHK -- Yes --> CLAMP[Clamp to Umax and Freeze Integrator]
    CHK -- No --> PWM[Update PWM Compare Register]
    CLAMP --> PWM
    PWM --> SHIFT[Shift History e k minus 1 = e k]
    SHIFT --> WAIT
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Analog vs Digital Controller

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ANALOG[Analog Controller Op amp based]
        A1[Error e t] --> A2[Op amp PID Network RC]
        A2 --> A3[Direct PWM or Gating Signal]
    end
    subgraph DIGITAL[Digital Controller MCU DSP FPGA based]
        D1[Error e k] --> D2[Anti Alias Filter]
        D2 --> D3[ADC Sampler]
        D3 --> D4[Software PID Algorithm]
        D4 --> D5[DAC or PWM Module]
        D5 --> D6[Gating Signal]
    end
    ANALOG --- DIGITAL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading note:** Every analog block on the left has a digital counterpart on the right. The **PID tuning resistors/capacitors** of the analog version are **replaced by numerical gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ in software** — this is what makes digital controllers *re-programmable* in the field.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**What is a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)? Why is it necessary in a digital controller for an electric drive?**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**
- A Zero-Order Hold is a reconstruction device that **holds the digital control output constant** over each sampling interval $T_s$.
- Mathematically, its transfer function is $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$.
- It is necessary because the processor outputs a **discrete number** every $T_s$, but the power converter (PWM/inverter) needs an **analog signal** that remains valid until the next update.
- It also models the natural behaviour of the **output register + PWM latch** in microcontrollers.
- Without the ZOH, the control signal would be an impulse train, which cannot drive the plant continuously.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**Compare the position form and velocity (incremental) form of a digital PID controller.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**
- **Position form:** $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \Sigma e(j) + (K_d/T_s)[e(k)-e(k-1)]$. Output is the **absolute** control word.
- **Velocity form:** $\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k)-e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + (K_d/T_s)[e(k)-2e(k-1)+e(k-2)]$. Output is the **change** in control word.
- **Velocity form advantages:** natural anti-windup, bumpless transfer in cascade control, no need to store the integral sum.
- **Position form advantages:** direct output, easier to interpret, simpler structure.
- In industrial drives, **velocity form is preferred** when saturation/anti-windup is critical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **digital controller used for speed control of an electric drive**. Describe the role of the anti-aliasing filter, ADC, processor, ZOH, and PWM generator. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Derive the z-domain transfer function of a **Zero-Order Hold cascaded with a first-order plant** $G_p(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$. Hence obtain the discrete-time pulse transfer function. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. Explanation points to be covered:
- **Reference** $n_{ref}$: User-commanded speed set-point.
- **Summing junction**: Forms error $e(k) = n_{ref} - n_{act}$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter (AAF)**: 2nd-order Butterworth, cutoff ≈ $f_s/2$. Removes frequencies above Nyquist limit that would otherwise fold back.
- **ADC**: Samples the filtered signal at $f_s = 1/T_s$. Resolution 10–16 bits. Introduces quantization error $\le Q/2$.
- **Processor (MCU/DSP/FPGA)**: Implements the discrete PID algorithm in software. Computes $u(k)$ every $T_s$.
- **Output Register + PWM**: Latches the controller output and modulates the duty cycle of the inverter switches.
- **ZOH**: Holds the control word constant during $[kT_s, (k+1)T_s]$. Transfer function $G_h(s) = (1-e^{-sT_s})/s$.
- **Power converter**: VSI (voltage source inverter) for AC drives, chopper for DC drives.
- **Motor + Load + Tachogenerator/Encoder**: Plant and feedback sensor.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**
- Start from $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\, \mathcal{Z}\{K/[s(\tau s+1)]\}$.
- Partial fraction: $K/[s(\tau s+1)] = K/s - K/(s + 1/\tau)$.
- Z-transform using standard tables: $K[1/(1-z^{-1}) - 1/(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau}z^{-1})]$.
- Multiply by $(1-z^{-1})$ and simplify:

$$
\boxed{\,G(z) \;=\; \frac{K\,(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau})\, z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau}\, z^{-1}}\,}
$$

*(Full step-by-step derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** above.)*

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Explain the **digital implementation of a PID controller** for an electric drive. Discuss in detail the **position form** and **velocity form**, and list the steps executed inside the processor every sampling period. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a sampling time $T_s$, derive the **discrete-time transfer function** of a digital PID controller in the z-domain starting from the analog PID law. Comment on the effect of sampling time on controller performance. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Digital PID implementation (7 marks):**
- A digital PID is the **discretized version** of the analog PID, computed every $T_s$.
- **Position form** equation (write on answer sheet):

$$
u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}[e(k) - e(k-1)]
$$

- **Velocity form** equation:

$$
\Delta u(k) = K_p[e(k)-e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]
$$

- **Processor task sequence (per $T_s$):**
  1. Trigger ADC read of feedback.
  2. Compute error.
  3. Compute P, I, D terms.
  4. Sum to get $u(k)$ (or $\Delta u(k)$).
  5. Anti-windup check and saturate output.
  6. Update PWM compare register.
  7. Shift history $e(k-2) \leftarrow e(k-1) \leftarrow e(k)$.
  8. Wait for next timer interrupt.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**
- Start with analog PID: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \, de/dt$.
- Replace integral by $T_s \sum e(j)$ and derivative by backward difference.
- Apply Z-transform:

$$
D(z) = K_p + \frac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(1 - z^{-1})
$$

- Final polynomial form:

$$
D(z) = \frac{(K_p + K_i T_s + K_d/T_s) - (2K_d/T_s + K_p)z^{-1} + (K_d/T_s)z^{-2}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

- **Effect of $T_s$:**
  - Smaller $T_s$ → controller behaves closer to analog PID, wider closed-loop bandwidth, less aliasing.
  - Larger $T_s$ → ZOH phase lag dominates, bandwidth reduces, stability margins shrink, possible limit cycling.
  - Rule of thumb: closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_{BW} \le f_s / 10$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Digital Controller Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ZOH** in the discrete plant derivation — most students write $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)\}$ directly, which is **wrong**. Always include $(1-z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$.
> 2. **Using forward Euler for derivative** instead of backward difference — loses 1 mark. Backward difference is preferred because it is **realizable** (uses only past samples).
> 3. **Mixing up position and velocity forms** in the PID — examiners award marks only for the correct equation matching the question.
> 4. **Not mentioning anti-aliasing filter** in the architecture question — this is a compulsory block; omitting it costs at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring quantization error** in Part A — write the equation $Q = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ even if not asked explicitly; it shows mastery.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **digital controller** = microprocessor + ADC + DAC/PWM + ZOH, sampling at $f_s = 1/T_s$.
- ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$ — choose $f_s \approx f_{sw}$ (PWM carrier).
- ✅ **ZOH transfer function:** $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$ — *always required* in plant discretization.
- ✅ **Canonical ZOH + first-order plant result:**

$$
G(z) = \frac{K(1 - e^{-T_s/\tau})\, z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-T_s/\tau}\, z^{-1}}
$$

- ✅ **Position-form PID:** output is $u(k)$; **Velocity-form PID:** output is $\Delta u(k)$.
- ✅ **Discretized PID transfer function:**

$$
D(z) = K_p + \frac{K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(1 - z^{-1})
$$

- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory before ADC.
- ✅ **One-sample computational delay** $T_d \approx T_s$ must be considered in stability analysis.
- ✅ **Anti-windup** = clamp integrator when PWM saturates at 100 % duty cycle.
- ✅ **32-bit MCUs (STM32, TI C2000)** are the workhorse of modern digital drives.
- ✅ **Sampling time $T_s$** is the single most important tuning choice — smaller is always better up to the computational limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motor controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motor controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Motor Controller** is a regulated electrical switching and power-conditioning device that governs the operating parameters of an electric motor — namely **starting, acceleration, running, speed regulation, braking, reversing, and stopping** — by coordinating the behaviour of power semiconductor devices, electromechanical contactors, protective relays, and control logic circuits. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT525 / Module 4), motor controllers form the *intelligent execution layer* of an automated industrial drive system, sitting between the **supervisory controller** (PLC / DCS / microcontroller) and the **power actuator** (the electric motor itself).

In the broader automation hierarchy, motor controllers are the *power-handling termination* of the **ISA-95 control loop** (Field-Level). They translate low-energy control signals (typically **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC**, or **digital I/O at 24 V DC**) into precisely metered, high-power electrical energy delivered to the motor windings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAUT525 – Module 4):** The module specifically demands understanding of *solid-state motor starters*, *chopper drives for DC motors*, *VFD-based AC motor controllers*, *overload and short-circuit protection schemes*, and the *PLC–controller interface architecture*. Students must be able to *select, parameterize, and troubleshoot* industrial motor controllers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **domestic ceiling fan regulator** (the rotary wall switch). The fan motor is fixed, but the regulator changes how much electrical "push" is delivered to it — that is exactly what a motor controller does, but at a vastly more sophisticated, automated, and protected level.

A more industrial analogy: A motor controller is to an electric motor what a **car's accelerator, brake, and gearbox** are to its engine. The engine (motor) produces raw mechanical energy; the controller decides *how fast, how hard, when to stop, and how to protect from damage*.

## 1.3 Classification of Motor Controllers (KTU Classification Framework)

Motor controllers are classified on the basis of **motor type**, **control strategy**, and **switching technology**:

### A. Based on Motor Type
| Motor Type | Typical Controller |
|---|---|
| **3-Phase Induction Motor** | DOL, Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, Soft-Starter, VFD |
| **DC Shunt / Series Motor** | Chopper, Phase-controlled Rectifier (SCR) |
| **BLDC / PMSM** | Electronic Commutation Controller (ESC) |
| **Stepper / Servo Motor** | Microstep Driver, Servo Amplifier |

### B. Based on Switching Technology
| Class | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Electromechanical (Contactor-based)** | Uses contactors + bimetallic / electronic OLR | DOL, Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer starters |
| **Solid-State (Semiconductor-based)** | Uses Thyristors (SCR), IGBT, MOSFET | Soft-Starters, VFDs, DC Choppers |
| **Hybrid** | Solid-state + electromechanical backup | High-reliability industrial panels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference:** *IEC 60947-4-1* standardizes low-voltage motor starters and controllers. *IEC 61800* governs adjustable-speed drives (ASDs). KTU 2024 questions often reference these standards implicitly.

## 1.4 Functional Building Blocks of a Generic Motor Controller

A modern motor controller, regardless of type, contains the following functional blocks:

1. **Power Section** — SCRs / IGBTs / contactors that physically switch the motor supply.
2. **Control Section** — Microcontroller / DSP / analogue logic that decides *when* to switch.
3. **Sensing & Feedback Section** — Current transformers (CTs), voltage dividers, thermistors, speed encoders.
4. **Protection Section** — Overload, short-circuit, earth-fault, phase-sequence, over/under-voltage protection.
5. **HMI / Communication Section** — Local keypad, MODBUS / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Contactor:** A *contactor* is a heavy-duty, electrically-controlled, normally-open (NO) electromechanical switch designed for high inrush currents (typically **8–10× rated current** at the instant of motor starting). It is the fundamental ON/OFF switching element of a motor controller, rated per *IEC 60947-4-1*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter — The Baseline

The **Direct-On-Line (DOL) starter** connects the motor stator windings *directly* to the full rated supply voltage at the moment of starting. It is the simplest, cheapest, and most commonly used starter for small motors (typically **up to 5 kW / 7.5 HP** in industrial practice; KTU reference limit: **≤ 5 kW**).

### Operating Logic
- **Start command (PB1) energizes contactor coil (K1).**
- **Main contacts (K1-Main) close, applying full line voltage to the motor.**
- **Auxiliary contact (K1-Aux) holds the start button latched (holding contact).**
- **Stop button (PB2) breaks the holding circuit → contactor de-energizes → motor coasts to stop.**

### Mathematical Analysis of DOL Starting

For an induction motor, the starting torque and starting current at DOL conditions are:

$$T_{st, DOL} = k \cdot s_{st} \cdot I_{st}^2$$

$$I_{st, DOL} = (5 \text{ to } 7) \cdot I_{FL}$$

where:
- $T_{st, DOL}$ = Starting torque at DOL (in N·m)
- $I_{st, DOL}$ = Starting current at DOL (in Ampere)
- $I_{FL}$ = Full-load current of the motor
- $s_{st}$ = Per-unit slip at starting
- $k$ = Machine constant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference:** At DOL, although the starting torque is *high* (~150–200% of full-load torque), the starting current is **excessively high (5–7 × full-load)**. This causes severe voltage dip in the supply network — which is precisely why reduced-voltage starters are used for large motors.

## 2.2 Star-Delta Starter

The **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) starter** is a reduced-voltage starting method for 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motors that are delta-connected during normal running. The motor is first connected in **star (Y)** configuration (reducing the per-phase voltage to $1/\sqrt{3}$ of line voltage) and then switched to **delta (Δ)** once the motor accelerates to ~75–80% of synchronous speed.

### Starting Current Reduction

In star connection, phase voltage = $V_L / \sqrt{3}$. Hence:

$$I_{st, \text{star}} = \frac{I_{st, DOL}}{3}$$

$$T_{st, \text{star}} = \frac{T_{st, DOL}}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1/3 Rule (KTU Favourite):** Both starting current AND starting torque are reduced to **1/3rd of their DOL values** in star connection. This is the single most important numerical relationship for KTU problems.

## 2.3 Auto-Transformer Starter

An **Auto-Transformer Starter** uses a tapped autotransformer to apply a reduced voltage (typically at **50%, 65%, or 80% taps**) to the motor during starting. The motor draws reduced current from the line, while the autotransformer secondary delivers a fraction of that to the motor.

### Reduction Equations

Let the autotransformer tap ratio be $x$ (per-unit, e.g., 0.5, 0.65, 0.8):

$$I_{st, \text{line}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL}$$

$$T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{st, DOL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Contrast with Star-Delta:** For the *same* voltage reduction, the auto-transformer starter delivers a **higher starting torque** than star-delta. For example, at 65% tap, torque is $0.65^2 = 0.4225$ of DOL, versus only $0.333$ for star-delta. This is why auto-transformer starters are preferred for **high-inertia, high-torque loads** like compressors and large conveyors.

## 2.4 Solid-State Soft Starter

A **Soft Starter** is a solid-state thyristor-based controller that gradually ramps up the voltage applied to the motor using **phase-angle control** of back-to-back connected SCR pairs (one per phase). It eliminates the mechanical shock of sudden DOL starting.

### Voltage Equation (Phase-Angle Control)

$$V_{rms} = V_{m} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$$

where:
- $V_m$ = Peak supply voltage
- $\alpha$ = Firing angle delay (in radians)
- $V_{rms}$ = RMS voltage applied to motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Starters vs. VFDs:** A soft starter **only controls voltage** (ramp-up and ramp-down). It does **not** control frequency. Hence it cannot provide continuous speed variation below synchronous speed. A VFD controls **both voltage and frequency** (V/f ratio), enabling full speed regulation.

## 2.5 Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)

The **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** or **AFD (Adjustable Frequency Drive)** is the most versatile AC motor controller. It converts the fixed-frequency mains supply (e.g., **50 Hz** in India / KTU context) into a variable-frequency, variable-voltage output suitable for the induction motor.

### Architecture (Three Stages)
1. **Rectifier Stage:** 6-pulse diode bridge → unregulated DC bus (typically **540 V DC** for 415 V AC input).
2. **DC Link:** Filter capacitor (and optional braking resistor/regen unit) to smooth the DC.
3. **Inverter Stage:** 6-IGBT bridge with **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** or **SVPWM** modulation to synthesize the variable-frequency output.

### The Fundamental V/f Relationship

For an induction motor, the air-gap flux is given by:

$$\Phi \propto \frac{V}{f}$$

To maintain **constant flux** (and hence constant torque capability) across the speed range, the V/f ratio must be held constant. This is the **V/f control law**:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Torque Region (0 to base frequency):** V/f is maintained constant → flux is constant → torque capability is constant.
> **Constant Power Region (above base frequency):** Voltage cannot exceed rated → V becomes fixed, frequency increases → flux weakens → torque falls as $1/f$, but power remains approximately constant (field-weakening region).

### Synchronous Speed Equation (Foundational)

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$

where $N_s$ = synchronous speed in RPM, $f$ = supply frequency in Hz, $P$ = number of poles.

## 2.6 DC Motor Chopper Controller

A **Chopper** is a DC–DC converter that switches a high-voltage DC supply ON and OFF at high frequency (typically **1–20 kHz**) to deliver a controllable average voltage to a DC motor.

### First-Quadrant Chopper (Step-Down / Buck)

$$V_{o, \text{avg}} = \alpha \cdot V_s$$

$$\text{Duty Cycle: } \alpha = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T}$$

where:
- $V_{o, \text{avg}}$ = Average output (armature) voltage
- $V_s$ = Supply (DC bus) voltage
- $T$ = Chopping period
- $\alpha$ = Duty ratio (0 to 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** For a separately-excited DC motor, armature voltage control via chopper gives continuous speed control below base speed. Speed is approximately $N \propto V_a$ (field flux held constant). Choppers are classified by **quadrants of operation** — Class A (1-quadrant), Class B (2-quadrant), Class C (4-quadrant).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Use / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOL starting current | $I_{st,DOL} = 5$ to $7 \times I_{FL}$ | For motors ≤ 5 kW |
| 2 | Star-Delta current reduction | $I_{st,Y} = I_{st,DOL} / 3$ | Y-Δ starter |
| 3 | Star-Delta torque reduction | $T_{st,Y} = T_{st,DOL} / 3$ | Y-Δ starter |
| 4 | Auto-transformer reduction | $I_{st,line} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$ | $x$ = tap fraction |
| 5 | Auto-transformer torque | $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}$ | $x$ = tap fraction |
| 6 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | All AC machines |
| 7 | Induction motor slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Per-unit (0 to 1) |
| 8 | V/f control law | $V / f = \text{const}$ | Below base frequency |
| 9 | Chopper output | $V_o = \alpha V_s$ | DC motor armature |
| 10 | Braking energy | $E_b = 0.5 J \omega^2$ | Energy dissipated in resistor |
| 11 | Thermal overload | $I^2 t = \text{const}$ | Bimetallic / electronic OLR |
| 12 | Torque per ampere | $T \propto \Phi I_a$ | DC motor torque equation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Warning (LaTeX-in-Tables):** In KTU answer scripts, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value or condition bars inside tables. The pipe `|` character breaks markdown rendering in the printed answer book. Stay alert when copying table values into Word/PDF.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Motor controllers are the **unsung backbone of industrial automation**:
- **Pump stations** use VFDs for **energy savings of 30–50%** through variable flow control.
- **HVAC systems** use soft starters to avoid water-hammer in chilled-water piping.
- **Elevators and cranes** use 4-quadrant choppers for precise regenerative braking.
- **Conveyor systems** use VFDs with S-curve ramps to prevent product slippage.
- **Textile machinery** uses servo controllers for synchronized multi-axis motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Note (KTU Industrial Visit Tip):** The APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University emphasises *industrial exposure*. Look for **Allen-Bradley PowerFlex**, **Siemens SINAMICS**, **ABB ACS**, or **Schneider Altivar** VFDs in plant visits — all support MODBUS / PROFINET communication, which is the integration point for PLC-based automation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Star-Delta Starting Current and Torque Reduction (Full Derivation)

This is the **most repeated KTU 14-mark problem**. We must derive rigorously that both starting current and starting torque fall to 1/3 of their DOL values.

### Step 1: Per-Phase Voltage in Star Connection
A 3-phase induction motor has stator phase windings rated for line voltage $V_L$ in delta connection. When reconfigured in star, the same windings are subjected to the **phase voltage** of the supply, not the line voltage. For a balanced Y connection:

$$V_{\text{phase, Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 2: Per-Phase Impedance
The per-phase impedance of the stator winding is **the same** in both star and delta — it is an intrinsic property of the winding. Let it be $Z_{ph}$.

### Step 3: Starting Current Per Phase (Star)
By Ohm's law, the starting current per phase when connected in star is:

$$I_{\text{phase, Y}} = \frac{V_{\text{phase, Y}}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{ph}}$$

In star, line current equals phase current:

$$I_{\text{line, Y}} = I_{\text{phase, Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}$$

### Step 4: Starting Current Per Phase (Delta — DOL Reference)
In delta, phase voltage equals line voltage:

$$V_{\text{phase, Δ}} = V_L$$

$$I_{\text{phase, Δ}} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

But in delta, the line current is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current:

$$I_{\text{line, Δ}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{phase, Δ}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

### Step 5: Ratio of Star to Delta Line Current

$$\frac{I_{\text{line, Y}}}{I_{\text{line, Δ}}} = \frac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph})}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L / Z_{ph}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{\text{line, Y}} = \frac{I_{\text{line, Δ}}}{3} = \frac{I_{st, DOL}}{3}$$

### Step 6: Torque Relationship
The starting torque of an induction motor is proportional to the square of the applied voltage (and hence per-phase voltage):

$$T_{st} \propto V_{\text{phase}}^2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{T_{st, Y}}{T_{st, Δ}} = \left( \frac{V_{\text{phase, Y}}}{V_{\text{phase, Δ}}} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{V_L} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad T_{st, Y} = \frac{T_{st, DOL}}{3} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{st, Y} = \frac{I_{st, DOL}}{3}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Auto-Transformer Starting Reduction (Full Derivation)

Let the autotransformer have a turns ratio such that the secondary voltage applied to the motor is $V_2 = x \cdot V_L$, where $x$ is the per-unit tap (e.g., **x = 0.65** for a 65% tap).

### Step 1: Motor Starting Current (Secondary Side)
The motor draws a current from the autotransformer secondary:

$$I_{motor} = \frac{V_2}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{x \cdot V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

### Step 2: Relation to DOL Current
The DOL starting current per phase (delta-connected) is $I_{st, DOL} = V_L / Z_{ph}$. Therefore:

$$I_{motor} = x \cdot I_{st, DOL}$$

### Step 3: Line Current (Primary of Autotransformer)
For an ideal autotransformer, the ampere-turn balance gives:

$$V_L \cdot I_{\text{line}} = V_2 \cdot I_{motor} = x V_L \cdot x I_{st, DOL}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL}$$

### Step 4: Torque Relationship
Torque is proportional to the square of the applied voltage:

$$\frac{T_{st, \text{auto}}}{T_{st, DOL}} = \left( \frac{V_2}{V_L} \right)^2 = x^2$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad I_{\text{line}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{st, \text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st, DOL}}$$

### Numerical Verification (KTU-Style)
Suppose a 15 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor has $I_{st, DOL} = 6 \cdot I_{FL}$ and $T_{st, DOL} = 2 \cdot T_{FL}$. Calculate the line current and starting torque when started through a 70% tap autotransformer.

**Given:** $x = 0.70$, $I_{FL} = 30$ A (assumed), $T_{FL} = 100$ N·m (assumed).

**Calculation of DOL values:**

$$I_{st, DOL} = 6 \times 30 = 180 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, DOL} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Autotransformer starting values:**

$$I_{\text{line}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL} = 0.70^2 \times 180 = 0.49 \times 180 = 88.2 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, \text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st, DOL} = 0.49 \times 200 = 98 \text{ N·m}$$

**Comparison with star-delta (same 15 kW motor):**

$$I_{st, Y} = 180 / 3 = 60 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, Y} = 200 / 3 \approx 66.67 \text{ N·m}$$

**Inference:** At the same reduced starting current of ~60 A, the auto-transformer provides *higher* torque (98 N·m vs 66.67 N·m). Hence autotransformer is superior for high-inertia loads.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — VFD Output Frequency and Speed Control

For a 4-pole, 3-phase induction motor rated for 50 Hz, calculate the frequency required to operate it at 900 RPM at full torque.

**Given:** $P = 4$, desired $N_r = 900$ RPM, full torque region ($V/f$ constant).

**Step 1:** Assume rated slip of 4% (typical for industrial motors).

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = 0.04 \implies N_s = \frac{N_r}{1 - s} = \frac{900}{0.96} = 937.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2:** Required frequency from synchronous speed equation:

$$f = \frac{N_s \cdot P}{120} = \frac{937.5 \times 4}{120} = \frac{3750}{120} = 31.25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3:** Required voltage (constant V/f):

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ V/Hz}$$

$$V_{required} = 8 \times 31.25 = 250 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** To run this 4-pole motor at 900 RPM with full torque, the VFD must output **31.25 Hz at 250 V**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** Students often confuse the synchronous speed $N_s$ with rotor speed $N_r$. Always remember: **$N_s$ depends only on frequency and pole count; $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$** where slip $s$ is typically 2–6% at full load.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DC Chopper Speed Equation

A **separately excited DC motor** has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, field flux $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $K_a = 50$ (machine constant), supply $V_s = 220$ V. The chopper operates at 2 kHz with $T_{ON} = 0.2$ ms. Find the no-load speed.

**Step 1:** Calculate the duty cycle:

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = \frac{T_{ON}}{1 / f} = T_{ON} \cdot f = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2000 = 0.4$$

**Step 2:** Average armature voltage:

$$V_a = \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = 88 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** No-load back-EMF (assume $I_a \approx 0$ at no-load):

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a \approx 88 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** No-load speed (using $E_b = K_a \Phi N$):

$$N_0 = \frac{E_b}{K_a \Phi} = \frac{88}{50 \times 0.05} = \frac{88}{2.5} = 35.2 \text{ RPS}$$

$$N_0 = 35.2 \times 60 = 2112 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5:** Inference: By reducing $\alpha$ (i.e., reducing $T_{ON}$), $V_a$ reduces → back-EMF reduces → speed reduces. This is the **armature voltage control** principle.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Trans (70%) | Soft-Starter | VFD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage at motor | 100% | 58% ($1/\sqrt{3}$) | 70% | Variable ramp | Variable |
| Starting current (line) | 6× I_FL | 2× I_FL | ~2.94× I_FL | Adjustable 2–5× | Adjustable |
| Starting torque (% of T_FL) | 150–200% | 50–67% | 98% | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| Speed control below base | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Energy saving | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | Limited | ✅ Up to 50% |
| Initial cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Highest |
| Typical application range | ≤ 5 kW | 5–20 kW | 10–100 kW | 5–500 kW | All ranges |
| Closed-loop control | No | No | No | Limited | ✅ Yes (PID) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** If the motor is below 5 kW → use **DOL**. If 5–20 kW and load has light starting torque → use **Star-Delta**. If load has high inertia and starting torque needed → use **Auto-Transformer** or **Soft-Starter**. If continuous speed variation or energy saving is required → use **VFD**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) Architecture

This block-level functional architecture shows the **three power-conversion stages** of a modern VFD and the **control / feedback paths** that govern its operation. The diagram is rendered as a top-down functional flow, which KTU students are expected to draw and explain in the ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeACInput[THREE PHASE AC INPUT 415V 50HZ] --> nodeRectifier[SIX PULSE DIODE RECTIFIER STAGE]
    nodeRectifier --> nodeDCLink[DC BUS LINK CAPACITOR SMOOTHING 540V DC]
    nodeDCLink --> nodeBrake[BRAKING UNIT AND RESISTOR]
    nodeBrake --> nodeInverter[SIX IGBT INVERTER BRIDGE SPWM MODULATION]
    nodeDCLink --> nodeInverter
    nodeInverter --> nodeMotor[THREE PHASE AC INDUCTION MOTOR]
    nodeMotor --> nodeEncoder[INCREMENTAL ENCODER SPEED FEEDBACK]
    nodeEncoder --> nodeCtrl[DSP MICROCONTROLLER VECTOR CONTROL]
    nodeCtrl --> nodeInverter
    nodeHMI[KEYPAD HMI DISPLAY] --> nodeCtrl
    nodePLC[SUPERVISORY PLC DCS] --> nodeCtrl
    nodeComm[FIELDBUS MODBUS PROFINET ETHERNET] --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl --> nodeProt[PROTECTION LOGIC OVERCURRENT OVERVOLTAGE THERMAL]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram in exam:** The forward power path is **AC Input → Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter → Motor**. The *feedback loop* is **Encoder → Controller → Inverter** (closed-loop speed control). The *braking unit* dumps regenerative energy into a resistor when the motor acts as a generator (e.g., during fast deceleration).

---

## 4.2 State Diagram — Star-Delta Starter Switching Sequence

The state diagram below captures the **timed transition logic** of a star-delta starter. KTU often asks students to *trace the contactor states* during a typical start-up.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    nodeS0[OFF STATE ALL CONTACTORS DE ENERGISED] --> nodeS1[PB1 PRESSED K1 MAIN ENERGISED]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[STAR CONTACTOR K2 CLOSES MOTOR RUNS IN STAR]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[TIMER REACHES PRESET TYPICALLY 5 TO 10 SECONDS]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[STAR CONTACTOR K2 OPENS TRANSITION INTERLOCK 50 MS]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[DELTA CONTACTOR K3 CLOSES MOTOR RUNS IN DELTA]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[PB2 PRESSED K1 DE ENERGISES MOTOR COASTS TO STOP]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeS0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Point:** Between state S4 and S5, a **50–100 ms interlock delay** is *mandatory* to prevent a direct short circuit between the star and delta contactors. This interlock is implemented using **mechanical auxiliary contacts** (normally-closed NC) or **timed logic in a PLC**.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Generic Motor Controller Functional Architecture

The diagram below represents the *universal architecture* of any motor controller — be it a simple DOL panel or a sophisticated VFD. It is the answer sketch KTU expects for *“Draw the block diagram of an industrial motor controller.”*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSupply[POWER SUPPLY 3PH 415V 50HZ] --> nodeFuse[FUSE OR MCB SHORT CIRCUIT PROTECTION]
    nodeFuse --> nodeContactor[POWER CONTACTOR K1 MAIN SWITCHING]
    nodeContactor --> nodeController[CORE CONTROL LOGIC SSR OR MICROCONTROLLER]
    nodeController --> nodeMotor[INDUCTION OR DC MOTOR]
    nodeMotor --> nodeCT[CURRENT TRANSFORMER CT SENSE]
    nodeCT --> nodeOLR[ELECTRONIC OVERLOAD RELAY EOLR]
    nodeOLR --> nodeController
    nodeMotor --> nodeTemp[THERMISTOR PT100 TEMPERATURE SENSE]
    nodeTemp --> nodeOLR
    nodePB[START STOP PUSH BUTTONS] --> nodeController
    nodePLC[PLC DIGITAL I/O 24V DC] --> nodeController
    nodeHMI[TOUCHSCREEN HMI INTERFACE] --> nodeController
    nodeBuzzer[BUZZER AND INDICATION LAMPS] --> nodeController
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always mention **IEC 60947-4-1** when describing the components, and **IEC 61800** if discussing a VFD. Including standard codes in your answer gives bonus marks and signals industrial-readiness.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram — VFD vs Soft-Starter vs Direct DOL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1[What is the application?] --> nodeQ2{Need continuous speed control below base speed?}
    nodeQ2 -->|YES| nodeVFD[USE VFD VARIABLE FREQUENCY DRIVE]
    nodeQ2 -->|NO| nodeQ3{Motor rating?}
    nodeQ3 -->|Below 5 kW| nodeDOL[USE DOL DIRECT ON LINE STARTER]
    nodeQ3 -->|5 to 20 kW| nodeQ4{High starting torque needed?}
    nodeQ3 -->|Above 20 kW| nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 -->|YES| nodeAuto[USE AUTO TRANSFORMER OR SOFT STARTER]
    nodeQ4 -->|NO| nodeSD[USE STAR DELTA STARTER]
    nodeAuto --> nodeQ5{Need ramp up and ramp down for smooth start?}
    nodeQ5 -->|YES| nodeSoft[USE SOLID STATE SOFT STARTER]
    nodeQ5 -->|NO| nodeAuto
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This flowchart embodies the *Selection Decision Tree* that KTU students should be able to reproduce from memory in a 7-mark design question. Mastering this single decision tree is worth a guaranteed 7 marks in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAUT525]**
*What is the difference between a soft starter and a VFD? Mention one industrial application for each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **soft starter** is a solid-state motor controller that uses *back-to-back thyristors* to gradually ramp up the **voltage** applied to a motor during start-up and ramp-down during stop. It does **not** change the supply frequency; hence it can only limit the inrush current — it cannot provide continuous speed control below synchronous speed.

A **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**, in contrast, is a complete **AC–DC–AC power conversion system** that varies *both voltage and frequency* of the motor supply. By maintaining a constant **V/f ratio**, it enables continuous speed control across the full operating range, with high starting torque and energy savings.

**Industrial applications:**
- **Soft Starter** — Used in *HVAC chilled water pumps* and *large fans* to eliminate water-hammer and mechanical shock during start/stop.
- **VFD** — Used in *conveyor belts, extruders, elevators, and machine tool spindles* where precise speed regulation and energy saving are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Writing *"VFD is used only for energy saving"* — this is incomplete. The VFD also provides **process control** (closed-loop PID), **soft start**, **fault diagnostics**, and **network communication**. Mention at least two of these for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAUT525]**
*Explain the function of a bimetallic thermal overload relay in a motor starter circuit. Why is an OLR essential even when the motor is protected by a circuit breaker?*

**Model Answer:**

A **bimetallic thermal Overload Relay (OLR)** protects a motor from *sustained overcurrent* that is not high enough to trip a circuit breaker. It contains a **bimetallic strip** made of two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients bonded together. When excessive current flows (typically **105–125% of set current**), the strip heats up and bends, mechanically actuating an auxiliary contact that breaks the contactor coil circuit, isolating the motor.

The OLR is essential because:
- A **circuit breaker (MCB/MCCB)** is designed for **instantaneous short-circuit protection** (magnetic trip, < 100 ms) and is *not* sensitive to small sustained overcurrents.
- An OLR has an **inverse-time characteristic** ($I^2 t = \text{const}$) — it trips faster for higher overcurrents, simulating the heating effect on motor windings.
- It is **motor-specific** — it can be set to the motor's actual full-load current (FLC), whereas a circuit breaker is sized for the wiring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *overload* (sustained, thermal, OLR handles) with *short-circuit* (instantaneous, high magnitude, breaker handles). Examiners *expect* this distinction. Get it wrong and lose 1.5 marks immediately.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer ONE of the two choices.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEAUT525 / Module 4]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 15 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of 28 A, full-load speed of 1455 RPM, full-load torque of 100 N·m, starting current of 6.5 × FLC, and starting torque of 2.0 × FLT. Calculate the starting current, starting torque, and supply-side current if the motor is started through:

   **(i)** Direct-on-line (DOL) starter.
   **(ii)** Star-Delta starter.
   **(iii)** 70% tap auto-transformer starter.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). What is the significance of maintaining a constant V/f ratio? State the field-weakening condition and its effect on torque. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Power = 15 kW, Voltage = 400 V, Frequency = 50 Hz, Poles = 4
- $I_{FL} = 28$ A, $N_{FL} = 1455$ RPM, $T_{FL} = 100$ N·m
- $I_{st, DOL} = 6.5 \times I_{FL}$, $T_{st, DOL} = 2.0 \times T_{FL}$

**Calculate DOL Values:**

$$I_{st, DOL} = 6.5 \times 28 = 182 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, DOL} = 2.0 \times 100 = 200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Calculate Star-Delta Values:**

$$I_{st, Y} = \frac{I_{st, DOL}}{3} = \frac{182}{3} = 60.67 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, Y} = \frac{T_{st, DOL}}{3} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67 \text{ N·m}$$

**Calculate Auto-Transformer Values (x = 0.70):**

$$I_{line, auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL} = (0.70)^2 \times 182 = 0.49 \times 182 = 89.18 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{st, auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{st, DOL} = 0.49 \times 200 = 98 \text{ N·m}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Starter Type | Line Current (A) | Starting Torque (N·m) |
|---|---|---|
| DOL | 182.00 | 200.00 |
| Star-Delta | 60.67 | 66.67 |
| Auto-Transformer (70%) | 89.18 | 98.00 |

**Inference:** For the same motor, the autotransformer starter gives *higher starting torque* (98 N·m) at a *moderate line current* (89.18 A) compared to star-delta — making it suitable for high-inertia loads.

---

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (refer Section 4.1):**

A VFD consists of three power stages:
1. **Rectifier (Converter):** A 6-pulse diode bridge converts 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC into unregulated DC at ~540 V.
2. **DC Link:** A large filter capacitor smooths the DC and stores energy. A **braking unit + resistor** is connected here to absorb regenerative energy during deceleration.
3. **Inverter:** A 6-IGBT bridge generates a PWM-modulated AC output of variable frequency and voltage.

**Significance of Constant V/f Ratio:**

The air-gap flux $\Phi$ of an induction motor is given by $\Phi \propto V/f$. If V/f is held constant, the flux remains constant, which in turn keeps the **torque-producing capability** of the motor constant across the speed range. This is called the **constant-torque region** of operation (0 to base frequency).

**Field-Weakening Region:**

Above the rated (base) frequency, the voltage cannot be increased beyond the rated value (motor insulation limit). Hence, $V$ becomes constant while $f$ continues to rise, causing the V/f ratio to drop. The flux $\Phi$ decreases, and the torque capability falls as $1/f$. However, the power $P = T \omega$ remains approximately constant — this is the **constant-power region** (field-weakening region).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Auto-Transformer vs Star-Delta):** Examiners often award a 2-mark bonus for students who explicitly state the *inference* (last paragraph) showing engineering judgment — that is, which starter is best for which load. Students who only compute numbers without concluding which starter is preferable for *this* motor lose 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEAUT525 / Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of a 4-quadrant chopper drive for a separately excited DC motor. Show how regenerative braking is achieved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare DOL, Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, Soft-Starter, and VFD starters based on: (i) voltage applied to motor, (ii) starting current, (iii) starting torque, (iv) speed control capability, and (v) typical applications. Justify which controller you would recommend for a **centrifugal pump** of 30 kW rating. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

A **4-quadrant chopper (Class-C chopper)** is built using **four IGBT switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an H-bridge configuration across the DC motor armature. The motor can operate in all four quadrants of the speed-torque plane:

| Quadrant | Mode | Active Switches | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Forward Motoring | S1, S2 | Energy flows from supply to motor |
| **II** | Forward Braking | S1, S4 (or S3, S2) | Motor acts as generator |
| **III** | Reverse Motoring | S3, S4 | Energy flows from supply to motor (reversed) |
| **IV** | Reverse Braking | S3, S2 (or S1, S4) | Motor acts as generator (reversed) |

**Regenerative Braking Operation (Forward Braking — Quadrant II):**

When the motor is running in forward direction and braking is commanded, the supply is disconnected and the motor's kinetic energy drives the back-EMF. With switches S1 and S4 ON, the motor's back-EMF pushes current through S1 → motor armature → S4 → back through the supply (or to a braking resistor). The energy of the rotating mass is returned to the source (or dissipated as heat in a braking resistor), causing the motor to decelerate rapidly.

**Equation for Energy Recovered:**

$$E_{brake} = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2$$

where $J$ = moment of inertia of the rotating mass (kg·m²), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s).

This energy, if not regenerated, is dissipated as heat in a **braking resistor**, often sized as:

$$R_{brake} = \frac{V_{s, \text{max}}^2}{P_{brake, \text{max}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse *dynamic braking* (resistor-based, dissipative) with *regenerative braking* (energy returned to source). Examiners in 2024 ESE specifically expect this distinction. Mention both and explain when each is used.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (as derived in Section 3.5):**

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Trans | Soft-Starter | VFD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage applied | 100% | 58% | Adjustable (50–80%) | Ramp 30–100% | Variable |
| Starting current (line) | 6× FL | 2× FL | $(x^2) \times 6$ FL | Adjustable 2–5× FL | Adjustable |
| Starting torque | 150–200% FL | 50–67% FL | $x^2 \times 200\%$ FL | Adjustable | Up to 200% FL |
| Speed control | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Full range |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Highest |

**Recommendation for a 30 kW Centrifugal Pump:**

**Recommended Controller: VFD**

**Justification:**
1. **Energy Savings (most important):** Centrifugal pumps follow the **Affinity Laws**: $P \propto N^3$. Reducing pump speed to 80% cuts power consumption to **~51%** — a *massive* energy saving.
2. **Process Control:** Flow rate can be precisely matched to demand by varying motor speed instead of using throttling valves (which waste energy).
3. **Soft Starting:** The VFD provides a smooth ramp-up, eliminating *water hammer* in the piping system, which is a common cause of pump failure.
4. **Long Payback Period:** A 30 kW pump running 8 hours/day with VFD control typically recovers the VFD's cost through energy savings in **6–18 months**.
5. **30 kW is within the practical range of a VFD** — economical to purchase and install.

**If cost is severely constrained, an alternative is a Soft-Starter** (sufficient for the soft-start requirement), but it lacks the energy-saving benefit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Choosing DOL for 30 kW):** If a student recommends DOL for a 30 kW pump, the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks because DOL is unsuitable for motors > 5 kW (high inrush disrupts the supply network). Always check the **kW rating** before selecting a starter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Motor Controllers**

- **Definition:** A motor controller is a switching and protection device that governs starting, stopping, speed, and braking of an electric motor.
- **IEC Standards:** *IEC 60947-4-1* (low-voltage starters), *IEC 61800* (adjustable-speed drives).
- **DOL Starter:** Direct connection; $I_{st} = 5$ to $7 \times I_{FL}$; suitable for motors **≤ 5 kW**.
- **Star-Delta Starter:** Both $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ reduce to **1/3** of DOL values; needs 50–100 ms interlock delay; suitable for **5–20 kW**.
- **Auto-Transformer Starter:** $I_{st,line} = x^2 \cdot I_{st, DOL}$ and $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{st, DOL}$; offers higher torque per unit current than star-delta; suitable for **high-inertia** loads.
- **Soft-Starter:** Thyristor-based phase-angle control; gradual voltage ramp; **no** frequency or speed control.
- **VFD (AFD):** AC → DC → AC conversion; **V/f = const.** for constant torque; field-weakening above base frequency.
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Chopper (DC):** $V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s$ where $\alpha = T_{ON}/T$; 1-quadrant, 2-quadrant, 4-quadrant types.
- **Braking Methods:** Plugging (reverse supply), Dynamic (resistor), Regenerative (energy returned to source).
- **Protection Devices:** MCB/MCCB (short-circuit), OLR (overload, inverse-time $I^2 t$), ELCB/RCD (earth fault), phase-sequence relay.
- **Contactor:** Heavy-duty NO switch; auxiliary contacts for holding and interlock.
- **Bimetallic OLR:** Two-metal strip; bends on heating; inverse-time characteristic.
- **Communication Protocols:** MODBUS RTU, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT — all are fieldbus options for VFD–PLC integration.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** < 5 kW → DOL; 5–20 kW light load → Star-Delta; high torque → Auto-Trans; soft start only → Soft-Starter; speed control + energy saving → **VFD**.
- **Common Pitfall 1:** Mixing up *overload* (sustained) and *short-circuit* (instantaneous) protection.
- **Common Pitfall 2:** Computing $N_r$ instead of $N_s$ in VFD problems — remember the slip.
- **Common Pitfall 3:** Writing V/f = constant above base frequency — wrong; it is **constant torque region** that obeys V/f = const, not the field-weakening region.
- **Key Constant:** $N_s = 120 f / P$ applies to *all* AC synchronous and induction machines.
- **Energy saving fact:** Centrifugal pump power $P \propto N^3$ — 10% speed reduction gives ~27% power saving (Affinity Law, 1st-kind, 3rd power).
- **Industrial brands (for industrial-visit vocabulary):** Siemens SINAMICS, ABB ACS, Allen-Bradley PowerFlex, Schneider Altivar, Danfoss VLT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axis controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Axis controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of modern industrial automation, an **Axis Controller** is a dedicated, high-performance motion control subsystem — typically a digital servo drive or CNC axis-drive module — that regulates the position, velocity, and torque of a single mechanical axis (linear slide, rotary table, spindle, robot joint, etc.) in real time. The axis controller closes inner current/torque, velocity, and outer position feedback loops, accepts setpoint trajectories generated by a higher-level numerical controller (CNC/PLC), and drives a permanent-magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), brushless DC (BLDC) motor, or induction motor through a PWM/space-vector inverter.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAUT525 – Electric Drives and Controls for Automation (Module 4)**, axis controllers are studied as the *building blocks of multi-axis motion-control systems*, with emphasis on the **cascade control architecture**, **PID/PI tuning**, **feed-forward compensation**, and the **interpolation logic** that links several axes together to produce synchronized motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** The official syllabus statement covers *"axis controllers – position, velocity, and current control loops; P/PI/PID tuning; feed-forward control; trajectory generation and interpolation in multi-axis systems."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> An **axis controller** is a hierarchical, multi-loop digital control unit that converts a commanded trajectory (position vs. time) of a single motion axis into a precisely regulated mechanical motion by closing nested feedback loops on **current → velocity → position**, with the inner loops being at least an order of magnitude faster than the outer loop (typical ratio 1 : 5 : 25).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road at exactly the posted speed limit.

- Your **eyes** measuring the speedometer → these are the **position/velocity sensors** (encoders, resolvers).
- Your **brain** deciding how hard to press the accelerator → this is the **outer position/velocity controller** (P/PI/PID).
- Your **foot on the pedal** controlling fuel flow → this is the **inner current/torque controller** (fast PI loop).
- The **engine, transmission, and wheels** → these are the **motor + power converter + mechanical load**.
- The **GPS showing the curve ahead** → this is the **feed-forward path**, which *predicts* what torque will be needed before the error even develops.

If you want *three cars* to drive side-by-side in perfect formation, you need a **traffic coordinator (interpolator)** who tells each driver (axis controller) where to be at every instant. That coordinator is the **CNC/PLC motion planner**, and the drivers are the **axis controllers**. The quality of formation-keeping is the **contouring accuracy** of the multi-axis system.

This "driver-car-GPS" mental image makes the cascade structure, the role of feed-forward, and the need for a master interpolator instantly intuitive.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standardized engineering quantities govern every axis-controller design and will be referenced throughout the KTU examination:

- **Sampling time of current loop: T_i = 50 µs to 100 µs (10 kHz – 20 kHz PWM)** — Industry standard for servo drives.
- **Sampling time of velocity loop: T_v = 0.5 ms to 1 ms (1 kHz – 2 kHz)**.
- **Sampling time of position loop: T_p = 1 ms to 4 ms (250 Hz – 1 kHz)**.
- **Bandwidth ratio rule: ω_current : ω_velocity : ω_position ≈ 10 : 5 : 1** (for critically-damped cascade).
- **Standard quadrature encoder resolution: 2^N lines per revolution**, with ×4 decoding giving **4 × 2^N counts/rev** (e.g., 10,000-line encoder → 40,000 counts/rev).
- **Servo-update cycle: 250 µs (4 kHz), 500 µs (2 kHz), 1 ms (1 kHz)** — typical CNC axis-controller scan times.
- **Position loop proportional gain unit: 1/s (s⁻¹)** — represents bandwidth in rad/s.
- **Velocity loop integral time constant: T_n = 0.5 s to 5 s** for industrial servos.
- **Following error at constant velocity v: e_ss = v / K_p** where **K_p** is the position-loop gain in **s⁻¹**.
- **Maximum permissible following error: e_max = ±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm** for precision CNC machining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in the exam:** KTU frequently asks *"Why is the inner loop faster than the outer loop?"* The answer lies in the **separation of time-scales**: a current disturbance (load impact) settles in < 1 ms, a velocity ripple settles in < 10 ms, and a position error settles in < 100 ms. Each outer loop treats the inner loop as an ideal, instantaneous plant.

---

## 1.4 Functional Role of an Axis Controller in a Multi-Axis Machine

A modern CNC machine or 6-axis robot contains **N identical axis controllers**, one per joint, coordinated by a **central interpolator (look-ahead planner)**. The hierarchy is:

1. **HMI / Part Program** → G-code (`G01 X100 Y50 F500`).
2. **Interpolator** → breaks the commanded path into 1-ms segments and produces per-axis position setpoints `x_r(k)`, `y_r(k)`, `z_r(k)`.
3. **N × Axis Controllers** → each independently closes the cascade loop and drives its motor.
4. **Feedback devices** → incremental/absolute encoders, linear scales, resolvers, Hall sensors.
5. **Mechanical axes** → ball-screw + LM guide, or direct-drive torque motor.

This **distributed-intelligence architecture** is what allows a 5-axis CNC to contour a turbine blade with ±5 µm accuracy at 5 m/min feed-rate.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Cascade Architecture (Why Three Loops?)

The single most examinable concept in Module 4 is the **three-loop cascade** (also called the **TVC structure**: Torque-Velocity-Cascaded position). Each loop has a distinct *plant*, *actuator*, and *disturbance spectrum*:

| Loop (innermost → outermost) | Plant (what is being controlled) | Actuator | Sensor | Disturbance rejected | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current / Torque loop** | Motor winding (R–L circuit) | Inverter IGBT/MOSFET (PWM) | Shunt resistor, Hall LEM, or AD converter on phase current | Load-torque ripple, supply transients, dead-time errors | **ω_i = 1–2 kHz** (electrical) |
| **Velocity loop** | Motor + load inertia J | Sum of torque-producing current (output of current loop) | Encoder/tacho (incremental encoder differentiated) | Friction, cogging, load inertia changes | **ω_v = 100–500 Hz** |
| **Position loop** | Velocity-integrated plant | Velocity command (output of velocity loop) | Position counter from encoder index | Mechanical backlash, thermal drift, gravity | **ω_p = 20–100 Hz** |

### 2.1.1 The 5–10× Bandwidth Rule (Golden Rule of Cascade Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule (Board-Favorite):** For a cascade loop to behave as if each inner loop is "instantaneous" relative to the next, the inner loop's closed-loop bandwidth must be **at least 5× to 10× larger** than the outer loop's bandwidth. If violated, the cascade becomes coupled and unstable.

Mathematically, if the **inner loop bandwidth = ω_i** and **outer loop crossover = ω_o**, then:

$$\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_o} \;\geq\; 5 \;\;\text{(minimum acceptable)}$$

$$\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_o} \;\geq\; 10 \;\;\text{(textbook ideal)}$$

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Plant Models (Per Loop)

### 2.2.1 Current Loop — Plant Transfer Function

The DC-link-fed PMSM/BLDC stator winding is modelled as a series R–L circuit, where R is stator resistance and L is the d-axis (or q-axis) inductance. The plant from inverter duty-ratio `d(s)` to phase current `I(s)` is:

$$G_i(s) = \frac{I(s)}{V_d(s)} = \frac{1/R}{1 + s\,\frac{L}{R}} = \frac{K_m}{1 + s\,T_a}$$

where:

- $T_a = L / R$ is the **electrical time constant** (typical: **0.5 ms to 5 ms**).
- $K_m = 1 / R$ (A/V) is the **static gain**.

For a PI current controller $C_i(s) = K_{pi} + K_{ii}/s$, the open-loop transfer function becomes Type-1, and the closed-loop is critically damped when:

$$K_{ii} = \frac{R}{L}, \qquad K_{pi} = \frac{L}{2\,T_{\Sigma i}}$$

where $T_{\Sigma i}$ is the sum of small time-delays (PWM update, ADC conversion, current sensing).

### 2.2.2 Velocity Loop — Plant Transfer Function

The plant from commanded torque `T_e(s)` (or equivalently, q-axis current `I_q(s)`) to angular velocity `ω(s)` is a **pure integrator** with inertia $J$ and viscous friction $B$:

$$G_v(s) = \frac{\omega(s)}{T_e(s)} = \frac{1/J}{s\,(1 + s\,T_m)} \quad\text{where}\quad T_m = \frac{J}{B}$$

In practice, the **friction term B is small**, so the plant is dominated by the integrator $1/(J s)$. A PI controller $C_v(s) = K_{pv} + K_{iv}/s$ yields a closed-loop with natural frequency $\omega_{nv} = \sqrt{K_{iv}/J}$ and damping $\zeta_v = K_{pv}/(2\sqrt{K_{iv} J})$.

### 2.2.3 Position Loop — Plant Transfer Function

The position plant is a **double integrator** (the velocity loop's integrator plus the position-integrator from ω to θ):

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{\omega_{ref}(s)} = \frac{1}{s}$$

A **P-only controller** $C_p(s) = K_{pp}$ is sufficient and is the **industry standard** (adding integral makes the system sluggish and risks overshoot). Closed-loop transfer function:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_{pp}}{s^2 + K_{pp}} = \frac{\omega_{np}^2}{s^2 + \omega_{np}^2}$$

This is a **critically-damped second-order system** with natural frequency $\omega_{np} = \sqrt{K_{pp}}$ (in rad/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a P-controller (not PI) for position?** Because the *velocity loop already contains an integrator*, adding a second integrator via I-action in the position loop produces a Type-2 system with poor stability margins and overshoot on direction reversal (a common KTU exam question: *"Why not use a PI position controller?"*).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-ready** — reproduce it verbatim in your answer scripts for full credit.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Symbol | Units | Used in |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_i(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{1 + s\,T_a}$ | Current-loop plant | A/V | Current controller design |
| 2 | $T_a = L/R$ | Electrical time constant | s | Motor data |
| 3 | $K_{pi} = \dfrac{L}{2\,T_{\Sigma i}}$ | Current P-gain (sym. optimum) | V/A | Current loop tuning |
| 4 | $K_{ii} = R/L$ | Current I-gain (sym. optimum) | V/(A·s) | Current loop tuning |
| 5 | $G_v(s) = \dfrac{1/J}{s\,(1 + s\,T_m)}$ | Velocity plant | rad/(N·m·s) | Velocity loop design |
| 6 | $K_{pv} = 2\zeta_v \omega_{nv} J$ | Velocity P-gain | N·m·s/rad | Velocity loop tuning |
| 7 | $K_{iv} = \omega_{nv}^2 J$ | Velocity I-gain | N·m/rad | Velocity loop tuning |
| 8 | $G_p(s) = 1/s$ | Position plant | — | Position loop design |
| 9 | $K_{pp} = \omega_{np}^2$ | Position P-gain (P-only) | s⁻² = 1/s² | Position loop tuning |
| 10 | $e_{ss}(\text{constant vel.}) = v / K_{pp}$ | Steady-state lag | mm or rad | Following-error check |
| 11 | $v_{ff} = K_{vff} \cdot \dot{x}_{ref}$ | Velocity feed-forward | mm/s | Trajectory tracking |
| 12 | $a_{ff} = K_{aff} \cdot J \cdot \ddot{x}_{ref}$ | Acceleration feed-forward | A (current) | Disturbance rejection |
| 13 | $\omega_i : \omega_v : \omega_p = 10 : 5 : 1$ | Bandwidth rule | Hz | Cascade design |
| 14 | $e_{max} = v_{max} / K_{pp}$ | Max following error | mm | Machine spec check |
| 15 | $T_{settle} \approx 4/\zeta\omega_n$ | Settling time (2% band) | s | Transient response |

**Symmetric Optimum / Technical Optimum Tuning** (very common in KTU derivations):

- **Current loop (symmetric optimum):** $K_{pi} = L / (2 T_{\Sigma i})$, $\;T_{ni} = L/R$
- **Velocity loop (technical optimum):** $T_{nv} = 2 T_{\Sigma v}$, $\;K_{pv} = J / (2 T_{\Sigma v})$
- **Position loop:** $K_{pp} = 1 / (4 T_{\Sigma p}^2)$ (for critically-damped response)

---

## 2.4 Feed-Forward Control — Anticipating the Trajectory

A **feedback-only** controller always lags the reference. To reduce following error *without increasing K_p* (which would reduce stability margins), we add **two feed-forward paths**:

1. **Velocity Feed-Forward (VFF):** bypasses the velocity integrator.
2. **Acceleration Feed-Forward (AFF):** directly commands the torque needed to accelerate the load.

The total velocity command becomes:

$$\omega_{cmd}(s) = K_{pp} \cdot e_{\theta}(s) + K_{vff} \cdot \dot{x}_{ref}(s)$$

The total torque/current command becomes:

$$I_{q,cmd}(s) = K_{iv}/s \cdot e_{\omega}(s) + K_{aff} \cdot J \cdot \ddot{x}_{ref}(s) + \underbrace{B \cdot \omega_{ref}(s)}_{\text{friction FF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** The **velocity feed-forward coefficient is set to unity (K_vff = 1)** for ideal tracking, and the **acceleration feed-forward is set to J (the total moving inertia)** for perfect disturbance cancellation. In practice, both are tuned to 70–100% to compensate for un-modelled friction and flexible modes.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Axis controllers are the workhorse of:

- **CNC machining centres** (Haas, DMG MORI, Mazak) — 3 to 5 axes, ±1 µm positioning.
- **Semiconductor lithography** (ASML, Nikon) — 6 axes of stage + 6 axes of mask, sub-nanometre precision.
- **Industrial robots** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) — 6 to 7 axes, < 50 µm repeatability.
- **Pick-and-place machines** (Siemens, ASM) — 12+ axes synchronized at kHz rates.
- **Pickup-and-place SMT heads** — 50,000 placements/hour with ±25 µm accuracy.
- **Servo presses & electric injection moulding machines** — high-bandwidth axis controllers replace hydraulics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry metric:** A state-of-the-art axis controller (e.g., Bosch Rexroth IndraDrive, Siemens SINAMICS S120) achieves **current-loop bandwidth 2 kHz, velocity-loop bandwidth 500 Hz, position-loop bandwidth 100 Hz**, with **following error < 1 µm at 1 m/s feed-rate**. These numbers come directly from the cascade design equations in this section — *this is why mastery of the formulas above guarantees 14-mark Part-B answers in the KTU ESE.*
