# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is **exhaustive**. Every algebraic transition is shown line-by-line. Use these derivations as a template for the 14-mark KTU ESE Part-B questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of the Position Loop (P-Controller)

**Given:**
- Position plant: $G_p(s) = 1/s$
- Position controller: $C_p(s) = K_{pp}$ (P-only, as per industrial standard)
- Velocity loop assumed to be ideal (unity gain, no lag)

**Find:** Closed-loop transfer function $\theta(s)/\theta_{ref}(s)$, natural frequency, and damping ratio.

**Step 1:** Write the open-loop transfer function.

$$G_{OL}(s) = C_p(s) \cdot G_p(s) = K_{pp} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K_{pp}}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard negative-feedback closed-loop formula $G_{CL} = G_{OL} / (1 + G_{OL})$.

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_{pp}/s}{1 + K_{pp}/s}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$ to clear the complex fraction.

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_{pp}}{s + K_{pp}}$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $K_{pp}$ to put in standard first-order form.

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s/K_{pp}} = \frac{1}{1 + s\,\tau_p}$$

where the **position-loop time constant** is $\tau_p = 1/K_{pp}$ (seconds).

**Step 5 (alternative cascade view):** If the velocity loop is modelled as a first-order lag $\dfrac{1}{1 + s T_v}$, then the position closed-loop becomes second-order:

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_{pp}/s}{1 + K_{pp}\cdot(1/s)\cdot(1/(1+sT_v))} = \frac{K_{pp}(1+sT_v)}{s(1+sT_v) + K_{pp}}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$s^2 T_v + s + K_{pp} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s^2 + \frac{s}{T_v} + \frac{K_{pp}}{T_v} = 0$$

Comparing with the standard second-order characteristic equation $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$\omega_{np} = \sqrt{\frac{K_{pp}}{T_v}}, \qquad \zeta_p = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K_{pp}\,T_v}}$$

**Step 6 (settling-time formula):** Using the 2% settling time $T_{settle} \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$:

$$T_{settle} = \frac{4}{2\zeta_p \omega_{np}} = \frac{4\,T_v}{\sqrt{K_{pp}\,T_v}} = 4\sqrt{T_v / K_{pp}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the answer script, present the **closed-loop transfer function in both forms** (first-order equivalent and full second-order) and explicitly state the natural frequency and damping ratio. This is the 7-mark *core* of any 14-mark position-controller problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Symmetric-Optimum Tuning of the Current Loop

**Given:** Current plant $G_i(s) = K_m / (1 + s T_a)$ with $K_m = 1/R$, $T_a = L/R$. A small time-delay $T_{\Sigma i}$ represents PWM, ADC, and computation delays (in series).

**Find:** PI gains $K_{pi}$, $K_{ii}$ that yield critical damping and minimum integrated absolute error (IAE).

**Step 1:** Total plant (including delay approximation $1/(1+sT_{\Sigma i})$):

$$G_{i,tot}(s) = \frac{K_m}{(1+sT_a)(1+sT_{\Sigma i})}$$

**Step 2:** Choose PI controller $C_i(s) = K_{pi} \cdot (1 + 1/(s T_{ni}))$. Open-loop TF:

$$G_{OL}(s) = K_{pi}\cdot\frac{1+sT_{ni}}{sT_{ni}} \cdot \frac{K_m}{(1+sT_a)(1+sT_{\Sigma i})}$$

**Step 3 (symmetric optimum pole-zero cancellation):** Choose the integral time $T_{ni}$ to cancel the dominant electrical pole $T_a$:

$$T_{ni} = T_a = L/R$$

This makes the open-loop transfer function:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_{pi} K_m}{s\,T_a (1+sT_{\Sigma i})}$$

**Step 4:** Open-loop is now a Type-1 system with one integrator and a small lag. Closed-loop characteristic equation:

$$1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s\,T_a(1+sT_{\Sigma i}) + K_{pi}K_m = 0$$

$$T_a T_{\Sigma i}\,s^2 + T_a\,s + K_{pi}K_m = 0$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $T_a T_{\Sigma i}$ to normalize:

$$s^2 + \frac{1}{T_{\Sigma i}}\,s + \frac{K_{pi}K_m}{T_a T_{\Sigma i}} = 0$$

**Step 6 (critical damping condition):** For critical damping, $\zeta = 1$, so the damping term equals $2\omega_n$. With $\omega_n^2 = K_{pi}K_m/(T_a T_{\Sigma i})$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = 1/T_{\Sigma i}$:

$$2\sqrt{\frac{K_{pi}K_m}{T_a T_{\Sigma i}}} = \frac{1}{T_{\Sigma i}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{4\,K_{pi}\,K_m}{T_a T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{1}{T_{\Sigma i}^2}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $K_{pi}$:

$$K_{pi} = \frac{T_a}{4\,K_m\,T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{(L/R)}{4\cdot(1/R)\cdot T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{L}{4\,T_{\Sigma i}}$$

**Step 8:** The integral gain is:

$$K_{ii} = \frac{K_{pi}}{T_{ni}} = \frac{L}{4\,T_{\Sigma i}}\cdot\frac{R}{L} = \frac{R}{4\,T_{\Sigma i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the factor of 4 (not 2):** The symmetric-optimum criterion uses *minimum IAE* (integrated absolute error), which is *not* the same as critical damping. Many KTU solutions mistakenly write $K_{pi} = L/(2T_{\Sigma i})$ — that is the **technical optimum** for the *velocity loop* with $T_{\Sigma v}$ ≈ $2T_{\Sigma i}$. Be precise in the exam.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity-Loop Technical-Optimum Tuning

**Given:** Velocity plant $G_v(s) = \dfrac{1/J}{s\,(1+s T_m)}$ with $T_m = J/B$. The current loop is closed and approximated as a first-order lag $1/(1+sT_{eq})$ where $T_{eq} \approx 2T_{\Sigma i}$.

**Find:** PI gains $K_{pv}$, $K_{iv}$ for technical-optimum (closed-loop critical damping).

**Step 1:** Total plant (current loop included):

$$G_{v,tot}(s) = \frac{1}{J s (1+sT_m)(1+sT_{eq})}$$

**Step 2:** Choose PI $C_v(s) = K_{pv}\cdot(1 + 1/(sT_{nv}))$. Cancel the dominant mechanical lag by setting $T_{nv} = T_m$:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_{pv}/J}{s^2\,T_m(1+sT_{eq})}$$

**Step 3:** Closed-loop characteristic equation (set $1+G_{OL}=0$):

$$s^2 T_m(1+sT_{eq}) + K_{pv}/J = 0$$

$$T_m T_{eq}\,s^3 + T_m\,s^2 + K_{pv}/J = 0$$

**Step 4 (technical optimum for a Type-2 plant):** The technical-optimum condition places both slow poles of the cubic at $s = -1/T_{eq}$ (double real pole). This forces:

$$(1+sT_{eq})^2 \cdot s \cdot \alpha = T_m T_{eq}\,s^3 + T_m\,s^2 + K_{pv}/J$$

Matching coefficients of $s^2$: $2\alpha T_{eq} = T_m \Rightarrow \alpha = T_m/(2T_{eq})$.
Matching coefficients of $s^3$: $\alpha T_{eq}^2 = T_m T_{eq} \Rightarrow \alpha = T_m/T_{eq}$.

**Conflict!** The technical-optimum for a Type-2 system must include a *low-pass filter* $1/(1+sT_{eq})$ in the controller output. Including this filter and re-deriving yields:

$$K_{pv} = \frac{J}{2\,T_{eq}}, \qquad K_{iv} = \frac{J}{2\,T_{eq}^2}$$

**Step 5 (final result, often used in textbooks):**

$$K_{pv} = \frac{J}{2\,T_{\Sigma v}}, \qquad K_{iv} = \frac{J}{(2\,T_{\Sigma v})^2} = \frac{J}{4\,T_{\Sigma v}^2}$$

where $T_{\Sigma v} = T_m + T_{eq}$ is the sum of small time constants in the velocity loop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Following-Error Formula with Feed-Forward

**Given:** Position loop with P-controller and velocity feed-forward:

$$\omega_{cmd} = K_{pp}\,(\theta_{ref} - \theta) + K_{vff}\,\dot{\theta}_{ref}$$

**Find:** Steady-state following error $e_{ss} = \theta_{ref} - \theta$ at constant velocity $v_0$.

**Step 1:** Plant (assuming velocity loop is ideal): $\dot{\theta} = \omega_{cmd}$.

$$\dot{\theta} = K_{pp}\,e(t) + K_{vff}\,v_0$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $e(t) = \theta_{ref} - \theta$ and substitute $\dot{\theta}_{ref} = v_0$:

$$\dot{e} = \dot{\theta}_{ref} - \dot{\theta} = v_0 - K_{pp}\,e - K_{vff}\,v_0$$

**Step 3:** At steady state, $\dot{e} = 0$ and $e = e_{ss}$:

$$0 = v_0 - K_{pp}\,e_{ss} - K_{vff}\,v_0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $e_{ss}$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v_0\,(1 - K_{vff})}{K_{pp}}$$

**Step 5 (interpretation):** With $K_{vff} = 1$ (ideal), $e_{ss} = 0$ — *perfect tracking*. With $K_{vff} = 0$ (no FF), $e_{ss} = v_0 / K_{pp}$ — proportional lag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** The KTU answer to "Why is VFF = 1 not always used in practice?" is: *sensor noise on the differentiated reference* and *flexible-mode excitation*. In real machines, $K_{vff} = 0.7$ to $0.95$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Procedure for a Complete Axis Controller

| Step | Action | Equation / Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify motor & load parameters | $R, L, J, B, K_t, K_e, P$ | Motor data-sheet |
| 2 | Compute electrical & mechanical TCs | $T_a = L/R$, $\;T_m = J/B$ | Time constants |
| 3 | Set PWM / sampling frequency | $f_{PWM} = 1/T_{\Sigma i}$ | Hardware choice |
| 4 | Tune current PI (symm. optimum) | $K_{pi} = L/(4T_{\Sigma i})$, $K_{ii} = R/(4T_{\Sigma i})$ | $C_i(s)$ |
| 5 | Verify current bandwidth | $\omega_i \approx 1/(2T_{\Sigma i})$ | > 1 kHz |
| 6 | Tune velocity PI (tech. optimum) | $K_{pv} = J/(2T_{\Sigma v})$, $K_{iv} = J/(4T_{\Sigma v}^2)$ | $C_v(s)$ |
| 7 | Verify velocity bandwidth | $\omega_v = K_{pv}/(2J) = 1/(2T_{\Sigma v})$ | 100–500 Hz |
| 8 | Tune position P-gain | $K_{pp} = \omega_{np}^2$, choose $\omega_{np} = \omega_v / 5$ | $C_p(s)$ |
| 9 | Compute expected lag | $e_{ss} = v_{max}/K_{pp}$ | < spec |
| 10 | Add VFF (K_vff = 0.9) and AFF (K_aff = 0.85·J) | See Section 2.4 | Final cascade |
| 11 | Verify stability margins | Bode plot, Nyquist | $\text{PM} > 45°$, $\text{GM} > 6$ dB |
| 12 | Commission on machine | Step & ramp response | Done |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (14-Mark Template)

**Problem:** A CNC X-axis has a PMSM with $R = 1.2\;\Omega$, $L = 6\;\text{mH}$, $J = 1.2\times 10^{-3}\;\text{kg·m}^2$, $B = 0.02\;\text{N·m·s/rad}$, $K_t = 0.6\;\text{N·m/A}$. PWM frequency = 10 kHz. Current-loop equivalent delay $T_{\Sigma i} = 100\;\mu s$. Compute all three loop gains and predict the steady-state following error at $v = 1\;\text{m/s}$ on a 5-mm pitch ball-screw. Choose $\omega_{np} = 50\;\text{rad/s}$ for the position loop.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Electrical TC:

$$T_a = L/R = 0.006/1.2 = 5 \;\text{ms}$$

**Step 2:** Current loop (symmetric optimum):

$$K_{pi} = \frac{L}{4T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{0.006}{4 \times 100\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.006}{4\times 10^{-4}} = 15\;\text{V/A}$$

$$K_{ii} = \frac{R}{4T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{1.2}{4\times 10^{-4}} = 3000\;\text{V/(A·s)}$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent velocity-loop delay: $T_{\Sigma v} = T_{eq} + T_m/2$. With $T_{eq} = 2T_{\Sigma i} = 0.2\;\text{ms}$ and $T_m = J/B = 1.2\times10^{-3}/0.02 = 60\;\text{ms}$, the mechanical lag dominates. We approximate $T_{\Sigma v} \approx T_m = 60\;\text{ms}$ (since $T_m \gg T_{eq}$).

**Step 4:** Velocity loop (technical optimum):

$$K_{pv} = \frac{J}{2T_{\Sigma v}} = \frac{1.2\times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.06} = 0.01\;\text{N·m·s/rad}$$

$$K_{iv} = \frac{J}{4T_{\Sigma v}^2} = \frac{1.2\times 10^{-3}}{4\times (0.06)^2} = \frac{1.2\times 10^{-3}}{0.0144} = 0.0833\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 5:** Position loop (P-only, $\omega_{np} = 50$ rad/s):

$$K_{pp} = \omega_{np}^2 = 2500\;\text{s}^{-2}$$

But $K_{pp}$ is in **rotational** units. Convert to linear: the ball-screw converts motor rad to linear mm via $x = r\theta$, with $r = 5/2\pi$ mm/rad = 0.796 mm/rad. Linear $K_{pp,lin} = K_{pp}/r^2 = 2500/0.634 = 3942\;\text{s}^{-2}$.

**Step 6:** Following error at 1 m/s:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v}{K_{pp,lin}} = \frac{1000\;\text{mm/s}}{3942\;\text{s}^{-2}} \approx 0.254\;\text{mm}$$

This exceeds the typical CNC spec of 0.05 mm. **Action:** Either increase $K_{pp}$ (reduce stability margin) or activate **velocity feed-forward**.

**Step 7:** With VFF (K_vff = 0.9):

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v\,(1 - K_{vff})}{K_{pp,lin}} = \frac{1000\times 0.1}{3942} \approx 0.025\;\text{mm}\;\;\checkmark$$

This is now within spec.

**Step 8:** Settling time estimate:

$$T_{settle} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta_p\,\omega_{np}} = \frac{4}{1\times 50} = 80\;\text{ms}$$

This satisfies the typical machine cycle requirement of < 150 ms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-time answer template (8 lines, 14 marks):**
> 1. State motor parameters and compute $T_a$, $T_m$.
> 2. Apply symmetric-optimum formula → $K_{pi}$, $K_{ii}$.
> 3. Apply technical-optimum formula → $K_{pv}$, $K_{iv}$.
> 4. Choose $K_{pp} = \omega_{np}^2$ from desired bandwidth.
> 5. Compute $e_{ss} = v/K_{pp,lin}$.
> 6. Apply VFF/AFF to reduce $e_{ss}$.
> 7. Verify stability via bandwidth rule $\omega_i > 5\omega_v > 25\omega_p$.
> 8. Comment on physical realizability and design trade-offs.
