# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**"List the three control loops inside an axis controller and state the typical bandwidth ratio between them."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three nested control loops, innermost to outermost, are:

1. **Current / Torque loop** — regulates stator phase current using PWM inverter feedback from Hall/LEM sensors. Bandwidth ≈ **1–2 kHz**.
2. **Velocity loop** — regulates shaft angular velocity using encoder-derived feedback. Bandwidth ≈ **100–500 Hz**.
3. **Position loop** — regulates mechanical position using position-counter feedback. Bandwidth ≈ **20–100 Hz**.

**Bandwidth ratio rule:** $\omega_{current} : \omega_{velocity} : \omega_{position} = 10 : 5 : 1$ (minimum 5 : 1 inner-to-outer separation is required for stable cascade operation).

(1 mark for naming each loop, 1 mark for correct bandwidth ranges, 1 mark for ratio.)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is a proportional (P) controller preferred over a PI controller for the position loop in a CNC axis controller?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CNC position loop already contains **two integrators** in its forward path: (i) the position integrator `1/s` inherent in the plant, and (ii) the integrator inside the velocity-loop PI controller. Adding a third integrator (I-action in the position loop) makes the open-loop transfer function **Type-3**, which:

1. Introduces severe overshoot and ringing on **direction reversal** (the most common motion in machining).
2. Reduces the **phase margin** below acceptable limits (< 30°), risking instability.
3. Slows the transient response because the I-term must first accumulate.

Therefore, the **P-only controller** $C_p(s) = K_{pp}$ is the industry standard. Steady-state accuracy is instead achieved by **velocity and acceleration feed-forward** paths, not by I-action.

(1 mark each for the three points above.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Choice (Select ONE of A or B)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 4]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-loop cascade architecture** of a CNC axis controller. State the role of each loop, its plant, sensor, and typical bandwidth.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A servo motor has $R = 0.8\;\Omega$, $L = 4\;\text{mH}$, $J = 2\times 10^{-3}\;\text{kg·m}^2$, $B = 0.01\;\text{N·m·s/rad}$. The current-loop equivalent delay is $T_{\Sigma i} = 80\;\mu s$. Design a **PI current controller using the symmetric-optimum method**. Verify the current-loop bandwidth.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Cascade Architecture [7 marks]:**

**Block diagram** (must be drawn in the answer script — use the Mermaid version in Section 4.1 as a template):

The cascade contains three nested loops closing from the inside out:

**1. Current / Torque loop [2 marks]:**
- **Plant:** Motor winding R–L circuit, $G_i(s) = (1/R) / (1 + sT_a)$, with $T_a = L/R$.
- **Sensor:** Phase-current shunt, Hall-effect LEM, or AD converter.
- **Controller:** $C_i(s) = K_{pi} + K_{ii}/s$ (PI), bandwidth ≈ 1–2 kHz.
- **Purpose:** Limits current to safe value, decouples motor dynamics from outer loops, rejects supply transients.

**2. Velocity loop [2 marks]:**
- **Plant:** $G_v(s) = (1/J) / [s(1 + sT_m)]$, with $T_m = J/B$.
- **Sensor:** Encoder differentiated, or tacho-generator.
- **Controller:** $C_v(s) = K_{pv} + K_{iv}/s$ (PI), bandwidth ≈ 100–500 Hz.
- **Purpose:** Regulates shaft speed, rejects friction and load disturbances.

**3. Position loop [2 marks]:**
- **Plant:** $G_p(s) = 1/s$ (integrator from velocity to position).
- **Sensor:** Quadrature encoder with ×4 decoding, or absolute SSI encoder, or linear scale.
- **Controller:** $C_p(s) = K_{pp}$ (P-only), bandwidth ≈ 20–100 Hz.
- **Purpose:** Tracks the commanded trajectory, achieves the specified contouring accuracy.

**Bandwidth rule [1 mark]:** $\omega_{current} : \omega_{velocity} : \omega_{position} = 10 : 5 : 1$.

---

**Part (b) — Current-Loop Design [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Electrical time constant** [1 mark]:

$$T_a = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{4\times 10^{-3}}{0.8} = 5\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Symmetric-optimum proportional gain** [1 mark]:

$$K_{pi} = \frac{L}{4\,T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{4\times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 80\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4\times 10^{-3}}{3.2\times 10^{-4}} = 12.5\;\text{V/A}$$

**Step 3: Symmetric-optimum integral gain** [1 mark]:

$$K_{ii} = \frac{R}{4\,T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{0.8}{4 \times 80\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.8}{3.2\times 10^{-4}} = 2500\;\text{V/(A·s)}$$

**Step 4: Closed-loop transfer function** [1 mark]:

The closed-loop current transfer function with these gains is approximately:

$$G_{i,CL}(s) \approx \frac{1}{1 + 2\,T_{\Sigma i}\,s + 2\,T_{\Sigma i}^2\,s^2}$$

**Step 5: Bandwidth verification** [1 mark]:

The -3 dB bandwidth of the current loop is:

$$\omega_i \approx \frac{1}{2\,T_{\Sigma i}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 80\times 10^{-6}} = 6250\;\text{rad/s} = \frac{6250}{2\pi} \approx 995\;\text{Hz} \approx 1\;\text{kHz}$$

This satisfies the industry standard of **> 1 kHz current-loop bandwidth** and is suitable for a high-performance CNC axis.

**Step 6: Comment on design choice** [1 mark]:

The symmetric-optimum criterion (factor 4) provides minimum integrated absolute error (IAE) and yields good disturbance rejection. The bandwidth of 1 kHz is well within the 10 kHz PWM switching capability, leaving a 10× safety margin for the velocity loop to be designed at ~100 Hz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 4, Alternative Choice]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function of the position loop** with a P-controller and **velocity feed-forward**. Show that with $K_{vff} = 1$, the **steady-state following error at constant velocity is zero**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A CNC X-axis has a 5 mm pitch ball-screw. The motor has $K_{pp} = 2500\;\text{s}^{-2}$ (rotational). Compute the **maximum permissible feed-rate** such that the following error is within **±50 µm**, assuming $K_{vff} = 0$. Repeat the calculation for $K_{vff} = 0.95$ and comment.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Position-Loop with VFF [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: System equations** [1 mark]:

Let $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta(t)$ be the position error, and let $K_{pp}$ be the position-loop proportional gain. The velocity command is:

$$\omega_{cmd}(t) = K_{pp}\,e(t) + K_{vff}\,\dot{\theta}_{ref}(t)$$

**Step 2: Plant integration** [1 mark]:

Assuming an ideal velocity loop ($\dot{\theta} = \omega_{cmd}$):

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = K_{pp}\,e(t) + K_{vff}\,\dot{\theta}_{ref}(t)$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the error** [1 mark]:

$$\dot{e}(t) = \dot{\theta}_{ref}(t) - \dot{\theta}(t) = \dot{\theta}_{ref}(t) - K_{pp}\,e(t) - K_{vff}\,\dot{\theta}_{ref}(t)$$

**Step 4: Steady-state condition** [1 mark]:

At steady state, $\dot{e} = 0$ and $\dot{\theta}_{ref} = v_0$ (constant velocity). Setting $\dot{e} = 0$:

$$0 = v_0\,(1 - K_{vff}) - K_{pp}\,e_{ss}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $e_{ss}$** [1 mark]:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v_0\,(1 - K_{vff})}{K_{pp}}$$

**Step 6: Special case $K_{vff} = 1$** [1 mark]:

Substituting $K_{vff} = 1$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v_0\,(1 - 1)}{K_{pp}} = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

Therefore, with perfect velocity feed-forward, **the steady-state following error is identically zero** for any constant velocity $v_0$.

**Step 7: Transient interpretation** [1 mark]:

The feed-forward term anticipates the motion demand. The feedback path only corrects for un-modelled disturbances (friction, load variations, sensor errors). The P-controller's role shifts from "doing the work" to "correcting residual errors," which improves both tracking and stability simultaneously.

---

**Part (b) — Following-Error Computation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Convert $K_{pp}$ to linear units** [1 mark]:

The ball-screw has pitch $p = 5\;\text{mm/rev}$, so the linear gain is:

$$K_{pp,lin} = \frac{K_{pp}}{r^2} = \frac{2500}{(p/2\pi)^2} = \frac{2500}{(5/2\pi)^2} = \frac{2500}{0.634} = 3942\;\text{s}^{-2}$$

(where $r = p/(2\pi)$ is the radius-equivalent in mm/rad.)

**Step 2: Following error formula** [1 mark]:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{v_0\,(1 - K_{vff})}{K_{pp,lin}}$$

**Step 3: Maximum feed-rate with $K_{vff} = 0$ and $e_{ss} \le 50\;\mu m$** [1 mark]:

$$v_{max} = \frac{e_{ss}\cdot K_{pp,lin}}{1 - K_{vff}} = \frac{0.05\;\text{mm} \times 3942\;\text{s}^{-2}}{1} = 197\;\text{mm/s}$$

**Step 4: With $K_{vff} = 0.95$** [1 mark]:

$$v_{max} = \frac{0.05\;\text{mm} \times 3942\;\text{s}^{-2}}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{197}{0.05} = 3943\;\text{mm/s} \approx 4\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5: Comparison and comment** [2 marks]:

Without feed-forward, the axis can move at only **197 mm/s** while maintaining 50 µm accuracy — unacceptable for high-speed machining. With $K_{vff} = 0.95$ (a practical value that leaves 5% feedback correction for un-modelled effects), the allowable feed-rate jumps to **~4 m/s**, a **20× improvement**. This demonstrates why feed-forward is **non-negotiable** in modern CNC and pick-and-place machines.

The trade-off: $K_{vff} > 1$ causes **overshoot** at corners, and $K_{vff}$ is therefore limited to ≤ 0.95 in industrial practice to leave margin for the feedback loop to stabilize direction reversals.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on axis-controller questions (read this carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "symmetric optimum" (factor 4) with "technical optimum" (factor 2).** Symmetric optimum = current loop (uses factor 4). Technical optimum = velocity loop (uses factor 2). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rotational $K_{pp}$ to linear $K_{pp,lin}$** when the question involves a ball-screw or linear axis. The 2π and pitch factors must appear.
> 3. **Using the wrong units for $K_{pp}$:** position-loop gain is in **s⁻²** (i.e., 1/s²), not dimensionless. Students often write the formula without units and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the bandwidth rule** (5:1 separation) — every cascade design question implicitly demands this check. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing the cascade block diagram without feed-forward paths** — when the question mentions "trajectory tracking," VFF and AFF are mandatory to show.
> 6. **Treating position and velocity as rotational** when the question states a ball-screw, leadscrew, or linear motor — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Writing "PI controller for position"** — this is *wrong* in CNC context. P-only is the answer; explain why.
> 8. **Not stating the 5–10× bandwidth rule explicitly** — this is a free 1-mark "easy" point that most students miss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 1 hour before the KTU ESE.**

- [ ] An **axis controller** is a **three-loop cascade**: current → velocity → position.
- [ ] **Bandwidth rule:** $\omega_i : \omega_v : \omega_p = 10 : 5 : 1$ (inner must be 5–10× faster than outer).
- [ ] **Current loop plant:** $G_i(s) = K_m / (1 + sT_a)$ with $T_a = L/R$.
- [ ] **Velocity loop plant:** $G_v(s) = (1/J) / [s(1 + sT_m)]$ with $T_m = J/B$.
- [ ] **Position loop plant:** $G_p(s) = 1/s$ (pure integrator).
- [ ] **Symmetric-optimum (current):** $K_{pi} = L/(4T_{\Sigma i})$, $K_{ii} = R/(4T_{\Sigma i})$.
- [ ] **Technical-optimum (velocity):** $K_{pv} = J/(2T_{\Sigma v})$, $K_{iv} = J/(4T_{\Sigma v}^2)$.
- [ ] **Position loop is P-only:** $K_{pp} = \omega_{np}^2$ (do NOT add I-action!).
- [ ] **Steady-state following error:** $e_{ss} = v_0\,(1 - K_{vff}) / K_{pp,lin}$.
- [ ] **Ideal VFF** ($K_{vff} = 1$) gives **zero** steady-state lag at constant velocity.
- [ ] **Practical VFF** uses $K_{vff} = 0.7$ to $0.95$ to allow feedback correction.
- [ ] **AFF** = $K_{aff} \cdot J \cdot \ddot{x}_{ref}$ compensates inertia, reducing torque disturbance.
- [ ] **Current-loop bandwidth:** 1–2 kHz, **velocity-loop:** 100–500 Hz, **position-loop:** 20–100 Hz.
- [ ] **PWM frequency:** 10–20 kHz (above audible, below excessive switching loss).
- [ ] **Settling time:** $T_{settle} \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ for 2% band.
- [ ] **Sampling time hierarchy:** $T_i = 50$–$100\;\mu s < T_v = 0.5$–$1\;\text{ms} < T_p = 1$–$4\;\text{ms}$.
- [ ] **Encoder resolution:** $\times 4$ decoding gives 4N counts/rev for an N-line encoder.
- [ ] **Ball-screw conversion:** $K_{pp,lin} = K_{pp,rot}/(p/2\pi)^2$ — never forget the pitch factor.
- [ ] **Inter-axis synchronization** is the job of the **interpolator**, not the axis controller.
- [ ] **Multi-axis machines** use **N identical axis controllers** + 1 master interpolator.
- [ ] **Feed-forward + feedback** is the *only* way to achieve both fast response AND zero steady-state error without sacrificing stability.
- [ ] **Industry standard: PM ≥ 45°, GM ≥ 6 dB** for each individual loop.
- [ ] **Common controllers:** Bosch Rexroth IndraDrive, Siemens SINAMICS S120, Fanuc αi, Yaskawa Σ-7, Beckhoff AX5000.
- [ ] **Single-line definition for viva:** *"An axis controller is a digital cascade servo that closes current, velocity, and position loops at 10 : 5 : 1 bandwidth ratio to convert a digital position setpoint into a precise mechanical motion."*

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Axis Controllers**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework, mapped to Course Outcomes CO4 and CO5, evaluated at Bloom's Taxonomy Levels 3 (Apply) and 4 (Analyse).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine tool controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine tool controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Tool Controllers (MTC)** are specialized electronic, electromechanical, or computer-based systems that precisely govern the motion, sequencing, and operational parameters of machine tools. They interpret programmed instructions and translate them into coordinated electrical signals that drive motors, actuators, and feedback devices to achieve accurate manufacturing operations such as turning, milling, drilling, grinding, and additive manufacturing.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT525)** and the broader **Industry 4.0** paradigm, machine tool controllers form the *intelligence backbone* of automated manufacturing cells. They integrate **electric drives**, **servo systems**, **feedback transducers**, and **programmable logic** to achieve micron-level positioning accuracy.

The taxonomy of machine tool controllers typically includes:

| Controller Class | Full Form | Year of Evolution | Core Capability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NC | Numerical Control | 1940s–1950s | Hard-wired logic, paper-tape input |
| CNC | Computer Numerical Control | 1970s | Microprocessor-based, soft-part programming |
| DNC | Direct Numerical Control | 1960s | Networked host–machine communication |
| PLC | Programmable Logic Controller | 1968 | Ladder-logic discrete control |
| HMC | Horizontal Machining Center Controller | 1980s | Multi-axis synchronized control |
| OAC | Open-architecture Controller | 2000s | PC-based, Ethernet/EtherCAT enabled |
| ICS | Industrial Cyber-Physical System Controller | 2010s+ | IoT, AI, digital twin integration |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4, students are expected to distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop machine tool controllers, understand the role of feedback transducers (encoders, resolvers, LVDTs), and evaluate the architecture of modern CNC systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are teaching a *blindfolded apprentice* to carve a wooden sculpture. You cannot see the chisel, but you can hear it and feel the wood resist. To control the apprentice, you need three things:

1. **A Script** — A sequence of instructions ("move right 5 cm, cut down 2 mm, advance 1 cm..."). This is the **part program** (G-code/M-code) of a CNC.
2. **A Communication Medium** — Your voice or hand signals. This represents the **servo drive amplifiers and pulse trains**.
3. **Feedback** — The apprentice reporting "I am at position X, force Y." This is the **encoder/resolver/LVDT feedback loop**.

A **Machine Tool Controller** is essentially the *"master craftsman" issuing commands to electric motors (the apprentice's muscles)* based on a pre-written script and continuous sensory feedback. Without the controller, the powerful motors would be blind and uncoordinated — capable of great force but unable to produce precise geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world anchor:** In a CNC lathe turning a turbine blade for an aircraft engine, the controller coordinates 5 axes simultaneously with a positioning tolerance of **±2 micrometres (0.002 mm)** — finer than a human hair (≈70 µm).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern machine tool controller performance evaluation:

- **Positioning Accuracy:** $\pm 0.001$ to $\pm 0.010$ **mm** (industrial CNC), $\pm 0.0001$ **mm** (ultra-precision).
- **Repeatability:** Typically **±0.0005 mm** in modern CNC machining centers.
- **Resolution:** Defined by the encoder's pulse count — for an encoder with $N$ pulses/rev driving a ballscrew of lead $L$ mm/rev, the linear resolution is $L/N$ mm.
- **Standard NC Coordinate System:** Right-hand rule with axes **X, Y, Z** (linear) and **A, B, C** (rotary).
- **Standard Servo Bandwidth:** **20 Hz to 100 Hz** for general machining; up to **500 Hz** for high-speed micro-machining.
- **IEEE 754 Floating Point:** Used in modern CNC interpolators for sub-micron path computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for the *difference between accuracy and repeatability*. Memorize: **Accuracy = closeness to target value**; **Repeatability = closeness of successive attempts to each other**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Machine Tool Controller

A modern closed-loop machine tool controller operates on the canonical **servo control loop** principle. The functional architecture is decomposed into six logical layers:

1. **Part Program Layer (G-code Interpreter)**
   - Accepts ISO 6983 (G/M-code) or ISO 14649 (STEP-NC) input.
   - Performs *lexical analysis*, *syntax validation*, and *block-by-block decoding*.
   - Extracts target positions, feed-rates, spindle speeds, and tool selections.

2. **Interpolator Layer**
   - Computes intermediate points between commanded end-points.
   - Three primary interpolator types: **Linear**, **Circular**, and **Helical** (spline in 5-axis).
   - Sampling period: typically $T_s = 1$ to $4$ **ms** for standard CNC.

3. **Trajectory Generator / Kinematic Transformer**
   - Converts tool-path points into *joint-space* coordinates using **inverse kinematics** for multi-axis machines.

4. **Servo Drive Layer (Current → Velocity → Position Loops)**
   - Three nested PI/PID loops:
     - Innermost: **Current loop** (bandwidth 1–2 kHz)
     - Middle: **Velocity loop** (bandwidth 100–500 Hz)
     - Outermost: **Position loop** (bandwidth 20–100 Hz)

5. **Feedback Transducer Layer**
   - Encoders (incremental/absolute), resolvers, LVDTs, glass scales.

6. **Supervisory / HMI Layer**
   - Operator interface, alarms, diagnostics, predictive maintenance telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three nested loops?** Each successive loop is roughly **5× slower** than the inner one. This cascade ensures stability: the current loop regulates torque (fastest, since motor electrical time constant is in milliseconds); velocity loop regulates speed; position loop regulates spatial location.

---

## 2.2 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Machine Tool Controllers

| Property | Open-Loop Controller | Closed-Loop Controller |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback | None | Position + Velocity transducers |
| Actuator | Stepper motor (common) | Servo motor (BLDC/PMSM/induction) |
| Accuracy | Moderate (5–25 µm typical) | High (0.5–5 µm typical) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Error detection | No | Yes (following error alarm) |
| Lost motion | Significant | Minimal |
| Heat generation | Higher (holding torque losses) | Lower (only on demand) |
| Use case | Light-load prototyping, 3D printers | Production CNC, aerospace, medical |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every high-yield formula you need for Module 4 numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Convention:** $P$ = pulse count, $L$ = lead, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $V$ = feed velocity, $f_{enc}$ = encoder frequency, $e$ = following error.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Linear Resolution} = \dfrac{L}{P}$ | Distance per encoder pulse | mm |
| 2 | $f_{enc} = \dfrac{V \cdot P}{L}$ | Encoder pulse frequency at feed rate $V$ | Hz (must be $< f_{max}$ of counter) |
| 3 | $V_{feed} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{P}$ | Feed velocity from pulse frequency $f$ | mm/s |
| 4 | $e(t) = \theta_{cmd}(t) - \theta_{act}(t)$ | Following error in position loop | rad or mm |
| 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{V_{cmd}}{e_{ss}}$ | Position-loop proportional gain | dimensionless or (mm/s)/mm |
| 6 | $T_{settling} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% settling time of 2nd-order servo | seconds |
| 7 | $BW \approx 0.35 / t_{rise}$ | Servo bandwidth from rise time | Hz |
| 8 | $G_{open}(s) = \dfrac{K_p K_v K_t}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)}$ | DC servo open-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)$ | Velocity error constant | 1/s |
| 10 | $\eta_{ballscrew} = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Ballscrew mechanical efficiency | dimensionless (0.85–0.95) |
| 11 | $F_{cutting} = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | Orthogonal cutting force (Kienzle model) | N |
| 12 | $P_{spindle} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60\,000 \cdot \eta}$ | Spindle motor power from cutting | kW |
| 13 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta L}{V_{prog}}$ | Block execution time | seconds |
| 14 | $N_{look-ahead} \ge \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2 \cdot a_{max} \cdot \Delta L}$ | Look-ahead buffer size for contouring | blocks |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Formulas 1–4 appear in nearly every Module 4 numerical. Memorize them as you would memorize Ohm's law.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Relevance

Machine tool controllers are the **strategic differentiator** in modern manufacturing competitiveness. Their engineering utility spans:

- **Aerospace structural parts** (wing skins, turbine discs) — controllers compensate for thermal drift and tool wear in real time.
- **Medical implants** (hip stems, dental crowns) — Ti-6Al-4V machining at 10,000 RPM under closed-loop torque control.
- **EV battery enclosures** — multi-axis laser welding controllers synchronize 6 robot arms with sub-millisecond precision.
- **Semiconductor lithography** — ASML-style controllers achieve 5 nm overlay accuracy through interferometric feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 Connection:** Modern controllers expose **OPC-UA**, **MQTT**, and **MTConnect** protocols, enabling *digital twins* and *predictive maintenance* via cloud analytics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Encoder Resolution to Linear Feed Velocity

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-mark):**
> A CNC machine uses a rotary incremental encoder of **2500 pulses/rev** mounted on a ballscrew with **5 mm lead**. The feed motor rotates at **1200 rpm**. Calculate:
> (a) Linear resolution per pulse.
> (b) Linear feed velocity.
> (c) Maximum measurable encoder frequency.

### Given Data

$$P = 2500 \text{ pulses/rev}, \quad L = 5 \text{ mm/rev}, \quad N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

### Part (a): Linear Resolution per Pulse

$$\text{Linear Resolution} = \frac{L}{P}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Linear Resolution} = \frac{5 \text{ mm/rev}}{2500 \text{ pulses/rev}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Linear Resolution} = 0.002 \text{ mm/pulse} = 2 \text{ µm}}$$

**Interpretation:** One pulse moves the table by **2 micrometres** — sufficient for general machining.

---

### Part (b): Linear Feed Velocity

**Step 1:** Convert motor speed to revolutions per second.

$$N_{rps} = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2:** Each revolution advances the nut by the lead $L$.

$$V_{feed} = N_{rps} \cdot L = 20 \text{ rev/s} \times 5 \text{ mm/rev}$$

$$\boxed{V_{feed} = 100 \text{ mm/s} = 6 \text{ m/min}}$$

**Verification using pulse frequency method:**

$$f_{enc} = \frac{V_{feed} \cdot P}{L} = \frac{100 \times 2500}{5} = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

$$V_{feed} = f_{enc} \cdot \frac{L}{P} = 50{,}000 \times \frac{5}{2500} = 100 \text{ mm/s} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Part (c): Maximum Encoder Frequency

The encoder frequency equals the pulse rate generated at the given speed:

$$f_{max} = N_{rps} \cdot P = 20 \times 2500$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rps by dividing by 60. Always check unit homogeneity before substituting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Following Error and Servo Bandwidth Trade-off

### Setup

Consider a simplified first-order position servo with proportional gain $K_p$ (units: 1/s) and commanded velocity $V_{cmd}$. The closed-loop transfer function relating actual position $X_{act}(s)$ to commanded position $X_{cmd}(s)$ is:

$$\frac{X_{act}(s)}{X_{cmd}(s)} = \frac{K_p}{s + K_p}$$

### Derivation of Steady-State Following Error

**Step 1:** Compute the error $E(s) = X_{cmd}(s) - X_{act}(s)$.

$$E(s) = X_{cmd}(s)\left[1 - \frac{K_p}{s + K_p}\right] = X_{cmd}(s) \cdot \frac{s}{s + K_p}$$

**Step 2:** Apply a *ramp* input corresponding to constant feed velocity: $X_{cmd}(s) = V_{cmd}/s^2$.

$$E(s) = \frac{V_{cmd}}{s^2} \cdot \frac{s}{s + K_p} = \frac{V_{cmd}}{s(s + K_p)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Final Value Theorem to obtain steady-state error.

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{V_{cmd}}{s + K_p} = \frac{V_{cmd}}{K_p}$$

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{V_{cmd}}{K_p}}$$

**Physical meaning:** To halve the following error at a given feed rate, the position-loop gain $K_p$ must be **doubled** — but this risks instability (reduced phase margin).

---

### Bandwidth Constraint

For a first-order system, the **−3 dB bandwidth** equals the gain:

$$BW = K_p \text{ (in rad/s)} = \frac{K_p}{2\pi} \text{ (in Hz)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical rule:** Increase $K_p$ to reduce $e_{ss}$, but ensure $BW \le 0.3 \times$ structural resonance frequency to avoid chatter.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Spindle Power Sizing for a Turning Operation

A CNC lathe performs a turning operation on mild steel with the following parameters:

- Depth of cut: $a_p = 2$ mm
- Feed: $f = 0.2$ mm/rev
- Cutting speed: $V_c = 120$ m/min
- Specific cutting force (Kienzle constant): $k_c = 2000$ N/mm²
- Machine mechanical efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1:** Compute the cutting force using the Kienzle model.

$$F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f = 2000 \times 2 \times 0.2$$

$$F_c = 800 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Convert cutting speed to mm/s for unit consistency.

$$V_c = 120 \text{ m/min} = \frac{120{,}000}{60} = 2000 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute required spindle power.

$$P_{spindle} = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{800 \times 2000}{60{,}000 \times 0.85}$$

$$P_{spindle} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{51{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{P_{spindle} \approx 31.37 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4:** Select a standard motor — typically **37 kW** with a service factor of 1.2.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NC vs. CNC vs. DNC

| Parameter | NC (Numerical Control) | CNC (Computer Numerical Control) | DNC (Direct Numerical Control) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage Medium | Paper tape, magnetic tape | Hard disk, SSD, USB | Host server + network |
| Programmability | Hard-wired (limited) | Soft-part programming | Soft-part programming |
| Macro Capability | None | Full (Fanuc Custom Macro, Siemens @) | Full + central library |
| Number of Machines | Single | Single (standalone) | Multiple (networked) |
| Data Throughput | Slow (sequential tape) | Fast (parallel buses) | Very fast (Ethernet/EtherCAT) |
| Cost (2024, INR) | Obsolete | ₹ 8 Lakh – ₹ 1.2 Cr | ₹ 25 Lakh – ₹ 5 Cr (cell) |
| Modern Relevance | Museum-grade | Industry workhorse | Smart factory standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to compare NC and CNC, always emphasize that CNC *retains NC's deterministic behavior* while adding *flexibility, macro programming, and adaptive features*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Closed-Loop CNC Controller

The diagram below illustrates the canonical signal flow from the part program down to the mechanical axis, including all feedback loops. This is the **single most important figure** for Module 4 examination answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator HMI Panel] --> B[Part Program Loader G-code M-code]
    B --> C[G-code Interpreter and Syntax Checker]
    C --> D[Block Look-ahead Buffer N blocks]
    D --> E[Trajectory Interpolator Linear Circular Spline]
    E --> F[Inverse Kinematics Solver Multi axis]
    F --> G[Position Loop PID Controller]
    G --> H[Velocity Loop PI Controller]
    H --> I[Current Loop PI Controller]
    I --> J[PWM Inverter Three phase Bridge]
    J --> K[Servo Motor PMSM or BLDC]
    K --> L[Ballscrew and Linear Axis]
    L --> M[Machine Tool Table and Spindle]
    M --> N[Linear Encoder or Glass Scale]
    K --> O[Rotary Encoder or Resolver]
    N --> F
    O --> H
    P[Spindle Drive VFD] --> Q[Spindle Motor Induction or PM]
    Q --> R[Tool and Workpiece Interface]
    S[Fieldbus EtherCAT Profinet] --> C
    S --> F
    S --> P
    T[Cloud OPC UA MTConnect] --> S
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **forward path** runs vertically downward: HMI → Interpreter → Interpolator → Kinematics → 3-Loop Cascade → PWM → Motor → Mechanical Axis.
- The **two feedback paths** return *position* (via linear encoder to the interpolator) and *velocity* (via rotary encoder to the velocity loop). This nested structure enforces the *cascade-control* principle.
- The **spindle subsystem** is parallel to the feed-axis subsystem, sharing the fieldbus but governed by a separate VFD.
- **External connectivity** (right side) shows the fieldbus feeding back to the cloud for Industry 4.0 telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's expectation:** When drawing this diagram in an exam, label every block with its function (e.g., "PID Position Loop") and every feedback path with the signal type (e.g., "Quad encoder A/B").

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Servo Drive Internal Loops

For deeper analysis, the following diagram zooms into the three cascaded loops inside a modern digital servo drive.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Position Command theta_cmd] --> B[Summing Junction Position Error]
    B --> C[Position Loop Compensator Kp]
    C --> D[Velocity Command omega_cmd]
    D --> E[Summing Junction Velocity Error]
    E --> F[Velocity Loop Compensator Kp Ki]
    F --> G[Current Command i_cmd]
    G --> H[Summing Junction Current Error]
    H --> I[Current Loop Compensator PI]
    I --> J[PWM Modulator]
    J --> K[Three phase Inverter IGBT]
    K --> L[Brushless Servo Motor PMSM]
    L --> M[Mechanical Load]
    M --> N[Tachogenerator or Encoder]
    N --> E
    L --> O[Position Sensor Encoder]
    O --> B
    P[Current Sensor Hall LEM] --> H
```

### Engineering Notes

- **Current loop** runs at **16 kHz** sampling rate → bandwidth ~1 kHz.
- **Velocity loop** runs at **4 kHz** → bandwidth ~200 Hz.
- **Position loop** runs at **1 kHz** → bandwidth ~50 Hz.
- This 4:1 ratio of sampling rates between successive loops is *essential* for closed-loop stability (Nyquist + phase margin rule).

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpenLoop["Open Loop System"]
        A1[Part Program] --> B1[Pulse Generator]
        B1 --> C1[Stepper Drive]
        C1 --> D1[Stepper Motor]
        D1 --> E1[Leadscrew]
        E1 --> F1[Machine Table]
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop["Closed Loop System"]
        A2[Part Program] --> B2[Interpolator]
        B2 --> C2[Servo Drive]
        C2 --> D2[Servo Motor]
        D2 --> E2[Ballscrew]
        E2 --> F2[Machine Table]
        F2 --> G2[Linear Encoder]
        G2 --> B2
    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** In an exam sketch, students often forget to draw the feedback arrow returning to the *position* summing junction (not to the motor). Always return the feedback to the *input* of the same loop that uses it.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Differentiate between NC, CNC, and DNC machine tool controllers. List two advantages of CNC over conventional NC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NC | CNC | DNC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage | Paper tape / punched tape | Internal memory / SSD | Central host computer |
| Programming | Hard-wired logic | Soft-part programming | Soft-part + networked library |
| Editing | Impossible at machine | On-machine editing | Centralized + distributed |
| Multi-machine | No | Standalone | Yes (networked cluster) |

**Two advantages of CNC over NC:**

1. **Flexibility:** Part programs can be created, edited, and stored digitally without rewiring or new punched tapes — drastically reducing setup time.
2. **Advanced features:** CNC supports macro programming, canned cycles, tool-length compensation, and parametric subprograms — NC cannot.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for table comparison; 2 marks for any two advantages with brief explanation.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Explain the function of a servo drive in a CNC machine tool controller. Why is it placed between the controller and the motor?**

**Model Answer:**

A **servo drive** is a power electronic amplifier that takes low-power command signals (typically $\pm 10$ V analog or pulse trains) from the CNC controller and converts them into high-power three-phase PWM voltages/currents capable of driving a servo motor with high dynamic response.

**Why placed between controller and motor:**

1. **Power amplification:** Controller outputs are milliwatts; motors need kilowatts.
2. **Current regulation:** Closed-loop current control protects motor windings and limits torque.
3. **Signal isolation:** Protects sensitive controller electronics from motor-side noise and back-EMF.
4. **Dynamic response:** Servo drives implement the inner current/velocity loops at kHz bandwidth, which is impossible to achieve inside the CNC controller itself.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE – Module 4 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a closed-loop CNC machine tool controller**. Identify and explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC vertical machining center uses a ballscrew with **10 mm lead** driven by a servo motor through a **gear ratio of 3:1** (motor:ballscrew). The motor has an **incremental encoder of 2048 pulses/rev**. Calculate:
  - (i) Linear resolution per pulse at the table. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Maximum linear feed velocity if the motor can spin at **3000 rpm**. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Maximum encoder pulse frequency. **(1 mark)**
  - (iv) If a glass scale with **0.5 µm resolution** is added as a direct position feedback, comment on the improvement in accuracy. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A CNC closed-loop controller comprises the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Part Program Input Unit** (1 mark): Accepts G-code/M-code from USB, Ethernet, or DNC network. Performs lexical decoding.
2. **Interpreter & Look-ahead Buffer** (1 mark): Validates syntax, extracts motion parameters, buffers $N$ blocks (typically 100–1000) to enable smooth corner transitions.
3. **Interpolator** (1 mark): Generates intermediate points along linear, circular, or spline paths at the servo sampling period (1–4 ms).
4. **Inverse Kinematics / Coordinate Transformer** (0.5 mark): Converts Cartesian tool-path points into joint angles/motor rotations, especially for 5-axis machines.
5. **Servo Drive (Cascaded Loops)** (2 marks): Three nested PI/PID loops — current (inner, ~1 kHz BW), velocity (middle, ~200 Hz BW), position (outer, ~50 Hz BW). These ensure precise motor control.
6. **Feedback Transducers** (1 mark): Encoders (rotary at motor, linear at table), resolvers, and current sensors. Provide closed-loop correction.
7. **HMI and Fieldbus** (0.5 mark): Operator interface, alarms, OPC-UA/MTConnect connectivity for Industry 4.0.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$L = 10$ mm/rev, $G = 3:1$ (motor rotates 3× for every ballscrew revolution), $P = 2048$ pulses/rev (motor side), $N_{max} = 3000$ rpm (motor).

**(i) Linear resolution at the table** (2 marks):

The ballscrew advances $L = 10$ mm per revolution. The motor rotates $G = 3$ revolutions per ballscrew revolution, generating $G \cdot P = 3 \times 2048 = 6144$ pulses per ballscrew revolution.

$$\Delta x = \frac{L}{G \cdot P} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{3 \times 2048}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{10}{6144} \approx 0.00163 \text{ mm/pulse} \approx 1.63 \text{ µm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum feed velocity** (2 marks):

Motor speed in rps: $N_{rps} = 3000/60 = 50$ rev/s.

Ballscrew speed: $N_{bs} = N_{rps}/G = 50/3 \approx 16.67$ rev/s.

$$V_{max} = N_{bs} \cdot L = \frac{50}{3} \times 10$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 166.67 \text{ mm/s} = 10 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(iii) Maximum encoder pulse frequency** (1 mark):

$$f_{max} = N_{rps} \cdot P = 50 \times 2048$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} = 102{,}400 \text{ Hz} = 102.4 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iv) Glass scale improvement** (2 marks):

The motor encoder provides *indirect* (semi-closed-loop) feedback — it measures motor rotation but cannot detect ballscrew errors (lead error, thermal expansion, backlash). A **direct measurement** glass scale at the table eliminates these secondary errors, improving:

- **Positioning accuracy** from ~5 µm to ~1 µm.
- **Repeatability** from ~2 µm to ~0.5 µm.
- **Thermal drift compensation** (real-time correction).

This configuration is termed *fully-closed-loop* control and is mandatory for high-precision aerospace and optics machining.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **three cascaded control loops** (current, velocity, position) inside a digital servo drive. Justify why the bandwidth of each successive outer loop must be approximately **5–10× lower** than the inner loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine performs a slot-milling operation on aluminum alloy with:
  - Spindle speed $N = 8000$ rpm, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, 4-flute cutter, depth of cut $a_p = 3$ mm, width $a_e = 6$ mm.
  - Specific cutting energy $K_c = 0.8$ N·m/mm³, machine efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.
  Calculate: (i) Material removal rate (MRR) **(2 marks)**, (ii) Spindle power required **(2 marks)**, (iii) Spindle torque **(2 marks)**, (iv) Justify the choice of a **closed-loop servo controller** versus an open-loop stepper drive for this application **(1 mark)**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The three loops form a **cascade** with progressively slower response (2 marks for diagram; 5 marks for explanation):

1. **Current (Torque) Loop — Innermost (1.5 marks)**
   - Regulates motor phase current using PWM inverter.
   - Bandwidth: ~1 kHz.
   - Feedback: Hall-effect or shunt-based current sensors (LEM).
   - Compensator: PI with anti-windup.
   - Function: Limits torque to safe values, provides motor protection, enables fast acceleration.

2. **Velocity Loop — Middle (1.5 marks)**
   - Regulates motor speed using current command as output.
   - Bandwidth: ~200 Hz.
   - Feedback: Incremental encoder (differentiate position).
   - Compensator: PI + feed-forward.
   - Function: Maintains commanded feed-rate, damps load disturbances.

3. **Position Loop — Outermost (1.5 marks)**
   - Compares commanded position with measured position.
   - Bandwidth: ~50 Hz.
   - Feedback: Encoder (or glass scale for full-closed).
   - Compensator: P + feed-forward + friction compensation.
   - Function: Drives following error to zero, ensures path accuracy.

**Justification for 5–10× bandwidth ratio (2.5 marks):**

According to **Bode's stability criterion** and **Nyquist separation principle**, each outer loop must see the inner loop as a *quasi-static gain* at its crossover frequency. If bandwidths were equal, the loops would *interact dynamically*, causing oscillation, overshoot, or instability.

Mathematically, for a cascade of unity-gain crossovers at $\omega_{c,i}$, the phase lag from each inner loop at the outer crossover is approximately:

$$\phi_{lag} \approx -\arctan\left(\frac{\omega_{c,outer}}{\omega_{c,inner}}\right)$$

A **5–10× separation** keeps phase lag under 11°–6°, preserving phase margin and ensuring stable response. Practically, this is implemented by *sequential loop closure with progressive tuning* — current loop first (with motor locked), then velocity, then position.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $N = 8000$ rpm, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, $z = 4$ flutes, $a_p = 3$ mm, $a_e = 6$ mm, $K_c = 0.8$ N·m/mm³, $\eta = 0.80$.

**(i) Material Removal Rate (MRR) — 2 marks:**

Feed per revolution:
$$f = f_z \cdot z = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.2 \text{ mm/rev}$$

Feed velocity:
$$V_f = f \cdot N = 0.2 \text{ mm/rev} \times 8000 \text{ rev/min} = 1600 \text{ mm/min}$$

MRR:
$$\text{MRR} = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e = 1600 \times 3 \times 6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = 28{,}800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 28.8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(ii) Spindle Power — 2 marks:**

$$P_{cut} = \text{MRR} \cdot K_c \quad \text{(Note: convert units — $K_c$ is J/mm³ = N·m/mm³)}$$

$$P_{cut} = \frac{28{,}800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \times 0.8 \text{ N·m/mm}^3}{60{,}000 \text{ (to kW)}}$$

$$P_{cut} = \frac{28{,}800 \times 0.8}{60{,}000} \text{ kW} = 0.384 \text{ kW}$$

Including mechanical efficiency:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{cut}}{\eta} = \frac{0.384}{0.80}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} = 0.48 \text{ kW} = 480 \text{ W}}$$

**(iii) Spindle Torque — 2 marks:**

Spindle angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 8000}{60} \approx 837.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T = \frac{P_{motor}}{\omega} = \frac{480}{837.76}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 0.573 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iv) Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop Justification — 1 mark:**

At 8000 rpm with cutting torque variation, **open-loop stepper systems** lose steps under load and cannot recover without operator intervention. The **closed-loop servo controller** continuously corrects position via encoder feedback, sustains higher acceleration, and provides alarms on following error — essential for reliable production runs and unattended machining.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversions:** Forgetting rpm → rps or mm/min → mm/s. Marks deducted silently for *physically impossible* answers (e.g., feed velocity in km/s).
> 2. **Glass-scale question:** Many students *redraw* the same diagram and add the encoder — they fail to mention that the *indirect* encoder feedback must be **disconnected** and the *direct* glass-scale becomes the primary position feedback.
> 3. **Cascade tuning:** Writing "tune all loops simultaneously" is **wrong**. Always say "tune inner loop first with outer loops disabled, then progress outward."
> 4. **MRR formula:** Conflating MRR with chip load. MRR = $V_f \times a_p \times a_e$, not feed × spindle speed.
> 5. **Forgetting efficiency:** KTU frequently tests whether students include $\eta$ in the power formula. Omitting it costs 1 full mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Machine tool controllers** are programmable systems that coordinate electric drives to perform precise manufacturing operations.
- The **3-tier hierarchy** is: NC → CNC → DNC, each adding programmability and connectivity.
- A **CNC controller** has six layers: Program → Interpreter → Interpolator → Kinematics → Servo Drive → Motor/Mechanical.
- **Open-loop** uses stepper motors; **closed-loop** uses servo motors with encoder/resolver feedback.
- **Resolution formula:** $\Delta x = L / (G \cdot P)$ — must account for gear ratio if present.
- **Following error** in a P-controller = $V_{cmd}/K_p$ — inversely proportional to position-loop gain.
- **Cascade bandwidth ratio:** Each outer loop must be 5–10× slower than the inner one for stability.
- **Three nested servo loops:** Current (~1 kHz) → Velocity (~200 Hz) → Position (~50 Hz).
- **Direct (glass-scale) feedback** eliminates ballscrew errors → *fully closed-loop* control.
- **MRR** for milling: $V_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$; for turning: $V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$.
- **Spindle power:** $P = (F_c \cdot V_c) / (60{,}000 \cdot \eta)$; **Torque** = $P/\omega$.
- **Industry 4.0 controllers** support OPC-UA, MTConnect, MQTT, and digital twins.
- **Accuracy ≠ Repeatability** — accuracy targets the true value; repeatability targets consistency.
- **Standard NC axes:** X, Y, Z (linear) + A, B, C (rotary), using the right-hand rule.
- **Modern servo motors:** PMSM (most common), BLDC, or synchronous reluctance for CNC spindles.
- **Communication buses:** EtherCAT (≤ 12.5 µs cycle), Profinet, SERCOS — all real-time Ethernet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT525 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROLS FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ PROGRAMMABLE LOGIC CONTROLLERS (PLC) — FOUNDATIONAL OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the foundational core of **Module 4** in *PEAUT525 – Electric Drives and Controls for Automation*. It carries **high weightage** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and is a mandatory topic for any industrial automation role.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, industrial-grade, solid-state digital computer specifically designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automation equipment. It operates using a specialized, instruction-oriented programming language (most commonly **Ladder Logic**, **Function Block Diagrams**, or **Structured Text**) and continuously monitors the state of input devices, executes user-defined logic, and generates corresponding outputs to drive actuators, motors, solenoids, and relays.

Unlike a general-purpose computer, a PLC is engineered to survive **harsh industrial environments** characterized by electrical noise, vibration, extreme temperatures, humidity, and airborne contaminants. It is the direct, modern electronic replacement for the bulky, hard-wired electromagnetic relay control panels that dominated pre-1970s industrial automation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin (KTU Board Favorite):** The first PLC was developed by **Modicon (Bedford Associates)** in **1968**, specifically to replace the complex relay systems at the **Hydramatic Division of General Motors (GM)**. This origin story is a recurring question in KTU University Examinations.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Electrical Switchboard"

Imagine a traditional **household switchboard**:
- **Inputs** are the switches you press (lights, fans).
- **Logic** is hard-wired inside the walls (the copper wires connecting the switch to the bulb).
- **Outputs** are the bulbs/fans themselves.

If you want to change the behavior (e.g., "turn on the fan only after 5 PM AND when humidity is high"), you must physically re-wire the wall — expensive, slow, and messy.

A **PLC** turns that switchboard into a **re-programmable digital brain**:

| Component | Household Switchboard (Relay) | PLC Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Push Buttons / Sensors | Physical Switches | **Input Module** (Discrete/Analog I/O) |
| Copper Wiring Logic | Hard-Wired Relays | **CPU + User Program** (Ladder Logic) |
| Bulbs / Motors | Output Devices | **Output Module** (Relay/Transistor/Triac) |
| Power Supply | Mains Electricity | **Dedicated 24 V DC / 120–230 V AC PSU** |

You simply **type new logic** on a programming PC and **download it** to the PLC — no wires need to be physically moved. The PLC is therefore the **"brain" of any modern automated factory line**, including bottling plants, elevators, traffic signals, and CNC machine tools.

---

## 1.3 Physical Operating Constants & Standards (KTU Mandatory)

The following constants and standards define a PLC's operating envelope and are frequently tested:

- **Operating Temperature:** **0 °C to 55 °C** (industrial-rated)
- **Storage Temperature:** **−40 °C to +85 °C**
- **Relative Humidity:** **5 % to 95 %** (non-condensing)
- **Standard Supply Voltage:** **24 V DC** (control logic), **120/230 V AC** (field supply)
- **IEC 61131-3:** The **international standard** governing PLC programming languages (Ladder Diagram, Function Block Diagram, Structured Text, Instruction List, Sequential Function Chart).
- **Scan Time:** Typically **1 ms to 20 ms** per cycle (varies by program length).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The **IEC 61131-3** standard is the "Constitution" of PLC programming. Remember the **5 standard languages**: **L**adder, **F**unction **B**lock, **S**tructured **T**ext, **I**nstruction **L**ist, **S**equential **F**unction **C**hart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — PLC ARCHITECTURE, SCAN CYCLE & LADDER LOGIC

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a PLC

A PLC is structurally divided into **four major functional sub-systems**, plus an optional programming device:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**
   - Contains the **microprocessor** (often 32-bit), **system memory (ROM)**, **user memory (RAM/Flash/EEPROM)**, and a **system bus**.
   - Executes the control program stored in user memory.
   - Houses the **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)**, **flags/status registers**, and **timers/counters**.

2. **Memory Architecture**
   - **System ROM:** Stores the firmware (operating system, scan scheduler, I/O drivers).
   - **RAM (Volatile):** Stores temporary data, status bits, and the live process image.
   - **EEPROM/Flash (Non-Volatile):** Stores the user program permanently, even when power is removed (battery-backed RAM is also used).

3. **Input Module (I/O Interface — Input Section)**
   - Interfaces field devices (limit switches, proximity sensors, pushbuttons, thermocouples) to the CPU.
   - Provides **electrical isolation** using **optocouplers** to protect the CPU from voltage spikes.
   - Handles **signal conditioning** (debouncing, noise filtering, level shifting).
   - Types: **Discrete (DI)**, **Analog (AI)** such as 4–20 mA / 0–10 V.

4. **Output Module (I/O Interface — Output Section)**
   - Drives field actuators (contactors, solenoids, valves, indicator lamps, VFDs).
   - Three switching technologies:
     - **Relay Output:** Universal (AC/DC), slow (~10 ms), mechanical wear.
     - **Transistor Output:** DC only, very fast (<1 µs), no moving parts.
     - **Triac Output:** AC only, zero-crossing switching.

5. **Power Supply Unit (PSU)**
   - Converts mains AC to regulated DC voltages (typically **+5 V DC** for logic, **±15 V DC** for analog, **+24 V DC** for I/O).

6. **Programming Device (PG)**
   - A laptop or handheld pendant running PLC manufacturer software (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Allen-Bradley RSLogix, Mitsubishi GX Works). It **uploads/downloads** programs and performs **online monitoring/debugging**.

---

## 2.2 The Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of a PLC

A PLC does not run a program once and stop. It executes a **continuous, repetitive loop** called the **Scan Cycle**. This cycle consists of **four sequential phases**:

| Phase | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Input Scan** | CPU reads the **physical status** of all input terminals and writes them into a dedicated memory area called the **Process Input Image (PII)** table. |
| 2 | **Program Scan** | CPU executes the user program (Ladder Logic) **top-to-bottom, left-to-right**, using ONLY the values stored in the PII. Outputs are NOT written to the physical world yet. |
| 3 | **Output Scan** | CPU copies the contents of the **Process Output Image (POI)** table to the physical output terminals, energizing or de-energizing the connected actuators. |
| 4 | **Housekeeping** | CPU performs self-diagnostics, updates internal timers, services communication requests (HMI/MODBUS), and prepares for the next scan. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Because the program reads a **snapshot** of inputs (the PII), the CPU does not get confused by rapid input changes during program execution. This guarantees **deterministic, reproducible behavior** — the single most important property of any industrial controller.

---

## 2.3 Ladder Logic Fundamentals (LD — Ladder Diagram)

Ladder logic was designed to mimic the **schematic diagrams of hard-wired relay control panels**, making it instantly familiar to electricians.

- **Rungs:** Horizontal lines representing one logic statement.
- **Left Vertical Rail:** Conceptually connected to **L (Live)**.
- **Right Vertical Rail:** Conceptually connected to **N (Neutral)**.
- **Contacts:** `---‖---` (Normally Open, NO) and `---‖/---` (Normally Closed, NC).
- **Coils:** `---( )---` (Output coil), `---(/)---` (Negated coil).

The Boolean logic equivalents:
- **Series (AND):** `---‖---‖---( )---` ⇒ $Q = A \cdot B$
- **Parallel (OR):** Two rungs in parallel ⇒ $Q = A + B$
- **NC contact in series:** `---‖/---( )---` ⇒ $Q = \overline{A}$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Table

The following mathematical relationships define the **timing performance and memory sizing** of any PLC system. These equations are **frequently asked** in Part A and Part B questions.

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Scan Time** ($T_{scan}$) | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | Sum of input, program, output, and housekeeping times | ms |
| **Program Scan Time** ($T_{prog}$) | $T_{prog} = N_{inst} \cdot t_{avg}$ | Where $N_{inst}$ is the number of executed instructions, $t_{avg}$ is the average instruction execution time (typically ~0.1 µs to 10 µs per instruction) | ms |
| **Maximum I/O Count** | $N_{max} = \frac{T_{scan,max} - T_{house}}{t_{avg}}$ | Maximum instructions a CPU can process within a fixed scan budget | instructions |
| **Response Time (Worst Case)** | $T_{resp,max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan}$ | From input change to corresponding output change | ms |
| **Response Time (Best Case)** | $T_{resp,min} = T_{scan}$ | If input changes just before the input scan phase | ms |
| **Throughput Rate** | $f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Scans completed per second | Hz |
| **Boolean AND** | $Q = A \land B$ | Contacts in series | dimensionless |
| **Boolean OR** | $Q = A \lor B$ | Contacts in parallel | dimensionless |
| **Boolean NOT (Inversion)** | $Q = \overline{A}$ | Normally Closed contact | dimensionless |
| **Ton Timer (On-Delay)** | $Q = T_{preset}$ reached after rising edge | Output energizes $T_{preset}$ seconds after input turns ON | seconds |
| **Counter Up (CTU)** | $CV_{k} = CV_{k-1} + 1$ | Counter increments on each rising edge of input pulse | pulses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** In the formula table above, note how the **Response Time** uses the vertical bar symbol in $T_{resp,max}$ — this is a subscript, not a mathematical absolute-value bar. In a markdown table cell, the bar is replaced by `\vert` or by subscript notation as shown, so as not to break table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLCs are the invisible workhorses behind nearly every automated system on Earth:

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Robotic welding, painting, and torqueing operations (a single modern car plant uses 200+ PLCs).
- **Traffic Light Control:** Time-of-day adaptive signaling with pedestrian safety interlocks.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Sequential dosing, filtration backwash cycles, and pH control.
- **Elevators:** Floor selection logic, door interlock safety, and emergency homing.
- **Conveyor & Packaging:** Bottle-filling, capping, and labeling synchronization.

> Without PLCs, modern mass production at the scale and quality we expect would be physically and economically impossible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

This section provides complete, classroom-grade mathematical derivations of every important relationship used in PLC design, sizing, and ladder logic analysis. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Scan Time Equation

The PLC scan cycle is a **serial, non-overlapping** sequence of four phases. Therefore, by the **additivity of time intervals in a sequential process**, the total scan time is the arithmetic sum of the four sub-phase durations.

**Step 1 — Define the four phases symbolically.**

Let:
- $T_{in}$ = Time consumed by the input scan phase (reading all input modules).
- $T_{prog}$ = Time consumed by the program execution phase.
- $T_{out}$ = Time consumed by the output scan phase (writing to all output modules).
- $T_{house}$ = Time consumed by housekeeping (diagnostics, comms).

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of sequential time accumulation.**

Since these phases occur **one after another** without overlap, total elapsed time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express each component in measurable terms.**

- Input scan time depends on the number of input modules and the I/O bus speed: $T_{in} = \frac{N_{in}}{f_{bus}}$ where $N_{in}$ is the count of input words and $f_{bus}$ is the backplane frequency.
- Program scan time depends on the instruction count: $T_{prog} = N_{inst} \cdot t_{avg}$.
- Output scan time: $T_{out} = \frac{N_{out}}{f_{bus}}$.
- Housekeeping is a fixed overhead (typically **0.5 ms to 2 ms** depending on the PLC family).

**Step 4 — Final substituted form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= \frac{N_{in}}{f_{bus}} + N_{inst} \cdot t_{avg} + \frac{N_{out}}{f_{bus}} + T_{house}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Worst-Case and Best-Case Response Time

The **response time** $T_{resp}$ is defined as the elapsed time between an **input change in the physical world** and the corresponding **output change at the physical output terminal**.

**Step 1 — Identify the critical timing windows.**

The input change can occur at any random instant within a single scan cycle. There are two extreme cases:

- **Best Case (Minimum Delay):** The input changes **just before** the Input Scan phase begins. The PLC reads it immediately, executes the program, and updates the output — all within the same scan. The total delay is approximately **one full scan time**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,min} \approx T_{scan}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Worst Case (Maximum Delay):** The input changes **just after** the Input Scan phase has finished. The PLC will not notice it until the **next** scan's input phase. Therefore, the input must wait for almost a full scan cycle to be detected, plus another full scan cycle for the program and output to react. Total delay is approximately **two scan cycles**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,max} \approx 2 \cdot T_{scan}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical engineering bound.**

For safety-critical interlocks (e.g., emergency stop circuits), designers must always use **$T_{resp,max}$** as the design basis to guarantee that no failure mode can outpace the safety logic.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Scan Time Budget

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Typical 7-Mark Question:** A PLC system has $N_{in} = 32$ inputs, $N_{out} = 32$ outputs, and the user program contains $N_{inst} = 500$ instructions. The backplane operates at $f_{bus} = 8$ MHz, the average instruction time is $t_{avg} = 2$ µs per instruction, and $T_{house} = 1.5$ ms. Compute the **total scan time** and the **worst-case response time**.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute input scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= \frac{N_{in}}{f_{bus}} \\
T_{in} &= \frac{32}{8 \times 10^6} \\
T_{in} &= 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_{in} &= 0.004 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute program scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prog} &= N_{inst} \cdot t_{avg} \\
T_{prog} &= 500 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \\
T_{prog} &= 1000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_{prog} &= 1.0 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute output scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= \frac{N_{out}}{f_{bus}} \\
T_{out} &= \frac{32}{8 \times 10^6} \\
T_{out} &= 0.004 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Compute total scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} \\
T_{scan} &= 0.004 + 1.0 + 0.004 + 1.5 \\
T_{scan} &= 2.508 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 5: Compute worst-case response time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,max} &= 2 \cdot T_{scan} \\
T_{resp,max} &= 2 \times 2.508 \\
T_{resp,max} &= 5.016 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The PLC can react to any process signal within **~5 ms** in the absolute worst case — fast enough for nearly all mechanical automation. A modern bottling line requires only ~10–50 ms response, so this PLC is comfortably sized.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boolean Logic to Ladder Conversion Table

This is the **most frequently tested** conversion in KTU exams. Given below is a complete analytical reference matrix:

| Boolean Expression | Boolean Symbol | Ladder Logic Equivalent | Verbal Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q = A$ | Identity | Single NO contact `---‖---(Q)---` | Direct pass-through |
| $Q = \overline{A}$ | NOT | Single NC contact `---‖/---(Q)---` | Inversion |
| $Q = A \cdot B$ | AND | Two NO contacts **in series** | Both must be true |
| $Q = A + B$ | OR | Two NO contacts **in parallel** | Either must be true |
| $Q = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | NAND | NC output coil `---‖---‖---(Q)---` with NC coil | Series inversion |
| $Q = \overline{A + B}$ | NOR | Parallel branches feeding NC coil `---(/)---` | Parallel inversion |
| $Q = A \oplus B$ | XOR | $(A \text{ AND } \overline{B}) \text{ OR } (\overline{A} \text{ AND } B)$ | Exclusive OR |
| $Q = A \cdot \overline{B} + \overline{A} \cdot B$ | XOR expanded | Two parallel rungs, each with one NO + one NC in series | XOR ladder form |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Timer (On-Delay, TON) Output Equation

The PLC's **On-Delay Timer** is mathematically a first-order time-delayed activation function:

**Step 1 — Define the input edge detector.** The timer starts measuring elapsed time $t_e$ only when the input $I$ transitions from $0 \to 1$ (rising edge).

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e(k) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } I(k) = 0 \\
t_e(k-1) + \Delta t, & \text{if } I(k) = 1
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta t$ is the time-base resolution (typically **10 ms**, **100 ms**, or **1 s**).

**Step 2 — Define the preset threshold.** The user sets $T_{preset}$ in software.

**Step 3 — Define the output.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } t_e \geq T_{preset} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the output $Q$ energizes **only after** the input has been continuously ON for the preset duration — hence the name "On-Delay."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS, SCHEMATICS & ARCHITECTURE FLOWS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete PLC Internal Architecture

The following diagram maps every internal sub-system of a PLC and shows the bidirectional flow of data, control signals, and program instructions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MainsAC([Mains AC Supply 230V]) --> PSU1[Power Supply Unit PSU]
    PSU1 --> V5[Regulated +5V DC for CPU Logic]
    PSU1 --> V24[Regulated +24V DC for Field I/O]
    PSU1 --> V15[Regulated plus or minus 15V DC for Analog]

    ProgPC([Programming PC or HMI]) -- Ethernet or RS232 or USB --> CPU1[CPU Module]

    subgraph CPU_Block[CPU and Memory Subsystem]
        CPU1 --> ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        CPU1 --> ROM[System ROM Firmware]
        CPU1 --> RAM[Volatile RAM Working Memory]
        CPU1 --> Flash[Non Volatile Flash User Program]
        CPU1 --> PII[Process Input Image Table]
        CPU1 --> POI[Process Output Image Table]
    end

    V24 --> Backplane[Backplane Communication Bus]
    V5 --> Backplane
    Backplane --> CPU1
    Backplane --> INMod[Input Module DI and AI]
    Backplane --> OUTMod[Output Module DO and AO]

    INMod --> Opto1[Optical Isolation Barrier]
    Opto1 --> FieldIN[Field Devices Pushbuttons Sensors Limit Switches]

    OUTMod --> Driver[Power Driver Relay or Transistor or Triac]
    Driver --> FieldOUT[Field Actuators Motors Contactors Solenoids Lamps]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — PLC Scan Cycle Execution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON]) --> Init[Initialize Hardware and Self Test]
    Init --> ReadInput[Phase 1 Input Scan Read all physical inputs into Process Input Image]
    ReadInput --> ExecProg[Phase 2 Program Scan Execute Ladder Logic top to bottom]
    ExecProg --> WriteOutput[Phase 3 Output Scan Copy Process Output Image to physical outputs]
    WriteOutput --> Housekeep[Phase 4 Housekeeping Diagnostics Comms Timer Updates]
    Housekeep --> LoopCheck{Is RUN mode active?}
    LoopCheck -- Yes --> ReadInput
    LoopCheck -- No --> Stop([STOP mode Halt execution])
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Ladder Logic Equivalent of $Q = A \cdot B + C$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L([Left Power Rail L]) --> A1
    A1[NO Contact A] --> A2[NO Contact B]
    A2 --> A3((Output Coil Q))

    L --> C1[NO Contact C]
    C1 --> A3
    A3 --> N([Right Power Rail N])

    subgraph Rung1[Rung 1 Series AND branch]
        A1
        A2
    end
    subgraph Rung2[Rung 2 Parallel OR branch]
        C1
    end
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- **Rung 1** implements the AND term $A \cdot B$ (contacts A and B in series).
- **Rung 2** implements the OR term $+ C$ (contact C in parallel).
- The combined Boolean equation realized by this ladder is exactly $Q = (A \cdot B) + C$.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Input/Output Interface and Signal Conditioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSensor([Proximity Sensor PNP]) --> Term1[Input Terminal Block TB1]
    Term1 --> Debounce[RC Debounce Filter Network]
    Debounce --> Opto[Optocoupler Isolation Barrier]
    Opto --> PIIReg[Process Input Image Register in CPU RAM]

    PIIReg --> CPUProg[CPU executes Ladder Logic]
    CPUProg --> POIReg[Process Output Image Register]
    POIReg --> Driver2[Output Driver Transistor Bank]
    Driver2 --> Contactor([AC Contactor Coil 24V DC])
    Contactor --> Motor([3 Phase Induction Motor M])
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

This section contains examination-ready questions modeled directly on actual KTU University papers from previous years. Each question is tagged, fully solved, and accompanied by examiner pitfall warnings.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define a Programmable Logic Controller. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.**

**Model Answer:**

A Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a **ruggedized, solid-state, digital computing device** specifically designed for the real-time automated control of industrial machinery and processes. It executes user-defined logic stored in non-volatile memory and uses a cyclic scan architecture to read inputs, execute logic, and drive outputs.

Four distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Ruggedized construction** — designed to operate in extreme industrial conditions (heat, dust, vibration, electrical noise) where general PCs would fail.
2. **Deterministic, cyclic scan architecture** — guarantees predictable response times; a PC's OS is non-deterministic.
3. **Discrete and analog I/O built-in** — PLCs have native, optically isolated input/output modules for sensors and actuators, whereas PCs require expensive add-on cards.
4. **IEC 61131-3 programming languages** — Ladder Logic, FBD, and Structured Text are industrial standards; general PCs use C/Python/Java with no industrial standardization.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write "PLC is a computer" without specifying **"industrial-grade / ruggedized."** This loses the 1 mark that distinguishes the definition. Always emphasize the *industrial* nature.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Explain the four phases of the PLC scan cycle with a neat block diagram description.**

**Model Answer:**

The PLC scan cycle consists of four sequential phases, repeated continuously while the PLC is in RUN mode:

1. **Input Scan Phase:** The CPU reads the ON/OFF status of every physical input device and stores these states in a dedicated memory region called the **Process Input Image (PII)** table.

2. **Program Scan Phase:** The CPU executes the user's ladder logic program, rung by rung from top to bottom and left to right, using **only** the values present in the PII. Intermediate results are NOT immediately written to physical outputs.

3. **Output Scan Phase:** After the program completes, the CPU transfers the contents of the **Process Output Image (POI)** table to the physical output terminals, energizing or de-energizing the connected field devices.

4. **Housekeeping Phase:** The CPU performs self-diagnostics, services communication requests (HMI, MODBUS, Ethernet), updates internal timer/counter values, and prepares for the next scan cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is to say the program **"directly controls outputs."** This is wrong — the program writes to the **POI**, which is then transferred to physical outputs **only at the end of the program scan**. Missing this distinction costs a full mark.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice — Attempt ANY ONE)

---

### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a PLC and explain the function of **each block** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PLC has $N_{in} = 64$ inputs and $N_{out} = 64$ outputs. The CPU executes $N_{inst} = 1500$ instructions per scan, with average instruction time $t_{avg} = 1.5$ µs. The backplane bus runs at $f_{bus} = 10$ MHz, and housekeeping takes $T_{house} = 2$ ms. Compute **(i)** total scan time and **(ii)** worst-case input-to-output response time. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to 3A(a):**

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid schematic) consists of **five major blocks**:

**1. CPU Module:**
The CPU contains the microprocessor (e.g., 32-bit ARM Cortex), the system ROM (firmware), volatile RAM (working memory), non-volatile Flash/EEPROM (user program), and the process image tables (PII and POI). It performs all arithmetic, logic, and sequencing operations.

**2. Memory Subsystem:**
- *ROM* stores the operating system.
- *RAM* holds runtime data, intermediate coil states, timer/counter current values.
- *Flash/EEPROM* holds the user program permanently even after power-off.

**3. Input Module:**
Receives signals from field sensors (pushbuttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, thermocouples). Performs **electrical isolation** via optocouplers, **signal conditioning** (debouncing, level shifting), and **analog-to-digital conversion** for analog inputs. Standard voltages: 24 V DC, 110 V AC, 230 V AC.

**4. Output Module:**
Drives field actuators using one of three switching technologies: **relay outputs** (universal, slow), **transistor outputs** (DC only, fast), or **triac outputs** (AC only, zero-crossing). Also provides isolation between the CPU and high-power field devices.

**5. Power Supply Unit (PSU):**
Converts mains AC (120 V / 230 V) to regulated DC voltages — typically +5 V for logic, +24 V for I/O, and ±15 V for analog conditioning. Often includes battery backup for memory retention.

**6. Programming Device (Auxiliary):**
A laptop or handheld terminal used to write, upload, download, and monitor the ladder program.

---

#### **Solution to 3A(b):**

**Step 1: Input scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= \frac{N_{in}}{f_{bus}} \\
T_{in} &= \frac{64}{10 \times 10^6} \\
T_{in} &= 6.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_{in} &= 0.0064 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Program scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prog} &= N_{inst} \cdot t_{avg} \\
T_{prog} &= 1500 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \\
T_{prog} &= 2250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_{prog} &= 2.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Output scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= \frac{N_{out}}{f_{bus}} \\
T_{out} &= \frac{64}{10 \times 10^6} \\
T_{out} &= 0.0064 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} \\
T_{scan} &= 0.0064 + 2.25 + 0.0064 + 2 \\
T_{scan} &= 4.2628 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Worst-case response time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,max} &= 2 \cdot T_{scan} \\
T_{resp,max} &= 2 \times 4.2628 \\
T_{resp,max} &= 8.5256 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(i)** Total scan time $\approx \mathbf{4.26 \text{ ms}}$
- **(ii)** Worst-case response time $\approx \mathbf{8.53 \text{ ms}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to add the housekeeping time ($T_{house}$) or mistakenly use $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$ both for inputs (ignoring outputs). Always show **all four** components separately and **unit-convert** at every step (µs → ms). Showing the final answer without intermediate steps can lead to a 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** Explain the **Ladder Logic programming language** with suitable examples. Show how the Boolean functions $Q = A \cdot B + \overline{C}$ and $Q = A \oplus B$ are realized in ladder. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **differences between Relay Logic and PLC-based control**. Discuss the advantages of PLCs over conventional relay panels. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to 3B(a):**

Ladder Logic (LD), standardized under **IEC 61131-3**, is a graphical programming language that visually resembles the schematic of an electrical relay circuit. It consists of two vertical "rails" (left = L, right = N) connected by horizontal "rungs." Each rung contains **contacts** (input conditions) on the left and a **coil** (output action) on the right.

**Example 1: Realization of $Q = A \cdot B + \overline{C}$**

- **Rung 1:** NO contact A `---‖---` in series with NO contact B `---‖---` in series with NC contact C `---‖/---`, terminating in output coil Q `---(Q)---`.
- This rung alone implements $Q = A \cdot B \cdot \overline{C}$.

- **Rung 2 (parallel):** NO contact A `---‖---` in series with NO contact B `---‖---` (again) in series with NC contact C `---‖/---`, terminating in **the same** output coil Q.

- **Combined Boolean logic:** Two identical rungs in parallel implement OR, so the total equation realized is exactly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = (A \cdot B \cdot \overline{C}) + (A \cdot B \cdot \overline{C}) = A \cdot B \cdot \overline{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

To implement the more general form, **Rung 2** would simply contain only contact C as an NC element, so the parallel combination becomes $A \cdot B \cdot \overline{C} + \overline{C}$ which simplifies via Boolean algebra to $\overline{C}$ — therefore **proper rung design** is critical.

**Example 2: Realization of $Q = A \oplus B$ (Exclusive OR)**

Recall the expanded Boolean form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot \overline{B} + \overline{A} \cdot B
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Rung 1:** NO contact A `---‖---` in series with NC contact B `---‖/---`, terminating in coil Q.
- **Rung 2 (parallel):** NC contact A `---‖/---` in series with NO contact B `---‖---`, terminating in the same coil Q.

This two-rung ladder correctly implements the XOR function.

---

#### **Solution to 3B(b):**

| Parameter | Conventional Relay Logic | PLC-Based Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Implementation** | Hard-wired copper conductors and electromechanical relays | Software program in non-volatile memory |
| **Modification** | Requires physical re-wiring (hours, downtime) | Program edit on PC, download in seconds |
| **Cost at Scale** | Increases linearly with I/O count | Marginal cost per I/O is low |
| **Reliability** | Mechanical wear, contact pitting, coil burnout | Solid-state, MTBF > 100,000 hours |
| **Diagnostics** | None — fault-finding by manual probing | Online monitoring, error logs, HMI fault display |
| **Documentation** | Paper schematic (often outdated) | Live program is self-documenting |
| **Space & Weight** | Large cabinets, heavy copper bus bars | Compact modular form factor |
| **Communication** | Standalone only | Native Ethernet, MODBUS, Profibus, Profinet |

**Key Advantages of PLCs:**
1. **Flexibility:** Logic can be modified without re-wiring.
2. **Compactness:** Hundreds of relays fit into one CPU.
3. **Reliability:** Solid-state, no moving contacts to wear out.
4. **Communication:** Seamless integration with HMIs, SCADA, and other PLCs.
5. **Diagnostics:** Built-in fault detection reduces downtime.
6. **Documentation:** Program files serve as up-to-date schematics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list only 3–4 advantages without contrasting with relay logic in a table format. KTU examiners expect a **comparative table** to score the full 7 marks. A simple bullet list usually receives only 4–5 marks.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 2-Minute Revision Snapshot — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] PLC = **Industrial-grade, ruggedized digital computer** for real-time process control.
- [x] First PLC by **Modicon in 1968** for **General Motors**.
- [x] Programming standard: **IEC 61131-3** → 5 languages (LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC).
- [x] Four main blocks: **CPU, Memory, Input Module, Output Module** (+ PSU + Programming Device).
- [x] Memory types: **ROM (firmware), RAM (volatile), Flash/EEPROM (user program)**.
- [x] Output technologies: **Relay (universal/slow), Transistor (DC/fast), Triac (AC/zero-crossing)**.
- [x] Optical isolation via **optocouplers** in every I/O module.
- [x] **Scan Cycle** = Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- [x] **Total Scan Time** formula: $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$.
- [x] **Worst-case response time** = $2 \cdot T_{scan}$; **Best case** = $T_{scan}$.
- [x] **Series contacts** in ladder = Boolean **AND** ($Q = A \cdot B$).
- [x] **Parallel rungs** in ladder = Boolean **OR** ($Q = A + B$).
- [x] **NC contact** = Boolean **NOT** / inversion ($Q = \overline{A}$).
- [x] **On-Delay Timer (TON)** output turns ON **only after** input has been continuously ON for $T_{preset}$ seconds.
- [x] PLC advantages over relays: **flexibility, reliability, compactness, diagnostics, communication**.
- [x] Determinism: PLC scan architecture guarantees **predictable, repeatable timing** — the cornerstone of safety-critical automation.
